content: Examples of exercise movements that take place in these planes include standing barbell curl (sagittal plane), standing lateral dumbbell raise (frontal plane), and dumbbell fly (transverse plane).
summary: This chunk provides examples of exercises performed in different anatomical planes.

content: Strength is the ability to exert force. The weight that a person can lift is probably the oldest quantitative measure of strength. Technological developments have popularized the use of isometric strength testing and, more recently, isokinetic strength testing. Individual differences in the ability to exert force at different speeds may limit the value of isometric and low-speed resistance tests in predicting performance in sports that involve high-speed acceleration. Knuttgen and Kraemer suggested a definition of strength as the maximal force a muscle or muscle group can generate at a specified velocity. Controlling and monitoring velocity during strength testing is required for more meaningful strength scores related to sport ability.
summary: This chunk discusses different definitions and methods of measuring strength.

content: Power is defined as the time rate of doing work. Work is the product of the force exerted on an object and the distance the object moves in the direction of the force. Power can be calculated as the product of force on an object and the object’s velocity in the direction of the force. Power can also be calculated as the product of the object’s velocity and the force on the object in the direction of its travel.
summary: This chunk defines power and its relationship to work and force.

content: Power is defined as the time rate of doing work. Power can be calculated as the product of force on an object and the object’s velocity in the direction of the force. Power can also be calculated as the product of the object’s velocity and the force on the object in the direction of its travel. In the International System of Units (SI), force is measured in newtons (N), distance in meters (m), work in joules (J, i.e., newton-meters, or N·m), time in seconds (s), and power in watts (W, i.e., J/s).
summary: Power is defined as the rate of doing work, calculated as force times velocity. It is measured in watts (J/s).

content: Work is the product of the force exerted on an object and the distance the object moves in the direction of the force. The net work performed when a weight is lifted is equal to the magnitude of the weight multiplied by the vertical distance the weight is lifted. To calculate the work involved in lifting a 100 kg (220-pound) barbell 2 m (6.6 feet) per repetition for 10 repetitions, the weight in SI units (newtons) is calculated by multiplying the mass of the bar in kilograms by the local acceleration due to gravity in meters per second squared. The work for 10 repetitions is calculated by multiplying the weight in newtons by the vertical distance in meters and the number of repetitions. The vertical travel of the bar for one repetition of each exercise is measured for each individual by subtracting the height of the bar relative to the floor at its low position from the height of the bar at its high position. For weight-stack exercises, the vertical travel of the stack is measured.
summary: Work is the product of force and distance. It is calculated by multiplying the weight, distance lifted, and number of repetitions.

content: The work and power equations apply to an object moving from one location to another. Work and power are also required to start an object rotating about an axis or to change the velocity at which it rotates, even if the object as a whole does not move through space at all. The angle through which an object rotates is called its angular displacement, and the SI unit for which is the radian (rad); 1 rad = 180° ÷ p = 57.3°, where p = 3.14. Angular velocity is the object’s rotational speed, measured in radians per second (rad/s).
summary: Work and power apply to both linear and rotational motion.

content: Torque is expressed in newton-meters (N·m), but should not be confused with work, which is also expressed in newton-meters. The difference is that the distance component of the torque unit refers to the length of the moment arm (which is perpendicular to the line of action of the force), while the distance component of the work unit refers to the distance moved along the line of action of the force. Just as for movement through space, the work done in rotating an object is measured in joules (J), and power in watts (W) (47). This equation is used to calculate rotational work: Work = Torque ∙ Angular Displacement (4.4). Equation 4.3 is used to calculate rotational power, just as it was used to calculate linear power.
summary: Torque is distinct from work, though both are measured in newton-meters. Work and power apply to rotational motion as well.

content: The discrepancy between the common and scientific definitions of power has led to misunderstandings. For example, in the sport of powerlifting, which involves high forces but relatively low movement speeds, less mechanical power is produced than in several other sports, including Olympic lifting (10). Despite the discrepancy, the sport of powerlifting is unlikely to be renamed. However, the strength and conditioning professional should use the word power only in its scientific sense in all other contexts to avoid ambiguity.
summary: The common understanding of power differs from the scientific definition. This can lead to confusion, especially in sports like powerlifting.

content: Furthermore, although the word strength is often associated with slow speeds and the word power with high speeds of movement, both variables reflect the ability to exert force at a given speed. Power is a direct mathematical function of force and velocity. Therefore, if at any instant, any two of the variables force, velocity, and power are known, the third can be calculated. If an individual can generate high force or high power at a particular velocity of movement, precisely the same ability is being described, that is, the ability to accelerate a mass at that particular speed. Therefore, it is not correct to associate strength with low speed and power with high speed. Strength is the capacity to exert force at any given speed, and power is the mathematical product of force and velocity at whatever speed.
summary: Strength and power are both related to the ability to exert force, but at different speeds. Power is the product of force and velocity.

content: What is critical is the ability to exert force at speeds characteristic of a given sport to overcome gravity and accelerate the body or an implement. For a sport movement made relatively slow by high resistance, low-speed strength is critical, whereas for a movement that is very fast due to low resistance, high-speed strength is important. For example, when offensive and defensive American football linemen push against each other, their speed of movement is slowed by the muscular force exerted by the opposing player as well as the inertia of the opposing player’s body mass. Because the muscles are prevented from contracting at high speed, the ability to exert force and power at low speed is an important component of performance. In contrast, a badminton player’s muscles quickly reach high velocity as a result of the minimal inertial resistance of the lightweight racket and the player’s arm. Therefore, the ability to exert force and power at high speed is critical to making rapid adjustments in a stroke.
summary: The required speed for strength and power depends on the sport. Some sports require low-speed strength, while others require high-speed strength.

content: Several biomechanical factors are involved in the manifestation of human strength, including neural control, muscle cross-sectional area, muscle fiber arrangement, muscle length, joint angle, muscle contraction velocity, joint angular velocity, and body size. Neural control affects the maximal force output of a muscle by determining which and how many motor units are involved in a muscle contraction (recruitment) and the rate at which the motor units are fired (rate coding) (8). Generally, muscle force is greater when: (a) more motor units are involved in a contraction, (b) the motor units are greater in size, or (c) the rate of firing is faster. Much of the improvement in strength evidenced in the first few weeks of resistance training is attributable to neural adaptations, as the brain learns how to generate
summary: Human strength is influenced by various factors, including neural control, muscle characteristics, and movement mechanics.

content: The ability to exert force and power at high speed is critical to making rapid adjustments in a stroke.  Several biomechanical factors are involved in the manifestation of human strength, including neural control, muscle cross-sectional area, muscle fiber arrangement, muscle length, joint angle, muscle contraction velocity, joint angular velocity, and body size.
summary: This chunk discusses the importance of force and power in activities like swimming and the various biomechanical factors that contribute to human strength.

content: Neural control affects the maximal force output of a muscle by determining which and how many motor units are involved in a muscle contraction (recruitment) and the rate at which the motor units are fired (rate coding) (8). Generally, muscle force is greater when: (a) more motor units are involved in a contraction, (b) the motor units are greater in size, or (c) the rate of firing is faster.
summary: This chunk explains how neural control, specifically motor unit recruitment and firing rate, influences muscle force generation.

content: Much of the improvement in strength evidenced in the first few weeks of resistance training is attributable to neural adaptations, as the brain learns how to generate more efficient motor unit recruitment and firing patterns. It is not unusual for novice resistance trainees to become discouraged when they cannot maintain the rate of increase of the first few training weeks. Continued improvement will result if novice resistance trainees adhere to the training regimen, although via slower mechanisms.
summary: This chunk describes the initial rapid strength gains in resistance training due to neural adaptations and the gradual slowing of progress over time.

content: All else being equal, the force a muscle can exert is related to its cross-sectional area rather than to its volume. If two athletes of similar percent body fat but different height have the same biceps circumference, their upper arm muscle cross-sectional areas are about the same. Although the taller (and therefore heavier) athlete’s longer muscle makes for greater muscle volume, the strength of the two athletes’ biceps should be about the same. With the same strength but greater body weight, the taller athlete has less ability to lift and accelerate his or her own body, as when performing calisthenics or gymnastics. Most elite gymnasts are not very tall.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between muscle cross-sectional area, volume, and strength, highlighting the advantage of smaller body size for certain activities.

content: Resistance training increases both the strength and cross-sectional area of muscle. Maximally contracting muscles have been found capable of generating forces of 23 to 145 psi (16-100 N/cm2) of muscle cross-sectional area. This wide range can be partially accounted for by the variation in the arrangement and alignment of sarcomeres in relation to the long axis of the muscle.
summary: This chunk discusses the effects of resistance training on muscle strength and cross-sectional area, and the factors contributing to the variability in muscle force generation.

content: A pennate muscle has fibers that align obliquely with the tendon, creating a featherlike arrangement. The angle of pennation is defined as the angle between the muscle fibers and an imaginary line between the muscle’s origin and insertion; 0° corresponds to no pennation. Many human muscles are pennated, but few have angles of pennation in excess of 15°. The angle of pennation does not remain constant for a given muscle, but increases as the muscle shortens.
summary: This chunk defines and describes the characteristics of pennate muscle fibers and their angle of pennation.

content: Any factor that affects angle of pennation would thus affect strength and velocity of shortening as long as the cross-sectional area remains the same. Muscles with greater pennation have more sarcomeres in parallel and fewer sarcomeres in series; they are therefore better able to generate force but have a lower maximal shortening velocity than nonpennate muscle. Pennation can be somewhat disadvantageous for generating eccentric, isometric, or low-speed concentric force. Angle of pennation may vary depending on hereditary factors and even training, which could help account for some of the differences in strength and speed seen in individuals who seem to have muscle of the same size.
summary: This chunk explores the relationship between pennation angle, muscle force, and shortening velocity, and its potential influence on individual differences in strength and speed.

content: Although there is a trade-off associated with pennation and it is not the most advantageous arrangement for all muscles, many skeletal muscles are pennate.
summary: This chunk acknowledges the trade-offs associated with pennation but emphasizes its prevalence in skeletal muscles.

content: When a muscle is at its resting length, the actin and myosin filaments lie next to each other, so that a maximal number of potential cross-bridge sites are available. Thus, the muscle can generate the greatest force at its resting length. When the muscle is stretched much beyond its resting length, a smaller proportion of the actin and myosin filaments lie next to each other. Because there are fewer potential cross-bridge sites, the muscle cannot generate as much force as it can at its resting length. When the muscle contracts too much below its resting length, the actin filaments overlap and the number of cross-bridge sites is reduced as well, thereby decreasing force generation capability.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between muscle length, actin-myosin overlap, and force generation.

content: Because all body movements, even those occurring in a straight line, take place by means of rotation about a joint or joints, the forces that muscles produce must be manifested as torques. The amount of torque that can be exerted about a given body joint varies throughout the joint’s range of motion largely because of the relationship of force versus muscle length as well as the ever-changing leverage brought about by the dynamic geometry of the muscles, tendons, and internal joint structures. Additional factors include the type of exercise (isotonic, isometric, etc.), the body joint in question, the muscles used at that joint, and the speed of contraction.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of torque and how it relates to muscle force, joint movement, and various factors influencing torque generation.

content: Classic experiments by A.V. Hill showed that the force capability of muscle declines as the velocity of contraction increases. The relationship is not linear; the decline in force capability is steepest over the lower range of movement speeds.
summary: This chunk describes the inverse relationship between muscle force and contraction velocity, as demonstrated by A.V. Hill's experiments.

content: There are three basic types of muscle action during which forces are generated within the muscle that pull the muscle’s ends toward each other if not prevented from doing so by external forces. The term muscle action is preferable to contraction, because the latter means ‘shortening,’ which does not accurately describe two of the three muscle actions. Concentric muscle action is a muscle action in which the muscle shortens because the contractile force is greater than the resistive force. The forces generated within the muscle and acting to shorten it are greater than the external forces acting at its tendons to stretch it.
summary: This chunk introduces the three types of muscle actions: concentric, eccentric, and isometric, emphasizing the importance of distinguishing between them.

content: Swimming and cycling involve concentric muscle action almost exclusively. Eccentric muscle action is a muscle action in which the muscle lengthens because the contractile force is less than the resistive force.
summary: This chunk provides examples of activities that primarily involve concentric muscle action and defines eccentric muscle action.

content: The term muscle action is preferable to contraction, because the latter means ‘shortening,’ which does not accurately describe two of the three muscle actions. Concentric muscle action is a muscle action in which the muscle shortens because the contractile force is greater than the resistive force. The forces generated within the muscle and acting to shorten it are greater than the external forces acting at its tendons to stretch it. Swimming and cycling involve concentric muscle action almost exclusively.
summary: This chunk defines muscle action, highlighting the three types: concentric, eccentric, and isometric, and provides examples of exercises primarily involving concentric muscle action.

content: Eccentric muscle action is a muscle action in which the muscle lengthens because the contractile force is less than the resistive force. The forces generated within the muscle and acting to shorten it are less than the external forces acting at its tendons to stretch it (which increases the risk of soreness and injury [35]). This occurs during the lowering phase of any resistance exercise. During standard resistance training, the eccentric force exerted by the muscle keeps the weight from being accelerated downward by gravitational force. Thus, the weight moves steadily downward rather than picking up speed and impacting the floor or the athlete’s body.
summary: This chunk describes eccentric muscle action, explaining its characteristics, the risk of soreness and injury, and its role in resistance training.

content: Isometric muscle action is a muscle action in which the muscle length does not change because the contractile force is equal to the resistive force. The forces generated within the muscle and acting to shorten it are equal to the external forces acting at its tendons to stretch it. During a sit-up with the trunk held straight, the abdominal muscles act isometrically to maintain the rigidity of the trunk, while the hip flexors carry out the sit-up movement. In contrast, the abdominal muscles act concentrically and eccentrically during the raising and lowering phases of the curl-up exercise, respectively.
summary: This chunk defines isometric muscle action, providing examples of its application in different exercises.

content: Muscle torque varies with joint angular velocity according to the type of muscular action (figure 4.13). Tests have shown that during isokinetic (constant-speed) concentric exercise by human subjects, torque capability declines as angular velocity increases. In contrast, during eccentric exercise, as joint angular velocity increases, maximal torque capability increases until about 90°/s (1.57 rad/s), after which it declines gradually (27, 45). That means that the greatest muscle force can be obtained during eccentric muscle action.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between muscle torque, joint angular velocity, and different muscle actions, highlighting the greater force potential during eccentric muscle action.

content: Athletes often employ ‘cheating’ movements when a weight cannot be lifted using strict form. For example, an individual who reaches a ‘sticking point’ in the biceps curl exercise due to the limit of concentric elbow flexor strength usually leans the torso back, allowing the elbow flexors to exert increased force by operating isometrically or eccentrically and thereby enabling continued movement of the bar.
summary: This chunk discusses how athletes sometimes use unconventional techniques to overcome lifting limitations, emphasizing the role of isometric and eccentric muscle actions.

content: In sport activities such as sprinting and jumping, the ratio of the strength of the muscles involved in the movement to the mass of the body parts being accelerated is critical. Thus, the strength-to-mass ratio directly reflects an athlete’s ability to accelerate. If, after training, an athlete increases body mass by 15% but increases force capability by only 10%, the strength-to-mass ratio, and thus the athlete’s ability to accelerate, is reduced.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of strength-to-mass ratio for athletic performance, particularly in explosive movements.

content: A sprinter or jumper may benefit by experimenting with muscle mass to determine the highest strength-to-mass ratio, which would result in the best possible performance. In sports involving weight classification, the strength-to-mass ratio is extremely important. If all competitors have the same body mass, the strongest one has a decided advantage.
summary: This chunk explores the strategic considerations of manipulating body mass to optimize strength-to-mass ratio in different sporting contexts.

content: It is normal for the strength-to-mass ratio of larger athletes to be lower than that of smaller athletes because when body size increases, muscle volume (and concomitantly body weight) increases proportionately more than does muscle cross-sectional area (and concomitantly strength) (3). Trial and error can help an athlete determine the weight category in which his or her strength is highest relative to that of other athletes in the weight class. Once an athlete finds his or her optimal weight category, he or she can focus on maximizing strength and minimizing body mass within that category.
summary: This chunk explains the physiological basis for the lower strength-to-mass ratio in larger athletes and suggests strategies for weight class optimization.

content: Smaller athletes are stronger pound for pound than larger athletes. A muscle’s maximal contractile force is fairly proportional to its cross-sectional area, which is related to the square (second power) of linear body dimensions. A muscle’s mass is proportional to its volume, which is related to the cube (third power) of linear body dimensions. As body size increases, body mass increases more rapidly than does muscle strength. Given constant body proportions, the smaller athlete has a higher strength-to-mass ratio than does the larger athlete.
summary: This chunk provides a comparative analysis of strength-to-mass ratio in athletes of different sizes, highlighting the advantages of smaller athletes.

content: The most obvious method for comparing the performances of athletes in different weight categories is to divide the weight lifted by the athlete’s body weight. Such an adjustment is biased against larger athletes because it does not take into account the expected drop in the strength-to-mass ratio with increasing body size. Various formulas have been derived to compare loads lifted more equitably. In the classic formula, the load lifted is divided by body weight to the two-thirds power, thus accounting for the relationship of cross-sectional area versus volume. Other formulas have been developed because the classic formula seemed to favor athletes of middle body weight over lighter and heavier athletes.
summary: This chunk discusses methods for comparing athletic performance across different weight categories, acknowledging the limitations of simple weight-to-load ratios and introducing alternative formulas.

content: The classic formula’s determination that the performances of medium-weight athletes are usually the best may indeed be unbiased. Because of the bell-shaped curve describing the normal distribution of anthropometric characteristics among the population, the body weights of a majority of people are clustered close to the mean.
summary: This chunk concludes by considering the potential bias in performance comparisons and the influence of population distribution on weight categories.

content: Various formulas have been derived to compare loads lifted more equitably. One would expect the weight category with the largest number of competitors to produce the best performers. Because of the bell-shaped curve describing the normal distribution of anthropometric characteristics among the population, the body weights of a majority of people are clustered close to the mean. The classic formula’s determination that the performances of medium-weight athletes are usually the best may indeed be unbiased. Other formulas have been developed because the classic formula seemed to favor athletes of middle body weight over lighter and heavier athletes.
summary: This chunk discusses the rationale behind various formulas used to compare weightlifting performance, considering the distribution of body weights and the potential bias towards medium-weight athletes.

content: The classic formula, the load lifted is divided by body weight to the two-thirds power, thus accounting for the relationship of cross-sectional area versus volume.
summary: This chunk explains the classic formula for comparing weightlifting performance, which accounts for the relationship between cross-sectional area and volume.

content: The most common sources of resistance for strength training exercises are gravity, inertia, friction, fluid resistance, and elasticity.
summary: This chunk lists the common sources of resistance encountered in strength training exercises.

content: The downward force on an object from the pull of gravity, otherwise called the object’s weight, is equal to the object’s mass times the local acceleration due to gravity: Fg = m · ag. The acceleration due to gravity can vary by geographic location. Weighing a barbell on a calibrated spring or electronic scale shows its actual weight. A balance scale determines only the object’s mass, so its weight (Fg) must be calculated using equation 4.5 if a spring or electronic scale is not available.
summary: This chunk defines weight, explains its relationship to mass and gravity, and describes methods for measuring weight.

content: Popular terminology for weight and mass is often incorrect. Some barbell and stack-machine plates are labeled in pounds. The pound is a unit of force, not mass. Only the mass of a barbell plate stays constant, while its weight varies according to the local acceleration due to gravity. The kilogram designation on a weight plate refers to its mass. It is not correct to say that an object weighs a certain number of kilograms, since weight refers to force, not mass. One should say “The mass of the barbell is 85 kg.”
summary: This chunk clarifies the distinction between weight and mass, addresses common misconceptions in terminology, and provides correct ways to express weight and mass.

content: The amount of mass an individual can lift will be slightly affected by terrestrial location, due to variations in the acceleration due to gravity around the globe. The gravitational force on an object always acts downward.
summary: This chunk explains how variations in gravity can slightly affect the amount of weight an individual can lift.

content: The weight exerts less resistive torque when it is horizontally closer to a joint and more resistive torque when it is horizontally farther from a joint. In an arm curl, the horizontal distance from the elbow to the barbell is greatest when the forearm is horizontal. The moment arm decreases as the forearm rotates either upward or downward away from the horizontal, decreasing the resistive torque arising from the weight. When the weight is directly above or below the elbow pivot point, there is no resistive torque from the weight.
summary: This chunk describes the relationship between weight, torque, and joint position, explaining how the horizontal distance from a joint affects the resistive torque.

content: Exercise technique can affect the resistive torque pattern during an exercise and can shift stress among muscle groups. In the back squat, a more forward inclination of the trunk brings the weight horizontally closer to the knees, thus reducing the resistive torque about the knees that the quadriceps must counteract. In the back squat, a more forward inclination of the trunk brings the weight horizontally farther from the hip, increasing the resistive torque about the hip that the gluteus and hamstring muscles must counteract.
summary: This chunk explains how exercise technique can influence the distribution of torque and muscle activation during exercises like the back squat.

content: When the barbell is positioned as low as possible on the upper back, the athlete must incline the trunk relatively far forward to keep the center of mass of body plus bar over the feet, thereby avoiding a fall. Because the bar is then horizontally far from the hip and close to the knee, stress is focused on the hip extensors and to a lesser extent on the knee extensors.
summary: This chunk describes the biomechanics of the back squat, particularly the impact of barbell position on trunk inclination and muscle activation.

content: As with free weights, gravity is the source of resistance for weight-stack machines. By means of pulleys, cams, cables, and gears, weight-stack machines provide increased control over the direction and pattern of resistance. The likelihood of injury as a result of being hit by, tripping over, or being trapped under a weight is reduced when using weight-stack machines. It requires less skill to maintain control of a weight stack than a free weight. Machines can be designed to provide resistance to body movements that are difficult to resist with free weights (e.g., lat pulldown, hip adduction and abduction, leg curl). To some extent, the pattern of resistance can be engineered into a machine.
summary: This chunk compares free weights and weight-stack machines, highlighting the advantages and disadvantages of each.

content: Many people who fear they lack the coordination or technique to safely lift free weights feel confident when using machines. It is quicker and easier to select a weight by inserting a pin in a stack than by mounting plates on a bar.
summary: This chunk discusses the psychological and practical benefits of using weight-stack machines for certain individuals.

content: Free weight exercises are often performed in the standing position with the weight supported by the entire body, taxing a larger portion of the body’s musculature and skeleton than a weight-stack machine would. Such weight-bearing exercise promotes bone mineralization, helping to prevent osteoporosis in later life (44). The movement of a free weight is constrained by the athlete rather than by a machine, requiring muscles to work in stabilization as well as support.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the advantages of free weight exercises for overall strength, bone health, and muscle activation.

content: Many people who fear they lack the coordination or technique to safely lift free weights feel confident when using machines. Free weight exercises are often performed in the standing position with the weight supported by the entire body, taxing a larger portion of the body’s musculature and skeleton than a weight-stack machine would. Such weight-bearing exercise promotes bone mineralization, helping to prevent osteoporosis in later life (44).
summary: Free weights are often preferred over machines due to their full-body engagement and bone-strengthening benefits.

content: It is quicker and easier to select a weight by inserting a pin in a stack than by mounting plates on a bar. Machines tend to isolate single muscle groups; the lifting of free weights involves the more natural coordination of several muscle groups. The movement of a free weight is constrained by the athlete rather than by a machine, requiring muscles to work in stabilization as well as support.
summary: Free weights offer advantages in terms of weight selection and natural movement patterns compared to machines.

content: Structural exercises, such as the power clean and the snatch, are particularly useful in providing training stimulus for a major portion of the body’s musculature. The lifting and acceleration of objects represent a major part of sport and other physically demanding activities.
summary: Compound exercises like power cleans and snatches effectively engage large muscle groups, mimicking real-world movements.

content: Nautilus Sports/Medical Industries popularized the concept of tailoring resistive torque through the range of joint motion by creating an exercise machine that uses a cam of variable radius that changes the length of the moment arm through which the weight stack acts (figure 4.14). The rationale was to provide more resistance at points in the range of motion where the muscles could exert greater torque and less resistance where the muscles could apply less torque.
summary: Cam-based machines aim to provide variable resistance throughout a movement, mimicking natural muscle torque patterns.

content: For the system to work as planned, however, the athlete has to move at a constant, slow angular velocity, which is difficult to do consistently. Cam-based machines frequently fail to match normal human torque capability patterns (14, 26).
summary: Cam-based machines can be challenging to use effectively and may not accurately replicate natural movement patterns.

content: A barbell or weight stack, when accelerated, exerts inertial force on the athlete. The upward force an athlete exerts equals the weight lifted plus any inertial force, which is the mass times the upward acceleration of the bar. Horizontal bar acceleration occurs if the athlete exerts net force on the bar directed to the front, back, left, or right.
summary: Accelerating a barbell or weight stack introduces inertial force, requiring the athlete to exert additional force.

content: All exercises involve some acceleration at the beginning to bring the bar from a zero to an upward velocity and some deceleration near the top of the exercise to bring the bar’s velocity back to zero. With this acceleration pattern, the agonist muscles receive resistance in excess of bar weight early in the range of motion, but resistance less than bar weight toward the end of the range of motion.
summary: Acceleration and deceleration are inherent to all weightlifting exercises, influencing resistance patterns throughout the movement.

content: The athlete decelerates the bar by either: (a) reducing upward force on the bar to less than bar weight to let some or all of the bar’s weight decelerate it or (b) pushing down against the bar using the antagonist muscles.
summary: Athletes control the deceleration of a weight by adjusting their upward force or engaging antagonist muscles.

content: Compared to a slow exercise with minimal acceleration of a given weight, an exercise involving higher acceleration (an “explosive” exercise) provides greater resistance to the muscles involved early in the range of motion and less resistance to the muscles involved toward the end of the range of motion. However, heavier weights can be handled in accelerative exercises than in slow exercises, allowing near-maximal resistance to be attained for all muscles involved in the exercise.
summary: Explosive exercises involve greater resistance early in the movement and less resistance later, allowing for heavier weights and full-body engagement.

content: During a power clean of a heavy weight, for example, the strong leg, hip, and back muscles accelerate the bar vertically to a high enough velocity that, even though the weaker upper body muscles cannot exert vertical force equal to the bar’s weight, the bar continues to travel upward until the force of gravity decelerates it to zero velocity at the highest bar position.
summary: Explosive exercises like the power clean demonstrate how acceleration allows for heavier weights to be lifted.

content: Although acceleration changes the nature of an exercise and makes resistance patterns less predictable, acceleration in resistance training is not necessarily undesirable. Because acceleration is characteristic of natural movements in sport and daily life, resistance training exercises involving acceleration probably produce desirable neuromuscular training effects.
summary: Acceleration in training mimics real-world movements and can enhance neuromuscular function.

content: Olympic lifting exercises such as the snatch and the clean and jerk are effective for improving the ability to produce high accelerations against heavy resistance. Acceleration and deceleration are characteristic of virtually all natural movements.
summary: Olympic lifts are excellent for developing explosive power and improving the ability to accelerate and decelerate.

content: For example, sprinting requires the athlete’s arms and legs to go through repeated cycles of acceleration and deceleration. Throwing a baseball, discus, shot, or javelin all involve sequences of body movements that accelerate the objects to high release speeds.
summary: Acceleration and deceleration are fundamental to many sports and everyday activities.

content: Because acceleration is a particular kind of movement pattern, training with accelerative movements can provide specificity of training. That is why explosive exercises, such as the power clean and high pull, are used in training for many different sports in which the leg and hip muscles provide force for accelerating the body.
summary: Training with accelerative movements can enhance performance in sports requiring explosive power.

content: The bracketing technique, in which the athlete performs the sport movement with less than normal and greater than normal resistance, is another form of acceleration training.
summary: The bracketing technique involves varying resistance to simulate real-world movement patterns.

content: According to the force–velocity relationship of muscle, a shot-putter who trains with an extra-heavy shot develops greater forces during the  When a heavier shot is used in a putting exercise, the muscle contracts at a relatively low speed due to the inertia of the heavier implement. When a lighter shot is used in a putting exercise, the lower inertia of the shot enables the putter to accelerate the shot more rapidly and reach a higher speed of release.
summary: Training with varying shot weights can improve force production and throwing speed.

content: The same principle of using different weights for varying resistance can be applied to sprint training, where an athlete pulls a small parachute for greater resistance at lower speed and is pulled by an elastic cord for lower resistance at greater speed.
summary: Varying resistance during sprint training can enhance performance at different speeds.

content: When a weight is held in a static position or moved at a constant velocity, it exerts constant resistance only in the downward direction. Upward or lateral acceleration of a weight requires additional force.
summary: Accelerating a weight in any direction requires additional force beyond the weight's gravity.

content: Friction is the resistive force encountered when one attempts to move an object while it is pressed against another object. Exercise devices that use friction as the main source of resistance include belt- or brake pad–resisted cycle ergometers and wrist curl devices.
summary: Friction-based exercise devices provide resistance through the force required to overcome friction.

content: The formula FR = k · FN represents the resistive force (FR) encountered in friction-based exercise devices, where k is the coefficient of friction and FN is the normal force.
summary: The resistive force in friction-based devices is determined by the coefficient of friction and the normal force.

content: The coefficient of friction for initiating movement is different from the coefficient of friction for maintaining movement.
summary: Friction varies depending on whether the object is being started or maintained in motion.

content: Upward or lateral acceleration of a weight requires additional force. It takes more force to initiate movement between two surfaces in contact than to maintain previously initiated movement.  For outdoor training, weighted sleds do not provide consistently repeatable resistance.  It takes more force to get a weighted sled moving than to keep it moving because the coefficient of static friction is always greater than the coefficient of sliding friction. Once a weighted sled is moving, the coefficient of sliding friction stays relatively constant, so that friction resistance does not change as speed increases.
summary: This chunk discusses the forces involved in initiating and maintaining movement, specifically focusing on the challenges of using weighted sleds for training.

content: Friction is the resistive force encountered when one attempts to move an object while it is pressed against another object. The formula FR = k · FN represents the resistive force (FR) encountered in friction-based exercise devices, where k is the coefficient of friction and FN is the normal force. The coefficient of friction for initiating movement is different from the coefficient of friction for maintaining movement. A friction-resisted exercise device requires a relatively high force to initiate movement and a relatively constant force after movement has begun, regardless of the movement speed. Resistance provided by friction-based devices can be adjusted through a mechanism that alters the normal force. Exercise devices that use friction as the main source of resistance include belt- or brake pad–resisted cycle ergometers and wrist curl devices.
summary: This chunk defines friction and its role in exercise devices, explaining how it affects resistance and how it can be manipulated.

content: A weighted sled used in training for football or track is an example of a device that is resisted by both friction and inertia. The resistance due to a sled’s inertia is directly proportional to both the sled’s mass and its acceleration. The resistance due to the friction between a sled’s runners and the ground is proportional to both the friction coefficient between surfaces in contact and the net force pressing the sled against the ground, which equals the gravitational force minus any upward force exerted by the individual pushing the sled. Mass can be added to a weighted sled to increase the gravitational force. The friction coefficient varies with the surface on which the sled rests (e.g., sand, bare soil, dry grass, wet grass).
summary: This chunk describes the specific resistance forces acting on a weighted sled and how these forces are influenced by various factors.

content: It takes more force to get a weighted sled moving than to keep it moving because the coefficient of static friction is always greater than the coefficient of sliding friction. Once a weighted sled is moving, the coefficient of sliding friction stays relatively constant, so that friction resistance does not change as speed increases.  Weighted sleds are useful in providing horizontal resistance, which cannot be directly provided by weights.
summary: This chunk focuses on the differences in friction coefficients and their implications for moving a weighted sled.

content: The resistive force encountered by an object moving through a fluid (liquid or gas), or by a fluid moving past or around an object or through an orifice, is called fluid resistance. Fluid resistance is a significant factor in sport activities such as swimming, rowing, golf, sprinting, discus throwing, and baseball pitching. Fluid-resisted exercise machines most often use cylinders in which a piston forces fluid through an orifice as the exercise movement is performed. The resistive force in fluid-resisted exercise machines is greater when the piston is pushed faster, when the orifice is smaller, or when the fluid viscosity is higher. Resistance is roughly proportional to the velocity of piston movement. FR = k · v (4.7) FR is the resistive force. k is a constant that reflects the physical characteristics of the cylinder and piston, the viscosity of the fluid, and the number, size, and shape of the orifices. v is piston velocity relative to the cylinder.
summary: This chunk defines fluid resistance and its importance in various sports, explaining how fluid-resisted exercise machines work.

content: Fluid cylinders provide resistance that increases with speed. Fluid cylinders allow rapid acceleration early in the exercise movement and little acceleration after higher speeds are reached. Movement speed is thus kept within an intermediate range. Fluid-resisted machines limit changes in velocity to a certain extent. Some machines have adjustment knobs that allow the orifice size to be changed. A larger orifice allows the user to reach a higher movement speed before the fluid resistive force curtails the ability to accelerate.
summary: This chunk describes the characteristics of fluid-resisted exercise machines, focusing on how they provide resistance and control movement speed.

content: Fluid-resisted machines do not generally provide an eccentric exercise phase, but they may if they incorporate an internal pump. With a free weight, a muscle group acts concentrically while raising the weight and eccentrically while lowering it. With fluid-resisted machines without eccentric resistance, a muscle group acts concentrically while performing the primary exercise movement, and the antagonist muscle group acts concentrically while returning to the starting position. Whereas free weights or weight machines involve alternate concentric and eccentric actions of the same muscle with little or no rest in between, fluid-resisted machines generally involve alternate concentric actions of antagonistic muscle groups; each muscle group rests while its antagonist works. The lack of eccentric muscle action with fluid-resisted machines means that such exercise probably does not provide optimal training for the many sport movements that involve eccentric muscle actions (e.g., running, jumping, and throwing).
summary: This chunk compares the eccentric and concentric muscle actions in free weight exercises versus fluid-resisted machines, highlighting the limitations of the latter for certain types of training.

content: A number of exercise devices, particularly those designed for home use, have elastic components such as springs, bands, bows, or rods as their source of resistance.
summary: This chunk introduces elastic resistance as a common feature in exercise devices, particularly those for home use.

content: With a free weight, a muscle group acts concentrically while raising the weight and eccentrically while lowering it. Whereas free weights or weight machines involve alternate concentric and eccentric actions of the same muscle with little or no rest in between, fluid-resisted machines generally involve alternate concentric actions of antagonistic muscle groups; each muscle group rests while its antagonist works. The lack of eccentric muscle action with fluid-resisted machines means that such exercise probably does not provide optimal training for the many sport movements that involve eccentric muscle actions (e.g., running, jumping, and throwing).
summary: Describes the concentric and eccentric actions of muscles during different types of resistance training.

content: A number of exercise devices, particularly those designed for home use, have elastic components such as springs, bands, bows, or rods as their source of resistance. The resistance provided by a standard elastic component is proportional to the distance it is stretched: FR = k · x (4.8) FR is the resistive force. k is a constant that reflects the physical characteristics of the elastic component. x is the distance that the elastic component is stretched beyond its resting length. The most obvious characteristic of elastic resistance is that the more the elastic component is stretched, the greater the resistance.
summary: Explains the properties of elastic resistance in exercise devices.

content: The problem with devices using elastic resistance is that every exercise movement begins with low resistance and ends with high resistance. Another problem with elasticity-resisted machines is that the adjustability of resistance is usually limited by the number of elastic components that are available to provide resistance to a movement. An effective resistance exercise device must incorporate enough variation in resistive force that the number of repetitions the trainee can perform is kept within a desirable range.
summary: Discusses the limitations of elastic resistance in exercise devices.

content: There are products that provide resistance to vertical jumping with elastic bands as a means of developing jumping power. The elastic bands provide little resistance early in the jump when the large gluteus and quadriceps muscles are capable of exerting great force. The bands provide the greatest resistance while the jumper is in the air—serving mainly to pull the jumper back to the ground, rather than resist the muscles, and to increase the speed at which the jumper hits the ground on landing, which may increase injury risk.
summary: Critiques the use of elastic bands for developing jumping power.

content: Power equals the product of force and velocity. When force is exerted on a weight in the direction opposite to the one in which the weight is moving (as when a weight is lowered in a controlled manner), calculated power has a negative sign, as does calculated work. All such “negative” power and work occur during eccentric muscle activity, such as lowering a weight or decelerating at the end of a rapid movement. Strictly speaking, there is no such thing as negative work or power. The term negative work really refers to work performed on, rather than by, a muscle.
summary: Defines power and work in the context of eccentric muscle activity.

content: The rate at which repetitions are performed determines the power output.
summary: Explains the relationship between repetition rate and power output.

content: As with any physical activity, there is a degree of risk with resistance training. Rates of injury are the highest for team sports; intermediate for running and aerobics; and lowest for cycling, walking, and resistance training, the latter of which has about four injuries per 1,000 hours of participation. A study of collegiate American football players (50) showed only 0.35 resistance training–related injuries per 100 players per season. Injuries due to resistance training accounted for only 0.74% of the in-season injury-related time loss of the players.
summary: Compares the risk of injury across different physical activities, highlighting the relatively low risk of resistance training.

content: Humans normally stand upright, with the vertebral bones stacked one on top of another, separated by rubbery disks. The advantage we gain from our upright posture and free use of the arms and hands is accompanied by the disadvantage of having our intervertebral disks under compressive force even when we are merely standing, sitting, walking, or running, and under even more compressive force when we are lifting and carrying. When we are in a standing position, any force we exert with the upper body must be transmitted through the back to the legs and ground.
summary: Describes the biomechanics of the human spine and the forces acting on it during upright posture and movement.

content: The back muscles act at a great mechanical disadvantage and must generate forces much greater than the weight of an object lifted. Back injury can be extremely debilitating, persistent, and difficult to remedy. Thus, every effort should be made to avoid back injury during resistance training.
summary: Emphasizes the importance of avoiding back injury during resistance training due to the high risk and severity of back injuries.

content: It has been observed that 85% to 90% of all intervertebral disk herniations occur at the disk between the lowest two lumbar vertebrae (L4 and L5) or between the lowest lumbar and the top sacral vertebra (L5 and S1) (5). When a weight is supported in the hands or on the shoulders and the trunk is inclined forward, there is great torque about the lower intervertebral disks due to the large horizontal distance between the lower back and the weight.
summary: Highlights the specific locations of intervertebral disk herniations and the biomechanical factors that contribute to them.

content: The back muscles operate at an extremely low mechanical advantage because the perpendicular distance from the line of action of the spinal erector muscles to the intervertebral disks is much shorter (about 2 inches, or 5 cm) than the horizontal distance from the weight to the disks. As a result, the muscles must exert forces that frequently exceed 10 times the weight lifted. These forces act to squeeze the intervertebral disks between the adjacent vertebral bodies and can lead to injury.
summary: Explains the mechanical disadvantage of back muscles during lifting and the resulting risk of injury.

content: The flat-back lifting posture has been found to be better overall than a rounded (opposite of arched) back in minimizing L5/S1 compressive forces and ligament strain (2). A normal, slightly arched (lordotic) back has been found to be superior to a rounded back for avoiding injury to vertebrae, disks, facet joints, ligaments, and muscles of the back. In addition, the low back muscles are capable of exerting considerably higher forces when the back is arched rather than rounded (20).
summary: Recommends a flat-back or slightly arched lifting posture to minimize back injury risk.

content: The vertebral column is naturally S-shaped, being slightly rounded (kyphotic) in the thoracic spine and lordotic in the lumbar spine. The wedged shape of the vertebrae gives the spine its natural curve. However, the intervertebral disks are flat when the back is in its S shape. When the lower back is rounded, the ventral (toward the anterior) edges of the vertebral bodies squeeze the front portions of the intervertebral disks. Extreme arching of the back results in squeezing the dorsal (toward the posterior) portions of the disks. Such uneven squeezing of the intervertebral disks likely increases the risk of disk rupture. Exercises should generally be performed with the lower back in a moderately arched position.
summary: The natural S-shape of the spine and the impact of different back positions on intervertebral disks are discussed, emphasizing the importance of a moderately arched lower back during exercises.

content: As a result, the muscles must exert forces that frequently exceed 10 times the weight lifted. These forces act to squeeze the intervertebral disks between the adjacent vertebral bodies and can lead to injury. A normal, slightly arched (lordotic) back has been found to be superior to a rounded back for avoiding injury to vertebrae, disks, facet joints, ligaments, and muscles of the back. In addition, the low back muscles are capable of exerting considerably higher forces when the back is arched rather than rounded (20). The flat-back lifting posture has been found to be better overall than a rounded (opposite of arched) back in minimizing L5/S1 compressive forces and ligament strain (2).
summary: The forces involved in lifting and the importance of maintaining a neutral or slightly arched back to minimize injury risk are highlighted.

content: The abdomen is composed mainly of fluid and normally contains very little gas, making it virtually incompressible. The abdominal fluids and tissue kept under pressure by tensing surrounding muscle (deep abdominal muscles and diaphragm) have been described as a ‘fluid ball’ that aids in supporting the vertebral column during resistance training. Such support may significantly reduce both the forces required by the erector spinae muscles to perform an exercise and the associated compressive forces on the disks.
summary: The role of intra-abdominal pressure generated by the diaphragm and abdominal muscles in supporting the spine during exercise is explained.

content: The Valsalva maneuver is not necessary for generation of intra-abdominal pressure. In the Valsalva maneuver, the glottis is closed, thus keeping air from escaping the lungs, and the muscles of the abdomen and rib cage contract, creating rigid compartments of liquid in the lower torso and air in the upper torso. An advantage of the Valsalva maneuver is that it increases the rigidity of the entire torso, making it easier to support heavy loads. Pressure in the chest associated with the Valsalva maneuver can have the undesirable side effect of exerting compressive force on the heart, making it more difficult for blood to return to the heart. The Valsalva maneuver can transiently elevate blood pressure to high levels, with the potential risk of blackout.
summary: The Valsalva maneuver and its potential benefits and risks are described, emphasizing safer alternatives for generating intra-abdominal pressure.

content: The diaphragm and the abdominal muscles can contract without the glottis being closed, however, creating the fluid ball in the abdomen without pressurizing the chest compartment. This must be regarded as the safest way to add support to the lower spine without building up pressure in the chest and is the technique that should be used for most resistance training. One can build up intra-abdominal pressure without building up chest pressure by consciously keeping the airway open. During a strenuous repetition, the abdominal muscles and diaphragm contract reflexively, even with the airway open.
summary: Safer techniques for generating intra-abdominal pressure without the risks associated with the Valsalva maneuver are presented.

content: Athletes, particularly those who compete in Olympic lifting or powerlifting, may choose to use the Valsalva maneuver if they recognize and accept the risks involved and have the experience to avoid increasing pressure to the point of blackout. Weightlifting belts have been shown to increase intra-abdominal pressure during resistance training and are therefore probably effective in improving safety. It has been cautioned, however, that if an athlete performs all of the exercises with a belt, the abdominal muscles that produce intra-abdominal pressure might not get enough training stimulus to develop optimally. It is particularly risky for an individual who has become accustomed to wearing a belt to suddenly perform an exercise without one, because the abdominal musculature might not be capable of generating enough intra-abdominal pressure to significantly reduce erector spinae muscle forces. The resulting excessive compressive forces on the disks could increase the chance of back injury. A weight belt is not needed for exercises that do not directly affect the lower back. For exercises directly stressing the back, an individual should refrain from wearing a belt.
summary: The use of weight belts and the Valsalva maneuver in weightlifting is discussed, along with the potential benefits and risks.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat exercise, its primary muscle targets, and a typical training prescription are described.

content: An experiment conducted by Harman and Frykman showed that wraps can improve performance. Through a spring effect alone, heavy wraps around the knees added an average of 25 pounds (110 N) to squat lifting force. The notion that wraps work only by stabilizing the knee, lessening the athlete’s fear of injury, or providing a kinesthetic cue is incorrect. The wraps actually provide direct help in extending the knee. On the basis of the lack of evidence that knee wraps prevent injury and the opinion of a number of health practitioners who assert that knee wraps can actually cause injury, athletes should probably minimize the use of wraps.
summary: The effects of knee wraps on squat performance and the potential risks associated with their use are discussed.

content: An experiment conducted by Harman and Frykman showed that wraps can improve performance. Through a spring effect alone, heavy wraps around the knees added an average of 25 pounds (110 N) to squat lifting force. The notion that wraps work only by stabilizing the knee, lessening the athlete’s fear of injury, or providing a kinesthetic cue is incorrect. The wraps actually provide direct help in extending the knee.
summary: Knee wraps enhance squat performance through a spring effect and direct knee extension assistance, contrary to the belief that they primarily function for stabilization or psychological cues.

content: If used at all, knee wraps should be limited to the sets with the heaviest loads. On the basis of the lack of evidence that knee wraps prevent injury and the opinion of a number of health practitioners who assert that knee wraps can actually cause injury, athletes should probably minimize the use of wraps.
summary: Knee wrap usage should be minimized, restricted to heavy lifting sets, due to a lack of injury prevention evidence and potential for harm.

content: The concept of specificity, widely recognized in the field of resistance training, holds that training is most effective when resistance exercises are similar to the sport activity in which improvement is sought. Although all athletes should use well-rounded, whole-body exercise routines, supplementary exercises specific to the sport can provide a training advantage and reduce the likelihood of injury.
summary: Specificity in training emphasizes exercises resembling the target sport to maximize effectiveness and reduce injury risk. While whole-body routines are essential, sport-specific exercises offer additional benefits.

content: The simplest and most straightforward way to implement the principle of specificity is to select exercises similar to the target activity with regard to the joints about which movements occur and the directions of movement. In addition, joint ranges of motion in the training exercises should be at least as great as those in the target activity.
summary: Specificity is achieved by selecting exercises that mimic the target activity's joint movements and range of motion.

content: Biomechanical analysis of human movement can be used to quantitatively analyze the target activity. In the absence of the requisite equipment and expertise, however, simple visual observation is adequate for identifying the basic features of a sport movement. Exercises can then be selected that involve similar movement around the same joints. Slow-motion videotape can facilitate the observation. Moderately priced software is available (e.g., Dartfish) that enables more detailed analysis of sport movements captured in digital video.
summary: Analyzing a sport movement, either quantitatively or visually, helps identify key joint actions, guiding exercise selection for specificity.

content: Only movements in the frontal, sagittal, and transverse planes are considered because, although few body movements occur only in these three major planes, there is enough overlap of training effects that exercising muscles within the planes also strengthens them for movements between the planes.
summary: Training focuses on movements within the frontal, sagittal, and transverse planes, as exercises targeting these planes effectively strengthen muscles used in various movement combinations.

content: Specificity is a major consideration when one is designing an exercise program to improve performance in a particular sport activity. The sport movement must be analyzed qualitatively or quantitatively to determine the specific joint movements that contribute to the whole-body.
summary: Specificity in exercise program design is crucial for improving performance in a particular sport, requiring analysis of the sport's specific joint movements.

content: The wrist—frontal plane involves ulnar deviation. The exercise specific wrist curl targets ulnar deviation. Baseball bat swing involves ulnar deviation. The wrist—frontal plane involves radial deviation. The exercise specific wrist curl targets radial deviation. Golf backswing involves radial deviation.
summary: Wrist movements in the frontal plane (ulnar and radial deviation) are targeted by specific wrist curls, mirroring actions in baseball and golf.

content: The wrist—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise wrist curl targets flexion. Basketball free throw involves flexion. The wrist—sagittal plane involves extension. The exercise wrist extension targets extension. Racquetball backhand involves extension.
summary: Wrist movements in the sagittal plane (flexion and extension) are targeted by specific wrist curls, mirroring actions in basketball and racquetball.

content: The shoulder—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise front shoulder raise targets flexion. Boxing uppercut punch involves flexion. The shoulder—sagittal plane involves extension. The exercise neutral-grip seated row targets extension. Freestyle swimming stroke involves extension.
summary: Shoulder movements in the sagittal plane (flexion and extension) are targeted by specific exercises, mirroring actions in boxing and swimming.

content: The elbow—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise biceps curl targets flexion. Bowling involves flexion. The elbow—sagittal plane involves extension.
summary: Elbow movements in the sagittal plane (flexion and extension) are targeted by specific exercises, mirroring actions in bowling.

content: Freestyle swimming stroke involves extension. Shot put involves extension. The elbow—sagittal plane involves extension. The exercise triceps pushdown targets extension.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving extension in various body parts.

content: The elbow—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise biceps curl targets flexion. Bowling involves flexion. Somersault involves flexion. The neck—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise neck machine targets flexion.
summary: This chunk focuses on exercises and movements involving flexion in the elbow and neck.

content: The shoulder—transverse plane involves internal rotation. The exercise arm wrestle movement (with dumbbell or cable) targets internal rotation. Baseball pitch involves internal rotation.
summary: This chunk details exercises and movements involving internal rotation of the shoulder.

content: The shoulder—transverse plane involves external rotation. The exercise reverse arm wrestle movement targets external rotation. Karate block involves external rotation.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving external rotation of the shoulder.

content: The shoulder—frontal plane involves adduction. The exercise wide-grip lat pulldown targets adduction. Swimming breast stroke involves adduction. The shoulder—transverse plane (upper arm to 90 degrees to trunk) involves adduction. The exercise dumbbell chest fly targets adduction. Tennis forehand involves adduction.
summary: This chunk focuses on exercises and movements involving adduction of the shoulder.

content: The shoulder—frontal plane involves abduction. The exercise wide-grip shoulder press targets abduction. Springboard diving involves abduction. The shoulder—transverse plane (upper arm to 90 degrees to trunk) involves abduction. The exercise bent-over lateral raise targets abduction. Tennis backhand involves abduction.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving abduction of the shoulder.

content: The neck—sagittal plane involves extension. The exercise dynamic back bridge targets extension. Back flip involves extension.
summary: This chunk focuses on exercises and movements involving extension of the neck.

content: The neck—frontal plane involves left tilt. The exercise neck machine targets left tilt. Slalom skiing involves left tilt.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving left tilt of the neck.

content: The neck—frontal plane involves right tilt. The exercise neck machine targets right tilt. Slalom skiing involves right tilt.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving right tilt of the neck.

content: The neck—transverse plane involves left rotation. The exercise manual resistance targets left rotation. Wrestling movement involves left rotation.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving left rotation of the neck.

content: The exercise neck machine targets right tilt. Slalom skiing involves right tilt. The exercise side bend targets right tilt. Basketball hook shot involves right tilt.
summary: Exercises and movements involving right tilt include neck machine, slalom skiing, side bend, and basketball hook shot.

content: The neck—transverse plane involves left rotation. The exercise manual resistance targets left rotation. Wrestling movement involves left rotation.
summary: Left rotation is involved in neck transverse plane movements, manual resistance exercises, and wrestling.

content: The neck—transverse plane involves right rotation. The exercise manual resistance targets right rotation. Wrestling movement involves right rotation.
summary: Right rotation is involved in neck transverse plane movements, manual resistance exercises, and wrestling.

content: The lower back—frontal plane involves left tilt. The exercise medicine ball overhead hook throw targets left tilt. Gymnastics side aerial involves left tilt.
summary: Left tilt is involved in lower back frontal plane movements, medicine ball overhead hook throws, and gymnastics side aerials.

content: The lower back—frontal plane involves right tilt.
summary: Right tilt is involved in lower back frontal plane movements.

content: The lower back—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise sit-up targets flexion. Javelin throw follow-through involves flexion.
summary: Flexion is involved in lower back sagittal plane movements, sit-ups, and javelin throw follow-throughs.

content: The lower back—sagittal plane involves extension. The exercise stiff-leg deadlift targets extension. Back flip involves extension.
summary: Extension is involved in lower back sagittal plane movements, stiff-leg deadlifts, and back flips.

content: The hip—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise leg raise targets flexion. American football punt involves flexion.
summary: Flexion is involved in hip sagittal plane movements, leg raises, and American football punts.

content: The hip—sagittal plane involves extension. The exercise back squat targets extension. Long jump take-off involves extension.
summary: Extension is involved in hip sagittal plane movements, back squats, and long jump take-offs.

content: The lower back—transverse plane involves left rotation. The exercise medicine ball side toss targets left rotation. Baseball batting involves left rotation.
summary: Left rotation is involved in lower back transverse plane movements, medicine ball side tosses, and baseball batting.

content: The lower back—transverse plane involves right rotation. The exercise torso machine targets right rotation. Golf swing involves right rotation.
summary: Right rotation is involved in lower back transverse plane movements, torso machine exercises, and golf swings.

content: The hip—transverse plane involves internal rotation. The exercise resisted internal rotation targets internal rotation. Basketball pivot movement involves internal rotation.
summary: Internal rotation is involved in hip transverse plane movements, resisted internal rotation exercises, and basketball pivot movements.

content: The hip—transverse plane involves external rotation. The exercise resisted external rotation targets external rotation. Figure skating turn involves external rotation.
summary: External rotation is involved in hip transverse plane movements, resisted external rotation exercises, and figure skating turns.

content: The hip—frontal plane involves adduction. The exercise standing adduction machine targets adduction.
summary: Adduction is involved in hip frontal plane movements and standing adduction machine exercises.

content: The hip—transverse plane involves external rotation. The exercise resisted external rotation targets external rotation. Figure skating turn involves external rotation.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving hip external rotation.

content: The hip—frontal plane involves adduction. The exercise standing adduction machine targets adduction. Soccer side step involves adduction.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving hip adduction.

content: The hip—frontal plane involves abduction. The exercise standing abduction machine targets abduction. Rollerblading involves abduction.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving hip abduction.

content: The hip—transverse plane (upper leg to 90 degrees to trunk) involves adduction. The exercise adduction machine targets adduction. Karate in-sweep involves adduction.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving hip adduction in a specific plane.

content: The hip—transverse plane (upper leg to 90 degrees to trunk) involves abduction. The exercise seated abduction machine targets abduction. Wrestling escape involves abduction.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving hip abduction in a specific plane.

content: The knee—sagittal plane involves flexion. The exercise leg (knee) curl targets flexion. Diving tuck involves flexion.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving knee flexion.

content: The knee—sagittal plane involves extension. The exercise leg (knee) extension targets extension. Volleyball block involves extension.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving knee extension.

content: The ankle—frontal plane involves inversion. The exercise resisted inversion targets inversion. Soccer dribbling involves inversion.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving ankle inversion.

content: The ankle—frontal plane involves eversion. The exercise resisted eversion targets eversion. Speed skating involves eversion.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving ankle eversion.

content: The ankle—sagittal plane involves dorsiflexion. The exercise toe raise targets dorsiflexion. Running involves dorsiflexion.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving ankle dorsiflexion.

content: The ankle—sagittal plane involves plantar flexion. The exercise calf (heel) raise targets plantar flexion. High jump involves plantar flexion.
summary: This chunk describes exercises and movements involving ankle plantar flexion.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats.
summary: This chunk describes the squat exercise and its effects on muscle activation.

content: A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: This chunk provides a typical squat prescription for strength training.

content: Anaerobic training consists of high-intensity, intermittent bouts of exercise such as weight training, plyometric drills, and speed, agility, and interval training.
summary: This chunk defines anaerobic training and provides examples of exercises included in such programs.

content: In certain anaerobic training programs, aerobic metabolism is also highly involved and therefore concurrently trained (114).
summary: This chunk explains that some anaerobic training programs may also involve aerobic metabolism.

content: Performance adaptations to anaerobic training are specific to the training program; as a result, improvements in muscular strength, power, hypertrophy, muscular endurance, and motor skill performance will occur.
summary: This chunk describes the specific performance adaptations that result from anaerobic training.

content: Some exercises that stress the phosphagen system (e.g., sprints, plyometric drills) are under 10 seconds in duration and provide almost complete recovery between sets (e.g., 5-7 minutes) to enable training at maximal intensities (26).
summary: This chunk describes the characteristics of exercises that primarily utilize the phosphagen system.

content: A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: A typical squat prescription for strength development involves 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.

content: Anaerobic training consists of high-intensity, intermittent bouts of exercise such as weight training, plyometric drills, and speed, agility, and interval training. Some exercises that stress the phosphagen system (e.g., sprints, plyometric drills) are under 10 seconds in duration and provide almost complete recovery between sets (e.g., 5-7 minutes) to enable training at maximal intensities (26). Speed and power training depend greatly on optimal neural recruitment for maximal performance (and high training quality), which requires recovery and minimal fatigue. Glycolytic conditioning uses shorter rest intervals during high-intensity exercise.
summary: Anaerobic training encompasses high-intensity exercises like weight training and plyometrics, requiring maximal effort and recovery. It includes phosphagen- and glycolytic-based conditioning.

content: Performance adaptations to anaerobic training are specific to the training program; as a result, improvements in muscular strength, power, hypertrophy, muscular endurance, and motor skill performance will occur.  In addition to the demands of training, the metabolic demands of each sport (and practices) also contribute to the adaptations accompanying anaerobic training (table 5.1). Critical physiological adaptations to the nervous, muscular, connective tissue, endocrine, and cardiovascular systems enable an individual to improve athletic performance following anaerobic training (see table 5.2). Adaptations range from those that take place in the early phase of training to those that take place through years of training. The majority of research has typically dealt with adaptations in the early to intermediate stages of training (i.e., 4 to 24 weeks).
summary: Anaerobic training adaptations are specific to the program and can improve various aspects of performance, including strength, power, hypertrophy, and motor skills. These adaptations occur across different training phases and involve multiple physiological systems.
