content: Understanding how the individual systems of the human body adapt to physical activity provides a knowledge base from which the strength and conditioning professional can predict the outcome of a specific training program. Anaerobic training may elicit adaptations along the neuromuscular chain beginning in the higher brain centers and continuing down to the level of individual muscle fibers (figure 5.1). An increase in neural drive is critical to the athlete striving to maximize strength and power. The increase in neural drive is thought to occur via increases in agonist (i.e., those major muscles involved in a specific movement or exercise) muscle recruitment, firing rate, and the timing and pattern of discharge during high-intensity muscular contractions. A reduction in inhibitory mechanisms (i.e., from Golgi tendon organs) is also thought to occur. Although it is not clear how these mechanisms coexist, it is apparent that neural adaptations are complex and typically occur before structural changes in skeletal muscle occur. The ability to increase motor unit activation begins in the motor cortex with the intent to produce maximal levels of muscular force and power. The primary motor cortex activity increases when the
summary: Understanding how the body adapts to training allows professionals to predict outcomes. Anaerobic training impacts the neuromuscular system, increasing neural drive and muscle activation, which precedes structural changes.

content: Resistance training adaptations increase muscular strength. Resistance training adaptations increase muscular endurance for high power output. Resistance training adaptations may cause aerobic power to remain unchanged or increase slightly. Resistance training adaptations increase maximal rate of force production. Resistance training adaptations increase vertical jump ability. Resistance training adaptations increase anaerobic power. Resistance training adaptations improve sprint speed. Resistance training adaptations increase muscle fiber size. Resistance training adaptations may cause capillary density to remain unchanged or decrease. Resistance training adaptations decrease mitochondrial density. Resistance training adaptations may cause myofibrillar packing density to remain unchanged. Resistance training adaptations increase myofibrillar volume. Resistance training adaptations increase cytoplasmic density. Resistance training adaptations increase myosin heavy-chain protein amount. Resistance training adaptations increase creatine phosphokinase enzyme activity. Resistance training adaptations increase myokinase enzyme activity. Resistance training adaptations increase phosphofructokinase enzyme activity. Resistance training adaptations may cause lactate dehydrogenase enzyme activity to remain unchanged or vary. Resistance training adaptations increase sodium-potassium ATPase enzyme activity.
summary: Resistance training elicits numerous adaptations, including increased strength, power, muscle size, and enzyme activity, while potentially affecting aerobic capacity and capillary/mitochondrial density.

content: Resistance training adaptations increase creatine phosphokinase enzyme activity. Resistance training adaptations increase myokinase enzyme activity. Resistance training adaptations increase phosphofructokinase enzyme activity. Resistance training adaptations may cause lactate dehydrogenase enzyme activity to remain unchanged or vary. Resistance training adaptations increase sodium-potassium ATPase enzyme activity.
summary: Resistance training increases activity of several key enzymes involved in energy production and muscle function.

content: Resistance training adaptations increase stored ATP. Resistance training adaptations increase stored creatine phosphate. Resistance training adaptations increase stored glycogen. Resistance training adaptations may cause stored triglycerides to increase.
summary: Resistance training enhances the body's energy stores, including ATP, creatine phosphate, glycogen, and potentially triglycerides.

content: Resistance training adaptations may cause bone density to remain unchanged or increase. Resistance training adaptations may increase ligament strength. Resistance training adaptations may increase tendon strength. Resistance training adaptations may increase collagen content.
summary: Resistance training can positively impact connective tissues, potentially increasing bone density, ligament, tendon, and collagen strength.

content: Resistance training adaptations decrease % body fat. Resistance training adaptations increase fat-free mass.
summary: Resistance training leads to body composition improvements, reducing body fat and increasing lean mass.

content: Adaptations to Anaerobic Training Programs is on page 97.
summary: Information about adaptations to anaerobic training programs can be found on page 97.

content: Electrical stimulation is more effective than voluntary activations in eliciting gains in untrained individuals or in those rehabilitating from injury.
summary: Electrical stimulation is a more effective method for muscle growth and rehabilitation compared to voluntary contractions in untrained or recovering individuals.

content: A study by Adams and colleagues (7) using magnetic resonance imaging showed that only 71% of muscle tissue was activated during maximal effort in untrained individuals. Training can greatly reduce the 71% muscle tissue activation deficit during maximal effort in untrained individuals.
summary: Untrained individuals activate only 71% of their muscle tissue during maximal effort, but training can significantly improve this activation.

content: The functional unit of the neuromuscular system is the motor unit. A motor unit consists of the alpha motor neuron and the associated muscle fibers that it activates.
summary: The motor unit is the basic functional unit of the neuromuscular system, composed of a motor neuron and its connected muscle fibers.

content: Motor neurons may innervate <10 muscle fibers for small, intricate muscles yet innervate >100 fibers for large, powerful trunk and limb muscles.
summary: The number of muscle fibers innervated by a motor neuron varies depending on the muscle size and function, ranging from a few for small muscles to over 100 for large muscles.

content: When maximal force is desired from a muscle, all the available motor units must be activated. Different types of motor unit firing rates or frequencies affect muscle force. These firing rates are an adaptive mechanism improved by heavy resistance training.
summary: Maximizing muscle force requires activating all available motor units, and the firing rate of these units influences force production, which can be improved through heavy resistance training.

content: Maximal strength and power increases of agonist muscles generally result from an increase in recruitment, rate of firing, synchronization of firing, or a combination of these factors.
summary: Strength and power gains in agonist muscles are primarily achieved through increased motor unit recruitment, firing rate, and synchronization.

content: The recruitment and decruitment of motor units in an orderly manner is governed by the size principle. Motor units are recruited in order according to their recruitment thresholds and firing rates, which results in a continuum of voluntary force in the agonist muscle.
summary: The size principle dictates the recruitment of motor units based on their size and firing threshold, allowing for a gradual increase in muscle force.

content: Because most muscles contain a range of Type I and II muscle fibers (as classified by their motor unit type), force production can range from very low levels to maximum.
summary: The diverse fiber types within muscles allow for a wide range of force production, from low to maximum.

content: Before a high-threshold motor unit is recruited, all of the motor units below it are recruited sequentially.
summary: Motor units are recruited in a hierarchical manner, with lower-threshold units activated before higher-threshold units.

content: With heavy resistance training, all muscle fibers get larger, because for the most part they are all recruited to produce higher levels of force with heavier weights.
summary: Heavy resistance training stimulates muscle growth in all fibers as they are progressively recruited to handle increasing loads.

content: Motor units high in the recruitment order are used primarily for high force, speed, or power production.
summary: Motor units recruited later are responsible for generating high force, speed, or power.

content: Maximal force production requires not only the recruitment of a maximal percentage of available motor units, including the high-threshold motor units, but also the recruitment of these motor units at very high firing rates.
summary: Achieving maximum force requires recruiting a high percentage of motor units, including high-threshold units, and firing them at high rates.

content: Once a motor unit is recruited, less activation is needed in order for it to be rerecruited. This may have important ramifications for strength and power training as these high-threshold motor units may be more readily reactivated subsequent to previous recruitment.
summary: Once activated, motor units require less stimulation for subsequent activation, which can benefit strength and power training by facilitating the reuse of high-threshold units.

content: With heavy resistance training, all muscle fibers get larger because they are all recruited in consecutive order by their size to produce high levels of force.
summary: Heavy resistance training stimulates muscle growth in all fibers as they are progressively recruited based on size to handle increasing loads.

content: In advanced lifters, the central nervous system might adapt by allowing these athletes to recruit some motor units not in consecutive order, recruiting larger ones first to help with greater production of power or speed in a movement.
summary: Experienced lifters may develop the ability to recruit motor units in a non-sequential manner, prioritizing larger units for enhanced power and speed.

content: A few exceptions to the size principle exist. Selective recruitment of fast-twitch motor units may occur under certain circumstances that allow an athlete to inhibit lower-threshold motor units and in their place activate the higher-threshold motor units critical to optimal speed and power performance.
summary: While the size principle generally governs motor unit recruitment, exceptions exist, particularly in situations requiring rapid force changes or ballistic movements, where fast-twitch motor units are selectively activated.

content: Rapid changes of direction in force production and ballistic muscular contractions, comparable to movements in plyometric, are examples of circumstances where selective recruitment of fast-twitch motor units may occur.
summary: Plyometric exercises and movements requiring rapid force changes are examples where selective recruitment of fast-twitch motor units is advantageous.

content: With heavy resistance training, all muscle fibers get larger because they are all recruited in consecutive order by their size to produce high levels of force. A few exceptions to the size principle exist.  Selective recruitment of fast-twitch motor units may occur under certain circumstances that allow an athlete to inhibit lower-threshold motor units and in their place activate the higher-threshold motor units critical to optimal speed and power performance. Rapid changes of direction in force production and ballistic muscular contractions, comparable to movements in plyometric, are examples of circumstances where selective recruitment of fast-twitch motor units may occur. Speed, power, and agility training have been shown to lead to preferential recruitment of fast-twitch motor units.
summary: This chunk explains the size principle in muscle recruitment and how it can be overridden for explosive movements.

content: It would be very difficult for an athlete to generate enough angular velocity and power to attain maximal height for the vertical jump test if that athlete had to recruit the entire slow-twitch motor unit pool prior to activation of the fast-twitch units. Because the time between the countermovement and the subsequent jump takeoff is often less than 0.4 seconds, there simply is not enough time to recruit all of the motor units in order and still be able to perform an explosive jump. Selective recruitment appears to be a beneficial intrinsic neural mechanism favoring explosive exercise. Specific training may enhance selective recruitment, which may improve sport performance.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of selective recruitment for explosive movements like jumping.

content: Variations in recruitment order may benefit high-velocity power training in which time of force production is vital to success.  Another critical element in recruitment is the level of tissue activation resulting from chronic resistance training for muscular hypertrophy. A muscle does not require as much neural activation to lift a given load after its size increases. A study by Ploutz and colleagues reported that fewer quadriceps muscle fibers were activated to lift a particular load after nine weeks of resistance training that resulted in a 5% increase in muscle size. These results demonstrate the importance of progressive overload during resistance training for allowing continual recruitment of an optimal amount of muscle tissue.
summary: This chunk discusses how muscle hypertrophy affects neural recruitment and the importance of progressive overload.

content: Other potential motor unit adaptations include changes in the rate and sequence of firing. A positive relationship exists between the amount of force produced and the rate of motor unit firing. The role of increasing firing rate (vs. recruitment) appears to be dependent on muscle size; smaller muscles rely more on an increased firing rate to enhance force production, whereas larger muscles rely more on recruitment. There is evidence to show that anaerobic training can enhance the firing rates of recruited motor units. In addition, high firing rates at the onset of ballistic muscle contraction are especially critical to increasing the rate of force development. Thus, the ability to increase firing rate appears to be essential to anaerobic training for maximal force and power development.
summary: This chunk explains the role of motor unit firing rate in force production and its importance for anaerobic training.

content: Resistance training may result in a more synchronized pattern (i.e., the firing of two or more motor units at a fixed interval) of activation during the exertion of large forces, rather than the customary asynchronous pattern usually common to motor function. Although the specific role played by motor unit synchronization during anaerobic training is unclear, it may be that synchronization is more critical to the timing of force production and less significant with regard to the overall level of force developed.
summary: This chunk discusses the potential for motor unit synchronization to improve force production.

content: The neuromuscular junction (NMJ) is the interface between the nerve and skeletal muscle and is another potential site for the occurrence of neural adaptations from anaerobic training. The majority of studies on this subject have examined the impact of exercise training on the NMJ in animals and have shown that the NMJ adapts to exercise. Insights into NMJ changes with different intensities of exercise were demonstrated by Deschenes and colleagues, who examined the effects of high-intensity versus low-intensity treadmill exercise training on NMJ adaptations in the soleus muscle of rats. Both high- and low-intensity running increased the area of the NMJ. High-intensity training produced more dispersed, irregularly shaped synapses and a greater total length of nerve terminal branching compared to low-intensity training. Another study showed increased end-plate perimeter length and area, as well as greater dispersion of acetylcholine receptors within the end-plate region after seven weeks of resistance training.
summary: This chunk explains how the neuromuscular junction adapts to exercise training.

content: Thus, anaerobic training appears to produce morphological changes in the NMJ that are conducive to enhanced neural transmission. Anaerobic training may enhance the reflex (i.e., muscle spindle or stretch reflex) response, thereby enhancing the magnitude and rate of force development. Resistance training has been shown to increase reflex potentiation by 19% to 55%. In addition, resistance-trained athletes (weightlifters, bodybuilders) have greater reflex potentiation in the soleus muscle compared to untrained individuals.
summary: This chunk discusses how anaerobic training can enhance reflexes and force development.

content: Electromyography (EMG) is a common research tool used to examine the magnitude of neural activation following training. An increase in EMG indicates greater neural activation; however, the magnitude of EMG alone does not necessarily indicate the extent of muscle fiber recruitment or force production. Several EMG studies have examined the effects of resistance and power training during periods of at least five weeks to over one year. Collectively, these studies have shown strength and power increases of up to 73%. Approximately 70% of these studies have demonstrated increases in EMG following the training period, whereas the remaining 30% have not shown changes in EMG. Neural adaptations (improved motor learning and coordination) predominate early in training without significant increases in muscle hypertrophy. The onset of muscle hypertrophy is associated with declines in EMG.
summary: This chunk explains the use of EMG in studying neural adaptations to training and the relationship between EMG, muscle hypertrophy, and training.

content: Several EMG studies have examined the effects of resistance and power training during periods of at least five weeks to over one year. Collectively, these studies have shown strength and power increases of up to 73%. Approximately 70% of these studies have demonstrated increases in EMG following the training period, whereas the remaining 30% have not shown changes in EMG. Neural adaptations (improved motor learning and coordination) predominate early in training without significant increases in muscle hypertrophy. The onset of muscle hypertrophy is associated with declines in EMG. With advancement in training it appears that there exists an interplay between neural and hypertrophic mechanisms that contributes to further gains in strength and power.
summary: This chunk summarizes the findings of EMG studies on resistance and power training, highlighting the initial dominance of neural adaptations and the later interplay with muscle hypertrophy.

content: Sale (187, 188, 189) reported that dramatic increases in neural adaptations take place early in the training program (6 to 10 weeks). As the duration of training increases (>10 weeks), muscle hypertrophy eventually occurs and contributes more than neural adaptations do to the strength and power gains observed. Although muscle hypertrophy eventually plateaus, if the athlete includes variation and progressive overload in training, neural adaptations will contribute to subsequent performance improvements. This pattern appears to replicate as training continues.
summary: This chunk details the temporal progression of neural adaptations and muscle hypertrophy during training, emphasizing the continued role of neural adaptations even after hypertrophy plateaus.

content: As the athlete progresses in training, the type of program used may be one of the most important factors to consider. Neural factors may be especially important for strength gains in programs of very high intensity (>85% of 1-repetition maximum [1RM]). Power training also appears to provide a potent stimulus to the nervous system and results in higher EMG posttraining.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of program type, particularly for high-intensity programs and power training, in maximizing neural adaptations and strength gains.

content: Cross-education occurs. That is, training only one limb can result in an increase in strength in the untrained limb. A recent review of the literature has shown that strength in the untrained limb may increase up to 22%, with an average strength increase of about 8%. The increase in strength in the untrained limb is accompanied by greater EMG, thereby suggesting that a central neural adaptation accounts for most of the strength gain.
summary: This chunk explains the phenomenon of cross-education, where training one limb leads to strength gains in the untrained limb, primarily due to neural adaptations.

content: In untrained individuals, a bilateral deficit occurs. This means that the force produced when both limbs are contracting together is less than the sum of the forces they produce when contracting unilaterally. Research has shown that the corresponding EMG is lower during bilateral contractions, this suggests that neural mechanisms are, at least in part, contributing factors. The bilateral deficit is reduced with bilateral training.
summary: This chunk describes the bilateral deficit observed in untrained individuals, where combined force production is less than the sum of individual limb forces, and how bilateral training can mitigate this deficit.

content: The muscle activity of the antagonists during agonist movements has been shown to change in response to anaerobic training. Cocontraction of antagonist muscles is a mechanism to increase joint stability and reduce the risk of injury. However, too much antagonist activity can oppose agonist movement and therefore provide resistance to maximal force production. Anaerobic training has produced variable responses in antagonist muscle cocontraction. Some studies have shown reduced antagonist cocontraction following resistance training, whereas others do not demonstrate a change. In addition, sprint and plyometric training have been shown to change the timing of cocontraction activation.
summary: This chunk explores the impact of anaerobic training on antagonist muscle cocontraction, highlighting its role in joint stability, potential limitations, and variations in response to different training modalities.

content: Although the specific role of altering antagonist cocontraction patterns is unclear, it does appear that the adaptations enhance force and power production and injury prevention. That is, greater antagonist activity may be seen during ballistic movements in which joint stability is a prime concern, but may be reduced during phases in which force production is needed (and joint stability is sufficient). It does appear that antagonist cocontraction is higher when people are unfamiliar with the task.
summary: This chunk discusses the potential benefits of altered antagonist cocontraction patterns for force production, power output, and injury prevention, while acknowledging the need for further research to fully understand its role.

content: Skeletal muscle adapts to anaerobic training primarily by increasing its size, facilitating fiber type transitions, and enhancing its biochemical and ultrastructural components (i.e., architecture, enzyme activity, and substrate concentrations). Collectively, these changes ultimately result in enhanced muscular strength, power, and muscular endurance, which are critical to athletic success.
summary: This chunk outlines the primary adaptations of skeletal muscle to anaerobic training, emphasizing the multifaceted nature of these changes and their contribution to athletic performance.

content: Muscle hypertrophy refers to muscular enlargement resulting from training, primarily owing to an increase in the cross-sectional area (CSA) of the existing fibers. There is a positive relationship between muscle CSA and strength. The process of hypertrophy involves an increase in the net accretion (i.e., an increase in synthesis, reduction in degradation, or both) of the contractile proteins actin and myosin within the myofibril. The synthesis of other proteins (i.e., structural proteins such as titin and nebulin) also occurs in proportion to the myofilament changes. The new myofilaments are added to the periphery of the myofibril, resulting in an increase in its diameter. These adaptations create the cumulative effect of enlarging the fiber and, collectively, the associated muscle or muscle group.
summary: This chunk defines muscle hypertrophy, explains its relationship to strength, and details the molecular mechanisms involved in the process of muscle fiber enlargement.

content: Mechanical loading leads to a series of intracellular processes that ultimately regulate gene expression and subsequent protein synthesis. Several proteins (e.g., Ankrd2, Smpx, Sehrl) have recently been identified that are responsive to mechanical stress and that increase in activity prior to hypertrophy. Recent studies have shown that resistance training has the potential to alter the activity of nearly 70 genes. Upregulation of factors (e.g., myogenin and MyoD) involved with muscle regeneration (a process known as myogenesis) and downregulation of inhibitory growth factors (e.g., myostatin) have been shown following resistance exercise.
summary: This chunk describes the molecular pathways triggered by mechanical loading, highlighting the role of gene expression, specific proteins, and growth factors in muscle hypertrophy.

content: Muscle fiber hypertrophy appears to require a longer period of training time (>16 workouts). It may take at least a few weeks before muscle hypertrophy becomes evident during resistance training. With the initiation of a heavy resistance training program, changes in the types of muscle proteins (e.g., fast myosin heavy chains) start to take place within several workouts. Similar to power and strength improvements, initial gains in hypertrophy are the greatest, with the rate of muscle growth lessening over time.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy takes time to manifest, requiring consistent training and showing initial rapid gains followed by a gradual slowdown.

content: The process of hypertrophy involves both an increase in the synthesis of the contractile proteins actin and myosin within the myofibril and an increase in the number of myofibrils within a muscle fiber. The new myofilaments are added to the external layers of the myofibril, resulting in an increase in its diameter. Muscle fiber disruption and damage during intense resistance exercise are also a stimulus for muscle growth.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy occurs through increased protein synthesis within muscle fibers and the addition of new myofibrils, stimulated by exercise-induced damage.

content: Mechanical loading leads to a series of intracellular processes that ultimately regulate gene expression and subsequent protein synthesis. Several proteins (e.g., Ankrd2, Smpx, Sehrl) have recently been identified that are responsive to mechanical stress and that increase in activity prior to hypertrophy. Recent studies have shown that resistance training has the potential to alter the activity of nearly 70 genes. Upregulation of factors (e.g., myogenin and MyoD) involved with muscle regeneration (a process known as myogenesis) and downregulation of inhibitory growth factors (e.g., myostatin) have been shown following resistance exercise.
summary: Mechanical stress from exercise triggers intracellular pathways that regulate gene expression, leading to increased protein synthesis and muscle growth.

content: Protein synthesis is elevated after acute resistance exercise and remains elevated for up to 48 hours. The magnitude of protein synthesis depends on carbohydrate and protein intake, amino acid availability, timing of nutrient intake, mechanical stress of the weight training workout, muscle cell hydration levels, and the anabolic hormonal and subsequent receptor response.
summary: Resistance exercise elevates protein synthesis, which is influenced by various factors including nutrition, hydration, and hormonal response.

content: The muscle fiber type is important in any discussion of the magnitude of muscle hypertrophy during anaerobic training. Muscle fibers, especially the high-threshold motor units governed by the size principle, must be activated in order to stimulate hypertrophy. During resistance training, both Type I and II muscle fibers are recruited and are therefore presented with a potent stimulus for adaptation. Resistance training typically results in increases in both Type I and Type II muscle fiber area. This fiber hypertrophy translates into increases in the CSA of the intact muscle after several months of training. However, muscle fiber hypertrophy does not occur uniformly between the two major fiber types. It has been shown that Type II fibers manifest greater increases in size than Type I fibers (92). It has been argued that the ultimate potential for hypertrophy may reside in the relative proportion of Type II fibers within a given athlete’s muscles. That is, athletes who genetically possess a relatively large proportion of fast-twitch fibers may have a greater potential for increasing muscle mass with resistance training than individuals possessing predominately slow-twitch fibers.
summary: Muscle fiber type influences hypertrophy, with Type II fibers generally showing greater increases in size than Type I fibers. Athletes with a higher proportion of fast-twitch fibers may have greater potential for muscle growth.

content: Optimal muscle growth may result from maximizing the combination of mechanical and metabolic stimuli via training periodization. Mechanical factors include the lifting of heavy loads, inclusion of eccentric muscle actions, and low-to-moderate volume (all characteristic of strength training); metabolic factors center around low or moderate to moderately high intensity training with high volume using short rest intervals (characteristic of bodybuilding training). The mechanical factors result in optimal recruitment of muscle fibers (as muscle fibers need to be recruited prior to growth), growth factor expression, and potential disruption to the sarcomeres, all of which increase muscle CSA. In addition, the metabolic factors stress the glycolytic energy system and result in increased metabolites that may be involved in muscle growth. These programs elicit the most potent anabolic hormonal response.
summary: Maximizing both mechanical and metabolic stress through training periodization is key for optimal muscle growth. Strength training emphasizes heavy loads and low volume, while bodybuilding training focuses on high volume and shorter rest intervals.

content: Another mechanism of increasing muscle size that has been debated by exercise scientists for years is hyperplasia. Hyperplasia results in an increase in the number of muscle fibers via longitudinal fiber splitting as a response to high-intensity resistance training. Hyperplasia has been shown to occur in animals. Adaptations to anaerobic training programs are controversial in human studies. Some reject the idea of hyperplasia in humans (144, 147) and other studies provide some support (145, 149, 209). Some cross-sectional studies comparing resistance-trained athletes to untrained individuals showed a greater number of fibers in the trained population. It is difficult to determine if this was due to genetics or hyperplasia. A longitudinal study from McCall and colleagues (155) showed indirect evidence for hyperplasia (i.e., a subpopulation of subjects exhibited greater fiber number, although there was no change when all subjects were analyzed) in the biceps brachii muscle following high-intensity resistance training. Hyperplasia cannot be completely ruled out, but it does not appear to be a major strategy for muscle tissue adaptation to resistance training and, if it occurs at all, involves only a small amount of the stimulated tissue (maybe less than 10%) if the conditions are optimal (59, 60). One may hypothesize that if hyperplasia occurs, it may be in response to muscle fibers reaching a theoretical upper limit in size, which may occur in athletes using anabolic steroids and other growth agents or undergoing long-term training that produces large to extreme levels of hypertrophy.
summary: Hyperplasia, an increase in muscle fiber number, is debated in humans. While some evidence suggests its occurrence, it appears to be a minor contributor to muscle growth and may be limited to extreme training conditions.

content: The magnitude of hypertrophy ultimately depends on the training stimulus or program prescribed. Athletes studied during two years of heavy resistance training showed increases in strength that paralleled optimization of training intensity, although muscle fiber hypertrophy contributed little to increased lifting performance.
summary: The extent of muscle hypertrophy depends on the specific training program, with strength gains potentially exceeding hypertrophy improvements.

content: Muscle fibers represent a continuum from the most oxidative to the least oxidative type (see figure 5.3 and the description of muscle fiber types in chapter 1). The continuum is as follows: I, Ic, IIc, IIac, IIa, IIax, and IIx, with a concomitant myosin heavy chain (MHC) expression (i.e., MHCI, IIa, and IIx) (200). Although the proportions of Type I and II fibers are genetically determined, changes within each subtype can occur with anaerobic training.
summary: Muscle fibers exist on a continuum of oxidative types, with genetic predisposition influencing fiber proportions. Anaerobic training can induce changes within each fiber subtype.

content: It has been shown that Type II fibers manifest greater increases in size than Type I fibers (92). It has been argued that the ultimate potential for hypertrophy may reside in the relative proportion of Type II fibers within a given athlete’s muscles. That is, athletes who genetically possess a relatively large proportion of fast-twitch fibers may have a greater potential for increasing muscle mass with resistance training than individuals possessing predominately slow-twitch fibers.
summary: Type II muscle fibers exhibit greater hypertrophy potential compared to Type I fibers, suggesting a genetic predisposition for muscle growth based on fiber type distribution.

content: The pattern of neural stimulation dictates the extent of fiber type adaptations to anaerobic training. With training and activation of high-threshold motor units, there is a transition from Type IIx to IIa. In other words, Type IIx muscle fibers change their myosin adenosine triphosphatase (ATPase) isoform content and become progressively more oxidative IIa fibers. While some view the IIx fibers as strength and power fibers, if activated enough they become IIa fibers. Although still powerful, they become more oxidative and fatigue resistant with training.
summary: Anaerobic training influences fiber type adaptations based on neural stimulation patterns. Type IIx fibers can transition to more oxidative Type IIa fibers with consistent activation, enhancing fatigue resistance.

content: It appears that Type IIx fibers are ‘reservoir’ fibers that, upon consistent activation, change into a more oxidative form along the continuum (i.e., to an intermediate fiber Type IIax to IIa) (27, 215). It has become apparent that changes in the muscle fiber type continuum and associated MHCs occur early in a resistance training program.
summary: Type IIx fibers act as a reservoir, transitioning to more oxidative types with consistent activation. These fiber type adaptations and associated MHC changes occur early in resistance training programs.

content: Staron and colleagues (201) examined the effects of a high-intensity resistance training protocol (multiple sets of the squat, leg press, and knee extension exercises using 6- to 12RM loads and 2-minute rest periods) performed by men and women two times per week. Women experienced a significant decrease in the Type IIx percentage in muscle fibers after two weeks of training (four workouts). Men experienced a significant decrease in the Type IIx percentage in muscle fibers after four weeks of training (eight workouts). Over an eight-week training program, Type IIx fiber types decreased to about 7% of the total muscle fibers in both men and women.
summary: A high-intensity resistance training program led to a decrease in Type IIx fiber percentage in both men and women over an eight-week period, with faster reductions observed in women.

content: Analysis of the MHCs showed that in the early phase of training, IIx MHCs were replaced with IIa MHCs. Detraining results in an increase in Type IIx fibers and a reduction in Type IIa fibers. Detraining may result in an overshoot of Type IIx fibers (i.e., higher IIx percentages than observed pretraining). Transformation from Type I to Type II or vice versa appears less probable.
summary: Resistance training initially shifts MHC expression from IIx to IIa. Detraining reverses this trend, potentially exceeding pre-training IIx percentages. Type I to Type II or vice versa transformation is less likely.

content: This fiber hypertrophy translates into increases in the CSA of the intact muscle after several months of training.
summary: Muscle fiber hypertrophy leads to increased cross-sectional area (CSA) of the muscle over time.

content: Resistance training increases myofibrillar volume. Resistance training increases cytoplasmic density. Resistance training increases sarcoplasmic reticulum and T-tubule density. Resistance training increases sodium-potassium ATPase activity.
summary: Resistance training induces various structural and functional adaptations within muscle fibers, including increased myofibrillar volume, cytoplasmic density, sarcoplasmic reticulum and T-tubule density, and sodium-potassium ATPase activity.

content: Sprint training enhances calcium release. Resistance training increases the angle of pennation in pennate muscles. Strength-trained athletes have larger pennation angles in the triceps brachii and vastus lateralis muscles compared to untrained individuals. Testosterone administration in addition to resistance training augments the increase in pennation angle. Larger pennation angles accommodate greater protein deposition.
summary: Sprint training enhances calcium release, while resistance training increases pennation angles in pennate muscles, potentially facilitated by testosterone. Larger pennation angles allow for greater protein deposition.

content: Strength-trained athletes have greater fascicle length in muscles. Sprinters have greater fascicle length in the gastrocnemius and vastus lateralis muscles compared to distance runners. The combination of resistance, sprint, and jump training increases rectus femoris fascicle length. Sprint and jump training increase vastus lateralis fascicle length.
summary: Strength training and specific training modalities (sprint, jump) lead to increased fascicle length in various muscles, with differences observed between sprinting and distance running.

content: Heavy resistance training reduces mitochondrial density in the trained muscles. The number of mitochondria may remain constant or increase with muscle hypertrophy.
summary: Heavy resistance training may decrease mitochondrial density, while muscle hypertrophy can lead to either stable or increased mitochondrial numbers.

content: Sprinters have greater fascicle length in the gastrocnemius and vastus lateralis muscles compared to distance runners. The combination of resistance, sprint, and jump training increases rectus femoris fascicle length. Sprint and jump training increase vastus lateralis fascicle length.
summary: Sprinters and athletes engaging in resistance, sprint, and jump training exhibit greater fascicle length in specific leg muscles compared to distance runners.

content: Heavy resistance training reduces mitochondrial density in the trained muscles. The number of mitochondria may remain constant or increase with muscle hypertrophy. Mitochondrial density is expressed relative to muscle area.
summary: Heavy resistance training can decrease mitochondrial density, while muscle hypertrophy may lead to either stable or increased mitochondrial numbers, always measured relative to muscle size.

content: Muscle hypertrophy results in decreased capillary density. The number of capillaries per fiber may increase with muscle hypertrophy. Powerlifters and Olympic weightlifters show significantly lower capillary densities than control subjects. Bodybuilders have capillary densities similar to those of nonathletes.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy often reduces overall capillary density, though capillaries per fiber may increase. Powerlifters and Olympic weightlifters have lower capillary densities than controls, while bodybuilders have densities comparable to nonathletes.

content: Bodybuilding workouts produce high blood lactate concentrations. Having more capillaries per fiber may assist in the clearance of metabolites from exercising muscle. Anaerobic exercise results in substantial reductions in muscle and blood pH. Consistent changes in pH via training can improve buffering capacity. Increasing buffering capacity allows the athlete to tolerate higher concentrations of lactic acid.
summary: Bodybuilding workouts elevate lactate levels. Increased capillaries per fiber aid metabolite clearance. Anaerobic exercise lowers muscle and blood pH, but consistent training improves buffering capacity, allowing athletes to tolerate higher lactate concentrations.

content: Interval training has been shown to increase buffering capacity significantly—by 16% to 38%. The interval training program needs to be performed at a high enough intensity above the lactate threshold. Athletes competing in anaerobic team sports have higher buffering capacity than endurance athletes and untrained control subjects.
summary: Interval training, performed above the lactate threshold, significantly increases buffering capacity, exceeding that of endurance athletes and untrained individuals, particularly in anaerobic team sport athletes.

content: Anaerobic training designed to enhance high-intensity muscular endurance is essential to muscle substrate content and enzyme activity changes. Heavy resistance training increases energy substrate levels and their availability in muscle. Repeated bouts of intermittent high-intensity contractions that reduce adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and creatine phosphate (CP) concentrations can increase the storage capacity of these high-energy compounds via a ‘supercompensation’ effect. MacDougall and colleagues (153) reported a 28% increase in resting CP and an 18% increase in ATP concentrations following five months of resistance training (i.e., three to five sets of 8-10 repetitions with 2-minute rest periods). Increases in glycogen content of up to 112% have been shown following five months of resistance training. Bodybuilding-style programs (stressing anaerobic glycolysis) may be a potent stimulus for glycogen content enhancement.
summary: Anaerobic training improves muscle substrate content and enzyme activity. Heavy resistance training increases energy substrate availability. Resistance training can supercompensate ATP and CP stores, and bodybuilding-style programs enhance glycogen content.

content: Bone, tendons, ligaments, fascia, and cartilage are examples of connective tissue. Exercise creates mechanical forces that cause deformation of specific regions of the skeleton. These forces, created by muscular actions on the tendinous insertion into bone, can be bending, compressive, or torsional in nature.
summary: Connective tissues like bone, tendons, and ligaments are affected by exercise-induced mechanical forces, which can be bending, compressive, or torsional.

content: In response to mechanical loading, osteoblasts migrate to the bone surface and begin bone modeling (figure 5.4). Osteoblasts manufacture and secrete proteins—primarily collagen molecules—that are deposited in the spaces between bone cells to increase strength. These proteins form the bone matrix and eventually become mineralized as calcium phosphate crystals (hydroxyapatite). New bone formation occurs predominantly on the outer surface of the bone (periosteum), increasing diameter and strength.
summary: Mechanical loading stimulates osteoblasts, which build new bone by depositing collagen and mineralizing it, primarily on the outer bone surface, increasing strength and diameter.

content: Bone adaptations occur at different rates in the axial and appendicular skeleton owing to differing amounts of trabecular and cortical bone. Cortical bone is dense and forms a compact outer shell that is bridged by trabecular bone. The spaces between the trabecular plates are occupied by bone marrow that consists of adipose tissue and blood products. Blood vessels from the marrow cavity extend into the dense cortical bone through a network of vertical and horizontal canals. Trabecular bone is able to respond more rapidly to stimuli than cortical bone.
summary: Bone adaptation rates vary between the axial and appendicular skeletons due to differences in trabecular and cortical bone composition. Trabecular bone responds faster to stimuli than cortical bone.

content: The term minimal essential strain (MES) refers to the threshold stimulus that initiates new bone formation. Consistently exceeding the MES signals osteoblasts to migrate to the given region and form bone. Forces that fall below the MES do not present a stimulus for new bone formation. Physical activities that generate forces exceeding the MES are those activities that represent an increase in intensity and are weight bearing; these are most effective for bone formation.
summary: Minimal essential strain (MES) is the threshold force needed for bone formation. Exceeding MES stimulates bone growth, while forces below it do not. Weight-bearing activities exceeding MES are most effective for bone formation.

content: Trabecular bone is able to respond more rapidly to stimuli than cortical bone.  Bone cells regulate bone so that forces experienced on a regular basis do not exceed the MES, establishing a margin of safety against fracture. Strain registered by bone is a function of the force per unit area of bone (stress). The MES is thought to be approximately 1/10 of the force required to fracture bone.  Forces that reach or exceed a threshold stimulus initiate new bone formation in the area experiencing the mechanical strain.
summary: Bone responds to mechanical strain, with trabecular bone being more responsive. The minimal essential strain (MES) is the threshold for bone formation, and bone cells regulate forces to prevent exceeding the MES.

content: The term minimal essential strain (MES) refers to the threshold stimulus that initiates new bone formation. Consistently exceeding the MES signals osteoblasts to migrate to the given region and form bone. Forces that fall below the MES do not present a stimulus for new bone formation. Physical activities that generate forces exceeding the MES are those activities that represent an increase in intensity and are weight bearing; these are most effective for bone formation.
summary: The minimal essential strain (MES) is the threshold stimulus for bone formation.  Forces exceeding the MES stimulate bone growth, while forces below it do not.

content: Increasing the diameter of bone allows the force to be distributed over a larger surface area, thereby decreasing the amount of mechanical stress. Following bone growth, the same force that previously exceeded the MES will now be below the MES threshold—demonstrating the importance of progressive overload for increasing bone size and strength.
summary: Bone adapts to stress by increasing in size, distributing force over a larger area and reducing stress.  Progressive overload is essential for continued bone growth.

content: Muscle strength and hypertrophy gains increase the force exerted on the bones. Stronger forces of muscular contraction increase the mechanical stress on bone, and bone must subsequently increase in mass and strength to provide a sufficient support structure for hypertrophied muscles. Thus, an increase in muscle strength or mass may result in a corresponding increase in bone mineral density (BMD), or the quantity of mineral deposited in a given area of bone.
summary: Increased muscle strength and hypertrophy place greater stress on bones, leading to increased bone mass and density (BMD).

content: Interestingly, inactivity or immobilization has the opposite effect on bone mass, resulting in a more rapid rate of loss of bone matrix and BMD. Numerous studies have shown a positive correlation between BMD and muscle strength and mass. Researchers have reported that resistance-trained athletes have higher BMD than age-matched sedentary control subjects.
summary: Inactivity leads to bone loss, while physical activity, particularly resistance training, increases bone mass and density (BMD).

content: In certain populations, physical activity seems to influence bone mass, area, and width more than BMD. Thus, exercise that stimulates muscle hypertrophy and strength gains appears to stimulate bone growth.
summary: Physical activity can positively influence various aspects of bone health, including mass, area, width, and BMD.

content: The time course for bone adaptations is rather long. Quantitatively increasing BMD via resistance training is a long-term process—approximately six months or longer—and depends on the structure of the program. However, the process of adaptation begins within the first few workouts.
summary: Bone adaptations take time, with significant BMD increases occurring over several months. However, the process begins with the first few workouts.

content: The process of osteogenesis involves secretion of substances into the blood (substances specific to bone only) that can be measured. Thus, an elevation in an osteogenic marker is an early indicator of bone formation and presumably a precursor to increases in BMD, provided that the stimulus is maintained over a long training period.
summary: Early indicators of bone formation include elevated osteogenic markers, which suggest that bone building is occurring.

content: Anaerobic training programs designed to stimulate bone growth need to incorporate specificity of loading, speed and direction of loading, volume, proper exercise selection, progressive overload, and variation. Specificity of loading entails using exercises that directly load a particular region of the skeleton. If the body interprets these forces as new or unusual, they will stimulate bone growth in the area receiving the strain.
summary: Effective anaerobic training programs for bone growth require specificity of loading, progressive overload, and variation in exercises.

content: As a simple yet practical example, running may be a good stimulus for increasing BMD in the femur but the wrong choice for loading the wrist. The concept of specificity of loading becomes particularly important when a strength and conditioning professional prescribes exercises to increase bone mass in regions of the skeleton most commonly affected by osteoporosis—a disease in which BMD and bone mass become
summary: Exercise selection should be specific to the target bone region for optimal bone growth.

content: High-impact loading exercises for the lower body, such as gymnastics, volleyball, or basketball, selectively increase BMD at clinically relevant sites such as the hip and spine more than lower-impact activities do. Increases in BMD may be seen in highly trained college athletes with already high levels of BMD. These changes in BMD are independent of reproductive hormonal status if the stimulus is sufficient.
summary: High-impact activities are particularly effective for increasing BMD in specific areas like the hip and spine.

content: The exercises should involve multiple joints, should direct the force vectors through the spine and hip (i.e., structural exercises), and should apply loads heavier than single-joint assistance exercises. Cussler and colleagues (37) showed a linear relationship between the amount of weight lifted over the course of one year of training and increases in BMD. Their findings pointed to exercise specificity in that the squat was most effective for increasing BMD of the trochanter of the femur (37).
summary: Structural exercises that engage multiple joints and apply heavier loads are most effective for increasing bone mass.

content: Use of single-joint exercises should be limited because these isolate a single muscle group by stabilizing the body with equipment support rather than skeletal support. Structural exercises such as the back squat, power clean, deadlift, snatch, and push jerk (for the axial skeleton and lower body) and the shoulder press (for the upper body) are recommended for increasing bone strength.
summary: Prioritize structural exercises over single-joint exercises for maximizing bone strength gains.

content: The principle of progressive overload—progressively placing greater than normal demands on the exercising musculature—applies to training to increase bone mass. Although the maximal strength of bone is maintained well above the voluntary force capabilities of the associated musculature, bone responds to higher forces (e.g., 1- to 10RM loads) that are repetitively applied. The adaptive response ensures that forces do not exceed a critical level that increases the risk of stress fractures (microfractures in bone due to structural fatigue).
summary: Progressive overload is essential for increasing bone mass, with bone adapting to higher forces within safe limits.

content: Use of single-joint exercises should be limited because these isolate a single muscle group by stabilizing the body with equipment support rather than skeletal support.
summary: Single-joint exercises should be limited because they rely on equipment support instead of skeletal support, isolating muscle groups.

content: Structural exercises such as the back squat, power clean, deadlift, snatch, and push jerk (for the axial skeleton and lower body) and the shoulder press (for the upper body) are recommended for increasing bone strength.  The principle of progressive overload—progressively placing greater than normal demands on the exercising musculature—applies to training to increase bone mass. Although the maximal strength of bone is maintained well above the voluntary force capabilities of the associated musculature, bone responds to higher forces (e.g., 1- to 10RM loads) that are repetitively applied. The adaptive response ensures that forces do not exceed a critical level that increases the risk of stress fractures (microfractures in bone due to structural fatigue).
summary: Structural exercises like squats, deadlifts, and presses are recommended for bone strength, utilizing progressive overload with higher forces (1-10RM) to stimulate bone growth without exceeding a risk of stress fractures.

content: Support for progressive overload comes from studies that have compared BMD of various groups of athletes to that of nonathletes. Elite adolescent weightlifters have been found to possess levels of bone mineralization that far exceed values found in untrained adults. This observation is interesting because it indicates that young bone may be more responsive to osteogenic stimuli than mature bone. Evidence indicates that physical activity during growth modulates the external geometry and trabecular architecture of bone, potentially enhancing skeletal strength. These studies have led to the hypothesis that, in order to avoid low bone mass in old age, individuals should train to maximally elevate their peak bone mass—the maximum bone mass achieved—during early adulthood.
summary: Studies show athletes, especially young weightlifters, have higher bone mineral density, suggesting young bone is more responsive to training.  Therefore, maximizing peak bone mass during early adulthood is crucial for preventing low bone mass later in life.

content: The internal architecture of the human skeleton has a mechanism through which it compensates for new strain patterns experienced by the bone. To optimally dissipate the imposed forces, the direction of the collagen fibers within the bone matrix may change to conform to the lines of stress experienced by the bone. Changing the distribution (and direction) of the force vectors by using a variety of exercises continually presents a unique stimulus for new bone formation within a given region of bone.
summary: Bone adapts to new stress patterns by changing collagen fiber direction to dissipate forces.  Varying exercises provide diverse stimuli for new bone formation.

content: Overall, if the magnitude of the load, rate of force application, or both are sufficient, it is not typically necessary to perform more than a total of 30 to 35 repetitions, as a greater volume of loading is not likely to provide any additional stimulus for bone growth. The components of mechanical load that stimulate bone growth are the magnitude of the load (intensity), rate (speed) of loading, direction of the forces, and volume of loading (number of repetitions). Programs designed to stimulate new bone formation should incorporate the concepts of specificity of loading, proper exercise selection, progressive overload, and variation. The exercises selected should be structural and weight bearing.
summary: For bone growth, sufficient load intensity, speed, direction, and volume (up to 30-35 reps) are key.  Programs should emphasize structural, weight-bearing exercises with progressive overload and variation.

content: Tendons, ligaments, fascia, and cartilage are complex and dynamic structures that are the critical link between muscles and bones. The primary structural component of all connective tissue is the collagen fiber (Type I for bone, tendon, and ligaments and Type II for cartilage). The parent protein, procollagen, is synthesized and secreted by fibroblasts. Procollagen molecules consist of three protein strands twisted around each other in a triple helix. Procollagen leaves the cell with protective extensions on the ends to prevent premature collagen formation. Cleavage of the extensions via enzymes results in the formation of active collagen that aligns with other collagen molecules to form a long filament. Measurement of these enzymes provides an indication of collagen metabolism. Enzyme levels increase in response to training, thereby showing increased net Type I collagen synthesis.
summary: Connective tissues like tendons, ligaments, and fascia are primarily composed of collagen fibers, synthesized by fibroblasts.  Collagen formation is a complex process involving procollagen, enzymatic cleavage, and alignment, with training increasing collagen synthesis.

content: The parallel arrangement of filaments is called a microfibril. Collagen has a striated (striped) appearance under a light microscope, somewhat like skeletal muscle, owing to the orderly alignment of the gaps between the collagen molecules within a microfibril. Microfibrils become arranged into fibers, and the fibers into larger bundles. The true strength of collagen comes from the strong chemical bonds (cross-linking) that form between adjacent collagen molecules throughout the collagen bundles. Collagen bundles are bunched together longitudinally to form tendons or ligaments, or are arranged into sheets with the layers oriented in different directions, as found in bone, cartilage, and fascia.
summary: Collagen filaments assemble into microfibrils, fibers, and bundles, with cross-linking providing strength.  Collagen bundles form tendons, ligaments, and sheets within bone, cartilage, and fascia.

content: Tendons and ligaments are composed primarily of tightly packed, parallel arrangements of collagen bundles. Mature tendons and ligaments contain relatively few cells. Ligaments contain elastic fibers (elastin) in addition to collagen. A certain amount of stretch is needed within a ligament to allow normal joint motion. Tendons and ligaments attach to bone with great strength, allowing the maximal transmission of forces.
summary: Tendons and ligaments are primarily composed of tightly packed collagen bundles, with ligaments containing additional elastic fibers.  They attach to bone, transmitting forces and allowing joint motion.

content: The fibrous connective tissues that surround and separate the different organizational levels within skeletal muscle are referred to as fascia. Fascia have sheets of fibrocollagenous support tissue containing bundles of collagen fibers arranged in different planes to provide resistance to forces from different directions. Fascia within muscles converge together near the end of the muscle to form a tendon through which the force of muscle contraction is transmitted to bone.
summary: Fascia are connective tissues surrounding and supporting skeletal muscle, providing resistance to forces and converging to form tendons that transmit muscle force to bone.

content: Compared with that of muscle tissue, tendon metabolism is much slower due to poorer vascularity and circulation (99). The increase in blood flow to skeletal muscle via exercise is not paralleled by the same flow perfusion in tendons (107).
summary: Tendon metabolism is slower than muscle metabolism due to poorer blood flow, meaning exercise-induced blood flow increases primarily benefit muscle tissue.

content: The fibrous connective tissues that surround and separate the different organizational levels within skeletal muscle are referred to as fascia. Fascia have sheets of fibrocollagenous support tissue containing bundles of collagen fibers arranged in different planes to provide resistance to forces from different directions. Fascia within muscles converge together near the end of the muscle to form a tendon through which the force of muscle contraction is transmitted to bone.
summary: Fascia are connective tissues surrounding muscles, providing support and transmitting force to bones through tendons.

content: Compared with that of muscle tissue, tendon metabolism is much slower due to poorer vascularity and circulation (99). The increase in blood flow to skeletal muscle via exercise is not paralleled by the same flow perfusion in tendons (107).
summary: Tendons have slower metabolism compared to muscle tissue due to poorer blood flow.

content: The primary stimulus for growth of tendons, ligaments, and fascia is the mechanical forces created during exercise. The degree of tissue adaptation appears to be proportional to the intensity of exercise. Consistent anaerobic exercise that exceeds the threshold of strain stimulates connective tissue changes.
summary: Mechanical forces from exercise stimulate the growth of tendons, ligaments, and fascia, with intensity influencing adaptation.

content: Empirical evidence suggests that connective tissues must increase their functional capabilities in response to increased muscle strength and hypertrophy. The sites where connective tissues can increase strength and load-bearing capacity are at the junctions between the tendon (and ligament) and bone surface, within the body of the tendon or ligament, and in the network of fascia within skeletal muscle.
summary: Connective tissues adapt to increased muscle strength and hypertrophy by increasing their load-bearing capacity at various points.

content: Stronger muscles pull with greater force on their bony attachments and cause an increase in bone mass at the tendon-bone junction and along the line over which the forces are distributed. Such adaptation occurs in the collateral knee ligaments of experimental animals following several weeks of treadmill running (210). Additionally, animals trained on treadmills with uneven surfaces have shown even greater tendon-bone junction strength compared with animals trained on smooth surfaces (6).
summary: Stronger muscles increase bone mass at tendon-bone junctions, and uneven surfaces enhance this adaptation.

content: The adaptation that occurs at the tendon-bone junction is quite effective. The point of ligament rupture in animals moves from the tendon-bone junction in untrained animals to within the body of the tendon or ligament after chronic exercise training (34).
summary: Chronic exercise training strengthens tendons, shifting the point of ligament rupture from the tendon-bone junction.

content: High-intensity anaerobic training results in connective tissue growth and other ultrastructural changes that enhance force transmission. Specific changes within a tendon that contribute to its increase in size and strength include an increase in collagen fibril diameter, a greater number of covalent cross-links within the hypertrophied fiber, an increase in the number of collagen fibrils, and an increase in the packing density of collagen fibrils. Collectively, these adaptations increase the tendon’s ability to withstand greater tensional forces (161).
summary: High-intensity anaerobic training strengthens tendons through various structural changes, increasing their ability to withstand force.

content: Muscle hypertrophy in animals relates to an increase in the number and size of fibroblasts, thereby resulting in a greater supply of total collagen. Activation of fibroblasts and subsequent growth of the connective tissue network are prerequisites for hypertrophy of active muscle (98, 212). This may explain why biopsies of trained athletes have shown that hypertrophied muscle contains greater total collagen but that the collagen content remains proportional to the existing muscle mass (161).
summary: Muscle hypertrophy relies on fibroblast growth and collagen production, ensuring that collagen content scales proportionally with muscle mass.

content: Recent studies indicate that tendon stiffness (force transmission per unit of strain, or tendon elongation) increases as a result of resistance training (138). In fact, Kubo and colleagues (136) reported a 15% to 19% increase in Achilles tendon stiffness following eight weeks of resistance training. The intensity is critical, as heavy loads (80% of 1RM) increase tendon stiffness but light loads (20% of 1RM) do not (137).
summary: Resistance training, particularly with heavy loads, increases tendon stiffness.

content: Cartilage is a dense connective tissue capable of withstanding considerable force without damage to its structure. The main functions of cartilage are to provide a smooth joint articulating surface, act as a shock absorber for forces directed through the joint, and aid in the attachment of connective tissue to the skeleton.
summary: Cartilage is a tough connective tissue that provides a smooth joint surface, absorbs shock, and connects tissues to bone.

content: Exercise of low to moderate intensity does not markedly change the collagen content of connective tissue. High-intensity loading results in a net growth of the involved connective tissues. Weight-bearing forces and complete movement throughout the range of motion seem to be essential to maintaining tissue viability.
summary: Connective tissue growth is stimulated by high-intensity loading, while weight-bearing exercise is crucial for tissue health.

content: Moderate aerobic exercise seems adequate for increasing cartilage thickness. Strenuous exercise does not appear to cause degenerative joint disease.
summary: Moderate aerobic exercise promotes cartilage thickness, and strenuous exercise does not lead to joint degeneration.

content: A feature unique to cartilage is that it lacks its own blood supply and must depend on diffusion of oxygen and nutrients from synovial fluid (which is why cartilage does not easily repair itself following injury). Two primary types of cartilage are significant in relation to physical activity. Hyaline cartilage (articular cartilage) is found on the articulating surfaces of bones. Fibrous cartilage is a very tough form of cartilage found in the intervertebral disks of the spine and at the junctions where tendons attach to bone.
summary: Cartilage lacks its own blood supply and relies on diffusion from synovial fluid. Hyaline cartilage covers joint surfaces, while fibrous cartilage is found in intervertebral disks and tendon attachments.

content: The fact that articular cartilage gets its nutrient supply by diffusion from synovial fluid links joint mobility to joint health. Movement about a joint creates changes in pressure in the joint capsule that drive nutrients from the synovial fluid toward the articular cartilage of the joint (199). Immobilization of a joint prevents proper diffusion of oxygen and essential nutrients throughout the joint, resulting in the death of the chondrocytes and resorption of the cartilage matrix.
summary: Joint movement promotes nutrient flow to cartilage, while immobilization hinders it, leading to cartilage damage.

content: Further support is evidenced in a knee joint, where the surfaces of the joint that experience the greatest degree of weight bearing are thicker than non-weight-bearing surfaces (169). Animal studies evaluating the effect of chronic aerobic exercise on articular cartilage have shown increased articular cartilage thickness (184). Less is known about chronic anaerobic training and cartilage changes in humans, although it is thought that training has a positive effect on cartilage structure.
summary: Weight-bearing surfaces of joints are thicker, and aerobic exercise increases cartilage thickness. While less is known about anaerobic training's effects, it is believed to be beneficial for cartilage.

content: Hormones play a multitude of important regulatory roles in adaptation to anaerobic training (112, 113).
summary: Hormones play a crucial role in adapting to anaerobic training.

content: Immobilization of a joint prevents proper diffusion of oxygen and essential nutrients throughout the joint, resulting in the death of the chondrocytes and resorption of the cartilage matrix.  Animal studies evaluating the effect of chronic aerobic exercise on articular cartilage have shown increased articular cartilage thickness.
summary: Immobilization harms cartilage, while chronic aerobic exercise promotes cartilage thickness.

content: Less is known about chronic anaerobic training and cartilage changes in humans, although it is thought that training has a positive effect on cartilage structure.
summary: The impact of chronic anaerobic training on cartilage is less understood but potentially beneficial.

content: Hormones play a multitude of important regulatory roles in adaptation to anaerobic training. Anabolic hormones such as testosterone, insulin, insulin-like growth factors, and the growth hormone ‘superfamily’ all influence the development of muscle, bone, and connective tissue.
summary: Anabolic hormones are crucial for adaptation to anaerobic training, influencing muscle, bone, and connective tissue development.

content: Hormones are involved in a wide variety of homeostatic mechanisms that are dedicated to keeping the body’s functions within a normal range during rest and exercise. Acute elevations in circulating hormones (resulting from either increased secretion, reduced hepatic clearance, plasma volume reductions, or reduced degradation rates) observed during and immediately following a workout lead to a greater likelihood of interaction either with receptors on target tissue cell membranes (e.g., peptide hormones) or with nuclear or cytoplasmic receptors located within the target tissue (e.g., steroid hormones).
summary: Hormones regulate homeostasis and their levels fluctuate during exercise, interacting with receptors to influence various bodily functions.

content: Coinciding with blood hormonal concentrations is the number of available receptors for binding. Interaction with the receptor initiates a series of events ultimately leading to muscle size and strength gains.
summary: Hormone binding to receptors triggers processes that result in muscle growth and strength gains.

content: Endocrine adaptations and responses to anaerobic training fall within four general classifications: (1) acute changes during and after exercise, (2) chronic changes in resting concentrations, (3) chronic changes in the acute response to a workout, and (4) changes in receptor content.
summary: Hormonal adaptations to anaerobic training encompass acute changes during exercise, chronic resting levels, and long-term adjustments in response and receptor sensitivity.

content: Anaerobic exercise, especially resistance exercise, has been shown to result in elevated testosterone (total and free), growth hormone and its molecular variants, and cortisol for up to 15 to 30 minutes postexercise in men. Testosterone elevations are most prominent in men, although some studies have shown slight testosterone elevations in women. Interestingly, the acute elevation in free testosterone has been shown to be greater in resistance-trained men than aerobic endurance-trained men.
summary: Anaerobic exercise, particularly resistance training, elevates testosterone, growth hormone, and cortisol in men, with greater free testosterone increases in resistance-trained individuals compared to endurance-trained ones.

content: Hormonal fluctuations occur quickly and then stabilize in response to homeostatic challenges both from the initial demands of acute exercise and over longer-term training. Cortisol and growth hormone elevation is greatest when large muscle mass exercises are performed and during workouts with moderate to high intensity and volume and short rest intervals. High correlations between blood lactate and growth hormone concentrations have been reported. It is thought that hydrogen ion accumulation may be the primary factor influencing growth hormone and cortisol release.
summary: Hormonal fluctuations are rapid and stabilize, with cortisol and growth hormone peaking during high-intensity, high-volume workouts. Lactate accumulation likely influences this response.

content: When large muscle mass exercises are performed early in a workout, they have a positive effect (i.e., produce greater strength and hypertrophy changes) on smaller muscle mass exercises performed later due, in part, to an enhanced testosterone response.
summary: Performing large muscle mass exercises early in a workout can positively impact subsequent smaller muscle mass exercises due to a boosted testosterone response.

content: The acute anabolic hormonal response to anaerobic exercise is critical for exercise performance and subsequent training adaptations. Upregulation of anabolic hormone receptors is important for mediating the hormonal effects.
summary: The acute hormonal response to anaerobic exercise is crucial for performance and adaptation, and receptor upregulation enhances this response.

content: The acute response of circulating IGF-I is often delayed and dependent upon the acute growth hormone response. IGF-I has a counterpart produced in skeletal muscle in response to mechanical loading (a mechano growth factor), which acts independently of growth hormone and is upregulated following resistance exercise.
summary: IGF-I response is linked to growth hormone, and a muscle-specific mechano growth factor also contributes to muscle growth independent of growth hormone.

content: Insulin secretion rates parallel blood glucose and amino acid changes. Insulin is mostly affected by supplementation before, during, or after exercise and not by the anaerobic exercise stimulus.
summary: Insulin secretion is influenced by blood glucose and amino acids, and exercise itself has a lesser impact on insulin levels compared to supplementation.

content: Catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine) reflect the acute demands of anaerobic exercise and are important for increasing force production, muscle contraction rate, energy availability, and augmentation of other hormones such as testosterone. Resistance exercise increases concentrations of epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine.
summary: Catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine) are elevated during anaerobic exercise, enhancing force production, energy availability, and other hormonal responses.

content: The magnitude of epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine concentration increases may be dependent on the force of muscle contraction, amount of muscle stimulated, volume of resistance exercise, and rest period length. As a result of consistent resistance training, the acute hormonal response to an anaerobic workout may improve as the individual is gradually able to exert more effort in successive training sessions. Twelve weeks of resistance training in elderly persons was shown to promote greater acute growth hormone response to a workout.
summary: The magnitude of catecholamine response is influenced by exercise intensity, volume, and rest periods. Consistent training improves the hormonal response to workouts, even in older adults.

content: The greater hormonal response patterns to maximal exercise appear to augment the individual’s ability to tolerate and sustain prolonged high exercise intensities. Progressive overload is a critical training tenet for potentially enhancing the acute hormonal response.
summary: Improved hormonal responses to high-intensity exercise enhance tolerance and sustainability. Progressive overload is crucial for maximizing hormonal adaptations.

content: Consistent chronic changes in resting hormonal concentrations are less likely—as several studies have shown either elevations, reductions, or no change in testosterone, growth hormone, IGF-I, and cortisol over time. Resting concentrations reflect the current state of muscle tissue such that short-term elevations or reductions may occur at various stages, depending on substantial changes in the volume and intensity of training or nutritional factors. This may be especially true for testosterone; however, growth hormone overnight pulsatility may be altered as a result of resistance exercise.
summary: Chronic changes in resting hormone levels are less consistent, reflecting current training status and nutritional factors. Testosterone and growth hormone may be particularly influenced by training.

content: The elevation during and immediately following a workout may present receptors with enough of a stimulus to affect tissue remodeling without attenuating receptor content or function. Chronic elevations in an anabolic hormone may be counterproductive over the long term. Receptors tend to downregulate over time when exposed consistently to high levels of hormones. This is why anabolic steroid users repeatedly cycle drug use rather than maintaining consistently high doses. Receptor desensitization reduces the impact of the quantum of hormone released.
summary: Acute hormonal elevations are beneficial for tissue remodeling, while chronic elevations can lead to receptor desensitization and reduced effectiveness. This is why steroid users cycle their drug use.

content: The elevation during and immediately following a workout may present receptors with enough of a stimulus to affect tissue remodeling without attenuating receptor content or function.
