The lack of consistent resting changes, coupled with acute elevations during and following a workout, may be viewed as advantageous for reducing receptor downregulation and optimizing tissue remodeling.
summary: Acute workout elevations in hormones may be beneficial for tissue remodeling without causing receptor downregulation.

content: Receptors tend to downregulate over time when exposed consistently to high levels of hormones. Receptor desensitization reduces the impact of the quantum of hormone released. This is why anabolic steroid users repeatedly cycle drug use rather than maintaining consistently high doses.
summary: Chronic exposure to high hormone levels leads to receptor downregulation, necessitating cycling for optimal effectiveness.

content: Receptor content is important in mediating the adaptations elicited by the hormonal response. Androgen receptor (AR) content depends on several factors including muscle fiber type. Resistance training has been shown to upregulate AR content within 48 to 72 hours after the workout. The resistance exercise stimulus appears to mediate the magnitude of acute AR modifications.
summary: AR content is influenced by factors like muscle fiber type and resistance training, which can upregulate AR levels.

content: Ratamess and colleagues (179) compared one set versus six sets of 10 repetitions of squats and reported no differences in AR content following the single-set protocol. The higher-volume protocol elicited significant downregulation of AR content 1 hour after the workout. This study also demonstrated that when sufficient volume is reached, AR protein content may initially downregulate, prior to the upregulation that has been shown in other studies. However, Kraemer and colleagues (133) have shown that consumption of a protein-carbohydrate supplement before and after the workout attenuates this AR downregulation.
summary: Resistance training volume influences AR content, with higher volume potentially causing initial downregulation, but supplementation can mitigate this.

content: Acute and chronic anaerobic exercise have a significant impact on cardiovascular and respiratory function. This has been evidenced by enhanced cardiac function and dimensions in anaerobic athletes. Resistance training can benefit the cardiovascular system, but in a manner different from conventional aerobic endurance training. Improved ability of the heart, lungs, and circulatory system to function under conditions of high pressure and force production can prepare the athlete’s body for extreme competitive demands.
summary: Anaerobic exercise, including resistance training, positively impacts cardiovascular function differently from aerobic training.

content: An acute bout of anaerobic exercise significantly increases the cardiovascular responses, especially if the individual uses the Valsalva maneuver. Heart rate, stroke volume, cardiac output, and blood pressure increase significantly during resistance exercise. Peak blood pressures of 320/250 mmHg and a heart rate of 170 beats/min have been reported during a high-intensity (i.e., 95% of 1RM) leg press exercise.
summary: Resistance exercise significantly elevates cardiovascular responses, including heart rate, stroke volume, cardiac output, and blood pressure.

content: Generally, the blood pressure response increases nonlinearly with the magnitude of active muscle mass and is higher during the concentric phase of each repetition than during the eccentric phase, especially at the sticking point of an exercise. Although large elevations in blood pressure have been shown, no data exist to indicate that resistance training has any negative effects on resting blood pressure.
summary: Blood pressure during resistance exercise is influenced by muscle mass and exercise phase, but training doesn't negatively impact resting blood pressure.

content: In addition, intrathoracic pressure increases and plasma volume reductions of up to 22% have been reported. Acute anaerobic exercise results in increased cardiac output, stroke volume, heart rate, oxygen uptake, systolic blood pressure, and blood flow to active muscles. During a set of resistance exercise, stroke volume and cardiac output increase mostly during the eccentric phase of each repetition, especially when the Valsalva maneuver is used.
summary: Resistance exercise induces various cardiovascular changes, including increased cardiac output, stroke volume, and blood flow to active muscles.

content: Because the concentric phase of a repetition is much more difficult and elevations in intrathoracic and intra-abdominal pressures are more prominent (via the Valsalva maneuver), limiting venous return and reducing end-diastolic volume, the hemodynamic response of resistance exercise is delayed such that cardiac output increases more during the eccentric phase or during the rest period between sets. This is especially true for an individual’s heart rate response; during the first 5 seconds after completion of a set, heart rate is higher than during the set.
summary: Cardiac output and heart rate responses during resistance exercise are influenced by the Valsalva maneuver and exercise phases, with the eccentric phase and rest periods showing greater increases.

content: The degree to which blood flow is increased in the working muscles during resistance training depends on intensity of resistance, length of time of effort (i.e., the number of repetitions performed), and size of the muscle mass. Lower resistances lifted for many repetitions produce responses relatively similar to those with aerobic exercise. However, heavy resistance exercise decreases blood flow to the working muscles. Muscular contractions greater than 20% of maximal voluntary contraction impede blood flow during a set, but blood flow increases during the rest period (reactive hyperemia) after the set. Interestingly, the lack of blood flow (and subsequent increase in metabolites such as lactic acid and reduction in pH) is a potent stimulus for muscle growth.
summary: Blood flow during resistance training is influenced by intensity, repetitions, and muscle mass, with heavy resistance potentially decreasing blood flow but reactive hyperemia promoting muscle growth.

content: Overall, the magnitude of the acute cardiovascular responses depends on the intensity and volume of exercise, muscle mass involvement, rest period length, and contraction velocity. Resting heart rate may not change significantly in response to anaerobic training. Short-term resistance training has been found to lead to decreases of 5% to 12% in resting heart rate. Resistance-trained athletes (bodybuilders, powerlifters, weightlifters) have average or lower than average resting heart rates (60-78 beats/min) compared to untrained individuals. Longitudinal studies have shown either no change in resting heart rate or reductions of 4% to 13%. Resting blood pressure may decrease slightly or not change at all. A meta-analysis showed that both systolic and diastolic blood pressure decreased by 2% to 4% as an adaptation to resistance training.
summary: Resistance training can positively impact resting heart rate and blood pressure, with long-term adaptations leading to lower resting values.

content: Short-term resistance training has been found to lead to decreases of 5% to 12% in resting heart rate. Resistance-trained athletes (bodybuilders, powerlifters, weightlifters) have average or lower than average resting heart rates (60-78 beats/min) compared to untrained individuals. Longitudinal studies have shown either no change in resting heart rate or reductions of 4% to 13%.
summary: Resistance training can lower resting heart rate in both short and long term, with trained individuals generally having lower resting heart rates than untrained individuals.

content: Resting blood pressure may decrease slightly or not change at all. A meta-analysis showed that both systolic and diastolic blood pressure decreased by 2% to 4% as an adaptation to resistance training. It appears that the response is greatest in those individuals who initially have a slightly elevated blood pressure.
summary: Resistance training may slightly lower resting blood pressure, with the greatest benefit seen in individuals with initially elevated blood pressure.

content: The rate-pressure product (heart rate x systolic blood pressure; a measure of myocardial work) has been shown to either remain constant or decrease following resistance training. Stroke volume has been shown to increase in absolute magnitude, but not relative to body surface area or lean body mass. Stroke volume will increase as lean tissue mass increases during long-term resistance training.
summary: Resistance training may reduce myocardial work and increase stroke volume, with the increase in stroke volume being proportional to increases in lean body mass.

content: Resistance training may either not change or slightly decrease total cholesterol and low-density lipoproteins and increase high-density lipoproteins.
summary: Resistance training may have a slightly positive effect on blood lipid profiles, potentially increasing HDL cholesterol while decreasing LDL cholesterol.

content: Heavy resistance training does little to enhance resting cardiac function, but greater improvements may be caused by adaptations to a high-volume program with short rest periods (i.e., bodybuilding, circuit training) in which the overall continuity of the exercise stress in a workout is much higher.
summary: High-volume resistance training with short rest periods may lead to greater improvements in cardiac function compared to heavy, low-volume training.

content: Chronic resistance training also alters cardiac dimensions. Increased left ventricular wall thickness and mass have been reported, but the increase disappears when expressed relative to body surface area or lean body mass. It is thought that this increase may result from exposure to intermittently elevated blood pressures and increases in intrathoracic pressure in addition to accommodating changes from increases in lean body mass and body size.
summary: Chronic resistance training can increase left ventricular wall thickness and mass, but these changes normalize when adjusted for body size and lean mass. This may be due to adaptations to increased blood pressure and intrathoracic pressure.

content: Highly resistance-trained athletes have greater than normal absolute posterior left ventricular and intraventricular septum wall thickness. Little or no change in left ventricular chamber size or volume is observed with resistance training; this is a major difference between resistance exercise and aerobic exercise.
summary: Highly trained athletes exhibit increased left ventricular wall thickness, but not chamber size, distinguishing resistance training from aerobic exercise.

content: Greater than normal absolute left and right ventricular end-diastolic and end-systolic volumes have been reported in bodybuilders but not weightlifters. It is important to note that bodybuilders frequently incorporate aerobic exercise into their training programs; therefore, it is possible that some of these adaptations may have been brought about, in part, by aerobic endurance training.
summary: Bodybuilders, but not weightlifters, show increased ventricular volumes, potentially influenced by their inclusion of aerobic training.

content: Bodybuilders as well as weightlifters have greater than normal absolute and relative (to lean body mass and body surface) left atrial internal dimensions, with the bodybuilders showing a significantly greater dimension.
summary: Both bodybuilders and weightlifters exhibit enlarged left atrial dimensions, with bodybuilders showing a more pronounced increase.

content: Chronic resistance training reduces the cardiovascular response to an acute bout of resistance exercise of a given absolute intensity or workload. Short-term studies have shown that resistance training results in adaptations that blunt the acute increases in heart rate, blood pressure, and double product caused by the resistance training workout.
summary: Chronic resistance training reduces the cardiovascular strain during exercise, minimizing acute increases in heart rate, blood pressure, and myocardial work.

content: Male bodybuilders have lower systolic and diastolic blood pressures and heart rates during sets of 50% to 100% of 1RM performed to momentary muscular failure than both sedentary and lesser-trained men.
summary: Bodybuilders exhibit lower blood pressure and heart rate during resistance exercise compared to sedentary and less-trained individuals.

content: Bodybuilders’ peak cardiac output and stroke volume are significantly greater than those of powerlifters. It is thought that these adaptations result from a decreased afterload on the left ventricle that in turn increases cardiac output and decreases myocardial oxygen consumption.
summary: Bodybuilders demonstrate greater cardiac output and stroke volume compared to powerlifters, likely due to reduced afterload on the left ventricle.

content: Lastly, oxygen extraction is generally not improved with resistance training using heavy loads and low volume. It is enhanced to a greater extent with continuous aerobic exercise or perhaps slightly with a resistance training program using high volume and short rest periods.
summary: Heavy resistance training does not significantly improve oxygen extraction, while high-volume, short-rest resistance training or aerobic exercise may offer some improvement.

content: Anaerobic training improves ventilation. During resistance exercise, ventilation is elevated during each set, but even greater during the first minute of recovery. Ventilations in excess of 60 L/min have been reported. Short rest intervals (30 seconds to 1 minute) produce the most substantial elevation in ventilation.
summary: Resistance exercise increases ventilation, with the greatest elevation occurring during the first minute of recovery after each set. Short rest intervals further enhance ventilation.

content: Training adaptations include increased tidal volume and breathing frequency with maximal exercise. With submaximal activity, breathing frequency is often reduced while tidal volume is increased.
summary: Resistance training leads to adaptations in breathing patterns, including increased tidal volume and breathing frequency during maximal exercise, and reduced breathing frequency with increased tidal volume during submaximal activity.

content: Ventilatory adaptations result from local, neural, or chemical adaptations in the specific muscles trained through exercise. Improved ventilation efficiency, as characterized by reduced ventilation equivalent for oxygen (the ratio of air ventilated to oxygen used by the tissues, V.E/V.O2), has been observed in trained versus untrained individuals.
summary: Ventilatory adaptations to resistance training are driven by local, neural, and chemical changes in trained muscles, resulting in improved ventilation efficiency.

content: Combining resistance and aerobic endurance training may interfere with strength and power gains primarily if the aerobic endurance training is high in intensity, volume, and frequency. Simultaneous sprint and aerobic endurance training decreased sprint speed and jump power.
summary: High-intensity, high-volume, and high-frequency aerobic training may interfere with strength and power gains from resistance training.

content: Most studies have shown no adverse effects on aerobic power resulting from heavy resistance exercise. A recent study has shown that added resistance training can hinder V.O2max improvements.
summary: While most studies show no negative impact of heavy resistance training on aerobic power, some evidence suggests it may hinder V.O2max improvements.

content: Women who performed both resistance exercise and aerobic endurance training had greater aerobic development than those who performed the aerobic endurance training alone.
summary: Combining resistance and aerobic training may enhance aerobic development in women compared to aerobic training alone.

content: The physiological mechanisms involved in such responses to simultaneous training remain unclear.
summary: The exact physiological mechanisms behind the effects of combined resistance and aerobic training are still under investigation.

content: Simultaneous sprint and aerobic endurance training decreased sprint speed and jump power.  The physiological mechanisms involved in such responses to simultaneous training remain unclear. Such adaptations may result in overtraining. Other potential factors may include inadequate recovery between workouts and residual fatigue from aerobic workouts during resistance exercise.
summary: Simultaneous sprint and aerobic endurance training negatively impacts performance and may lead to overtraining due to unclear physiological mechanisms and potential recovery issues.

content: Most studies have shown no adverse effects on aerobic power resulting from heavy resistance exercise. A recent study has shown that added resistance training can hinder V.O2max improvements. Women who performed both resistance exercise and aerobic endurance training had greater aerobic development than those who performed the aerobic endurance training alone.
summary: While resistance training generally doesn't negatively affect aerobic power, some studies suggest potential limitations on V.O2max improvements. Combining resistance and aerobic training may enhance aerobic development in women.

content: Kraemer and colleagues (125) conducted a study on the potential incompatibility of simultaneous high-intensity strength and aerobic endurance training. The S group increased 1RM strength and rate of strength development more than did the C group. Maximal oxygen consumption improvements were not affected by the simultaneous training.
summary: A study by Kraemer investigated the compatibility of simultaneous high-intensity strength and aerobic endurance training, finding that strength gains were greater in the strength training group, while aerobic capacity remained unaffected.

content: The transformation of Type IIx to Type IIa fibers was almost complete in the S and C groups. The UC and E groups (who performed only interval training) had about 9% Type IIx fibers after training. Heavy resistance training recruits more of the Type IIx fibers than high-intensity aerobic endurance interval training.
summary: Muscle fiber type adaptations varied based on training type. Strength training and combined training led to a greater transformation of Type IIx to Type IIa fibers compared to interval training alone.

content: The combined group increased muscle size only in Type IIa fibers, while the S group demonstrated increases in Type I, IIc, and IIa fibers. The lack of change in Type I fiber area and increase in Type IIa fiber area in the C group appear to represent a cellular adaptation that shows the antagonism of simultaneous strength and aerobic endurance stimuli. Strength training alone produced increases in both Type I and II muscle fiber areas.
summary: Muscle fiber hypertrophy patterns differed based on training type. Combined training primarily increased Type IIa fibers, while strength training led to increases in Type I, IIc, and IIa fibers, suggesting an antagonistic effect of simultaneous strength and aerobic endurance training.

content: The E group showed decreased size in Type I and IIc fibers, presumably due to higher observed cortisol levels (and reduced testosterone) and their physiological need for shorter distances between capillary and cell to enhance oxygen kinetics. Previous studies have shown decreases in muscle fiber size during aerobic endurance training.
summary: Aerobic endurance training led to decreased muscle fiber size in Type I and IIc fibers, potentially due to hormonal changes and physiological adaptations for oxygen delivery.

content: Several studies have shown an incompatibility between resistance training and aerobic endurance training. Some studies used three days a week of resistance training alternating with three days a week of aerobic endurance training. Other studies used four to six days a week of combined high-intensity resistance and aerobic endurance training. When both resistance training and aerobic endurance training are performed during the same workout, the incompatibility has not been shown as frequently.
summary: Research on the compatibility of resistance and aerobic training has yielded mixed results, with some studies showing incompatibility and others not, depending on training frequency and scheduling.

content: A review of more than 100 studies showed that mean strength increased approximately 40% in ‘untrained,’ 20% in ‘moderately trained,’ 16% in ‘trained,’ 10% in ‘advanced,’ and 2% in ‘elite’ participants over periods ranging from four weeks to two years.
summary: A comprehensive review of strength training studies revealed significant strength gains across various training levels, with the greatest improvements observed in untrained individuals.

content: Heavier loads are most effective for fiber recruitment. The effects of training are related to the type of exercise used, its intensity, and its volume. With trained athletes, higher intensity and volume of exercise are needed in order for adaptations to continue.
summary: Training effectiveness depends on load, exercise type, intensity, and volume. Trained individuals require higher training intensities and volumes to continue adapting.

content: Heavy resistance training with slow velocities of movement leads primarily to improvements in maximal strength. Power training (i.e., lifting light-to-moderate loads at high velocities) increases force output at higher velocities and rate of force development. Peak power output is maximized during the jump squat with loads corresponding to 30% to 60% of squat 1RM. For the upper body, peak power output can be maximized during the ballistic bench press throw using loads corresponding to 46% to 62% of 1RM bench press.
summary: Different training methods target specific adaptations. Heavy, slow resistance training improves maximal strength, while power training enhances force output at higher velocities. Optimal loads for power development vary depending on the exercise.

content: Cross-sectional data in anaerobic athletes have shown enhanced muscular endurance and subsequent muscular adaptations consistent with improved oxidative and buffering capacity. Skeletal muscle adaptations to anaerobic muscular endurance training include fiber type transitions and increases in mitochondrial and capillary numbers, buffering capacity, resistance to fatigue, and metabolic enzyme activity.
summary: Anaerobic muscular endurance training leads to adaptations such as improved muscular endurance, oxidative capacity, and resistance to fatigue, along with fiber type transitions and increased mitochondrial density.

content: Resistance training can increase fat-free mass and reduce body fat by 1% to 9%. Increases in lean tissue mass, daily metabolic rate, and energy expenditure during exercise are outcomes of resistance training.
summary: Resistance training promotes fat loss and increases lean mass, leading to a higher metabolic rate and increased energy expenditure during exercise.

content: Anaerobic training potentially can have a positive impact on flexibility, primarily if the individual has poor flexibility to begin with. The combination of resistance training and stretching appears to be the most effective method to improve flexibility with increasing muscle mass.
summary: Resistance training may improve flexibility, especially for individuals with initial limitations. Combining resistance training with stretching is the most effective approach for enhancing flexibility while building muscle mass.

content: Heavy resistance training does not significantly affect aerobic capacity unless the individual is initially deconditioned. The exception is in relatively untrained people, who can experience increases in V.O2max ranging from 5% to 8% as a result of resistance training.
summary: Resistance training generally does not significantly impact aerobic capacity in trained individuals. However, deconditioned individuals may experience improvements in V.O2max.

content: Increases in lean tissue mass, daily metabolic rate, and energy expenditure during exercise are outcomes of resistance training. Heavy resistance training does not significantly affect aerobic capacity unless the individual is initially deconditioned. The exception is in relatively untrained people, who can experience increases in V.O2max ranging from 5% to 8% as a result of resistance training.
summary: Resistance training leads to increased lean mass, metabolic rate, and exercise energy expenditure. It has minimal impact on aerobic capacity in trained individuals, but untrained individuals may see improvements.

content: Circuit training and programs using high volume and short rest periods (i.e., 30 seconds or less) have been shown to improve V.O2max.
summary: High-volume, short-rest circuit training can enhance aerobic capacity (V.O2max).

content: Anaerobic training enhances motor performance; the magnitude of change is based on the specificity of the exercises or modalities performed. Resistance training has been shown to increase running economy, vertical jump, sprint speed, tennis serve velocity, swinging and throwing velocity, and kicking performance.
summary: Anaerobic training improves performance, with specific gains depending on the exercises. Resistance training enhances various athletic performance metrics.

content: Training 3 days per week (resistance training and two days of aerobic endurance training) was better than training 2 days per week (combined resistance training and aerobic endurance training) for increasing leg press 1RM (25% vs. 13%).
summary: Training frequency impacts strength gains: 3 days per week (resistance and aerobic) is more effective than 2 days (combined).

content: Increasing the recovery period between workouts may decrease the incompatibility between resistance training and aerobic endurance training.
summary: Longer recovery periods may mitigate the potential negative effects of combining resistance training and aerobic endurance training.

content: Power development appears to be negatively affected more than strength during concurrent high-intensity resistance and aerobic endurance training. Häkkinen and colleagues (82) reported similar dynamic and isometric strength increases following 21 weeks of concurrent training or resistance training only; however, the resistance training–only group showed improvements in the rate of force development whereas concurrent training did not match this increase. Kraemer and colleagues (125) showed that a resistance training–only group increased muscle power whereas a combined group did not. The resistance training–only group also increased peak power in upper and lower body tests while the combined group did not. It appears that power development is much more susceptible to the antagonistic effects of combined strength and aerobic endurance training than is slow-velocity strength (125).
summary: Concurrent high-intensity resistance and aerobic training negatively impacts power development more than strength. Resistance training alone leads to greater power gains.

content: Leveritt and Abernethy (141) examined lifting performance 30 minutes following a 25-minute aerobic exercise workout and found that the number of repetitions performed during the squat was reduced by 13% to 36% over three sets.
summary: Aerobic exercise can negatively impact subsequent resistance training performance, reducing repetition capacity.

content: Overtraining has been a topic of great interest (64, 122, 139, 203) and can be defined as excessive frequency, volume, or intensity of training that results in extreme fatigue, illness, or injury (which is often due to a lack of sufficient rest, recovery, and perhaps nutrient intake.)
summary: Overtraining is excessive training volume, frequency, or intensity leading to fatigue, illness, or injury due to insufficient rest and recovery.

content: Excessive training on a short-term basis is called overreaching (65, 180). Recovery from overreaching is easily achieved within a few days of rest; consequently, overreaching is often a planned phase of many training programs. The rationale for overreaching is to overwork (to suppress performance and build up tolerance) and then taper to “rebound” in performance. It has been shown that short-term overreaching followed by a tapering period can result in substantial strength and power gains (180).
summary: Overreaching is short-term, planned excessive training followed by rest, aiming to improve performance through a temporary performance dip.

content: Overreaching can become overtraining syndrome if it continues beyond a reasonable period of time. The overtraining syndrome is the condition resulting from overtraining; it is sometimes referred to as staleness. The overtraining syndrome may include a plateau or decrease in performance. Many alternative terms have been suggested for overtraining, including burnout, chronic overwork, physical overstrain, and overfatigue. Some use the term overtraining only when a decline in performance occurs (25).
summary: Overtraining syndrome is prolonged overreaching, characterized by performance plateaus or declines, and is associated with various terms like burnout and staleness.

content: The following progression illustrates the overtraining continuum: Overload stimulus → Acute fatigue → Overreaching → Overtraining. The overtraining syndrome can last as long as six months, and recovery may be delayed. In the worst-case scenario, overtraining can ruin an athletic career (122). Long-term decreases in performance have not been demonstrated during resistance overtraining, however, and many athletes rebound after a period of recovery. Because rebounds are possible, it is difficult to determine when overtraining becomes chronically detrimental. In addition, some athletes respond to overreaching (180) whereas others do not. Simply doing too much too soon, or repetitive overuse, may result in overtraining and potentially an injury.
summary: Overtraining is a continuum progressing from overload to acute fatigue, overreaching, and ultimately overtraining syndrome. Recovery can be lengthy, and the long-term effects are not fully understood.

content: Two distinct types of overtraining syndromes have been suggested: sympathetic and parasympathetic syndromes. The sympathetic overtraining syndrome includes increased sympathetic activity at rest, whereas the parasympathetic overtraining syndrome includes increased parasympathetic activity at rest and with exercise. Some researchers feel that the sympathetic syndrome develops before the parasympathetic syndrome and predominates in younger athletes who train for speed or power, though few data exist to support this differentiation. All overtraining can eventually result in the parasympathetic syndrome (65). Strength and power overtraining arises when mistakes are made in the acute training program variables and a plateau below the athlete’s potential is observed. Performance reductions may persist for weeks or even months when the overtraining syndrome occurs.
summary: Two types of overtraining syndromes exist: sympathetic (increased sympathetic activity) and parasympathetic (increased parasympathetic activity). All overtraining eventually leads to the parasympathetic syndrome. Strength and power overtraining results from training errors and performance plateaus.

content: The sympathetic overtraining syndrome includes increased sympathetic activity at rest, whereas the parasympathetic overtraining syndrome includes increased parasympathetic activity at rest and with exercise. All overtraining can eventually result in the parasympathetic syndrome (65).
summary: Overtraining syndromes involve either increased sympathetic or parasympathetic activity, with the latter being the eventual outcome.

content: Some researchers feel that the sympathetic syndrome develops before the parasympathetic syndrome and predominates in younger athletes who train for speed or power, though few data exist to support this differentiation.
summary: There is a hypothesis that the sympathetic overtraining syndrome precedes the parasympathetic syndrome, particularly in younger athletes focused on speed and power.

content: Strength and power overtraining arises when mistakes are made in the acute training program variables and a plateau below the athlete’s potential is observed. Performance reductions may persist for weeks or even months when the overtraining syndrome occurs.
summary: Strength and power overtraining stems from errors in training variables, leading to performance plateaus and prolonged declines.

content: A common mistake is a rate of progression that is too high. That is, increasing either the volume or intensity. Both rapid increases in training volume and rapid increases in training intensity over a period of several weeks or months can result in greater structural damage over time and, potentially, overtraining.
summary: Rapid increases in training volume or intensity can contribute to overtraining due to excessive stress on the body.

content: Sources of overtraining must be examined—both sport-specific (e.g., in-season practices and competitions) and non-sport-specific training modalities (e.g., resistance training, plyometrics).
summary: Overtraining can arise from both sport-specific demands and external training modalities.

content: Only a few studies have examined overtraining models in the laboratory. Purposely causing the overtraining syndrome is not easy in the laboratory setting; therefore, monitoring athletes may be more practical as a way of documenting overtraining physiological responses and performance effects.
summary: Laboratory studies on overtraining are limited, making athlete monitoring a more practical approach to understanding its effects.

content: A study by Callister and colleagues (25) demonstrated that anaerobic overtraining in national-level judo athletes may present a different set of symptoms than aerobic overtraining. Fry and colleagues (66) examined intensity-specific overtraining (eight sets of the machine squat exercise with a 95% 1RM load for six consecutive days) and reported nonspecific performance decreases in isokinetic torque production, longer sprint times, and longer agility times.
summary: Different types of overtraining, such as anaerobic and intensity-specific, can manifest with distinct symptoms and performance decrements.

content: They also found that 1RM strength was preserved; the one day of rest per week appeared to enhance tolerance to the protocol. In a subsequent study by Fry and colleagues (69), the one day of rest was eliminated in the overtraining protocol so that the subjects performed 10 sets of 1RM over seven days. This resulted in a significant decrease (>9.9 pounds [4.5 kg]) in the 1RM in 73% of the subjects.
summary: Rest days appear crucial for mitigating the negative effects of intense training, as demonstrated by studies on intensity-specific overtraining.

content: Interestingly, some subjects made progress and did not reach an overtraining state. This demonstrated that the time course of overtraining greatly depends on individual responses, training status, and genetic endowment.
summary: Individual variability in response to training stress highlights the complexity of overtraining and its susceptibility to individual factors.

content: This study showed that an exercise stress that is low in volume but high (100%) in intensity, without a recovery day, can result in dramatic performance decrements in nonspecific variables (e.g., isokinetic knee extension peak torque, sprints) and training-specific variables.
summary: High-intensity training without adequate recovery can lead to significant performance declines across various domains.

content: Overtraining syndrome can occur if training is repeated consistently over time. Overtraining was classically thought to be a function of either chronic use of high intensity or high volume or a combination of the two. Intensity interacts with volume (i.e., sets x reps) to provide a potent stimulus.
summary: Overtraining results from prolonged, excessive training, often characterized by high intensity, high volume, or a combination of both.

content: Too rapid a rate of progression in resistance training can also result in overtraining. The number of sets is an important factor in the calculation of total work or volume of exercise. Volume has been shown to be important for continued gains in performance.
summary: Rapid progression in resistance training, particularly in terms of volume, can contribute to overtraining, while adequate volume is crucial for performance gains.

content: An excessively high volume of exercise can create a stimulus that exceeds the athlete’s ability to recover from the stress and may result in excessive soreness and residual fatigue.
summary: Excessive training volume can overwhelm the body's recovery capacity, leading to soreness and fatigue.

content: Training periodization consists of careful planning to avoid overtraining.
summary: Periodization, a structured training plan, is essential for preventing overtraining.

content: Overreaching has been shown to decrease resting concentrations of testosterone and IGF-I. Overreaching has been shown to augment the acute testosterone response to resistance exercise in trained individuals with previous exposure to overreaching.
summary: Overreaching, a short-term period of intensified training, can influence hormone levels, both decreasing resting concentrations and potentially enhancing acute responses.

content: Volume-related overtraining has been shown to increase cortisol and decrease resting luteinizing hormone and total and free testosterone concentrations. The exercise-induced elevation in total testosterone may be blunted during volume-related overtraining.
summary: Volume-related overtraining can disrupt hormonal balance, increasing cortisol and decreasing testosterone levels.

content: Intensity-related overtraining does not appear to alter resting concentrations of hormones. Endocrine responses appear to require longer than one week of monitoring in order to serve as adequate markers.
summary: Intensity-related overtraining does not seem to affect resting hormone levels, while endocrine responses require extended monitoring for accurate assessment.

content: The most effective markers are those associated with sympathetic overtraining syndrome because this may be the first response to anaerobic overtraining.
summary: Sympathetic overtraining syndrome markers are considered most reliable for early detection of overtraining, particularly in anaerobic training.

content: Mood disturbances, as determined from a profile of mood states, have been associated with increased training volumes. Heavy resistance training is accompanied by decreased vigor, motivation, and confidence; raised levels of tension, depression, anger, fatigue, confusion, anxiety, and irritability; and impaired concentration.
summary: Elevated training volume can negatively impact mood and psychological well-being, leading to various emotional and cognitive impairments.

content: Altered psychological characteristics are also related to changing endocrine profiles.
summary: Psychological changes often accompany hormonal shifts associated with overtraining.

content: Many athletes sense overtraining by the associated psychological alterations that are often observed before actual decrements in performance occur.
summary: Athletes often experience psychological changes before performance declines, serving as an early warning sign of overtraining.

content: Detraining is the cessation of anaerobic training or a substantial reduction in frequency, volume, intensity, or any combination of those three variables that results in decrements in performance and loss of some of the physiological adaptations associated with resistance training.
summary: Detraining refers to the loss of fitness gains and performance decline that occurs when training is reduced or stopped.

content: The magnitude of strength loss depends on the length of the detraining period and the training status of the individual. Detraining may occur in as few as two weeks and possibly sooner in well-trained individuals.
summary: The extent of strength loss during detraining varies depending on the duration and individual's training history, with well-trained individuals experiencing faster decline.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat, a compound exercise targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, is effective for strength development with specific variations influencing muscle activation.

content: Detraining is the cessation of anaerobic training or a substantial reduction in frequency, volume, intensity, or any combination of those three variables that results in decrements in performance and loss of some of the physiological adaptations associated with resistance training. Detraining may occur in as few as two weeks and possibly sooner in well-trained individuals. In recreationally trained men, very little change is seen during the first six weeks of detraining. Strength reductions appear related to neural mechanisms initially, with atrophy predominating as the detraining period extends. The amount of muscle strength retained is rarely lower than pre-training values, indicating that resistance training has a residual effect when the stimulus is removed. When the athlete returns to training, the rate of strength reattainment is high, suggesting the concept of ‘muscle memory’.”
summary: Detraining, the cessation of training or reduction in training variables, leads to performance decrements and loss of physiological adaptations. Strength loss initially involves neural mechanisms, followed by atrophy, but residual strength remains, allowing for rapid reattainment upon resuming training.

content: Altered psychological characteristics are also related to changing endocrine profiles. Many athletes sense overtraining by the associated psychological alterations that are often observed before actual decrements in performance occur.
summary: Psychological changes, potentially linked to endocrine shifts, can signal overtraining, often preceding performance decline.

content: A bout of aerobic exercise places a significant metabolic demand on the body, especially the cardiovascular and respiratory systems. Repeated exposure to the acute stress of exercise results in many changes in the function and responses of all body systems. A basic knowledge of the acute effects of aerobic exercise provides the foundation for an understanding of the chronic adaptations that are discussed in the next section. The primary function of the cardiovascular system during aerobic exercise is to deliver oxygen and other nutrients to the muscles. Cardiac output is the amount of blood pumped by the heart in liters per minute and is determined by the quantity of blood ejected with each beat (stroke volume) and the heart’s rate of pumping (heart rate): Q. = Stroke volume × Heart rate (6.1) where Q. is the cardiac output. Stroke volume is measured in milliliters of blood per beat, and heart rate is measured in beats (contractions) per minute (33).
summary: Aerobic exercise significantly impacts the cardiovascular and respiratory systems. Understanding acute responses to exercise is crucial for comprehending chronic adaptations and the cardiovascular system's role in delivering oxygen and nutrients to working muscles.

content: In the progression from rest to steady-state aerobic exercise, cardiac output initially increases rapidly, then more gradually, and subsequently reaches a plateau. With maximal exercise, cardiac output may increase to four times the resting level of about 5 L/min to a maximum of 20 to 22 L/min. Stroke volume begins to increase at the onset of exercise and continues to rise until the individual’s oxygen consumption is at approximately 50% to 60% of maximal oxygen uptake. At that point, stroke volume begins to plateau. College-aged men have maximal stroke volumes averaging between 100 and 115 ml of blood per beat; maximal stroke volumes for college-aged women are approximately 25% less, due to a smaller average body size.
summary: Cardiac output and stroke volume increase during aerobic exercise, reaching a plateau at a certain intensity. Stroke volume is influenced by individual characteristics, with men generally having higher maximal stroke volumes than women.

content: The first adaptation to aerobic endurance training is an increase in end-diastolic volume. The second adaptation to aerobic endurance training is due to the action of catecholamines including epinephrine and norepinephrine, which produce a more forceful ventricular contraction and greater systolic emptying of the heart. With aerobic exercise, the amount of blood returning to the heart (also called venous return) is increased, and thus end-diastolic volume is significantly increased. With the increased volume, the myocardial fibers become more stretched than at rest, resulting in a more forceful contraction and an increase in force of systolic ejection and greater cardiac emptying. The Frank-Starling mechanism is related to the concept that the force of contraction is a function of the length of the fibers of the muscle wall. This increase in cardiac emptying is characterized by an increase in the ejection fraction, the fraction of the end-diastolic volume ejected from the heart.
summary: Aerobic endurance training leads to adaptations in the heart, including increased end-diastolic volume and enhanced contractility, resulting in greater cardiac emptying and ejection fraction.

content: At the onset of exercise, or even with the anticipation of exercise, sympathetic stimulation increases myocardial contractility and consequently increases stroke volume. Just prior to and at the beginning of an exercise session, a reflex stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system results in an increase in heart rate. Heart rate increases linearly with increases in intensity during aerobic exercise. The rate of increase in heart rate, the actual heart rate response, and the maximal heart rate achieved relate to a variety of individual characteristics of the human system, including fitness and age, in addition to exercise workload.
summary: Sympathetic stimulation and reflex responses increase heart rate and stroke volume at the onset of exercise. Heart rate increases linearly with exercise intensity, influenced by individual factors like fitness and age.

content: Oxygen uptake is the amount of oxygen consumed by the body’s tissues. The oxygen demand of working muscles increases during an acute bout of aerobic exercise and is directly related to the mass of exercising muscle, metabolic efficiency, and exercise intensity. Aerobic exercise involving a larger mass of muscle or a greater level of work is likely to be associated with a higher total oxygen uptake. Increased metabolic efficiency allows for an increase in oxygen uptake, especially at maximal exercise. Maximal oxygen uptake is described as the greatest amount of oxygen that can be used at the cellular level for the entire body.
summary: Oxygen uptake increases during aerobic exercise, influenced by muscle mass, metabolic efficiency, and intensity. Maximal oxygen uptake represents the highest amount of oxygen the body can utilize.

content: This chapter describes the acute responses of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems to aerobic exercise and the associated physiological variables used to measure these responses. Also presented are chronic adaptations that occur with aerobic endurance training for each of the body systems and a brief discussion of designing an aerobic exercise program. The chapter concludes with discussions of external factors, such as altitude and blood doping, that influence responses to aerobic endurance training, as well as the deleterious impact of overtraining and detraining.
summary: This chapter explores acute and chronic responses of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems to aerobic exercise, including adaptations, program design, external factors, and the negative effects of overtraining and detraining.

content: Oxygen uptake is the amount of oxygen consumed by the body’s tissues. The oxygen demand of working muscles increases during an acute bout of aerobic exercise and is directly related to the mass of exercising muscle, metabolic efficiency, and exercise intensity. Aerobic exercise involving a larger mass of muscle or a greater level of work is likely to be associated with a higher total oxygen uptake. Increased metabolic efficiency allows for an increase in oxygen uptake, especially at maximal exercise.
summary: This chunk defines oxygen uptake and its relationship to exercise intensity, muscle mass, and metabolic efficiency.

content: Maximal oxygen uptake is described as the greatest amount of oxygen that can be used at the cellular level for the entire body. It has been found to correlate well with the degree of physical conditioning and is recognized as the most widely accepted measure of cardiorespiratory fitness. The capacity to use oxygen is related primarily to the ability of the heart and circulatory system to transport oxygen and the ability of body tissues to use it.
summary: This chunk defines maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max) and its significance as a measure of cardiorespiratory fitness.

content: Resting oxygen uptake is estimated at 3.5 ml of oxygen per kilogram of body weight per minute (ml · kg–1 · min–1); this value is defined as 1 metabolic equivalent of tasks (MET). Maximal oxygen uptake values in normal, healthy individuals generally range from 25 to 80 ml · kg–1 · min–1, or 7.1 to 22.9 METs, and depend on a variety of physiological parameters, including age and conditioning level.
summary: This chunk provides values for resting oxygen uptake and the typical range for maximal oxygen uptake.

content: Oxygen uptake (V.O2) can be calculated with the Fick equation, which expresses the relationship of cardiac output, oxygen uptake, and arteriovenous oxygen difference. V.O2 = Q. × a-v–O2 difference (6.2) where Q. is the cardiac output in milliliters per minute and a-v–O2 difference is the arteriovenous oxygen difference (the difference in the oxygen content between arterial and venous blood) in milliliters of oxygen per 100 ml of blood. V.O2 = Heart rate × Stroke volume × a-v–O2 difference; V. O2 = 72 beats/min × 65 ml blood/beat × 6 ml O2/100 ml blood = 281 ml O2/min
summary: This chunk explains the Fick equation for calculating oxygen uptake.

content: A means of estimating maximal heart rate is to subtract one’s age from 220; for example, the estimated maximal heart rate for a 47-year-old person is 220 – 47 (age in years) = 173 beats/min. The variance, or standard deviation, around this estimate is ±10 to 12 beats/min; thus the actual maximal heart rate for this individual could be expected to fall within the range of 161 to 185 beats/min. To express oxygen uptake in its common unit (i.e., ml · kg –1 · min–1), divide the result by the person’s weight in kilograms. For an 80 kg (176-pound) athlete, V. O2 = 281 ml O2/min ÷ 80 kg = 3.5 ml · kg–1 · min–1.
summary: This chunk provides information on estimating maximal heart rate and converting oxygen uptake to ml/kg/min.

content: Systolic blood pressure estimates the pressure exerted against the arterial walls as blood is forcefully ejected during ventricular contraction (systole). Systolic blood pressure, when combined with heart rate, can be used to describe the work of the heart. The rate-pressure product, or double product, is obtained using the equation: Rate-pressure product = Heart rate × Systolic blood pressure.
summary: This chunk defines systolic blood pressure and its relationship to heart rate and the rate-pressure product.

content: Diastolic blood pressure is used to estimate the pressure exerted against the arterial walls when no blood is being forcefully ejected through the vessels (diastole). Diastolic blood pressure provides an indication of peripheral resistance and can decrease with aerobic exercise due to vasodilation. In systemic circulation, pressure is highest in the aorta and arteries and rapidly falls off within the venous circulation. Because pumping by the heart is pulsatile, arterial pressure fluctuates between a systolic level of 120 mmHg and a diastolic level of 80 mmHg (approximate values). As blood flow continues through the systemic circulation, its pressure falls progressively to nearly 0 mmHg (venous pressure) by the time it reaches the termination of the vena cava in the right atrium.
summary: This chunk defines diastolic blood pressure, its relationship to peripheral resistance, and the typical blood pressure gradient in systemic circulation.

content: The mean arterial pressure is the average blood pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. The mean arterial pressure is usually less than the average of the systolic and diastolic pressures because the arterial pressure usually remains nearer the diastolic level than the systolic level during a greater portion of the cardiac cycle. Mean arterial blood pressure = [(Systolic blood pressure – Diastolic blood pressure) ÷ 3] + Diastolic blood pressure.
summary: This chunk defines mean arterial pressure and its calculation.

content: Normal resting blood pressure generally ranges from 110 to 139 mmHg systolic and from 60 to 89 mmHg diastolic. With maximal aerobic exercise, systolic pressure can normally rise to as much as 220 to 260 mmHg, while diastolic pressure remains at the resting level or decreases slightly.
summary: This chunk provides typical ranges for resting and exercise blood pressure.

content: Blood flow is a function of resistance in the blood vessels—as resistance is reduced, blood flow is increased, and as resistance increases, blood flow is decreased. The amount of resistance to blood flow is primarily a function of the diameter of the systemic blood vessels. The resistance of the entire systemic circulation is called total peripheral resistance. As blood vessels throughout the body become constricted, total peripheral resistance increases; with dilation, peripheral resistance decreases. Resistance to blood flow is increased with increasing viscosity of the blood and the length of the vessel.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between blood flow and resistance, and factors influencing resistance.

content: Vasoconstriction and vasodilation of blood vessels are the primary mechanisms for regulating regional blood flow. During aerobic exercise, blood flow to active muscles is considerably increased by the dilation of local arterioles; and at the same time, blood flow to other organ systems is reduced by constriction of the arterioles. At rest, 15% to 20% of cardiac output is distributed to skeletal muscle, whereas with vigorous exercise this value may rise to 90% of cardiac output. Acute aerobic exercise results in increased cardiac output, stroke volume, heart rate, oxygen uptake, systolic blood pressure, and blood flow to active muscles and a decrease in diastolic blood pressure.
summary: This chunk describes the role of vasoconstriction and vasodilation in regulating blood flow during exercise and the changes in cardiac output and blood flow during acute aerobic exercise.

content: Resistance to blood flow is increased with increasing viscosity of the blood and the length of the vessel. Vasoconstriction and vasodilation of blood vessels are the primary mechanisms for regulating regional blood flow. During aerobic exercise, blood flow to active muscles is considerably increased by the dilation of local arterioles; and at the same time, blood flow to other organ systems is reduced by constriction of the arterioles. At rest, 15% to 20% of cardiac output is distributed to skeletal muscle, whereas with vigorous exercise this value may rise to 90% of cardiac output.
summary: This chunk describes the regulation of blood flow, highlighting the role of vasoconstriction and vasodilation, and the significant increase in blood flow to active muscles during aerobic exercise.

content: Acute aerobic exercise results in increased cardiac output, stroke volume, heart rate, oxygen uptake, systolic blood pressure, and blood flow to active muscles and a decrease in diastolic blood pressure. Aerobic exercise provides for the greatest impact on both oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide production, as compared to other types of exercise.
summary: This chunk outlines the physiological responses to acute aerobic exercise, including increased cardiovascular function and oxygen uptake.

content: Significant increases in oxygen delivered to the tissue, carbon dioxide returned to the lungs, and minute ventilation provide for appropriate levels of alveolar gas concentrations during aerobic exercise. With aerobic exercise, increased minute ventilation occurs as a result of increases in the depth of breathing, frequency of breathing, or both.
summary: This chunk explains how aerobic exercise maintains appropriate alveolar gas concentrations through increased ventilation and gas exchange.

content: During strenuous exercise, the breathing frequency of healthy young adults usually increases from 12 to 15 breaths per minute at rest to 35 to 45 breaths per minute, while tidal volume (TV), the amount of air inhaled and exhaled with each breath, increases from resting values (of 0.4 to 1 L) to as much as 3 L or greater. Consequently, the minute ventilation can increase to 15 to 25 times the resting value, or to values of 90 to 150 L of air per minute.
summary: This chunk details the changes in breathing frequency and tidal volume during strenuous exercise, leading to a significant increase in minute ventilation.

content: During low- to moderate-intensity aerobic exercise, there is an increase in ventilation directly associated with both increased oxygen uptake and carbon dioxide production. In this instance, the increase in ventilation is primarily due to increased tidal volume. The ratio of minute ventilation to oxygen uptake is termed the ventilatory equivalent and ranges between 20 and 25 L of air per liter of oxygen consumed.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between ventilation, oxygen uptake, and carbon dioxide production during low- to moderate-intensity aerobic exercise.

content: In more intense exercise (generally above 45% to 65% of maximal oxygen uptake in untrained individuals and 70% to 90% in trained athletes), breathing frequency takes on a greater role. At these levels, minute ventilation rises disproportionately to the increases in oxygen uptake and begins to parallel the abrupt rise in blood lactate. At this point, the ventilatory equivalent may increase to 35 or 40 L of air per liter of oxygen consumed with this high-intensity activity.
summary: This chunk describes the changes in ventilatory response during high-intensity exercise, where breathing frequency becomes more dominant.

content: With inspiration, air enters the alveoli, the functional unit of the pulmonary system where gas exchange occurs. However, with inspiration, air also occupies areas of the respiratory passages: the nose, mouth, trachea, bronchi, and bronchioles. This area is not useful for gas exchange and is called the anatomical dead space. The normal volume of this air space is approximately 150 ml in young adults and increases with age.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of anatomical dead space, the volume of air in the respiratory passages that does not participate in gas exchange.

content: Because the respiratory passages stretch with deep breathing, the anatomical dead space increases as tidal volume increases. Nevertheless, the increase in tidal volume with deep breathing is proportionately greater than any increase in anatomical dead space. Thus, increasing tidal volume (deeper breathing) provides for more efficient ventilation than increasing frequency of breathing alone.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the relationship between tidal volume, anatomical dead space, and ventilation efficiency.

content: Physiological dead space refers to alveoli in which poor blood flow, poor ventilation, or other problems with the alveolar surface impair gas exchange. The physiological dead space in the lungs of healthy people is usually negligible because all or nearly all alveoli are functional. Certain types of lung disease such as chronic obstructive lung disease or pneumonia can significantly reduce alveolar function, increasing physiological dead space by as much as 10 times the volume of anatomical dead space.
summary: This chunk defines physiological dead space and explains how lung diseases can increase it.

content: During aerobic exercise, large amounts of oxygen diffuse from the capillaries into the tissues; increased levels of carbon dioxide move from the blood into the alveoli; and minute ventilation increases to maintain appropriate alveolar concentrations of these gases. Diffusion is the movement of oxygen and carbon dioxide across a cell membrane and is a function of the concentration of each gas and the resulting partial pressure exerted by the molecular motion of each gas.
summary: This chunk describes the gas exchange process during aerobic exercise, emphasizing the role of diffusion.

content: Diffusion results from the movement of gas from high concentration to low concentration. At the tissue level, where oxygen is utilized in metabolism and carbon dioxide is produced, the partial pressures of these gases in some instances differ considerably from those in arterial blood. At rest, the partial pressure of oxygen in the fluid immediately outside a muscle cell rapidly drops from 100 mmHg in arterial blood to as low as 40 mmHg, while the partial pressure of carbon dioxide is elevated above that of arterial blood to about 46 mmHg.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of diffusion and the partial pressure gradients of oxygen and carbon dioxide at rest.

content: During high-intensity aerobic exercise, the partial pressures of these gases are approximately 3 mmHg for oxygen and 90 mmHg for carbon dioxide. These pressure gradients cause the movement of gases across cell membranes. The diffusing capacities of oxygen and, in particular, carbon dioxide increase dramatically with exercise, which facilitates their exchange.
summary: This chunk describes the changes in partial pressures and diffusing capacities during high-intensity exercise.

content: Oxygen is carried in blood either dissolved in the plasma or combined with hemoglobin. Because oxygen is not readily soluble in fluids, only about 3 ml of oxygen can be carried per liter of plasma. This limited amount of oxygen that is transported in plasma contributes to the partial pressure of oxygen in blood and other body fluids, thus playing a role in the mechanisms that regulate breathing and in the diffusion of oxygen into alveolar blood and the cells of body tissues.
summary: This chunk explains the two ways oxygen is transported in blood and the limited capacity of plasma to carry oxygen.

content: Given the limited capacity of plasma to carry oxygen, the majority of oxygen in blood is carried by the hemoglobin. Men have about 15 to 16 g of hemoglobin per 100 ml of blood and women have about 14 g of hemoglobin per 100 ml of blood. One gram of hemoglobin can carry 1.34 ml of oxygen; thus, the oxygen-carrying capacity of 100 ml of blood is about 20 ml of oxygen in men and a little less in women.
summary: This chunk describes the role of hemoglobin in oxygen transport and the differences in oxygen-carrying capacity between men and women.

content: Because oxygen is not readily soluble in fluids, only about 3 ml of oxygen can be carried per liter of plasma. This limited amount of oxygen that is transported in plasma contributes to the partial pressure of oxygen in blood and other body fluids, thus playing a role in the mechanisms that regulate breathing and in the diffusion of oxygen into alveolar blood and the cells of body tissues. Given the limited capacity of plasma to carry oxygen, the majority of oxygen in blood is carried by the hemoglobin.
summary: Oxygen transport in blood: Plasma carries a limited amount, while hemoglobin is the primary carrier.

content: Men have about 15 to 16 g of hemoglobin per 100 ml of blood and women have about 14 g of hemoglobin per 100 ml of blood. One gram of hemoglobin can carry 1.34 ml of oxygen; thus, the oxygen-carrying capacity of 100 ml of blood is about 20 ml of oxygen in men and a little less in women.
summary: Hemoglobin concentration and oxygen-carrying capacity differ slightly between men and women.

content: The way in which carbon dioxide is removed from the system has some similarities to oxygen transport, but the vast amount of carbon dioxide is removed by a more complex process. After carbon dioxide is formed in the cell, it is transported out of the cell by diffusion and subsequently transported to the lungs. Only a limited quantity of carbon dioxide—about 5% of that produced during metabolism—is carried in the plasma; similar to the situation with oxygen, this limited amount of carbon dioxide contributes to establishing the partial pressure of carbon dioxide in blood. Some carbon dioxide is also transported via hemoglobin, but this too is a limited amount.
summary: Carbon dioxide transport: Initial transport via diffusion, with plasma and hemoglobin carrying limited amounts.

content: The greatest amount of carbon dioxide removal (approximately 70%) is from its combination with water and delivery to the lungs in the form of bicarbonate (HCO3–). The initial step in this reversible reaction is the combination of carbon dioxide in solution with water in the red blood cells to form carbonic acid (H2CO3). Carbonic acid then dissociates into hydrogen ions (H+) and bicarbonate ions (HCO3–). Bicarbonate ions are transported in the plasma to the lungs, where they are converted back into carbon dioxide and water, which are then exhaled.
summary: Bicarbonate formation is the primary mechanism for carbon dioxide removal from the blood.

content: PCO2 is 46 mmHg. PO2 is 100 mmHg. PCO2 is 40 mmHg. PO2 is 40 mmHg. PCO2 is 46 mmHg. PO2 is 40 mmHg. PCO2 is 40 mmHg. PO2 in inspired air is 149 mmHg. PCO2 in inspired air is 0.3 mmHg. PO2 is 159 mmHg. PCO2 is 0.3 mmHg.
summary: Partial pressures of oxygen (PO2) and carbon dioxide (PCO2) in blood and inspired air.

content: Aerobic endurance training increases capillarization. For optimal aerobic exercise performance, increasing maximal oxygen uptake is of paramount importance. One of the primary mechanisms for increasing maximal oxygen uptake is the enhancement of central cardiovascular function (cardiac output).
summary: Aerobic endurance training benefits: increased capillarization and enhanced cardiovascular function.

content: The normal discharge rate of the sinoatrial (SA) node ranges from 60 to 80 times per minute. Aerobic endurance training results in a significantly slower discharge rate of the SA node due to an increase in parasympathetic tone. Increased stroke volume also affects the resting heart rate—more blood is pumped per contraction so that the heart contracts less frequently.
summary: Aerobic endurance training's effect on heart rate: slower SA node discharge and lower resting heart rate.

content: Aerobic endurance training can increase the heart’s ability to pump blood per contraction at rest. Highly conditioned aerobic endurance athletes have resting heart rates that commonly range from 40 to 60 beats/min. The most significant change in cardiovascular function with long-term (6-12 months) aerobic endurance training is the increase in maximal cardiac output, resulting primarily from improved stroke volume. A significantly lower heart rate in response to a standardized submaximal level of work is another hallmark of aerobic endurance training. The heart rate increases more slowly in trained athletes than in sedentary people for a given workload.
summary: Aerobic endurance training's impact on cardiovascular function: increased stroke volume, lower resting heart rate, and slower heart rate response to exercise.

content: Because the maximal heart rate may actually decrease slightly with prolonged training, perhaps as a result of increased parasympathetic tone, the size of the left ventricle (both chamber volume and wall thickness) and the strength of contractions (increased contractility) are key to increasing stroke volume with submaximal as well as maximal exercise.
summary: Long-term aerobic training: potential for decreased maximal heart rate, but increased left ventricle size and contractility contribute to improved stroke volume.

content: In addition to delivering oxygen, nutrients, and hormones, the capillary circulation provides the means for removing heat and metabolic by-products.
summary: Capillary circulation's role: not only delivering but also removing waste products and heat.

content: The most significant change in cardiovascular function with long-term (6-12 months) aerobic endurance training is the increase in maximal cardiac output, resulting primarily from improved stroke volume. A significantly lower heart rate in response to a standardized submaximal level of work is another hallmark of aerobic endurance training. The heart rate increases more slowly in trained athletes than in sedentary people for a given workload. Because the maximal heart rate may actually decrease slightly with prolonged training, perhaps as a result of increased parasympathetic tone, the size of the left ventricle (both chamber volume and wall thickness) and the strength of contractions (increased contractility) are key to increasing stroke volume with submaximal as well as maximal exercise.
summary: Aerobic endurance training primarily increases maximal cardiac output through improved stroke volume, leading to a lower resting heart rate and slower heart rate increases during exercise.

content: In addition to delivering oxygen, nutrients, and hormones, the capillary circulation provides the means for removing heat and metabolic by-products. Increased capillarization has been observed in response to the increased density of muscle associated with aerobic endurance training and is a function of volume and intensity of training. This increase in capillary density decreases the diffusion distance for oxygen and metabolic substrates.
summary: Aerobic endurance training increases capillary density, improving the delivery of oxygen and removal of metabolic by-products.

content: Aerobic endurance training reduces the production of lactic acid. Aerobic endurance training changes hormone release, particularly catecholamine release at high-intensity exercise. Aerobic endurance training increases the rate of lactic acid removal.
summary: Aerobic endurance training improves lactate metabolism by reducing production and increasing removal.

content: The muscular component of an aerobic endurance training program involves submaximal muscle contractions extended over a large number of repetitions with little recovery. The relative intensity of aerobic endurance training is very low, and the overall volume is very high.
summary: Aerobic endurance training involves submaximal muscle contractions over a high volume.

content: Aerobic endurance training encourages relative increases in aerobic potential that are similar in Type I and Type II fibers. Type I fibers have a higher preexisting initial aerobic capacity. Type I fibers possess an oxidative capacity greater than that of Type II fibers both before and after training.
summary: Aerobic endurance training increases aerobic potential in both Type I and Type II muscle fibers, with Type I fibers already possessing a higher capacity.

content: If the intensity is sufficient, as in running repeated 800 m intervals, fast-twitch fibers (especially Type IIx fibers) also make a significant contribution to the effort. Chronic aerobic endurance training reduces the concentration of glycolytic enzymes and can reduce the overall muscle mass of Type II fibers. Selective hypertrophy of Type I muscle fibers occurs due to their increased recruitment during aerobic activities.
summary: While primarily targeting Type I fibers, high-intensity aerobic training can also engage Type II fibers. Chronic training reduces Type II fiber size and glycolytic enzymes, while increasing Type I fiber size.

content: The cross-sectional diameter of Type I fibers that undergo hypertrophy from aerobic training is not as great as that seen in Type II fibers adapted to resistance exercise. The hypertrophy of Type I fibers from a bodybuilding-style resistance training program is greater than the hypertrophy of Type I fibers from aerobic training. There is little evidence to show that Type II fibers change into Type I fibers as a result of aerobic endurance training.
summary: Aerobic training induces smaller Type I fiber hypertrophy compared to resistance training. There is no significant conversion of Type II fibers to Type I fibers.

content: There may be a gradual conversion within the two major Type II fiber subgroups—of Type IIx fibers to Type IIa fibers. Type IIa fibers possess a greater oxidative capacity than Type IIx fibers and have functional characteristics more similar to those of Type I fibers. The result of the conversion of Type IIx fibers to Type IIa fibers is a greater number of muscle fibers that can contribute to aerobic endurance performance.
summary: Aerobic training may shift Type IIx fibers towards a more oxidative Type IIa phenotype, increasing the number of fibers capable of aerobic work.

content: At the cellular level, muscular adaptations to aerobic exercise include an increase in the size and number of mitochondria and increased myoglobin content. Myoglobin is a protein that transports oxygen within the cell. Mitochondria are the organelles in cells that are responsible for aerobically producing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) via oxidation of glycogen. Mitochondria become larger and more numerous by training.
summary: Aerobic training increases mitochondrial density and myoglobin content, enhancing cellular ATP production and oxygen transport.

content: Ventilatory adaptations observed appear to be highly specific to activities that involve the type of exercise used in training. Training adaptations include increased tidal volume and breathing frequency with maximal exercise. With submaximal activity, breathing frequency is often reduced and tidal volume is increased.
summary: Ventilatory adaptations are specific to the type of training, with increased tidal volume and breathing frequency at maximal exercise and reduced breathing frequency with increased tidal volume at submaximal exercise.

content: Ventilatory adaptations result from local, neural, or chemical adaptations in the specific muscles trained through exercise. Neural adaptations play a significant role in the early stages of aerobic endurance training. Efficiency is increased and fatigue of the contractile mechanisms is delayed as a result of neural adaptations. Improved aerobic performance may result in a rotation of neural activity among synergists and among motor units within a muscle.
summary: Ventilatory adaptations are driven by local, neural, and chemical changes. Neural adaptations are crucial in early training, improving efficiency and delaying fatigue.

content: One of the fundamental adaptive responses to aerobic endurance training is an increase in the aerobic capacity of the trained musculature. An athlete can perform a given absolute intensity of exercise with greater ease after training. After training, an athlete can exercise at a greater relative intensity of a now-higher maximal aerobic power. Measuring an athlete’s maximal oxygen uptake before and after aerobic endurance training may not accurately portray his or her ability to perform during competition.
summary: Aerobic training increases aerobic capacity, allowing athletes to perform at higher intensities and with greater ease. However, maximal oxygen uptake alone may not fully reflect competitive performance.

content: An athlete who can run the marathon at a pace equal to 75% of maximal oxygen uptake may, after training, be able to maintain a pace that is 80% of maximal aerobic power.
summary: Training allows athletes to perform at a higher percentage of their maximal aerobic power during competition.

content: One of the fundamental adaptive responses to aerobic endurance training is an increase in the aerobic capacity of the trained musculature. Mitochondria are combined with an increase in the quantity of oxygen that can be delivered to mitochondria by the greater concentration of myoglobin, enhancing the capacity of the muscle tissue to extract and use oxygen. This adaptation is further augmented by increases in the level and activity of the enzymes involved in the aerobic metabolism of glucose (38) and a parallel increase in glycogen (30, 32) and triglyceride (64) stores.
summary: Aerobic endurance training increases muscle aerobic capacity through mitochondrial adaptations, oxygen delivery, and metabolic enzyme activity.

content: An athlete can perform a given absolute intensity of exercise with greater ease after training. After training, an athlete can exercise at a greater relative intensity of a now-higher maximal aerobic power. An athlete who can run the marathon at a pace equal to 75% of maximal oxygen uptake may, after training, be able to maintain a pace that is 80% of maximal aerobic power. Glycogen sparing (less glycogen use during exercise) and increased fat utilization within the muscle prolong performance.
summary: Training improves exercise performance by increasing maximal aerobic power and allowing athletes to exercise at higher intensities with greater ease.

content: The OBLA occurs at a higher percentage (up to 80-90%) of the trained athlete’s aerobic capacity. High-intensity aerobic endurance training augments the absolute secretion rates of many hormones, including epinephrine, in response to maximal exercise, although trained athletes have blunted responses to submaximal exercise. Their hormone concentrations equal those of their untrained counterparts at the same relative submaximal exercise intensity. The greater hormonal response patterns to maximal exercise appear to augment the athlete’s ability to tolerate and sustain prolonged high aerobic exercise intensities.
summary: Training shifts the onset of blood lactate accumulation (OBLA) to higher intensities and enhances hormonal responses to exercise.

content: Measuring an athlete’s maximal oxygen uptake before and after aerobic endurance training may not accurately portray his or her ability to perform during competition.
