A dose–response effect resembling a logarithmic relationship (see figure 9.2) is apparent between the concentration of exogenous anabolic steroid used and increases in lean body mass (68). For some time it was postulated that increases in body mass with androgen use came from an increase in body water (93). An increase in total body water is expected with an increase in muscle mass, since water constitutes a majority of the cellular weight; however, anabolic steroids may increase water retention as well by increasing interstitial and extracellular volume. Although water retention may explain why not all of the weight gain is sustained after cessation of anabolic steroid use, this issue is still not well understood.
summary: This chunk focuses on the impact of anabolic steroids on body mass, highlighting a study on powerlifters and discussing the potential role of water retention in weight gain.

content: In a study of experienced male bodybuilders, an eight-week cycle of nandrolone decanoate (200 mg/week, intramuscularly) resulted in a significant 2.2 kg (5-pound) increase in body mass (2.6 kg increase in fat-free mass and 0.4 kg decrease in fat mass), with no change in hydration of fat-free mass (200). In addition, the extracellular and intracellular water ratio was unaltered. Even after six weeks of discontinued androgen use, the body mass of the bodybuilders was still significantly greater than baseline levels (1.6 kg [3.5 pounds] greater), yet no hydration changes were seen. The increase in fat-free mass and possible reduction in fat mass may last for several months after cessation of use (69) (figure 9.3). Thus, athletes may derive a benefit from steroid use even if they stop taking the drugs long enough before competition to obtain a negative drug test. This is why unannounced, year-round drug testing of some elite athletes is important for the prevention of unfair drug use.
summary: This chunk details the findings of a study on bodybuilders using nandrolone decanoate, emphasizing the long-lasting effects of steroid use on body composition and the implications for drug testing.

content: Initially, researchers examining the ergogenicity of exogenously administered anabolic steroids were unable to see any significant performance effects (66, 70, 81, 131, 187). The scientific and medical community suggested that anabolic steroids had little influence on athletic performance. Anecdotal reports from gyms showed large strength improvements in athletes. Several initial studies used physiological doses of anabolic steroids, in contrast to the suprapharmacological doses typically taken by athletes. Subjects in some initial studies were shutting down their own endogenous production of testosterone and replacing it with an exogenous anabolic steroid. Several studies used a mode of exercise for strength assessment that was different than the training stimulus. Several studies used subjects who had only minimal resistance training experience.
summary: This chunk discusses the early research on anabolic steroids and the discrepancies between initial findings and anecdotal reports, highlighting methodological limitations in early studies.

content: Significant strength gains have been consistently reported when exogenous androgens have been administered to experienced resistance-trained athletes (4, 93, 147, 182, 206). Strength gains in experienced strength-trained athletes are generally quite small, relative to those seen in novice lifters. When strength-trained athletes begin to use anabolic steroids, their strength gains may be two- to threefold higher than those typically seen in similarly trained athletes who are not supplementing (93, 200, 206). In a case study of a bodybuilder who was cycling anabolic steroids, strength levels were elevated during the times of the year when the athlete was using anabolic steroids (3). During cycles when the athlete trained drug-free, strength levels tended to decline. The results in the case study are consistent with many of the anecdotal reports from competitive athletes.
summary: This chunk presents evidence of significant strength gains in experienced athletes using anabolic steroids, comparing their progress to that of non-supplementing athletes and citing a case study supporting these findings.

content: The principal mechanisms that appear to be responsible for increasing strength and lean body mass are increases in protein synthesis and inhibition of the catabolic effects resulting from high-intensity training. The anticatabolic activity of androgen administration is reflected by changes in the testosterone-to-cortisol ratio (T/C ratio) (168). A higher T/C ratio may not only give the athlete the ability to maintain a higher intensity and volume of training, but also enhance the recovery processes between exercise sessions. If the athlete can train harder and for a longer duration, the stimulus presented to the muscle will result in a greater physiological adaptation.
summary: This chunk explains the physiological mechanisms underlying the ergogenic effects of anabolic steroids, focusing on protein synthesis, catabolism, and the testosterone-to-cortisol ratio.

content: The results in the case study are consistent with many of the anecdotal reports from competitive athletes. The purported ergogenic benefits commonly attributed to anabolic steroid use are increased muscle mass, strength, and athletic performance, but these changes depend on the training status of the individual.
summary: Anecdotal evidence and case studies suggest anabolic steroids can increase muscle mass, strength, and athletic performance, but the effect depends on the individual's training level.

content: The principal mechanisms that appear to be responsible for increasing strength and lean body mass are increases in protein synthesis and inhibition of the catabolic effects resulting from high-intensity training. A higher T/C ratio may not only give the athlete the ability to maintain a higher intensity and volume of training, but also enhance the recovery processes between exercise sessions. If the athlete can train harder and for a longer duration, the stimulus presented to the muscle will result in a greater physiological adaptation.
summary: Increased strength and lean body mass are primarily due to increased protein synthesis and reduced muscle breakdown from intense training. A higher testosterone-to-cortisol ratio allows for more intense and frequent training, leading to greater muscle adaptation.

content: Anabolic steroid use is also associated with changes in aggression, arousal, and irritability. Elevations in arousal and self-esteem may be a positive side effect for the anabolic steroid user. Increases in aggressiveness can also be perceived as a benefit, especially for athletes participating in contact sports. Increased aggressiveness may not be confined to athletic performance.
summary: Anabolic steroids can cause psychological changes, including increased aggression, arousal, and irritability. While these effects may be beneficial for some athletes, they can also pose risks outside of sports.

content: The East Germans reportedly used anabolic steroids for this effect, delivering high doses to the central nervous system by taking steroids through the nose. Anecdotal reports suggest that this practice markedly increased aggressiveness and enhanced performance among athletes (56).
summary: Anecdotal evidence suggests that East German athletes used nasal steroid administration to enhance aggression and performance.

content: Anabolic steroid users experiencing increased aggressiveness may pose a threat both to themselves and to those they come in contact with (156, 157). Anabolic steroids are also associated with mood swings and psychotic episodes. Studies have shown that nearly 60% of anabolic steroid users experience increases in irritability and aggressiveness (158, 176).
summary: Increased aggression from anabolic steroid use can pose risks to both the user and others. Additionally, steroids are linked to mood swings and psychosis.

content: Pope and colleagues (160) reported significant elevations in aggressiveness and manic scores following 12 weeks of testosterone cypionate injections in a controlled double-blind crossover study. Most subjects in the study showed little psychological effect, and few developed prominent effects.
summary: A study found that testosterone injections increased aggression and manic scores in some participants, but most experienced minimal psychological effects.

content: A cause-and-effect relationship has yet to be identified in anabolic steroid users, but it does appear that individuals who experience psychological or behavioral changes recover when steroid use is discontinued (73). Psychosis may occur in some susceptible individuals or perhaps with doses that also activate corticosteroid-specific responses (corticosteroids have been known to produce psychosis).
summary: While a direct cause-and-effect relationship between anabolic steroids and psychological changes is unclear, discontinuing use often leads to symptom improvement. Psychosis may occur in some individuals, potentially due to steroid-induced corticosteroid responses.

content: The anticatabolic activity of androgen administration is reflected by changes in the testosterone-to-cortisol ratio (T/C ratio) (168).
summary: Androgen administration, such as through anabolic steroids, influences the testosterone-to-cortisol ratio, reducing the catabolic effects of stress.

content: Most information about adverse medical events related to anabolic steroid use comes from athletes who self-administer the drugs. Anecdotally, bodybuilders appear to have a disproportionately high rate of anabolic steroid use and adverse events compared to strength/power athletes. Bodybuilders often consume other drugs, such as diuretics, thyroid hormones, insulin, and anti-estrogens, which can both alleviate and exacerbate side effects.
summary: Information about anabolic steroid side effects primarily comes from self-administered use, with bodybuilders reporting higher rates of adverse events compared to strength athletes. Bodybuilders often combine steroids with other drugs, which can amplify or mitigate side effects.

content: There are differences between the side effects of anabolic steroid use under medical supervision and those associated with abuse. Many side effects linked to anabolic steroid abuse are reversible upon cessation of use.
summary: Side effects from medically supervised anabolic steroid use differ from those associated with abuse. Many abuse-related side effects are reversible upon stopping steroid use.

content: Some medical professionals believe that the problems associated with anabolic steroid use may be overstated.
summary: Some medical professionals believe that the negative consequences of anabolic steroid use are exaggerated.

content: The use of testosterone precursors, also called prohormones, as an ergogenic aid originated from a study showing a threefold increase in testosterone in healthy women who were given 100 mg of either androstenedione or dehydroepiandrosterone. Athletes continue to supplement with testosterone precursors (androstenedione, androstenediol, and dehydroepiandrosterone [DHEA]) because they believe they will increase testosterone concentrations and lead to performance improvements similar to those seen with anabolic steroids.
summary: The use of testosterone precursors as performance enhancers stems from a study showing increased testosterone in women after taking androstenedione or DHEA. Athletes continue to use these precursors, believing they mimic the effects of anabolic steroids.

content: Androstenedione and DHEA have only 1/5th and 1/10th the biological activity of testosterone, respectively. Most testosterone precursors are rapidly eliminated from circulation, similar to testosterone.
summary: Testosterone precursors, like androstenedione and DHEA, have significantly lower biological activity compared to testosterone and are quickly eliminated from the body.

content: Testosterone precursors were officially listed as controlled substances in the 2004 Anabolic Steroid Control Act passed by the U.S. Congress, requiring a physician's prescription.
summary: Due to their potential for misuse, testosterone precursors are now classified as controlled substances in the U.S., requiring a prescription.

content: Studies on the effectiveness of testosterone precursors have produced mixed results. No significant differences in strength or body composition were observed in middle-aged men performing a resistance training program while supplementing with either DHEA, androstenedione (100 mg), or a placebo for three months. When DHEA supplementation (150 mg) was examined in a group of younger males (19-29 years) on a two-weeks-on, one-week-off cycle for eight weeks, there were no gains in strength or lean tissue. The investigators in the DHEA study were unable to observe any changes in serum testosterone, estrone, estradiol, or lipid concentrations with supplementation. Even in studies using higher dosages (300 mg) of androstenedione for eight weeks, in a similar two-weeks-on, one-week-off protocol, no significant effect was observed in strength, muscle size, or testosterone concentrations. Androstenedione supplementation did cause an increase in serum concentrations of estradiol and estrone and was associated with lower high-density lipoprotein concentrations.
summary: Research on testosterone precursor effectiveness has yielded inconsistent results. Studies on DHEA and androstenedione supplementation in both younger and older men have generally failed to demonstrate significant improvements in strength, body composition, or testosterone levels.

content: Insulin is a very potent anabolic hormone. Insulin is secreted by the pancreas in response to elevations in blood glucose and amino acid concentrations. Insulin facilitates the uptake of glucose and amino acids into the cell. Insulin is considered an anabolic hormone because it increases protein synthesis.
summary: Insulin is a powerful anabolic hormone released by the pancreas in response to elevated blood sugar and amino acids. It promotes glucose and amino acid uptake into cells, ultimately increasing protein synthesis.

content: Androstenedione supplementation did cause an increase in serum concentrations of estradiol and estrone and was associated with lower high-density lipoprotein concentrations.
summary: Androstenedione supplementation increased estrogen levels and lowered HDL cholesterol.

content: Insulin is a very potent anabolic hormone. Insulin is secreted by the pancreas in response to elevations in blood glucose and amino acid concentrations. Insulin facilitates the uptake of glucose and amino acids into the cell. Insulin is considered an anabolic hormone because it increases protein synthesis.
summary: Insulin is a potent anabolic hormone secreted by the pancreas in response to elevated blood glucose and amino acids, promoting glucose and amino acid uptake and protein synthesis.

content: Insulin is a peptide hormone and its use as an ergogenic aid cannot be detected in the urine. Insulin is primarily taken by bodybuilders to potentiate the effects of growth hormone and insulin-like growth factors. The use of insulin among those who are not bodybuilders appears to be on the rise (165).
summary: Insulin, undetectable in urine, is primarily used by bodybuilders to enhance growth hormone and IGF-1 effects, with its use increasing among non-bodybuilders.

content: A major side effect of insulin use is hypoglycemia (i.e., low blood sugar), which can prove to be fatal.
summary: Insulin use carries the risk of hypoglycemia, a potentially fatal condition.

content: Human growth hormone (HGH) is a protein secreted from the anterior pituitary gland. HGH is anabolic due to its stimulation of bone and skeletal muscle growth. HGH has metabolic functions such as maintaining blood glucose levels, increasing the uptake of glucose and amino acids into muscle cells, and stimulating the release of fatty acids from fat cells.
summary: HGH, secreted by the pituitary gland, is anabolic, promoting bone and muscle growth, and has metabolic functions like regulating blood glucose and fatty acid release.

content: The primary source of pharmacological growth hormone is a relatively complicated molecule from which it is synthetically derived using recombinant DNA technology. Until 1986, the only source of HGH was the pituitaries of human cadavers. Because HGH receptors are unable to cross-react with growth hormone from animal sources, the financial cost of HGH was very high before 1986.
summary: Before 1986, HGH was derived from human cadavers, making it expensive. Recombinant DNA technology now provides a more affordable and readily available source.

content: The development of recombinant HGH provided clinicians with a relatively low-risk drug at a lower cost with greater availability. Clinicians can now prescribe recombinant HGH to increase stature in normal children and alter body composition in normal adults.
summary: Recombinant HGH offers a safer, more affordable, and widely available alternative, enabling its use in treating growth disorders and altering body composition.

content: Since the 1980s, athletes have become well aware of the anabolic potential of HGH and its ability to reduce body fat. The use of HGH by competitive athletes appears to be on the rise; it is either taken alone or stacked with anabolic steroids (165).
summary: Athletes have recognized HGH's anabolic and fat-reducing properties, leading to its increasing use, often combined with steroids.

content: It has been concluded that prohormones fall far short of providing the anabolic effects generally associated with androgens (37). The use of prohormones has not been studied in highly trained athletes. Oral administration has been the primary method of taking prohormones.
summary: Prohormones offer limited anabolic effects compared to androgens and lack research on their impact on trained athletes. Oral administration is the primary method of intake.

content: Leder and colleagues (127) demonstrated that 100 and 300 mg dosages of oral androstenedione did convert to testosterone; however, most of the conversion occurred in the liver, from which 89% of the converted testosterone is excreted without reentry into the circulation. Oral use of prohormones may not be as effective as other means of intake (e.g., injection). Continued study of the efficacy of the testosterone precursors for performance enhancement is still needed; specifically warranted is examination of other routes of ingestion in a trained competitive athletic population.
summary: Oral androstenedione converts to testosterone, but most is excreted. Oral prohormone use may be less effective than other methods, requiring further research on alternative routes of administration in athletes.

content: Human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) is a hormone obtained from the placenta of pregnant women and is very closely related in structure and function to luteinizing hormone. When injected into men, HCG can increase testicular testosterone production; testosterone levels can nearly double within four days after a large intramuscular injection (47).
summary: HCG, similar to luteinizing hormone, boosts testosterone production in men, potentially doubling levels within days of injection.

content: HCG is primarily used by athletes who are finishing a cycle of anabolic steroids and are looking to activate their own endogenous testosterone production (165). Because HCG can stimulate endogenous testosterone production, the thinking is that it can help stabilize the testosterone-to-epitestosterone ratio (T/E), which serves as an indicator of anabolic steroid use.
summary: Athletes often use HCG after steroid cycles to reactivate natural testosterone production, potentially stabilizing the T/E ratio and masking steroid use.

content: Because HCG can increase testosterone levels, many of the side effects associated with anabolic steroid use may become evident.
summary: HCG's testosterone-boosting effects can also bring about side effects commonly associated with steroid use.

content: Recombinant technology has increased the availability of HGH, but its cost, especially on the black market, is extremely high (between US$150 and US$170 per dose). Humatrope, Nutropin, Norditropin, Genotropin, Serostim, Saizen, and Protropin are brand names of HGH commonly used in the United States.
summary: Recombinant HGH is more accessible but remains expensive, with various brand names available in the US.

content: There appear to be no studies on the efficacy of HGH in athletic populations. Most investigations of HGH have focused on HGH as replacement therapy in growth hormone–deficient adults or children. Studies have consistently shown positive alterations in body composition (increases in lean body tissue with decreases in body fat) (1, 54, 99, 170, 171, 198).
summary: While HGH research primarily focuses on replacement therapy, studies demonstrate its effectiveness in improving body composition.

content: In men with established growth hormone deficiencies, nightly injections of recombinant HGH for six months resulted in an average increase of 12 pounds (5.4 kg) in lean body mass and a similar amount of fat loss (171). Most studies have not addressed the effect of HGH therapy on muscle strength and performance. One study showed no changes in isokinetic strength following 12 months of therapy (99). The subjects in the study that showed no changes in isokinetic strength did not perform any resistance training during the course of treatment.
summary: HGH therapy increases lean mass and reduces fat, but its impact on muscle strength and performance requires further investigation, particularly with resistance training.

content: Studies have consistently shown positive alterations in body composition (increases in lean body tissue with decreases in body fat) (1, 54, 99, 170, 171, 198). In men with established growth hormone deficiencies, nightly injections of recombinant HGH for six months resulted in an average increase of 12 pounds (5.4 kg) in lean body mass and a similar amount of fat loss (171).
summary: Studies demonstrate that growth hormone therapy can lead to positive body composition changes, including increased lean mass and decreased body fat.

content: Most studies have not addressed the effect of HGH therapy on muscle strength and performance. One study showed no changes in isokinetic strength following 12 months of therapy (99). The subjects in the study that showed no changes in isokinetic strength did not perform any resistance training during the course of treatment.
summary: Research on the impact of HGH on muscle strength and performance is limited, with some studies showing no significant changes.

content: A study involving trained adults given growth hormone (three days per week for six weeks) showed modest changes in body composition, but no strength assessment was performed (54). Anecdotal reports suggest impressive musculoskeletal performance changes in athletes using HGH.
summary: While some studies show modest body composition changes with HGH, anecdotal evidence suggests potential performance benefits for athletes, requiring further research.

content: The scientific literature does not provide support for the efficacy of HGH use in athletic populations. The use of HGH by athletes appears to be increasing, owing to its perceived efficacy and the fact that it cannot be detected in random drug tests.
summary: Despite its perceived benefits, scientific evidence does not support the efficacy of HGH for athletic performance, and its use is increasing due to its undetectability in drug tests.

content: Growth hormone is a protein molecule. Injection is necessary in order to avoid the complete metabolization of growth hormone and maintain its effectiveness. Oral ingestion does not result in any benefit.
summary: Growth hormone is a protein that requires injection for effective delivery, as oral ingestion is ineffective.

content: As a peptide hormone, HGH is not detected in the urine via a drug test, and many athletes may opt to use it for this reason. Many of the actions of growth hormone are mediated through insulin-like growth factor I (IGF-I), another peptide hormone, which is produced and secreted from the liver in response to growth hormone stimulation.
summary: HGH's undetectability in urine drug tests and its mechanism of action through IGF-I contribute to its potential misuse by athletes.

content: Insulin-like growth factor I is now being synthesized using recombinant DNA technology and will likely produce the same effects as HGH.
summary: Recombinant DNA technology allows for the synthesis of IGF-I, which is expected to have similar effects to HGH.

content: Excessive secretion of growth hormone during childhood causes gigantism, a condition in which a person becomes abnormally tall. After puberty, once linear growth has stopped, excess secretion of growth hormone causes acromegaly, a disfiguring disease characterized by a widening of the bones, arthritis, organ enlargement, and metabolic abnormalities.
summary: Excessive growth hormone secretion during childhood leads to gigantism, while after puberty, it causes acromegaly, a debilitating condition.

content: Side effects appear to be minimal for even up to two years of replacement therapy (1, 99, 198). Athletes who supplement with HGH generally use dosages that far exceed the doses commonly administered in replacement treatment.
summary: While HGH replacement therapy has minimal side effects, athletes often use significantly higher doses, potentially increasing health risks.

content: Although growth hormone used as replacement therapy for people with growth hormone or IGF-I deficiency can be effective and can have minimal adverse consequences, the dosages that are likely used by athletes may pose a significant risk for acromegaly.
summary: While HGH replacement therapy is generally safe, the doses used by athletes may increase the risk of acromegaly.

content: Erythropoietin (EPO) is another protein hormone that can be produced by recombinant DNA techniques and is reportedly being abused by athletes (2). Erythropoietin is produced in the kidneys and stimulates the production of new red blood cells. Its level increases in response to aerobic endurance exercise such as marathon running (174).
summary: EPO, a hormone that stimulates red blood cell production, is being misused by athletes for performance enhancement.

content: In certain types of anemia, especially in kidney patients with  Injections of EPO are generally associated with elevations in both hematocrit and hemoglobin.
summary: EPO is used to treat anemia, and injections increase hematocrit and hemoglobin levels.

content: When EPO was given to normal men during six weeks of treatment, hematocrit levels increased by 44.5% to 50%, hemoglobin concentrations increased by 10%, aerobic capacity increased between 6% and 8%, and the time to exhaustion improved by up to 17% (24, 64).
summary: EPO administration in healthy men resulted in significant increases in hematocrit, hemoglobin, aerobic capacity, and time to exhaustion.

content: The enhanced oxygen-carrying capacity of the blood makes EPO an effective ergogenic aid for the aerobic endurance athlete.
summary: EPO enhances oxygen-carrying capacity, making it a performance-enhancing drug for endurance athletes.

content: The mechanism for increasing hemoglobin levels has been reported to be related to the elevated hematocrit and a decrease in plasma volume (132). The change in plasma volume is thought to be mediated by a downregulation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis (132).
summary: EPO's effect on hemoglobin levels is linked to increased hematocrit and decreased plasma volume, potentially mediated by the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone axis.

content: The increase in hematocrit presents a significant health risk. Increases in red blood cell number increase blood viscosity. This poses several problems that include increased risk of blood clotting, elevations in systolic blood pressure, and a compromised thermoregulatory system.
summary: Elevated hematocrit, a result of EPO use, poses health risks such as increased blood clotting, blood pressure, and compromised thermoregulation.

content: During aerobic endurance events, the additional problem of dehydration could compound cardiovascular risks by eliminating any safety margin in the balance between performance advantages from artificially increased hematocrit and decrements from increased blood viscosity.
summary: Dehydration during endurance events exacerbates cardiovascular risks associated with EPO use due to the balance between performance benefits and increased blood viscosity.

content: The deaths of a number of bike racing cyclists have been related to EPO administration (79). The primary risk associated with EPO is its lack of predictability compared to red blood cell infusion.
summary: EPO use has been linked to cyclist deaths, primarily due to its unpredictable nature compared to red blood cell infusions.

content: Once EPO is injected into the body, the stimulus for producing red blood cells is no longer under control. Consequently, aerobic endurance athletes should stay away from this drug because of the significant cardiovascular risk, leading to possible death, associated with its administration.
summary: EPO's uncontrolled stimulation of red blood cell production poses significant cardiovascular risks, making it dangerous for endurance athletes.

content: Synthetic b-adrenergic agonists, or b-agonists, are substances chemically related to epinephrine, a hormone produced in the adrenal medulla that regulates physiological effects, such as lipolysis (the breakdown of fat) and thermogenesis (increased energy expenditure for heat production). b-Agonists were originally developed for the treatment of asthma and other life-threatening medical conditions.
summary: b-Agonists, similar to epinephrine, are used to treat asthma and other medical conditions, but their potential for misuse in athletics exists.

content: The primary risk associated with EPO is its lack of predictability compared to red blood cell infusion. Once EPO is injected into the body, the stimulus for producing red blood cells is no longer under control. Consequently, aerobic endurance athletes should stay away from this drug because of the significant cardiovascular risk, leading to possible death, associated with its administration.
summary: EPO use carries significant cardiovascular risks, making it dangerous for endurance athletes.

content: Synthetic b-adrenergic agonists, or b-agonists, are substances chemically related to epinephrine, a hormone produced in the adrenal medulla that regulates physiological effects, such as lipolysis (the breakdown of fat) and thermogenesis (increased energy expenditure for heat production). b-Agonists were originally developed for the treatment of asthma and other life-threatening medical conditions. Some of these compounds have been found to have specific effects on body composition, such as increases in lean mass and decreases in stored fat; because of this, these drugs are sometimes referred to as partitioning agents (23).
summary: b-Agonists are substances similar to epinephrine that can affect body composition and were originally developed for medical purposes.

content: The implicit proof of the effectiveness of b-agonists is demonstrated in their increased use in livestock. The main drugs used in animals are ractopamine, cimaterol, and clenbuterol. Without question, these drugs work in the species for which they are intended, increasing protein synthesis and muscle mass and decreasing fat mass through enhanced lipolysis and lowered lipogenesis (fat synthesis and storage) (72).
summary: b-Agonsits are effective in livestock, increasing muscle mass and decreasing fat mass.

content: Clenbuterol is a b2-agonist that is generally used to reverse bronchial constriction. In recent years athletes have begun to use it as an ergogenic aid to increase lean muscle tissue and reduce subcutaneous fat (161). This has been based on studies in a rodent model demonstrating that clenbuterol can increase muscle protein synthesis (133, 166). Though studies in humans are limited, several findings have indicated an ergogenic potential of b2-agonists for strength improvements (135, 136).
summary: Clenbuterol, a b2-agonist, is used by athletes to increase muscle mass and reduce fat, though human studies are limited.

content: Athletes generally use clenbuterol in doses twice the recommended amounts administered for clinical purposes, in a cyclic fashion (three weeks on alternated with three weeks off, with a two-days-on, two-days-off cycle during the “on” week) (161). It is believed that this cycling regimen avoids b2-receptor downregulation (57). Athletes consume clenbuterol in a capsule form, in contrast to the inhalation route that is often used for relieving bronchial constriction.
summary: Athletes use clenbuterol in a cyclical fashion to avoid receptor downregulation and consume it orally, unlike its clinical use.

content: Although a number of potential side effects have been suggested (i.e., transient tachycardia, hyperthermia, tremors, dizziness, palpitations, and malaise), actual documented events are quite limited. In addition, the scarcity of data on the ergogenicity of clenbuterol in humans makes determining its efficacy difficult.
summary: While potential side effects exist, documented events are limited, and more research is needed to determine clenbuterol's efficacy in humans.

content: b-Blockers are a class of drugs that block the b-adrenergic receptors, preventing the catecholamines (i.e., norepinephrine and epinephrine) from binding. b-Blockers are generally prescribed by cardiologists for the treatment of a wide variety of cardiovascular diseases, including hypertension. The ergogenic benefit of these drugs may reside in their ability to reduce anxiety and tremors during performance (100). Thus, athletes who rely on steady, controlled movements during performance (i.e., archers or marksmen) would appear to benefit from these drugs.
summary: b-Blockers block b-adrenergic receptors and are used to treat cardiovascular diseases. They may benefit athletes requiring steady movements by reducing anxiety and tremors.

content: Several studies have shown that b-blockers can improve both slow and fast shooting accuracy (9, 125, 191). In shooters who administered b-blockers in two different doses (80 mg vs. 40 mg of oxprenolol), the group taking the higher dose shot with greater accuracy (9). Tesch (191) reported that bowlers whose performance was improved during blockade with oxprenolol had significantly greater heart rates before, during, and after competition than subjects whose performance did not improve while on b-blockers.
summary: Studies show b-blockers can improve shooting accuracy, with higher doses potentially leading to greater improvement.

content: b-Blockers may also have an ergolytic effect (reduce performance). Studies have shown that b-blockers impair the cardiovascular response to exercise by reducing oxygen and substrate delivery to exercising muscles (210).
summary: b-Blockers can also negatively impact performance by reducing cardiovascular response to exercise.

content: Risks associated with these drugs include bronchiospasms in individuals with asthma, light-headedness (due to decreases in blood pressure), increased fatigue, and hypoglycemia in type 2 diabetics (b-blockers can increase insulin secretion).
summary: Potential risks of b-blockers include bronchiospasms, light-headedness, fatigue, and hypoglycemia in diabetics.

content: More research is needed to examine the effect of many ergogenic aids in athletic populations. Athletes should not misconstrue a lack of scientific information as an indication of safety. The sport supplement industry throughout the world has exploded, with more than 600 sport nutrition companies marketing over 4,000 products that produce annual sales of more than US$4 billion in the United States alone (165). To enhance their marketing potential and compete for sales, many of these companies make unsubstantiated claims concerning the efficacy of their products. At times, unscrupulous companies have knowingly laced their products with banned substances to enhance their effects. As a result, much confusion has been created among athletes regarding appropriate supplements and the ethics of companies. It has become critical for the athlete and coach to make educated choices.
summary: More research is needed on ergogenic aids, and athletes should be cautious due to unsubstantiated claims and potential contamination in the supplement industry.

content: Essential amino acids (EAA) are not produced in the body and must be obtained through the diet. Essential amino acids include isoleucine, leucine, valine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, and tryptophan.
summary: Essential amino acids are not produced by the body and must be consumed through diet.

content: At times, unscrupulous companies have knowingly laced their products with banned substances to enhance their effects. As a result, much confusion has been created among athletes regarding appropriate supplements and the ethics of companies. It has become critical for the athlete and coach to make educated choices.
summary: The supplement industry faces ethical challenges due to the use of banned substances, emphasizing the need for informed choices by athletes and coaches.

content: Essential amino acids (EAA) are not produced in the body and must be obtained through the diet. Essential amino acids include isoleucine, leucine, valine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, and tryptophan. High levels of the EAA can be found in any number of animal-based proteins or as supplements sold over-the-counter.
summary: Essential amino acids are crucial dietary components that must be obtained from external sources.

content: Scientists have demonstrated that the consumption of EAA can augment muscle protein synthesis in healthy human subjects (164, 193). Tipton and colleagues (193) examined the effects of EAA in six healthy adults, three men and three women. The subjects participated in resistance exercise (eight sets of eight repetitions at 80% of 1-repetition maximum [1RM]) and then consumed either 40 g EAA or a placebo. Investigators then examined the subjects’ muscle protein synthesis and found that the acute ingestion of EAA was very effective at stimulating muscle protein anabolism.
summary: Research shows that consuming essential amino acids can enhance muscle protein synthesis after resistance exercise.

content: In a follow-up study, using the same protocol (but with only 6 g EAA and 35 g sugar), Rasmussen and colleagues (164) demonstrated significantly greater anabolic drive—that is, the building of new muscle tissue—with the EAA supplement (when given shortly after resistance exercise) versus the placebo. Subsequently, Tipton and colleagues (194) examined the effects of consuming 6 g EAA plus 36 g of sugar, before or after resistance training, on muscle protein metabolism. They reported that when the EAA and sugar was consumed 30 minutes before resistance training, the acute (3 hours postexercise) anabolic response was 158% greater than when the EAA and sugar supplement was consumed post-training.
summary: Studies demonstrate that consuming EAA, particularly before resistance training, significantly boosts muscle protein synthesis.

content: As a result of these acute findings, Tipton and colleagues (194) and Rasmussen and colleagues (164) theorized that a person who consumed EAA before or after (or both before and after) every resistance training session over a period of weeks would experience greater changes in muscle mass than with training only. Some scientists, however, did not agree that the enhanced acute anabolic response of resistance training from EAA supplementation would chronically translate into greater gains in muscle mass, citing the Nadir effect (148).
summary: While acute studies show EAA's positive impact on muscle protein synthesis, some debate exists regarding its long-term effects on muscle mass gain.

content: Pacy and colleagues (148) theorized that the acute anabolic stimulus from dietary protein (EAA) intake, resistance exercise during the day, or both would be matched by a Nadir effect is a negative muscle protein balance equal in magnitude during the night. If the Nadir effect is true, then EAA supplementation before or after training (or both before and after) would be ineffective at enhancing the rate of muscle synthesis over time.
summary: The Nadir effect theory suggests that overnight muscle protein breakdown might negate the benefits of EAA supplementation.

content: Tipton and colleagues (192) examined the 3-hour and 24-hour response to ingesting 6 g EAA before and after a single session of resistance training. The acute augmented anabolic effect of resistance exercise from EAA supplementation was similar to previously published findings (193, 194) and reflected the 24-hour response. Tipton and colleagues (192) concluded that there was no Nadir effect. Tipton and colleagues (192) indicated that supplementing with EAA either before or after exercise (or both before and after) may indeed enhance the training adaptations usually seen with chronic resistance training.
summary: Research refutes the Nadir effect, suggesting that EAA supplementation can enhance muscle protein synthesis over time.

content: Willoughby and colleagues (211) examined the effects of supplementing with 6 g of EAA plus 14 g of milk proteins, 1 hour before and immediately after resistance training, for 10 weeks in young men. Prior to training, the subjects’ body composition, strength, and thigh mass were measured; subjects were then randomly assigned to protein plus EAA or the sugar placebo. The authors reported significant changes in fat mass (-6%), fat-free mass (+8%), thigh mass (+20%), and bench (+34%) and leg (+27%) press strength, which were all greater than the changes seen in the placebo group.
summary: A study found that supplementing with EAA and milk protein before and after resistance training for 10 weeks led to significant improvements in body composition and strength compared to a placebo.

content: Bird and colleagues (29) demonstrated that a 12-week resistance training program combined with 6% liquid carbohydrate plus 6 g EAA ingested between sets of each exercise session resulted in a 30% greater gain in fat-free mass than consumption of 6% carbohydrates. There appears to be evidence supporting the use of EAA consumed before or after (or both before and after) a workout to increase the effects (strength and muscle mass) of resistance training in men.
summary: Research indicates that consuming EAA during resistance training can enhance muscle mass gains compared to carbohydrates alone.

content: b-Hydroxy-b-methylbutyrate (HMB) is a derivative of the EAA leucine and its metabolite a-ketoisocaproate. It is believed that HMB has both anabolic and lipolytic effects.
summary: HMB, derived from leucine, is thought to have both muscle-building and fat-burning properties.

content: Significantly greater improvements in strength and lean body mass have been reported in previously untrained subjects following resistance training programs of four to eight weeks after supplementation than in subjects consuming a placebo (77, 116, 144, 150). No adverse effects on hepatic enzyme function, lipid profile, renal function, or the immune system have been noted following eight weeks of HMB supplementation (78).
summary: Studies show that HMB supplementation can enhance strength and lean mass gains in untrained individuals, with no reported adverse effects.

content: Studies of HMB administration have also demonstrated possible anticatabolic properties of the supplement (172, 196). This has been attributed to reduced enzyme markers of muscle damage and an enhanced recovery during periods of high muscular stress following HMB administration (120). Specifically, these studies have shown significantly lower creatine kinase and lactate dehydrogenase concentrations following a prolonged run (20 km or 12.4 miles) in aerobic endurance–trained athletes (120) or in the initial weeks of a resistance training program (144). The subjects in these studies supplemented with 3 g/day of HMB for periods lasting three to six weeks.
summary: HMB may possess anticatabolic properties, reducing muscle damage markers and aiding recovery after exercise.

content: During supplementation schedules of shorter duration (less than one week), the ability to attenuate the catabolic effects of exercise or enhance strength appears to be limited (177). The ergogenic effects of HMB in trained athletic populations are less clear. Studies using resistance-trained or competitive athletes were unable to duplicate the results seen in a recreationally trained population using similar supplementation schedules (123, 145, 149, 163).
summary: HMB's benefits appear to be more pronounced in untrained individuals, with less clear evidence in trained athletes.

content: A recent study by Hoffman and colleagues (101) examined the effects of a 10-day supplementation period on collegiate American football players during preseason training camp. No significant differences were noted between subjects supplementing with HMB and subjects consuming a placebo in any performance measure or in any marker of stress and fatigue.
summary: A study on collegiate football players found no significant performance benefits from HMB supplementation over a 10-day period.

content: During supplementation schedules of shorter duration (less than one week), the ability to attenuate the catabolic effects of exercise or enhance strength appears to be limited (177). Studies using resistance-trained or competitive athletes were unable to duplicate the results seen in a recreationally trained population using similar supplementation schedules (123, 145, 149, 163). A recent study by Hoffman and colleagues (101) examined the effects of a 10-day supplementation period on collegiate American football players during preseason training camp. No significant differences were noted between subjects supplementing with HMB and subjects consuming a placebo in any performance measure or in any marker of stress and fatigue. Recent studies of HMB supplementation do not support the efficacy of HMB supplementation in resistance-trained athletes. With only limited research available in trained subject populations, additional study appears warranted to further explore the effects of HMB during prolonged periods of supplementation and during various types of physical stress.
summary: Studies on HMB supplementation in trained athletes show limited efficacy, suggesting further research is needed to understand its effects during prolonged supplementation and various training types.

content: During high-intensity anaerobic exercise, a large accumulation of hydrogen ions (H+) is coupled with the use of performance-enhancing substances can reduce the pH within skeletal muscle and adversely affect performance (167). The ability to regulate H+ concentration in skeletal muscle during high-intensity exercise has been termed muscle buffering capacity (MBC) (21, 143). There is a strong positive relationship between MBC and exercise performance (repeated sprint ability, high-intensity exercise capacity, anaerobic threshold, and training volume) (62, 63). Researchers have demonstrated a positive relationship between exercise performance and MBC in athletes who participate in sports like basketball, soccer, hockey, cycling, crew (rowing), triathlons, and sprinting (30, 62, 63).
summary: Muscle buffering capacity (MBC) is crucial for performance during high-intensity exercise, with a strong positive correlation between MBC and performance in various sports.

content: Improving MBC by training or by nutritional means (b-alanine, sodium bicarbonate, and citrate) would improve performance in sports and activities that may be limited by H+ buildup.
summary: Strategies to enhance muscle buffering capacity, such as training and nutritional interventions, can improve performance in activities limited by H+ buildup.

content: b-Alanine is a nonessential amino acid that is common in many foods that we eat, such as chicken. By itself, b-alanine has limited ergogenic properties. When b-alanine enters the muscle cell, it becomes the rate-limiting substrate for carnosine synthesis (59). Harris and colleagues (91) reported that four weeks of supplementing with b-alanine (4 to 6 g/day) resulted in a mean increase of 64% in skeletal muscle b-alanine concentrations.
summary: b-Alanine, found in foods like chicken, becomes a key component in carnosine synthesis when consumed, potentially enhancing muscle buffering capacity.

content: In humans, carnosine is found primarily in fast-twitch skeletal muscle and is estimated to contribute up to 40% of the skeletal MBC of H+ produced during intense anaerobic exercise, thus encouraging a drop in pH (91, 96). Theoretically, increasing skeletal muscle carnosine levels through chronic training or b-alanine supplementation (or both) would improve MBC and most likely improve anaerobic performance. Carnosine concentrations in athletes such as sprinters and bodybuilders appear to be significantly higher than those in marathoners, untrained individuals, and people who are elderly (91, 188).
summary: Carnosine, primarily found in fast-twitch muscle, plays a significant role in buffering H+ during intense exercise. Increasing carnosine levels through training or supplementation may enhance anaerobic performance.

content: Suzuki and colleagues (188) recently examined the relationship between skeletal muscle carnosine levels and high-intensity exercise performance in trained cyclists. The authors reported a significant and positive relationship between carnosine concentration and mean power in a 30-second maximal sprint on a cycle ergometer. This finding supported the theory that skeletal muscle carnosine levels have a positive correlation with anaerobic performance because of the relationship between carnosine and MBC.
summary: Research shows a positive correlation between carnosine levels and high-intensity exercise performance, supporting the role of carnosine in buffering H+ and enhancing anaerobic capacity.

content: Hill and colleagues (96) examined the effect of b-alanine supplementation on muscle carnosine levels and exercise performance in untrained subjects. In a double-blind fashion, 25 male subjects (19-31 years) supplemented with either 4.0 g b-alanine or sugar placebo for the first week, then with up to 6.4 g for an additional nine weeks. Muscle carnosine levels (via muscle biopsy) and total work done (kilojoules) were measured at weeks 0, 4, and 10 during cycling to exhaustion at maximal power established from a graded exercise cycle ergometry test. Mean carnosine levels increased by 58% at week 4 and an additional 15% at week 10. Additionally, 13% and 16% increases in total work done during cycle ergometry were seen at weeks 4 and 10, respectively.
summary: A study on untrained men showed that b-alanine supplementation increased muscle carnosine levels and improved exercise performance, measured by increased total work done during cycling.

content: In another study, Stout and colleagues (183) examined the effects of b-alanine supplementation on physical working capacity at fatigue threshold (PWCFT) in untrained young men. In double-blind fashion, subjects consumed either 1.6 g of b-alanine or sugar placebo four times per day for 6 days, then 3.2 g/day for 22 days. Prior to and following supplementation, the subjects performed an incremental cycle ergometry test to determine PWCFT, which was measured from bipolar surface electromyography recorded from the vastus lateralis muscle. In theory, the PWCFT represents the highest exercise intensity a person can maintain without signs of fatigue and has been highly correlated with anaerobic threshold measurements (lactate and ventilatory thresholds). The results revealed a statistically significant increase in PWCFT (9%) in the b-alanine group compared to no change in the placebo group.
summary: b-Alanine supplementation improved physical working capacity at fatigue threshold (PWCFT) in untrained young men, suggesting a delay in fatigue onset during exercise.

content: In a follow-up study, Stout and colleagues (184) examined the effects of b-alanine supplementation in untrained college-aged women and reported significant increases in PWCFT (12.6%), ventilatory threshold (13.9%), and time to exhaustion (2.5%) during a graded exercise cycle ergometry test. The findings suggest that 28 days of b-alanine supplementation may increase the level of intensity that men and women can train or compete at without signs of rapid fatigue. Essentially, performance and quality of training would be augmented as the onset of fatigue threshold is elevated. b-Alanine supplementation (3.2 to 6.4 g/day) appears to elevate muscle carnosine levels, which may improve exercise performance in untrained men and women by delaying fatigue as a consequence of an improved MBC.
summary: b-Alanine supplementation improved PWCFT, ventilatory threshold, and time to exhaustion in untrained college-aged women, highlighting its potential to enhance training intensity and delay fatigue onset.

content: Sodium bicarbonate (SB) is an antacid (alkalinizing agent) that counteracts or neutralizes acid (low pH). Sodium bicarbonate is naturally formed in the body and is also found in baking soda.
summary: Sodium bicarbonate is a naturally occurring antacid that can neutralize acid buildup in the body.

content: The findings suggest that 28 days of b-alanine supplementation may increase the level of intensity that men and women can train or compete at without signs of rapid fatigue. Essentially, performance and quality of training would be augmented as the onset of fatigue threshold is elevated. b-Alanine supplementation (3.2 to 6.4 g/day) appears to elevate muscle carnosine levels, which may improve exercise performance in untrained men and women by delaying fatigue as a consequence of an improved MBC.
summary: b-Alanine supplementation may improve exercise performance by delaying fatigue and elevating the training intensity threshold.

content: Sodium bicarbonate (SB) is an antacid (alkalinizing agent) that counteracts or neutralizes acid (low pH). Sodium bicarbonate is naturally formed in the body and is also found in baking soda. Supplementing with SB has been shown to increase the pH of blood. A pH difference is created between the inside and outside of muscle cells that causes an accelerated movement of H+ out of the contracting muscle, helping to regulate intramuscular pH. Supplementing with SB has been shown to improve muscle buffering capacity (MBC) and in turn high-intensity exercise performance.
summary: Sodium bicarbonate (SB) supplementation increases blood pH, improves muscle buffering capacity, and enhances high-intensity exercise performance.

content: McNaughton and colleagues (138, 139) and Coombes and McNaughton (49) have demonstrated improved total work capacity, peak power, peak torque, and strength from acute SB supplementation in men and women. Edge and colleagues (62) examined the effects of eight weeks of SB ingestion during high-intensity interval training on cycling performance parameters. The results of Edge and colleagues (62) exhibited a significant increase in the effects of high-intensity interval training on anaerobic threshold, time to fatigue, and peak power with SB versus placebo. Edge and colleagues (62) suggested that increasing the ability to regulate H+, thus maintaining intracellular pH, may be vital to enhancing the benefits of intense training.
summary: Studies demonstrate that acute and chronic sodium bicarbonate supplementation improves various performance parameters during high-intensity exercise.

content: A dosage of 136 to 227 mg/pound of body weight (27-45 g for a 200-pound [91 kg] person) improves short-duration, high-intensity exercise performance and training. Dosages at 136 to 227 mg/pound of body weight have been associated with unpleasant side effects such as diarrhea, cramping, nausea, and vomiting. A more tolerable dose (90 mg/pound of body weight) has been shown to reduce these side effects; however, research has revealed that this dose does not improve exercise performance. It appears that a minimum SB dose of 136 mg/pound of body weight, 90 minutes prior to exercise, is needed to improve performance. Due to the severity of the side effects, many sport scientists recommend that athletes try SB supplementation during practice before using it as a precompetition aid.
summary: Sodium bicarbonate supplementation improves short-duration, high-intensity exercise performance, but optimal dosage and timing are crucial due to potential side effects.

content: Although sodium citrate (SC) is not actually a base, it can increase blood pH without the gastrointestinal distress that is commonly seen with SB supplementation. It is believed that once in the blood, SC actually breaks down into bicarbonate, thus increasing the extracellular pH. As a result, SC would help regulate intramuscular pH during high-intensity exercise by the same mechanism as SB. Most studies have demonstrated that SC, unlike SB, has no ergogenic effects during short-duration, high-intensity exercise. Improved performance in maximal exercise lasting between 2 and 15 minutes has been suggested. A dose of 200 mg/pound of body weight given 60 to 90 minutes before exercise also led to significant improvement (about 20% greater) in leg muscular endurance during maximal isometric knee extensions. More research, however, is needed before SC can be recommended before a workout or competition for performance enhancement.
summary: Sodium citrate (SC) may increase blood pH without gastrointestinal distress, but its ergogenic effects during exercise are less clear compared to sodium bicarbonate.

content: L-carnitine is synthesized from the amino acids lysine and methionine and is responsible for the transport of fatty acids from the cytosol into the mitochondria to be oxidized for energy. Carnitine’s role in lipid oxidation has generated interest in its efficacy as a dietary supplement, primarily to enhance exercise performance by increasing fat utilization and sparing muscle glycogen. Studies examining L-carnitine’s role as an ergogenic aid for increasing lipid oxidation have not shown clear efficacy in either human or rat models. Although Bacurau and colleagues (12) have shown enhanced fatty acid oxidation following three weeks of L-carnitine supplementation, which was attributed to a greater carnitine content in the muscle, most studies have been unable to demonstrate elevated muscle carnitine levels following supplementation. This may be related to limits in the amount of carnitine that can be absorbed through oral supplementation, or potentially related to limits in the amount of fat that can be transported into the mitochondria through the carnitine system due to feedback regulators within the muscle, such as malonyl CoA, which is a product of metabolism.
summary: L-carnitine's role in fat metabolism has led to its use as a dietary supplement, but research on its ergogenic effects is inconclusive.

content: Several studies have suggested that L-carnitine may enhance recovery from exercise. Decreases in pain and muscle damage have been demonstrated following high-intensity resistance exercise in untrained or recreationally trained individuals who supplemented with L-carnitine. Decreases in markers of metabolic stress have been demonstrated following high-intensity resistance exercise in untrained or recreationally trained individuals who supplemented with L-carnitine. Enhanced recovery has been demonstrated following high-intensity resistance exercise in untrained or recreationally trained individuals who supplemented with L-carnitine.
summary: L-carnitine supplementation may improve recovery from exercise by reducing pain, muscle damage, and metabolic stress.

content: The mechanisms that have been proposed for L-carnitine's effects involve enhancing blood flow regulation through an enhanced vasodilatory effect that reduces the magnitude of exercise-induced hypoxia. L-carnitine supplementation (2 g/day for three weeks) upregulates androgen receptors. L-carnitine supplementation (2 g/day for three weeks) increases IGF-binding proteins that preserve IGF-I concentrations. Up to 3 g of daily L-carnitine supplementation (for three weeks) appears to be well tolerated in healthy volunteers, with no adverse subjective, hematological, or metabolic events reported.
summary: L-carnitine's potential mechanisms for improving exercise performance and recovery include enhanced blood flow, androgen receptor upregulation, and IGF-binding protein increases.

content: Creatine is a nitrogenous organic compound that is synthesized naturally in the body, primarily in the liver. Creatine can also be synthesized in smaller amounts in both the kidneys and pancreas. The amino acids arginine, glycine, and methionine are the precursors for the synthesis of creatine in the liver, kidneys, and pancreas.
summary: Creatine is a naturally occurring compound synthesized in the body, primarily from the amino acids arginine, glycine, and methionine.

content: Creatine is a nitrogenous organic compound that is synthesized naturally in the body, primarily in the liver. Creatine can also be synthesized in smaller amounts in both the kidneys and pancreas. The amino acids arginine, glycine, and methionine are the precursors for the synthesis of creatine in the liver, kidneys, and pancreas. Creatine can also be obtained through dietary sources. Creatine is found in abundance in both meat and fish.
summary: Creatine is naturally synthesized in the body and can also be obtained through diet, primarily from meat and fish.

content: Approximately 98% of creatine is stored within skeletal muscle in either its free form (40%) or its phosphorylated form (60%). Smaller amounts of creatine are also stored in the heart, brain, and testes. Creatine is transported from its site of synthesis to the skeletal muscle via circulation.
summary: Creatine is primarily stored in skeletal muscle, with smaller amounts found in other tissues. It is transported through the bloodstream.

content: Creatine, in the form of creatine phosphate (CP; also called phosphocreatine [PCr]), has an essential role in energy metabolism as a substrate for the formation of adenosine triphosphate by rephosphorylating adenosine diphosphate (ADP), especially during short-duration, high-intensity exercise. The ability to rapidly rephosphorylate ADP is dependent upon the enzyme creatine kinase and the availability of CP within the muscle.
summary: Creatine phosphate plays a crucial role in energy production during high-intensity exercise by replenishing ATP.

content: As CP stores become depleted, the ability to perform high-intensity exercise declines. In short-duration sprints (e.g., 100 m sprint), the energy for fueling the activity is derived primarily through the hydrolysis of CP. As high-intensity exercise increases in duration, the ability of CP to serve as an energy source is drastically reduced.
summary: Creatine phosphate stores are depleted during high-intensity exercise, limiting performance duration.

content: During a 6-second bout of maximal exercise, CP levels within the muscle are reduced 35% to 57% from resting levels. As the duration of high-intensity exercise increases to 30 seconds, CP levels in the muscle are further reduced by approximately 64% to 80% from resting levels. During bouts of repeated high-intensity exercise, CP levels in the muscle are almost completely depleted.
summary: Creatine phosphate levels decrease significantly during high-intensity exercise, with depletion becoming more pronounced as duration increases.

content: As muscle CP concentrations decrease, the ability to perform maximal exercise is reduced. Sprint times were slower as CP concentrations were reduced.
summary: Lower creatine phosphate levels directly correlate with reduced exercise performance.

content: Creatine supplementation in athletes is based on the idea that maintaining muscle CP concentrations would improve the ability to sustain high-intensity exercise.
summary: Creatine supplementation aims to enhance muscle creatine phosphate stores, improving high-intensity exercise capacity.

content: Reports suggest that 48% of male collegiate athletes use, or have used, creatine during their preparation for competition. The prevalence of creatine use among strength/power athletes may approach more than 80% in certain sports. Creatine use has also gained popularity among high school athletes; 90% of athletes who supplement choose creatine.
summary: Creatine supplementation is widely used by athletes across various levels and sports.

content: Creatine supplementation is reported to increase the creatine content of muscles by approximately 20%. A saturation limit for creatine exists within muscle. Once creatine concentrations in skeletal muscle reach 150 to 160 mmol/kg dry weight, additional supplementation appears to be unable to further increase muscle creatine concentrations.
summary: Creatine supplementation increases muscle creatine content up to a saturation point, after which further supplementation is ineffective.

content: A typical creatine supplementation regimen involves a loading dose of 20 to 25 g daily for five days, or 0.3 g/kg body mass if an individual wishes to dose relative to body weight, followed by a maintenance dose of 2 g/day (111). If one ingests creatine without an initial loading dose, muscle creatine content will reach levels similar to those seen in people who do initially use a loading dose, but reaching that muscle creatine concentration will take longer (~30 days vs. 5 days). Muscle creatine levels will remain elevated as long as the maintenance dose is maintained (2 g/day or 0.03 g/kg body mass per day) (111). Once creatine supplementation is stopped, muscle creatine levels will return to baseline levels in approximately four weeks (67, 111).
summary: Creatine supplementation typically involves a loading phase followed by a maintenance phase, with muscle creatine levels returning to baseline after supplementation ceases.

content: Most studies examining the effect of creatine supplementation on strength performance have been fairly consistent in showing significant ergogenic benefits (22, 35, 61, 88, 105, 124, 128, 151, 202). Strength increases in the bench press, squat, and power clean may be two- to threefold higher in trained athletes supplementing with creatine compared to placebo (105, 151).
summary: Creatine supplementation consistently improves strength performance in trained athletes.

content: Creatine supplementation has been shown to increase strength and improve training by reducing fatigue and enhancing postworkout recovery.
summary: Creatine supplementation enhances training by reducing fatigue and improving recovery.

content: Most studies looking at the effect of creatine supplementation on a single bout of explosive exercise (sprint or jump performance) have not shown any significant performance improvements (48, 55, 141, 146, 178). When subjects have supplemented for an extended period of time (28-84 days), significant improvements in jump and power performance have been seen (88, 123, 202).
summary: Short-term creatine supplementation does not consistently improve explosive performance, but long-term supplementation can lead to improvements.

content: Creatine supplementation may improve the quality of each training session, which will result in better performance.
summary: Consistent creatine supplementation can lead to better overall training quality and performance.

content: Most studies demonstrating the efficacy of short-term (five to six days) creatine supplementation have used a loading dose (20 g/day) as the supplement regime. There appear to be only two investigations that have shown ergogenic benefits of low-dose creatine supplementation. One study used a dosing scheme of 7.7 g/day (0.1 g/kg) during 28 days of supplementation.
summary: Short-term creatine supplementation typically involves a loading dose, while low-dose regimens have shown limited efficacy.

content: L-carnitine supplementation (2 g/day for three weeks) increases IGF-binding proteins that preserve IGF-I concentrations. Up to 3 g of daily L-carnitine supplementation (for three weeks) appears to be well tolerated in healthy volunteers, with no adverse subjective, hematological, or metabolic events reported.
summary: L-carnitine supplementation appears safe and may increase IGF-binding proteins, preserving IGF-I levels.

content: When subjects have supplemented for an extended period of time (28-84 days), significant improvements in jump and power performance have been seen (88, 123, 202). Creatine supplementation may improve the quality of each training session, which will result in better performance. Most studies demonstrating the efficacy of short-term (five to six days) creatine supplementation have used a loading dose (20 g/day) as the supplement regime. There appear to be only two investigations that have shown ergogenic benefits of low-dose creatine supplementation. One study used a dosing scheme of 7.7 g/day (0.1 g/kg) during 28 days of supplementation. This was the first study to show efficacy with a low-dose and relatively short-duration supplementation regimen, although the supplementation period was much longer than is typically seen during short-term studies. Hoffman and colleagues (106) demonstrated that even a low-dose (6 g/day), short-duration (six days) supplementation regimen in active college-aged men may reduce fatigue.
summary: Creatine supplementation, both short-term and long-term, can improve jump and power performance, potentially through enhanced training quality and reduced fatigue.

content: Prolonged creatine supplementation has been generally associated with increases in body weight. These increases appear to primarily relate to increases in fat-free mass. The increase in body mass is believed to be partly related to an increase in total body water. An increase in creatine content within the muscle is thought to enhance the intracellular osmotic gradient, causing water to fill the cell. In addition, increasing the creatine content of muscle appears to be coupled with an increased rate of muscle contractile protein synthesis. Increase in body mass has at times been referred to as an unwanted side effect. However, in athletes supplementing with creatine, weight gain is often a desired outcome.
summary: Creatine supplementation can lead to weight gain, primarily due to increased muscle mass and water retention, which can be beneficial for athletes.

content: There have been a host of anecdotal reports of gastrointestinal, cardiovascular, and muscular problems, including muscle cramps, in association with creatine ingestion. Controlled studies have been unable to document any significant side effects from creatine supplementation. Even during prolonged supplementation (10-12 weeks), no increases in reported side effects were noted in subjects supplementing with creatine—either competitive athletes or recreationally trained individuals. Another concern with any supplement is the long-term health effects. A retrospective study on health variables in 26 former or current competitive athletes who had used creatine for up to four years indicated only occasional gastrointestinal upset during the loading phase. These disturbances ranged from “gas” to mild diarrhea.
summary: Despite anecdotal reports, controlled studies show creatine supplementation to be safe, with minimal side effects even during long-term use.

content: Other major concerns during creatine supplementation include a strain on the kidneys due to the high nitrogen content of creatine and increased creatine excretion during short-term ingestion. No renal dysfunction has been reported in either short-term (five days) or long-term (up to five years) creatine use.
summary: Concerns about creatine's impact on kidney function have not been substantiated by research, with no reported cases of renal dysfunction.

content: There is little question about the usefulness of stimulants to many types of athletic endeavors; the effects of stimulants include reduced fatigue, increased alertness, increased confidence, and even euphoria. Because stimulants have to be used at the time of competition to confer an ergogenic advantage, drug testing performed immediately after the competition can effectively reduce their abuse. Many stimulants (e.g., caffeine) are also available in food items, while others (e.g., ephedrine) may be found in decongestants and other medications.
summary: Stimulants are effective ergogenic aids, but their use is limited by timing restrictions and potential for abuse.

content: Caffeine is one of the most widely used drugs in the world. It is found in coffee, tea, soft drinks, chocolate, and various other foods. It is a central nervous system stimulant and its effects are similar to, yet weaker than, those associated with amphetamines. Caffeine has been used as a performance enhancer for more than 30 years and is one of the few ergogenic aids that may be used to an equal extent by aerobic and anaerobic athletes.
summary: Caffeine is a widely consumed stimulant with a long history as a performance enhancer, beneficial for both aerobic and anaerobic athletes.

content: For the aerobic athlete, caffeine is thought to prolong aerobic endurance exercise. The mechanisms that have been proposed to cause this effect involve an increase in fat oxidation through the mobilization of free fatty acids from adipose tissue or intramuscular fat stores. The greater use of fat as a primary energy source slows glycogen depletion and delays fatigue.
summary: Caffeine may enhance aerobic endurance by increasing fat oxidation and delaying glycogen depletion.

content: During short-duration, high-intensity exercise, the primary ergogenic effect attributed to caffeine is an enhancement of power production. Caffeine supplementation may enhance power production. Caffeine is thought to enhance excitation-contraction coupling, affecting neuromuscular transmission and mobilization of intracellular calcium ions from the sarcoplasmic reticulum. Caffeine ingestion is thought to enhance the kinetics of glycolytic regulatory enzymes such as phosphorylase.
summary: Caffeine improves power output during high-intensity exercise by enhancing excitation-contraction coupling and glycolytic enzyme activity.

content: Caffeine is an ergogenic aid that may enhance performance in both aerobic and anaerobic athletes. Initial studies indicated a 21-minute improvement in the time to exhaustion (from 75 minutes in a placebo trial to 96 minutes in the caffeine trial) during cycling at 80% of V.O2max. A number of additional studies demonstrated the ergogenic effect of caffeine during prolonged aerobic endurance activity. Caffeine in doses ranging from 3 to 9 mg/kg produced a significant ergogenic effect.
summary: Caffeine has been shown to improve performance in both aerobic and anaerobic activities, with doses of 3-9 mg/kg producing significant ergogenic effects.

content: Caffeine ingestion is thought to enhance the kinetics of glycolytic regulatory enzymes such as phosphorylase. Caffeine is an ergogenic aid that may enhance performance in both aerobic and anaerobic athletes. Initial studies indicated a 21-minute improvement in the time to exhaustion (from 75 minutes in a placebo trial to 96 minutes in the caffeine trial) during cycling at 80% of V.O2max. A number of additional studies demonstrated the ergogenic effect of caffeine during prolonged aerobic endurance activity. Caffeine in doses ranging from 3 to 9 mg/kg produced a significant ergogenic effect. Similar performance effects from caffeine have also been demonstrated during short-duration (approximately 5 minutes), high-intensity exercise.
summary: Caffeine enhances performance in both aerobic and anaerobic exercise by improving glycolytic enzyme kinetics and increasing time to exhaustion.

content: Caffeine ingestion (250 mg) was shown to improve sprint times during repeated 100 m sprints by an average of 3%. An additional study demonstrated a 7% increase in power performance during a series of 6-second sprints. The results of the studies on the ergogenic benefit of caffeine for power performance are inconclusive. If there is any performance benefit, it likely occurs in the trained athlete.
summary: Caffeine may improve sprint and power performance, but the evidence is mixed and may be more pronounced in trained athletes.

content: The ergogenic benefit with respect to improved aerobic endurance performance has been reported to range between 28% and 43%. The ergogenic benefit for aerobic endurance exercise may not be seen or may be seen only at a reduced rate when caffeine is provided in a food source such as coffee. Graham and colleagues suggested that although the bioavailability of caffeine is the same whether it is consumed in a food source or in an anhydrous form, some compound in coffee antagonizes the action of caffeine. A coffee product containing 450 mg of caffeine, 1,200 mg of garcinia cambogia (50% hydroxycitric acid), 360 mg of citrus aurantium extract (6%), and 225 mg of chromium polynicotinate can improve aerobic endurance performance 29% in aerobic endurance–trained subjects.
summary: Caffeine significantly improves aerobic endurance performance, but the effect may be reduced when consumed in coffee due to antagonistic compounds.

content: Caffeine from a food source and anhydrous caffeine have both shown significant performance effects. The extent of performance improvements appears to be greater when caffeine is ingested in tablet form.
summary: Both food-source and anhydrous caffeine improve performance, with tablet form potentially yielding greater benefits.

content: The side effects associated with caffeine include anxiety, gastrointestinal disturbances, restlessness, insomnia, tremors, and heart arrhythmias. Caffeine can act as a diuretic and may increase the risk for heat illness in high-temperature climates. Caffeine is also physically addicting, and discontinuation can result in some withdrawal symptoms. Caffeine intakes greater than 9 mg/kg appear to result in a greater risk for side effects.
summary: Caffeine can cause side effects such as anxiety, gastrointestinal issues, insomnia, and heart problems, and high doses increase the risk of adverse reactions.

content: Another b-agonist that is reported to be relatively popular, especially among bodybuilders, is ephedrine. Ephedrine is thought to have a strong thermogenic quality that bodybuilders desire in order to reduce body fat. Ephedrine also increases fat oxidation and spares muscle glycogen.
summary: Ephedrine is a popular supplement among bodybuilders due to its thermogenic effects and ability to promote fat oxidation.
