content: Studies examining the ergogenic effect of ephedrine have shown it to be effective only when it is taken in combination with caffeine. These results have been quite consistent with respect to improving aerobic endurance performance. Subjects ingested a caffeine-ephedrine mixture of 5 mg/kg caffeine and 1 mg/kg ephedrine (18). A subsequent study by the same investigators showed that at a lower dose (4 mg/kg caffeine and 0.8 mg/kg ephedrine), similar ergogenic benefits are realized with no side effects (19). The caffeine-ephedrine mixture appears to have a greater benefit than either supplement taken alone.
summary: Ephedrine is most effective when combined with caffeine, improving aerobic endurance performance.

content: Ephedra, also called ma huang, is a plant or herb that contains ephedrine. In the United States before 2004, most over-the-counter sport and weight loss products contained ephedra. In April 2004, the FDA banned all products containing ephedra after determining that it posed an unreasonable risk to those who used it. The FDA ban was based on a report published by the Rand Institute (175) indicating that 16,000 adverse events were linked to the use of ephedra-containing dietary supplements.
summary: Ephedra, containing ephedrine, was banned in the US due to its association with adverse health effects.

content: Shekelle and colleagues (175) reported that the use of ephedra-containing dietary supplements or ephedrine plus caffeine was associated with an increased incidence of nausea, vomiting, psychiatric symptoms such as anxiety and change in mood, autonomic hyperactivity, palpitations, and in a few cases death. As a result of the many adverse effects, ephedrine use has been banned by most sport governing bodies including the International Olympic Committee.
summary: Ephedrine use is associated with various adverse effects, leading to its ban in many sports and dietary supplements.

content: Citrus aurantium is from a fruit commonly known as ‘bitter orange’ and is often used as an Asian herbal medicine to treat digestive problems (74). Citrus aurantium is also a mild stimulant and is thought to contribute to appetite suppression and increased metabolic rate and lipolysis (74).
summary: Citrus aurantium, derived from bitter orange, is used as a traditional medicine and is believed to aid digestion and suppress appetite.

content: Citrus aurantium contains synephrine, a sympathomimetic agent, which some have suggested stimulates specific adrenergic receptors (b-3, but not b-1, b-2, or a-1) that in turn stimulate fat metabolism without any of the negative side effects generally associated with compounds that stimulate the other adrenergic receptors (42). Synephrine, a known active component of citrus aurantium, is thought to interact with b-3 receptors to increase lipolysis and minimize the cardiovascular effect typical of adrenergic amines (42). Synephrine has been shown to stimulate peripheral a-1 receptors, resulting in vasoconstriction and elevations in blood pressure (36).
summary: Citrus aurantium contains synephrine, which may stimulate fat metabolism and potentially have fewer cardiovascular side effects compared to other adrenergic stimulants.

content: Citrus aurantium is from a fruit commonly known as ‘bitter orange’ and is often used as an Asian herbal medicine to treat digestive problems (74). Citrus aurantium is also a mild stimulant and is thought to contribute to appetite suppression and increased metabolic rate and lipolysis (74).
summary: Citrus aurantium, a bitter orange fruit, is used medicinally and as a stimulant for appetite suppression and metabolism.

content: Citrus aurantium contains synephrine, a sympathomimetic agent, which some have suggested stimulates specific adrenergic receptors (b-3, but not b-1, b-2, or a-1) that in turn stimulate fat metabolism without any of the negative side effects generally associated with compounds that stimulate the other adrenergic receptors (42). Synephrine, a known active component of citrus aurantium, is thought to interact with b-3 receptors to increase lipolysis and minimize the cardiovascular effect typical of adrenergic amines (42).
summary: Citrus aurantium contains synephrine, which may stimulate fat metabolism through b-3 receptors without typical cardiovascular side effects.

content: Synephrine has been shown to stimulate peripheral a-1 receptors, resulting in vasoconstriction and elevations in blood pressure (36). Other research has shown that when citrus aurantium is ingested alone, no effect on blood pressure is seen (89), but in combination with other herbal products it may cause significant elevations in systolic blood pressure (89, 103). When citrus aurantium is combined with caffeine and other herbal products, significant improvements in time to fatigue have been reported (103).
summary: Synephrine can increase blood pressure, but its effects vary depending on combination with other substances. Citrus aurantium combined with caffeine and herbs may improve fatigue resistance.

content: Synephrine has been on the National Collegiate Athletic Association’s banned list of performance-enhancing drugs but not those of the World Anti-Doping Association or the U.S. Anti-Doping Association. Synephrine is presently being monitored by these two organizations for potential placement on the banned list of performance enhancement substances.
summary: Synephrine's status as a banned performance-enhancing drug is currently under review by international organizations.

content: Proper nutrition is an important consideration for athletes who seek to maximize their performance. No diet directly increases strength, power, or aerobic endurance, but an adequate diet allows athletes to train and compete to the best of their ability.
summary: Proper nutrition is essential for athletes to train and compete effectively, although no diet directly increases performance.

content: Athletes and professionals who work with athletes are often confused about nutritional guidelines for athletes. Athletes’ diets should follow basic nutritional principles for health and disease prevention. The physiological needs of competitive athletes often require diets that are quite different from a sedentary individual’s diet.
summary: Athletes require specific nutritional guidance, as their dietary needs differ from those of sedentary individuals.

content: The ideal diet for an athlete depends on many factors: age, body size, sex, genetics, and environmental training conditions, as well as on duration, frequency, and intensity of training. Requirements thus vary greatly between and within athletic groups. The effective strength and conditioning professional does not fall into a “one-size-fits-all” mentality when working with athletes. The best diet for an athlete is one that is individualized.
summary: An athlete's ideal diet is individualized based on various factors like age, genetics, training, and goals.

content: After completing this chapter, you will be able to identify the protein, carbohydrate, and fat recommendations for athletes. You will be able to discern between dietary recommendations for disease prevention and recommendations for performance. You will be able to identify and apply appropriate hydration practices for athletes. You will be able to apply precompetition and posttraining eating strategies and advise athletes on guidelines for weight gain and weight loss. You will be able to recognize signs and symptoms of eating disorders.
summary: This chapter will teach you about specific nutritional recommendations for athletes, including hydration, pre/post-training nutrition, and recognizing eating disorders.

content: You will be able to understand the importance of having an intervention and referral system in place for athletes suspected of having an eating disorder. You will be able to recognize the prevalence and etiologies of obesity. You will be able to assist in the assessment process for obese individuals. Although eating disorders are complex, the strength and conditioning professional should recognize signs of eating disorders, provide information on what preventive measures can be put into place, and explain how to deal with the situation when it arises.
summary: This chapter emphasizes the importance of recognizing and addressing eating disorders and obesity in athletes.

content: Obesity remains a prevalent problem. Strength and conditioning professionals need to understand the assessment and treatment of this complex and resistant condition. When athletes approach the strength and conditioning professional with complex nutritional issues, it is important to refer them to the proper resources, such as the team physician, a sport nutritionist, or a registered dietitian familiar with sport.
summary: Strength and conditioning professionals should be aware of obesity and refer athletes with complex nutritional issues to appropriate specialists.

content: The team physician is responsible for decisions related to the athlete’s care and medical needs. The nutritionist is responsible for the athlete’s dietary needs and works closely with the physician and strength and conditioning professional to communicate the proper messages to the athlete. Some of the responsibilities of the nutritionist are the following: Personalized nutritional counseling: weight loss and weight gain, strategies to improve performance, menu planning, dietary supplements.
summary: The team physician, nutritionist, and strength and conditioning professional collaborate to provide comprehensive care for athletes.

content: Designing a nutritional program for an athlete is challenging because no two athletes are alike. Athletes have diverse nutritional goals: to increase energy, to build muscle, to lose fat, to heal injuries, and to speed recovery between training and competition. The first step in nutritional counseling is to define the athlete’s goals. Most competitive athletes are interested in developing personal nutritional strategies to improve performance. Weight loss, weight gain, eating while traveling, nutritional supplements, precompetition eating, and improving performance are among the reasons athletes often seek the advice of a nutritionist.
summary: Nutritional counseling for athletes is individualized based on their unique goals and needs.

content: Counseling may be as simple as helping the athlete overcome barriers to eating breakfast or as complicated as acquiring a complete nutritional assessment, including analysis of food records, anthropometric measurements, and laboratory tests. It is imperative for the strength and conditioning professional to work closely with other sports medicine personnel who are helping the athlete so that consistent messages are communicated.
summary: Nutritional counseling for athletes can range from simple advice to comprehensive assessments, and clear communication between all involved professionals is crucial.

content: The first step in nutritional counseling is to define the athlete’s goals. Most competitive athletes are interested in developing personal nutritional strategies to improve performance. Weight loss, weight gain, eating while traveling, nutritional supplements, precompetition eating, and improving performance are among the reasons athletes often seek the advice of a nutritionist. Counseling may be as simple as helping the athlete overcome barriers to eating breakfast or as complicated as acquiring a complete nutritional assessment, including analysis of food records, anthropometric measurements, and laboratory tests.
summary: Nutritional counseling begins with defining the athlete's goals, which often involve performance enhancement, weight management, or addressing specific dietary concerns.

content: It is imperative for the strength and conditioning professional to work closely with other sports medicine personnel who are helping the athlete so that consistent messages are communicated. When working with athletes from various backgrounds and cultures, it is important to remember that many different eating styles can equate to an adequate diet; there is no one “right” diet for all athletes. Whether from a vegan diet, a typical Western diet, or any other diet, the human body needs adequate amounts of protein, carbohydrate, fat, vitamins, minerals, and water.
summary: Effective nutritional guidance requires collaboration with other healthcare professionals and acknowledges the diversity of dietary practices while emphasizing the fundamental nutritional needs of the body.

content: Most athletes have two basic dietary goals: eating to maximize performance and eating for optimal body composition. Some athletes also want diets that promote good health, that is, prevent chronic diseases such as heart disease and cancer.
summary: Athletes typically aim to optimize both performance and body composition through their diet, with some also prioritizing disease prevention.

content: Whether the diet is designed to enhance performance, prevent disease, or both, two fundamental components of the diet must be present: (1) appropriate calorie level and (2) appropriate nutrient levels to prevent nutrient deficiency or toxicity.
summary: Effective dietary plans for athletes must consider both adequate calorie intake and balanced nutrient levels to support health and performance.

content: One tool designed to provide guidance for evaluating nutrient adequacy of the diet is the Food Guide Pyramid, developed by the U.S. Department of Agriculture in 1992 and updated to MyPyramid in 2005. MyPyramid (www.MyPyramid.gov) has all the food groups from the original Food Guide Pyramid, but it also includes a graphic depiction of physical activity and is based on the latest nutritional science.
summary: The Food Guide Pyramid and its updated version, MyPyramid, provide a framework for evaluating dietary adequacy and promoting healthy eating habits.

content: After the original Food Guide Pyramid was developed in 1992, new standards for nutrition, the Dietary Reference Intakes (DRIs), were created by the National Academy of Sciences Institute of Medicine. The 2005 Dietary Guidelines for Americans (from the U.S. Department of Agriculture and the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services) were released.
summary: The development of the Dietary Reference Intakes (DRIs) and the 2005 Dietary Guidelines for Americans provided updated nutritional recommendations.

content: MyPyramid displays recommended types and amounts of food to eat daily. The color bands represent five food groups that are needed each day for health: Grains, Vegetables, Fruits, Milk, and Meat and beans. The narrow yellow band in the pyramid represents oils. Although they provide essential fatty acids and vitamin E, they are not a food group. When added to a balanced, varied diet, oils can serve as sources of calories for athletes with high caloric needs. For athletes with lower caloric requirements, these foods should be eaten in moderation but not completely omitted from the diet.
summary: MyPyramid visually illustrates recommended daily food intake across five major food groups, emphasizing the importance of balance and moderation.

content: MyPyramid is an excellent starting point from which to evaluate the adequacy of an athlete’s diet. If a diet provides a variety of foods from each group, it is likely adequate for vitamins and minerals. However, if the diet excludes an entire food group, specific nutrients may be lacking. The typical portion size is much larger than the standard “serving” size that the Food Guide Pyramid was based on. MyPyramid provides recommendations (with examples) in cups or ounces for the entire day that can be divided into several meals or snacks.
summary: MyPyramid serves as a guide for assessing dietary adequacy, highlighting the importance of consuming a variety of foods from each group and considering appropriate portion sizes.

content: Foods within each group share similar nutrient compositions and are considered interchangeable. The combination of an orange, an apple, and a pear provides more essential nutrients than three apples. The grains group provides carbohydrates (sugar and starch), as do fruits and vegetables. These foods are also the primary sources of dietary fiber, riboflavin, thiamin, niacin, folate, vitamin C, and beta carotene.
summary: Foods within each MyPyramid food group offer similar nutritional benefits, allowing for flexibility and variety in meal planning.

content: Meat, poultry, fish, dry beans, eggs, and nuts are major contributors of protein, iron, zinc, and vitamin B12 in the diet. Dairy products are excellent sources of dietary protein, calcium, and riboflavin. If a diet provides a variety of foods from each group, it is likely adequate for vitamins and minerals. Exclusion of meat from the diet increases the risk of inadequate protein, iron, zinc, Exclusion of milk and dairy products increases the risk of inadequate calcium and riboflavin intake. Exclusion of all animal foods means that the diet lacks vitamin B12. Exclusion of fruits and vegetables increases the risk of inadequate vitamin C and beta carotene intake. Exclusion of grains increases the risk of inadequate riboflavin, thiamin, and niacin.
summary: Each food group plays a crucial role in providing essential nutrients, and excluding entire groups can lead to deficiencies.

content: Each of the food groups provides essential nutrients. If one food group is omitted, key nutrients may be missing in the diet. The frequently seen version of MyPyramid is designed for the general population.
summary: A balanced diet should include foods from all food groups to ensure adequate nutrient intake. While MyPyramid is a general guideline, athletes may require adjustments based on their specific needs.

content: Exclusion of fruits and vegetables increases the risk of inadequate vitamin C and beta carotene intake. Each of the food groups provides essential nutrients. If one food group is omitted, key nutrients may be missing in the diet.
summary: Omitting food groups, like fruits and vegetables, can lead to deficiencies in essential nutrients.

content: Exclusion of grains increases the risk of inadequate riboflavin, thiamin, and niacin.  Each of the food groups provides essential nutrients. If one food group is omitted, key nutrients may be missing in the diet.
summary: Not consuming grains can result in deficiencies of riboflavin, thiamin, and niacin.

content: The frequently seen version of MyPyramid is designed for the general population. Although the basic guidelines in the pyramid are universal, athletes, especially elite and professional athletes, have different needs from those of the general population. The guidelines may need to be adjusted to meet the recommended dietary intakes for a specific athlete.
summary: Dietary guidelines, like MyPyramid, should be adapted for athletes based on their specific needs.

content: Consuming the food amounts shown in figure 10.1 provides about 2,000 kcal. Table 10.1 provides the suggested amounts of food to consume from the basic food groups and oils to meet recommended nutrient intakes at 12 different calorie levels.
summary: Food intake recommendations are provided based on calorie levels and food groups.

content: Dietary Reference Intakes (DRIs) are recommendations for 50 nutrients (14 vitamins, 18 minerals, and 18 macronutrients and related food components). In 2005, the DRIs replaced the ‘Recommended Dietary Allowances’ guidelines that had been used since 1941. Between 1997 and 2004, the National Academy of Sciences Institute of Medicine issued eight reports, with six containing information about nutrient recommendations and two containing information about assessment and planning in relation to dietary intakes.
summary: DRIs are comprehensive nutrient recommendations that replaced the older RDA guidelines.

content: The DRI report for each nutrient or food component includes the following information: 1. Estimated average requirement and its standard deviation by age and gender 2. Recommended dietary consumption based on the estimated average requirement 3. An adequate intake level when a recommended intake cannot be based on an estimated average requirement 4. Tolerable upper intake levels above which risk of toxicity increases.
summary: DRI reports provide detailed information about nutrient requirements, including estimated average requirements, recommended intakes, and tolerable upper intake levels.

content: A macronutrient is a nutrient that is required in significant amounts in the diet. Three important classes of macronutrients are protein, carbohydrates, and lipids (fats and related compounds). To support training and competition, athletes should consume a balanced diet that provides all the essential nutrients: carbohydrate, protein, fat, water, vitamins, and minerals.
summary: Macronutrients (protein, carbohydrates, and lipids) are essential dietary components for athletes.

content: Proteins, like carbohydrates and fats, are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms. Unlike carbohydrates and fats, proteins also contain nitrogen. Amino acids are the molecules that, when joined in groups of a few dozen to hundreds, form the thousands of proteins occurring in nature.
summary: Proteins are composed of amino acids, which are linked together to form proteins.

content: Proteins in the human body are composed of 20 amino acids. More than half of these can be synthesized by the human body and are commonly called ‘nonessential’ amino acids because they do not need to be consumed in the diet. Nine of the amino acids are ‘essential’ because the body cannot manufacture them and therefore they must be obtained through the diet.
summary: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins, with 9 being essential and needing to be consumed through diet.

content: Amino acids are joined together by peptide bonds. Two amino acids together are referred to as a dipeptide, and several amino acids together are referred to as a polypeptide.
summary: Amino acids are linked together by peptide bonds to form dipeptides and polypeptides.

content: Muscle tissue is often the first bodily structure that comes to mind when one considers protein’s existence in the body. The majority of the body’s protein exists as skeletal muscle, organs, and bone tissue. Tissues are mostly water and contain various amounts of protein. Protein makes up 20% of the weight of the heart, skeletal muscles, liver, and glands. Protein makes up 10% of brain tissue.
summary: Protein is a major component of muscle tissue, organs, and bone tissue.

content: Nonstructural or plasma proteins, such as enzymes, antibodies, lipoproteins, hormones, hemoglobin, albumin, and transferrin, constitute only a small proportion of the body’s protein.
summary: Nonstructural proteins, like enzymes and hormones, represent a smaller portion of the body's total protein.

content: The amino acid content of a dietary protein affects its value in supporting growth and tissue maintenance. Whether the protein supplies amino acids in amounts proportionate to the body’s needs determines the protein quality.
summary: The quality of a protein depends on its amino acid composition and its ability to meet the body's needs.

content: High-quality protein, protein of high biological value, and complete protein are synonymous terms used to refer to a protein with an amino acid pattern similar to that needed by the body. High-quality proteins include proteins of animal origin (those in eggs, meat, fish, poultry, and dairy products).
summary: High-quality proteins, like those from animal sources, provide a complete amino acid profile.

content: Proteins that are deficient in one or more of the essential amino acids (grains, beans, vegetables, and gelatin) are referred to as low-quality proteins, proteins of low biological value, or incomplete proteins. Plant proteins tend to be low in lysine (grains) or methionine and cysteine (beans). Of the plant proteins, soy is the highest in quality.
summary: Plant-based proteins often lack one or more essential amino acids, making them incomplete proteins.

content: When relying on the lower-quality proteins, it is important to consume a variety of plant foods that provide different amino acids, often referred to as complementary proteins, so that all essential amino acids are consumed over the course of the day. Common examples of complementary proteins are beans and rice, corn and beans, corn tortillas and refried beans, and peanut butter and bread.
summary: Combining different plant proteins throughout the day ensures adequate intake of all essential amino acids.

content: Plant proteins tend to be low in lysine (grains) or methionine and cysteine (beans).  When relying on the lower-quality proteins, it is important to consume a variety of plant foods that provide different amino acids, often referred to as complementary proteins, so that all essential amino acids are consumed over the course of the day. Common examples of complementary proteins are beans and rice, corn and beans, corn tortillas and refried beans, and peanut butter and bread. As a general rule, combining beans or legumes with grains provides the essential amino acids in appropriate ratios.
summary: Plant proteins often lack certain essential amino acids, necessitating the consumption of complementary protein sources throughout the day to ensure adequate intake.

content: Protein quality is one of the issues that vegans (those who consume only plant proteins—no meat, fish, poultry, eggs, or milk) must consider. Of the plant proteins, soy is the highest in quality. Although dietary recommendations are stated as protein requirements, the actual requirement is for amino acids.  It was believed for some time that complementary proteins had to be consumed at the same meal. However, it is now known that consumption of a variety of proteins from legumes, seeds, nuts, and grains over the course of the day is adequate.
summary: Vegans need to carefully plan their diets to ensure sufficient intake of all essential amino acids, as plant proteins often lack certain ones.

content: The need for dietary proteins/amino acids in sedentary, healthy adults results from the constant turnover of cells. During cell turnover—the constant breakdown and regeneration of cells—the immediate supplier of amino acids is the body’s free amino acid pool. The pool is replenished from dietary protein digestion, as well as the amino acids released from tissue turnover. Substantially more protein is turned over daily than is ordinarily consumed, indicating that amino acids are recycled. This process is not completely efficient, however, so dietary amino acid intake is required to replace losses.
summary: The body requires dietary protein to replenish amino acids lost through the constant turnover of cells.

content: When estimating the protein requirements for individuals, one must consider two key factors: caloric intake and biological value of the protein. Protein can be metabolized as a source of energy in a state of negative calorie balance, in which fewer calories are consumed than are expended. In this case, the protein cannot be used for the intended purpose of replacing the amino acid pool. Thus, an inverse relationship exists between caloric intake and protein requirement. When caloric intake goes down, protein requirement goes up.
summary: Protein requirements are influenced by both caloric intake and the biological value of the protein consumed.

content: Dietary protein requirements were derived from research on subjects who were consuming adequate calories. Requirements for individuals who are not consuming adequate calories are higher than the stated requirements. Approximately 65% to 75% of the protein in those studies was from foods of animal origin (meat, fish, poultry, dairy products, and eggs), proteins of high biological value. The higher the biological value of the protein, the lower the protein requirement. Assuming that caloric intake is adequate and that two-thirds or more of the protein is from animal sources, the recommended intake for protein for adults is 0.8 g/kg (0.36 g/pound) of body weight for both men and women. Expressed as a percent of daily caloric intake, a common protein intake recommendation is 10% to 15%.
summary: Dietary protein recommendations are based on studies with adequate caloric intake, and higher requirements may exist for individuals with lower caloric intake.

content: Recommendations to increase or decrease protein intake should be made on an individual basis after the normal diet has been analyzed and caloric intake considered. A mixed diet is the best source of high-quality protein. Strict vegetarians must plan their diet carefully to ensure an adequate intake of all essential amino acids.
summary: Protein intake recommendations should be individualized based on dietary analysis and caloric intake, and vegetarians need to be particularly mindful of ensuring adequate essential amino acid intake.

content: Athletes’ protein requirements are increased by training. Both aerobic endurance training and resistance training can increase protein need, although the exact mechanisms are unclear and may be different. For aerobic endurance athletes, the underlying mechanisms could include tissue repair and the use of the branched-chain amino acids for auxiliary fuel, whereas for strength and power athletes, the mechanisms are probably tissue repair and the maintenance of a positive nitrogen balance so that the hypertrophic stimulus is maximized.
summary: Athletes have increased protein requirements due to training, with potential mechanisms including tissue repair and fuel utilization.

content: Research indicates that the protein requirement of aerobic endurance athletes is slightly over 0.8 g/kg of body weight and can reach 1.4 g/kg of body weight, due in part to increased use of protein as a fuel source during exercise. Research has shown that heavy resistance training can increase requirements to as high as 1.7 g/kg of body weight. A general recommendation of 1.5 to 2.0 g/kg of body weight ensures adequate protein intake, assuming adequate caloric intake and a diet with at least 65% of the protein of high biological value. Athletes consuming a vegan diet or restricting calories may require more than 2.0 g/kg of body weight.
summary: Protein requirements for athletes vary based on training type, with endurance athletes needing slightly more than sedentary individuals and strength athletes requiring the most.

content: Concerns regarding potential negative effects of protein intakes greater than 0.8 g/kg of body weight have often been expressed. Proteins consumed in excess of the amounts needed for the synthesis of tissue are broken down. The nitrogen is excreted as urea in urine, and the remaining ketoacids are either used directly as sources of energy or converted to carbohydrate (gluconeogenesis) or body fat. The strength and conditioning professional should be aware that excessively high intakes, greater than 4.0 g/kg of body weight per day, are not advisable for athletes with impaired renal function or low calcium intake or those who are restricting fluid intake. Based on current research, it appears that the protein requirements for athletes are between 1.5 and 2.0 g/kg of body weight, assuming that caloric intake and protein quality are adequate.
summary: While protein is essential, excessive intake can be detrimental. Athletes should aim for 1.5-2.0 g/kg of body weight, considering individual factors and potential risks.

content: The primary role of carbohydrate in human physiology is to serve as an energy source.
summary: Carbohydrates are the body's primary energy source.

content: Proteins consumed in excess of the amounts needed for the synthesis of tissue are broken down. The nitrogen is excreted as urea in urine, and the remaining ketoacids are either used directly as sources of energy or converted to carbohydrate (gluconeogenesis) or body fat.
summary: Excess protein is broken down, with nitrogen excreted and remaining components used for energy or converted to other substances.

content: The strength and conditioning professional should be aware that excessively high intakes, greater than 4.0 g/kg of body weight per day, are not advisable for athletes with impaired renal function or low calcium intake or those who are restricting fluid intake. Based on current research, it appears that the protein requirements for athletes are between 1.5 and 2.0 g/kg of body weight, assuming that caloric intake and protein quality are adequate.
summary: Optimal protein intake for athletes is 1.5-2.0 g/kg body weight, with caution advised for those with specific health considerations.

content: The primary role of carbohydrate in human physiology is to serve as an energy source. The importance of carbohydrates in the diets of athletes and physical laborers was recognized as early as 1901. In 1939, diets high in carbohydrate content were found to enhance subjects’ ability to perform prolonged, heavy physical activity. Numerous studies have documented an ergogenic effect of carbohydrate intake and elevated muscle glycogen concentration on aerobic endurance performance, work output, and high-intensity intermittent activity. Additionally, high muscle glycogen concentrations may be beneficial to high-intensity exercise of short duration.
summary: Carbohydrates are essential for energy, with historical recognition of their benefits for athletic performance and modern research supporting their ergogenic effects.

content: Carbohydrates are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Carbohydrates can be classified into three groups according to the number of sugar (saccharide) units they contain: monosaccharides, disaccharides, and polysaccharides.
summary: Carbohydrates are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen and are categorized into monosaccharides, disaccharides, and polysaccharides.

content: Monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, and galactose) are single-sugar molecules. Glucose is the most common monosaccharide, the building block of many larger sugars. In the body, glucose is present as circulating sugar in the blood; is the primary energy substrate for cells; and composes glycogen, a polysaccharide stored in muscle and liver cells. Glucose in a free form is not sweet. It is not found on the grocery shelf. In foods, it is usually combined with other monosaccharides to form, for example, sucrose. Isolated glucose is used in intravenous fluids and sometimes in sport drinks. In this form, it is referred to as dextrose.
summary: Glucose is the primary monosaccharide, serving as a vital energy source and component of glycogen.

content: Fructose has the same chemical formula as glucose, but because the atoms are arranged differently, it tastes much sweeter and has different properties. Fructose accounts for the sweet taste of honey. Fructose occurs naturally in fruits and vegetables. In the body, fructose causes less insulin secretion than other sugars, which has made it a focus of much research in the area of aerobic endurance performance. However, large doses of fructose have been shown to increase the risk of gastric cramping and diarrhea, so applications of fructose as the primary source of carbohydrate during exercise are limited.
summary: Fructose, a naturally occurring sugar, has unique properties and potential benefits for endurance performance, but its high intake can lead to gastrointestinal issues.

content: All types of dietary carbohydrate—sugars as well as starches—are effective in supplying the athlete with glucose and glycogen. Consumption of a mix of sugars and starches is desirable. Athletes typically consume a variety of carbohydrates in their normal diets.
summary: A balanced intake of both sugars and starches provides athletes with the necessary glucose and glycogen for optimal performance.

content: The glycemic index (GI) classifies a food by how high and for how long it raises blood glucose (i.e., the food’s glycemic response). The reference food is glucose or white bread, which is given a rating of 100. Foods that are digested quickly and raise blood glucose (and insulin) rapidly have a high GI. Foods that take longer to digest and thus slowly increase blood glucose (and therefore stimulate less insulin) have a low GI. Contrary to previously held assumptions, sugars do not always rank higher than starches. Potatoes and instant rice rank higher than table sugar.
summary: The glycemic index (GI) measures how quickly a food raises blood glucose, with high GI foods causing rapid spikes and low GI foods promoting gradual increases.

content: Many factors affect the GI of a food, and this phenomenon limits its application. Cooking, processing, eating the food as part of a meal, eating a different amount, and eating at a different time of day all affect the GI of a food in an individual. What was eaten the day before can have a latent effect on the glycemic response of a food.
summary: The glycemic index is influenced by various factors, including food preparation, portion size, and individual circumstances, making its application complex.

content: If an athlete applies the GI to guide food choices, the selection, for example, of oatmeal or another high-GI food depends on the athlete’s goal. If the goal is to quickly replenish glucose and glycogen, foods that rapidly appear as glucose in the blood are desirable. Low-GI foods may be beneficial for maintaining stable blood sugar levels throughout the day.
summary: Athletes can utilize the glycemic index to strategically choose foods based on their performance goals, prioritizing rapid glucose replenishment or sustained energy levels.

content: The Glycemic Index (GI) of a banana is 74. The GI of cherries is 32. The GI of fruit cocktail (canned) is 79.
summary: Specific examples of foods and their glycemic index values are provided.

content: If the goal is to quickly replenish glucose and glycogen, foods that rapidly appear as glucose in the blood are desirable. The GI of watermelon is 103. The GI of raisins is 91. The GI of baked potatoes is 121. The GI of rice cakes is 117. The GI of jelly beans is 114. The GI of pretzels is 119. The GI of angel food cake is 95. The GI of vanilla wafers is 110. The GI of sponge cake is 66.
summary: Foods with a high Glycemic Index (GI) are quickly digested and raise blood glucose levels rapidly, aiding in glycogen replenishment.

content: The Glycemic Index (GI) of a banana is 74. The GI of fruit cocktail (canned) is 79. The GI of orange juice is 74. The GI of pineapple is 84. The GI of sweet potatoes is 77. The GI of corn is 78. The GI of oatmeal cookies is 79. The GI of popcorn is 79. The GI of potato chips is 77. The GI of grapefruits is 69.
summary: These fruits and foods have a moderate to high GI, indicating a relatively quick impact on blood sugar levels.

content: The GI of cherries is 32. The GI of chickpeas is 39. The GI of kidney beans is 39. The GI of green lentils is 42. The GI of lima beans is 46. The GI of pinto beans is 55. The GI of split peas is 45. The GI of a pear is 54. The GI of tomato soup is 54. The GI of corn chips is 60. The GI of an orange is 60. The GI of grapes is 66. The GI of lentil soup is 63. The GI of baked beans is 69.
summary: These foods have a lower GI, indicating a slower and more sustained release of glucose into the bloodstream.

content: Low-GI foods may risk of gastric cramping and diarrhea, so applications of fructose as the primary source of carbohydrate during exercise are limited.
summary: The potential for gastrointestinal discomfort limits the use of fructose as the primary carbohydrate source during exercise.

content: The GI of corn chips is 60. The GI of potato chips is 77. The GI of peanuts is 21. The GI of angel food cake is 95. The GI of sponge cake is 66. The GI of a croissant is 96. The GI of a cake doughnut is 108. The GI of a bran muffin is 85. The GI of waffles is 109. The GI of a bagel is 103. The GI of a hamburger bun is 87. The GI of a Kaiser roll is 104. The GI of wheat bread is 99. The GI of All-Bran cereal is 60. The GI of Cheerios cereal is 106. The GI of cornflakes cereal is 116. The GI of oat bran cereal is 78. The GI of puffed wheat cereal is 105. The GI of Rice Krispies cereal is 117. The GI of Shredded Wheat cereal is 107.
summary: This chunk lists the glycemic index (GI) values for various processed foods, including breads, cereals, snacks, and desserts, ranging from low to high.

content: The GI of barley is 36. The GI of white rice is 91. The GI of brown rice is 79. The GI of instant rice is 98.
summary: This chunk provides the GI values for different types of rice and barley.

content: The GI of linguine is 70. The GI of regular ice cream is 87. The GI of low-fat ice cream is 71. The GI of whole milk is 38. The GI of skim milk is 46. The GI of low-fat yogurt with added fruit is 44. The GI of low-fat yogurt with artificial sweetener is 20.
summary: This chunk lists the GI values for various dairy products and pasta.

content: The GI of honey is 78. The GI of fructose is 27. The GI of glucose is 141. The GI of sucrose is 97. The GI of lactose is 66. The GI of maltose is 150.
summary: This chunk presents the GI values for various sugars, including fructose, glucose, sucrose, lactose, and maltose.

content: The GI of an apple is 52. The GI of apple juice is 57.
summary: This chunk provides the GI values for an apple and its juice.

content: Diets low in fiber have been associated with several disorders, including diverticulosis, constipation, heart disease, cancer of the colon, and diabetes.
summary: This chunk highlights the health risks associated with low-fiber diets.

content: The GI of lactose is 66. The GI of maltose is 150. The GI of an apple is 52. The GI of apple juice is 57.
summary: This chunk lists the glycemic indices (GI) of various foods, including lactose, maltose, apple, and apple juice.

content: Diets low in fiber have been associated with several disorders, including diverticulosis, constipation, heart disease, cancer of the colon, and diabetes. A high-fiber diet is recommended by many public health organizations. The Dietary Reference Intake for fiber is 38 and 25 g/day for young men and women, respectively, based on the intake level observed to protect against coronary heart disease.
summary: This chunk discusses the importance of fiber in the diet, highlighting its health benefits and recommended intake.

content: Although appropriate for many individuals, this level of fiber may be excessive for some aerobic endurance athletes, specifically runners, who may experience ‘runner’s trots.’ On the other hand, increasing fiber for athletes who experience irritable bowel syndrome can be effective in normalizing bowel habits. A high fiber intake is often achieved in the typical American diet by a high-fiber cereal or a supplement.
summary: This chunk explores the potential impact of fiber intake on athletes, noting that while beneficial for some, it may be excessive for others and can be supplemented.

content: Fiber is also found in fruits, vegetables, nuts, seeds, legumes, and whole-grain products such as whole-grain bread, oatmeal, and popcorn.
summary: This chunk lists various food sources rich in fiber.

content: Roughly 50 to 100 g of carbohydrate per day is needed to prevent ketosis (high levels of ketones in the bloodstream). Beyond that need, carbohydrates provide fuel for energy. The Institute of Medicine recommends 45% to 65% of total daily calories as carbohydrate.
summary: This chunk outlines the role of carbohydrates in the body, emphasizing their importance for energy and preventing ketosis.

content: A high-carbohydrate diet is commonly recommended for all athletes. However, it is important to note that a variety of diets, with various carbohydrate, protein, and fat mixtures, have been shown to be equally effective in supporting training and performance. The notion that all athletes need and should consume diets very high in carbohydrates should be dispelled. Some athletes certainly benefit from a high-carbohydrate diet, but others do not benefit; and in the worst case, some athletes do not perform as well on a high-carbohydrate diet. Individualizing carbohydrate intake based on the training program, sport, and diet history is imperative.
summary: This chunk discusses the role of carbohydrates in athletic diets, emphasizing the importance of individualization and debunking the myth that all athletes need high-carbohydrate diets.

content: Aerobic endurance athletes who train for long durations (90 minutes or more daily) should replenish glycogen levels by consuming maximal levels of carbohydrate, approximately 8 to 10 g/kg of body weight. This is equivalent to 600 to 750 g of carbohydrate (2,400-3,000 kcal from carbohydrate) per day for an athlete weighing 165 pounds (75 kg). This level has been shown to adequately restore skeletal glycogen within 24 hours. Athletes who benefit from this level of carbohydrate intake include those engaged in continuous aerobic activity for more than an hour on most days, such as distance runners, road cyclists, triathletes, and cross-country skiers.
summary: This chunk details the high carbohydrate requirements for endurance athletes, explaining the rationale and providing specific recommendations.

content: More recently, research has shown that athletes engaged in high-intensity, intermittent activities, such as soccer players, also benefit from high-carbohydrate diets. However, the majority of power and sprint athletes do not train aerobically for more than an hour each day. Research on the carbohydrate needs of these athletes—such as American football players, sprinters, basketball players, wrestlers, and volleyball players—is limited.
summary: This chunk expands on the benefits of high-carbohydrate diets, noting their applicability to high-intensity intermittent activities and acknowledging the need for further research on other athletic populations.

content: Carbohydrate intake and muscle glycogen levels seem to have much less of an impact, if any, on strength performance. Intake of approximately half of that recommended for aerobic endurance athletes appears adequate to support training and performance of strength, sprint, and skill athletes, and thus an intake of 5 to 6 g/kg per day is reasonable. Some aerobic endurance athletes have maximal carbohydrate requirements, up to 10 g/kg per day. Most athletes do not deplete muscle glycogen on a daily basis, however, and therefore have lower carbohydrate requirements.
summary: This chunk discusses the carbohydrate needs of strength, sprint, and skill athletes, highlighting their lower requirements compared to endurance athletes.

content: Lipids include triglycerides, fatty acids, phospholipids, and cholesterol. Triglycerides are formed by the union of glycerol with three fatty acids. The majority of lipids found in foods and in the body are in the triglyceride form. Fat contains carbon, oxygen, and hydrogen atoms. Fats provide approximately 9 kcal/g, while carbohydrates and protein provide approximately 4 kcal/g.
summary: This chunk defines lipids and their components, emphasizing the role of triglycerides and their caloric density.

content: Saturated fatty acids contain all the hydrogen they can carry. Unsaturated fatty acids have some positions where hydrogen atoms are missing, and carbon atoms are joined together by double bonds. Fatty acids containing no double bonds are saturated. Fatty acids containing one double bond are called monounsaturated. Fatty acids containing two or more double bonds are polyunsaturated.
summary: This chunk describes the different types of fatty acids based on their saturation and the presence of double bonds.

content: Saturated fatty acids contain all the hydrogen they can carry. Fatty acids containing no double bonds are saturated. Most animal fats and tropical oils (e.g., coconut, palm kernel) are relatively high in saturated fatty acids.
summary: Definition and sources of saturated fatty acids.

content: Unsaturated fatty acids have some positions where hydrogen atoms are missing, and carbon atoms are joined together by double bonds. Fatty acids containing one double bond are called monounsaturated. Fatty acids containing two or more double bonds are polyunsaturated. Olive, peanut, and canola oils are high in monounsaturated fatty acids. Soy, corn, sunflower, and safflower oils are relatively high in polyunsaturated fatty acids.
summary: Definition and sources of unsaturated fatty acids.

content: Energy is stored primarily as adipose tissue in humans. Body fat is necessary for insulation and protection of organs, and for hormonal regulation. Fat serves as a carrier for the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K. Fat supplies the essential fatty acids, linoleic acid (omega-6) and linolenic acid (omega-3).
summary: Functions of fat in the body.

content: Linoleic acid and linolenic acid are necessary for the formation of healthy cell membranes, the proper development and functioning of the brain and nervous system, and the production of hormones. Fat is important because it is responsible for the characteristic flavor, aroma, and texture of many foods. Fat also promotes a feeling of fullness, or satiety, after a meal.
summary: Importance of essential fatty acids and fat in food.

content: Cholesterol is an important structural and functional component of cell membranes. Cholesterol is necessary for the production of bile salts, vitamin D, and several hormones, including the sex hormones (estrogen, androgen, and progesterone) and cortisol. Cholesterol is synthesized in the liver and intestine.
summary: Role of cholesterol in the body.

content: High levels of cholesterol or unfavorable ratios of lipoproteins are associated with increased risk of heart disease. High levels of HDLs protect against heart disease, and HDLs can be increased by exercise and weight loss. In patients with heart disease and high total cholesterol levels, a low-fat diet can decrease total cholesterol and decrease the ratio of total cholesterol to HDLs.
summary: Cholesterol and heart disease.

content: Although replacing saturated fat with carbohydrates has been a common public health recommendation, it is increasingly recognized that high carbohydrate intake can have the negative metabolic consequence of increasing triglycerides in some individuals. Scientists are currently examining the use of monounsaturated fats to replace certain saturated fats as an effective alternative to increasing carbohydrate for some individuals.
summary: Current thinking on fat and carbohydrate intake.

content: Individuals should consume 5% to 10% of energy from omega-6 (linoleic) fatty acids and 0.6% to 1.2% from omega-3 (linolenic) fatty acids. Because of the prevalence of these fatty acids in foods and their low intake requirements, fatty acid deficiency was once of concern only for individuals with malabsorption diseases. The availability of fat-free foods combined with the obsession to cut fat from the diet has made fatty acid deficiency a real concern for otherwise healthy individuals who overly restrict dietary fat.
summary: Recommended intake of omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids.

content: Approximately 34% of calories in the typical American diet are from fat. The Institute of Medicine recommends that fat should constitute 20% to 35% of the total calories consumed, with less than 10% from saturated fats (one-third of total fat intake). The Institute of Medicine also recommends keeping cholesterol as low as possible. These recommendations are given primarily to reduce the incidence of cardiovascular disease.
summary: Dietary fat recommendations.

content: It is predicted that reductions of dietary fat from current levels to recommended levels can achieve a reduction in total cholesterol and LDLs of about 5%. Fat guidelines for individual athletes, however, may be higher than standard “heart healthy” guidelines. Research shows that during periods of heavy aerobic endurance training, increasing dietary fat to as high as 50% of calories does not negatively affect plasma lipids. Indeed, fat intakes greater than 30% are common in elite athletes.
summary: Fat guidelines for athletes.

content: The Institute of Medicine set an acceptable lower limit of 20% of calories from fat, thus recognizing the necessity of eating enough fat for health. Very-low-fat diets, such as those sometimes prescribed for patients with severe heart disease, are not recommended for athletes. In certain populations, reducing dietary fat to 10% of total calories may actually worsen lipid profiles. Additionally, diets extremely low in fat (less than 15% of total calories) may decrease testosterone production, thus decreasing metabolism and muscle development.
summary: Fat intake for athletes and special populations.

content: The negative view of fat evolved from research conducted over the past three decades linking excess dietary fat intake to cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, and obesity. Reducing dietary fat to decrease the risk of any of these chronic conditions is obviously not the top goal of most young athletes.
summary: Historical context and current perspectives on dietary fat.

content: Very-low-fat diets, such as those sometimes prescribed for patients with severe heart disease, are not recommended for athletes. Additionally, diets extremely low in fat (less than 15% of total calories) may decrease testosterone production, thus decreasing metabolism and muscle development. Athletes want to eat to improve performance. It is a paradox, then, that there is so much focus on reducing dietary fat, because there are very few performance enhancements from significantly reducing fat in the athlete’s diet; in fact, an emphasis on low-fat or no-fat diets may prove negative for many.
summary: Athletes should avoid very-low-fat diets as they can negatively impact performance and health.

content: The negative view of fat evolved from research conducted over the past three decades linking excess dietary fat intake to cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, and obesity. Reducing dietary fat to decrease the risk of any of these chronic conditions is obviously not the top goal of most young athletes.
summary: The negative perception of fat stems from its association with chronic diseases, but this is not a primary concern for most young athletes.

content: Because of fat’s negative image, some athletes overly restrict dietary fat. This fat phobia is unfounded and can lead to diets void of meat and dairy products, which in turn increase the risk of deficiencies of nutrients such as protein, calcium, iron, and zinc. Fat phobia, or fear of eating fat, can lead to nutrient deficiencies, which harm performance. Athletes who eat very little or no fat should receive nutritional counseling and information.
summary: Athletes should avoid excessive fat restriction as it can lead to nutrient deficiencies and negatively impact performance.

content: Both intramuscular and circulating fatty acids are potential energy sources during exercise. Intramuscular fatty acids are more important during activity and circulating fatty acids (from adipose tissue or diet) are more important during recovery.
summary: Both intramuscular and circulating fatty acids contribute to energy production during exercise, with different roles depending on the activity phase.

content: Compared with the limited capacity of the body to store carbohydrate, fat stores are large and represent a vast source of fuel for exercise. At rest and during low-intensity exercise, a high percentage of the energy produced is derived from fatty acid oxidation. With increasing exercise intensities (over 70%-80% V.O2max), there is a gradual shift from fat to carbohydrate as the preferred source of fuel.
summary: Fat serves as a primary energy source during rest and low-intensity exercise, while carbohydrate becomes more important at higher intensities.

content: Nutritional interventions that may theoretically promote fat oxidation and decrease muscle glycogen depletion include ingestion of caffeine, consumption of medium-chain triglycerides, and exposure to high-fat, low-carbohydrate diets. Consuming caffeine has been shown to improve aerobic endurance capacity in some individuals. Supplementing with medium-chain triglycerides has been shown to have no effect on performance. Use of medium-chain triglycerides has caused gastric distress, thereby actually diminishing performance.
summary: Certain nutritional strategies, like caffeine intake and high-fat diets, may enhance fat utilization during exercise, but the effectiveness of medium-chain triglycerides is questionable.

content: Consumption of high-fat diets (38% of calories from fat) in the presence of adequate caloric intake has been shown to enhance performance in trained runners. High-fat diets (35% of calories) compared with lower-fat diets (24% of total calories) consumed by female soccer players resulted in a longer distance to exhaustion (7.0 miles [11.2 km] on higher fat vs. 6.0 miles [9.7 km] on lower fat) in treadmill tests replicating soccer performance. In untrained men, chronic consumption of a high-fat diet resulted in the same improvement in aerobic endurance performance as a high-carbohydrate diet.
summary: High-fat diets, when calorie intake is adequate, can improve performance in various athletes, including runners and soccer players.

content: Acute supplementation of fat does not appear to improve performance in those who were not necessarily consuming a high-fat diet previously. Because training increases the capacity of the muscle to use fatty acids and because the type of diet to which the body is adapted influences the outcome, the effects of high-fat diets vary, depending on the individual.
summary: The impact of high-fat diets on performance depends on individual training adaptations and dietary history.

content: To ensure adequate protein provision, fat is the nutrient of choice to decrease so that caloric intake can remain similar while carbohydrate is increased. Because fat is dense in calories and is highly palatable, decreasing dietary fat, if the diet has excess fat, can help reduce caloric intake. Manipulation of fat and carbohydrate may be necessary if medically indicated for an athlete who has high blood cholesterol levels or a family history of heart disease. This diet therapy should be guided by a registered dietitian.
summary: Adjusting fat intake can be beneficial for athletes, particularly when aiming to increase carbohydrate or reduce overall calories. Medical guidance is recommended for athletes with specific health concerns.

content: There are three reasons for athletes to reduce dietary fat: need to increase carbohydrate intake to support training type, need to reduce total caloric intake to achieve weight loss, and need to decrease elevated blood cholesterol.
summary: Athletes may need to reduce dietary fat for specific reasons, such as supporting training, managing weight, or addressing high cholesterol.

content: Vitamin A promotes growth and repair of body tissues, bone formation, and healthy skin and hair. It is essential for night vision. Good food sources of Vitamin A include liver (all sources), giblets, some cheese, egg yolk, whole milk, and butter.
summary: Vitamin A is crucial for growth, tissue repair, bone health, vision, and can be obtained from liver, giblets, cheese, egg yolk, whole milk, and butter.

content: Beta carotene serves as an antioxidant. Good food sources include sweet peppers, carrots, grape leaves, pumpkin, sweet potatoes, yams, broccoli, dandelion greens, chili peppers, mustard greens, spinach, kale, turnip greens, apricots, papaya, watermelon, peaches, asparagus, winter squash, cantaloupe, muskmelon, chard.
summary: Beta carotene, a potent antioxidant, is found in various fruits and vegetables like carrots, sweet potatoes, spinach, and apricots.

content: Vitamin D aids in the absorption of calcium and helps to build bone mass and prevent bone loss. It helps maintain blood levels of calcium and phosphorus. Good food sources of Vitamin D include fish (herring, salmon, oysters, catfish, sardines, tuna, shrimp, mackerel), milk, margarine, fortified breakfast cereals, egg yolks, and butter.
summary: Vitamin D is essential for calcium absorption, bone health, and blood mineral balance. It can be obtained from fish, milk, fortified foods, and egg yolks.

content: Vitamin E serves as an antioxidant and is needed for normal growth and development.
summary: Vitamin E acts as an antioxidant and is vital for growth and development.

content: Vitamin A is essential for vision, immune function, and cell growth. Good food sources include liver, giblets, some cheese, egg yolk, whole milk, and butter.
summary: Vitamin A is crucial for vision, immunity, and cell growth, found in liver, dairy, and eggs.

content: Beta carotene is a powerful antioxidant that the body converts to Vitamin A. It is abundant in sweet peppers, carrots, grape leaves, pumpkin, sweet potatoes, yams, broccoli, dandelion greens, chili peppers, mustard greens, spinach, kale, turnip greens, apricots, papaya, watermelon, peaches, asparagus, winter squash, cantaloupe, muskmelon, and chard.
summary: Beta carotene, a potent antioxidant, is a precursor to Vitamin A and is found in a wide variety of fruits and vegetables.

content: Vitamin D promotes calcium absorption, bone health, and blood regulation. Good sources include fish (herring, salmon, oysters, catfish, sardines, tuna, shrimp, mackerel), milk, margarine, fortified breakfast cereals, egg yolks, and butter.
summary: Vitamin D is vital for bone health, calcium absorption, and blood regulation, found in fatty fish, dairy, and fortified foods.

content: Vitamin E is a potent antioxidant that supports growth, development, and immune function. Good sources include oils (wheat germ, vegetable), mayonnaise, fortified breakfast cereals, nuts (almonds, hazelnuts, peanuts, hickory, pistachio), margarine, wheat germ, and peanut butter.
summary: Vitamin E, a powerful antioxidant, supports growth, development, and immune function, found in nuts, seeds, and oils.

content: Vitamin K is essential for blood clotting and bone health. Good sources include kale, Brussels sprouts, spinach, chard, cauliflower, broccoli, turnip and mustard greens, carrots, asparagus, avocados, bell peppers, strawberries, tomatoes, apples, and peaches.
summary: Vitamin K is crucial for blood clotting and bone health, found in leafy greens, cruciferous vegetables, and fruits.

content: Vitamin C is a potent antioxidant that supports immune function, wound healing, and iron absorption. Good sources include sweet peppers, broccoli, Brussels sprouts, cauliflower, strawberries, oranges, orange juice, limes, lemon juice, grapefruit, grapefruit juice, papayas, cantaloupe, tomatoes, tomato juice, asparagus, raw cabbage, spinach, pineapple, raspberries, potatoes, and onions.
summary: Vitamin C is a powerful antioxidant that boosts immunity, aids wound healing, and enhances iron absorption, found in a wide variety of fruits and vegetables.

content: Thiamin (B1) is essential for carbohydrate metabolism, nervous system function, and muscle health. Good sources include fortified breakfast cereals, sunflower seeds, peas, pork, oranges, orange juice, lima beans, pecans, and enriched rice.
summary: Thiamin (B1) is crucial for energy metabolism, nerve function, and muscle health, found in fortified cereals, pork, and legumes.

content: Riboflavin (B2) is essential for red blood cell formation, nervous system function, and metabolism of carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. Good sources include liver, wheat germ, brewer’s yeast, almonds, cheese, fortified breakfast cereal, whey protein, milk, eggs, lamb, pork, veal, beef, and broccoli.
summary: Riboflavin (B2) supports red blood cell production, nervous system function, and energy metabolism, found in dairy, eggs, and leafy greens.

content: Niacin is essential for carbohydrate, protein, and fat metabolism, as well as nervous system function. High intakes can lower elevated cholesterol. Good sources include soy protein, soy flour, textured vegetable protein, whey protein, beef, peanuts, peanut butter, sunflower seeds, and fortified breakfast cereals.
summary: Niacin supports energy metabolism, nervous system function, and may lower cholesterol, found in soy products, nuts, and fortified cereals.

content: Pyridoxine (B6) is essential for protein metabolism, nervous system function, and immune system function. Good sources include liver, bananas, fortified breakfast cereals, soybeans, chicken, tuna, raw carrots, beef, broccoli, spinach, potatoes, alfalfa sprouts, navy beans, peanut butter, garbanzo beans, walnuts, sunflower seeds, avocados, eggs, lima beans, cabbage, and salmon.
summary: Pyridoxine (B6) is crucial for protein metabolism, nervous system function, and immune health, found in a variety of foods.

content: Folic acid is essential for cell growth and development, red blood cell formation, and may reduce the risk of neural tube birth defects, heart disease, and cervical dysplasia. Good sources include brewer’s yeast, fortified breakfast cereals, liver, black-eyed peas, beans (pinto, black, lima, white, garbanzo, soy), peanuts, peanut butter, spinach, turnip greens, asparagus, mustard greens, seaweed, eggs, enriched bread, oranges, and orange juice.
summary: Folic acid is vital for cell growth, red blood cell production, and may reduce the risk of birth defects and chronic diseases, found in fortified foods, legumes, and leafy greens.

content: Cobalamin (B12) is essential for blood formation and a healthy nervous system. Good sources include liver, oysters, lamb, eggs, beef, shellfish, fish, poultry, pork, chicken, and fortified breakfast cereals.
summary: Cobalamin (B12) is crucial for blood cell production and nervous system health, found in animal products and fortified foods.

content: Biotin assists in the metabolism of fatty acids and the utilization of B vitamins. Good sources include nuts (peanuts, hazelnuts, almonds, cashews, macadamia), soybeans, peanut butter, black-eyed peas, liver, milk, egg yolks, yeast, cheese, cauliflower, carrots, avocados, and sweet potatoes.
summary: Biotin supports fatty acid metabolism and B vitamin utilization, found in nuts, seeds, and eggs.

content: Pantothenic acid is essential for normal growth and development. Good sources include liver, sunflower seeds, fortified breakfast cereals, egg yolks, whey protein, soy protein, peanuts, peanut butter, pecans, veal, enriched rice, broccoli, and lima beans.
summary: Pantothenic acid is crucial for growth and development, found in a variety of foods.

content: The human body requires various minerals for a wide variety of metabolic functions. Calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, iron, and the electrolytes sodium, potassium, and chloride are often called the major minerals.
summary: The body needs various minerals for diverse functions, with calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, iron, and electrolytes being major ones.

content: Calcium is essential for developing and maintaining healthy bones and teeth, nerve transmission, and muscle contraction. It reduces the risk of osteoporosis and may also reduce the risk of preeclampsia in pregnant women. Good sources include fruit juices and fruit drinks fortified with calcium, cheese, sardines, milk, cottage cheese, yogurt, ice cream, calcium-set tofu, turnip greens, Chinese cabbage, mustard greens, kale, and rutabaga.
summary: Calcium is vital for bone health, nerve function, muscle contraction, and may reduce the risk of osteoporosis and preeclampsia, found in dairy, leafy greens, and fortified foods.

content: Phosphorus works with calcium to develop and maintain strong bones and teeth. It enhances the use of other nutrients and is essential for energy metabolism, DNA structure, and cell membranes. Good sources include cheese, fish, beef, pork, whole-wheat products, cocoa powder, pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds, and almonds.
summary: Phosphorus supports bone health, energy metabolism, and cell structure, found in dairy, meat, nuts, and seeds.

content: Magnesium activates nearly 100 enzymes and helps nerves and muscles function.
summary: Magnesium is essential for enzyme activity and nerve and muscle function.

content: Phosphorus works with calcium to develop and maintain strong bones and teeth. Phosphorus is essential for energy metabolism, DNA structure, and cell membranes. Good food sources of phosphorus include cheese, fish, beef, pork, whole-wheat products, cocoa powder, pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds, and almonds.
summary: Phosphorus is crucial for bone health, energy metabolism, and cellular function, found in various foods like dairy, meat, and nuts.

content: Magnesium activates nearly 100 enzymes and helps nerves and muscles function. Magnesium is a constituent of bones and teeth. Good food sources of magnesium include bran (wheat and rice), cocoa powder, fortified breakfast cereals, seeds (pumpkin, sunflower), soybeans, nuts (almonds, pine nuts, hazelnuts, cashews, walnuts, peanuts), and spinach.
summary: Magnesium is vital for enzyme activity, nerve and muscle function, and bone health, abundant in whole grains, nuts, and leafy greens.

content: Molybdenum is needed for the metabolism of DNA and ribonucleic acid (RNA) and production of uric acid. Good food sources of molybdenum include milk, milk products, peas, beans, liver, and whole-grain products.
summary: Molybdenum plays a role in DNA and RNA metabolism, as well as uric acid production, found in dairy, legumes, and whole grains.

content: Manganese is necessary for the normal development of the skeletal and connective tissues. Manganese is involved in the metabolism of carbohydrates. Good food sources of manganese include wheat germ, wheat bran, rice bran, fortified breakfast cereals, rice cakes, nuts (peanuts, pecans, pine nuts, walnuts, almonds, hazelnuts), soybeans, mussels, and whole-wheat products (pastas, breads, and crackers).
summary: Manganese is essential for bone and connective tissue development, carbohydrate metabolism, and found in whole grains, nuts, and legumes.

content: Copper is involved in iron metabolism, nervous system functioning, bone health, and synthesis of proteins. Copper plays a role in the pigmentation of skin, hair, and eyes. Good food sources of copper include liver, shellfish (especially oysters), lobster, nuts (cashews, Brazil nuts, hazelnuts, walnuts, peanuts, almonds, pecans, pistachios), seeds (sunflower, pumpkin), fortified breakfast cereals, great northern beans.
summary: Copper is vital for iron absorption, nervous system function, bone health, protein synthesis, and pigmentation, found in organ meats, shellfish, nuts, and seeds.

content: Chromium aids in glucose metabolism and may help regulate blood sugar and insulin levels in people with diabetes. Good food sources of chromium include mushrooms (white), raw oysters, wine, apples, brewer’s yeast, beer, pork, and chicken.
summary: Chromium supports glucose metabolism and may help regulate blood sugar, found in mushrooms, oysters, and certain beverages and meats.

content: Iodine is part of the thyroid hormone. Iodine helps regulate growth, development, and energy metabolism. Good food sources of iodine include iodized salt, saltwater fish and seafood.
summary: Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone production, regulating growth, development, and metabolism, found in iodized salt and seafood.

content: Iron is necessary for red blood cell formation and function. Iron is a constituent of myoglobin and a component of enzyme systems. Good food sources of iron include liver, beef, lamb, pork, veal, poultry, clams, oysters, fortified breakfast cereals, enriched bread products, brewer’s yeast, nuts (pine nuts, cashews, almonds), and beans (kidney, green, garbanzo).
summary: Iron is crucial for red blood cell production, oxygen transport, and enzyme function, found in red meat, poultry, seafood, and fortified foods.

content: Selenium is an essential component of a key antioxidant enzyme. Selenium is necessary for normal growth and development and for the use of iodine in thyroid function. Good food sources of selenium include tenderloin of beef, pollack, trout, tuna, oysters, mackerel, flounder, liver, sunflower seeds, wheat bran, wheat germ, some pork, fortified breakfast cereals, perch, crab, clams, cod, haddock, and whole-wheat breads.
summary: Selenium is a powerful antioxidant, supports growth and development, and aids in thyroid function, found in seafood, meat, and whole grains.

content: Zinc is an essential part of more than 100 enzymes involved in digestion, metabolism, reproduction, and wound healing. Good food sources of zinc include oysters, beef, veal, lamb, pork, chicken, lima beans, black-eyed peas, and white beans.
summary: Zinc is essential for numerous bodily functions, including digestion, metabolism, reproduction, and wound healing, found in oysters, meat, and legumes.

content: Ultratrace minerals include arsenic, bromine, cadmium, fluorine, lead, lithium, manganese, molybdenum, nickel, silicon, tin, vanadium, and boron. As early as the 1970s, scientists speculated that deficiencies in one or more of the ultratrace elements contribute to the occurrence of human diseases.
summary: Ultratrace minerals, though needed in small amounts, may play a role in health, with some research suggesting deficiencies can contribute to diseases.

content: Boron has been linked to bone health.
summary: Boron may contribute to bone health.

content: Two minerals—iron and calcium—deserve additional attention. Athletes who do not consume enough dietary iron risk iron depletion and impaired performance. Inadequate dietary calcium can contribute to low bone density and risk for osteoporosis.
summary: Iron and calcium are particularly important for athletes, with deficiencies potentially leading to impaired performance and bone health issues.

content: Iron is a mineral that receives a great deal of attention from athletes. Iron is a constituent of hemoglobin and myoglobin and, as such, plays a role in oxygen transport and utilization of energy. Iron deficiency inhibits athletic performance. Deficiency of most vitamins and minerals is rare in the diets of developed countries; however, iron deficiency leading to anemia is a relatively common nutritional deficiency, especially in women and adolescents. The number of healthy women in the United States who have some degree of iron deficiency has been estimated to be as high as 58%. Iron deficiency also commonly occurs in growing teenage males.
summary: Iron is crucial for athletes due to its role in oxygen transport and energy production. Iron deficiency is common, particularly in women and adolescents.

content: Iron is a constituent of hemoglobin and myoglobin and, as such, plays a role in oxygen transport and utilization of energy. Iron deficiency inhibits athletic performance. Deficiency of most vitamins and minerals is rare in the diets of developed countries; however, iron deficiency leading to anemia is a relatively common nutritional deficiency, especially in women and adolescents. The number of healthy women in the United States who have some degree of iron deficiency has been estimated to be as high as 58%. Iron deficiency also commonly occurs in growing teenage males. Blood loss is the primary avenue of iron loss from the body. A number of other factors may contribute to low iron stores in athletes, including heavy training, gastrointestinal bleeding, breakdown of red blood cells, increased iron losses through sweating, decreased iron absorption, and inadequate dietary intake. Women who experience heavy menstrual bleeding may be at increased risk for iron deficiency, as may athletes who consume vegetarian diets because of the lower absorption of iron from plant foods.
summary: Iron is essential for athletic performance, and deficiency is common, particularly in women and adolescents.  Various factors can contribute to low iron stores.

content: Athletes found to be iron deficient through blood analysis should receive diet counseling and, when indicated, supplemental iron (103). Just as inadequate iron is problematic, excess iron is also harmful. Some individuals have a silent disease called hemochromatosis, in which the body stores excess iron. Frequently, the first sign of the disease is multiple-organ failure due to excess iron in heart and liver tissue. Men, because they do not lose blood through the menstrual cycle, are more susceptible to the disease. Supplemental iron intake by men should be recommended only after blood analysis showing depleted iron stores.
summary: Iron deficiency should be addressed with diet and supplementation, while excess iron can be harmful, particularly in men.

content: Except for the calcium contributed from maternal stores during fetal development, all calcium in the adult body is derived from external sources. Inadequate calcium intake inhibits bone mineral density. Low calcium intakes have been reported in many female athletes (9, 18, 32). Many women do not consume adequate calcium because of low caloric intake or avoidance of milk and dairy foods, the best sources of calcium (9, 18, 54, 63). Athletes who consume low-calcium diets may be at risk for osteopenia and osteoporosis, deterioration of bone tissue leading to increased bone fragility and risk of fracture. Although fracture risk is influenced by genetic, hormonal, and training factors, diet plays an essential role. Athletes should be encouraged to include dairy products and other calcium-rich foods in the diet. Those who are unable to meet calcium requirements via diet may need a calcium supplement.
summary: Calcium intake is crucial for bone health, and athletes, especially women, may be at risk for deficiencies.

content: Water plays a crucial role in exercise, especially for thermoregulation. Dehydration not only affects performance, but also causes serious complications, even death, if not managed properly. Water is the largest component of the body, representing from 45% to 70% of a person’s body. Muscle tissue is approximately 75% water, whereas fat tissue is about 20% water. Water affects athletic performance more than any other nutrient. Most athletes replace only about two-thirds of the water they sweat off during exercise. A systematic approach to water replacement is necessary because thirst is not a reliable indicator of fluid need for athletes who are practicing intensely in hot environmental conditions. Under normal environmental conditions, fluid balance is achieved by regulation of fluid intake through changes in thirst sensations and regulation of loss by the kidneys. An individual’s water requirement is the volume needed to replace urine loss, insensible loss from skin and lungs, and loss in feces. The Adequate Intake for water as set by the Food and Nutrition Board for total water intake for young men (ages 19 to 30 years) is 3.7 L/day. The Adequate Intake for water as set by the Food and Nutrition Board for total water intake for young women (ages 19 to 30 years) is 2.7 L/day. It is estimated that fluids (drinking water and beverages) provide about 3.0 L (101 fluid ounces; approximately 13 cups) and 2.2 L (74 fluid ounces; approximately 9 cups) per day for young men and women, respectively.
