Formative evaluation is a periodic reevaluation based on midtests administered during training, usually at regular intervals. Formative evaluation enables monitoring of an athlete's progress and adjustment of the training program for the athlete's individual needs. Formative evaluation also allows evaluation of different training methods and collection of normative data. Regular modification of the training program based on formative evaluation keeps the training program fresh and interesting and helps avoid physical and mental staleness.
summary: This chunk describes the purpose and benefits of pretests, midtests, and formative evaluation in athletic training.

content: Posttests are administered after the training period to determine the success of the training program in achieving the training objectives. Test results are only useful if the test actually measures what it is supposed to measure (validity) and if the measurement is repeatable (reliability).
summary: This chunk explains the role of posttests in evaluating training success and emphasizes the importance of test validity and reliability.

content: Validity refers to the degree to which a test or test item measures what it is supposed to measure and is the most important characteristic of testing. For tests of physical properties such as height and weight, validity is easy to establish. Close correspondence between the readings on a spring scale and the readings on a calibrated balance scale indicates validity of weighing with the spring scale. The validity of tests of basic sport abilities is more difficult to establish.
summary: This chunk discusses the concept of validity in testing, differentiating between easy-to-establish validity for physical properties and the challenges of establishing validity for sport-specific abilities.

content: Construct validity is the ability of a test to represent the underlying construct (the theory developed to organize and explain some aspects of existing knowledge and observations). Construct validity refers to overall validity, or the extent to which the test actually measures what it was designed to measure. Face validity is the appearance to the athlete and other casual observers that the test measures what it is purported to measure. If a test or test item has face validity, the athlete is more likely to respond to it positively. The assessment of face validity is generally informal and nonquantitative.
summary: This chunk defines construct validity and face validity, highlighting their importance in test design and athlete perception.

content: For tests of basic athletic abilities, however, face validity is desirable based on the assumption that anyone taking a test of physical ability wants to do well and is thus motivated by a test that appears to measure a relevant capability. Content validity is the assessment by experts that the testing covers all relevant subtopics or component abilities in appropriate proportions. The test developer should list the ability components to be assessed and make sure they are all represented on the test. The proportion of the total score attributable to a particular component ability should be proportional to the importance of that component to total performance. Content validity relates to actual validity while face validity relates to the appearance of validity to nonexperts.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the importance of face validity and content validity for tests of basic athletic abilities.

content: Criterion-referenced validity is the extent to which test scores are associated with some other measure of the same ability. There are three types of criterion-referenced validity: concurrent, predictive, and discriminant. Concurrent validity is the extent to which test scores are associated with those of other accepted tests that measure the same ability. A Pearson product moment correlation coefficient based on the scores on a new body fat assessment device and those from underwater weighing provides a measure of the concurrent validity of the new test. Convergent validity is evidenced by high positive correlation between results of the test being assessed and those of the recognized measure of the construct (the “gold standard”). A test may be preferable to the “gold standard” if it exhibits convergent validity with the standard but is less demanding in terms of time, equipment, expense, or expertise.
summary: This chunk defines criterion-referenced validity and its three types: concurrent, predictive, and discriminant.

content: Predictive validity is the extent to which the test score corresponds with future behavior or performance. One could calculate the statistical correlation between the overall score on a battery of tests used to assess potential for basketball and a measurement of actual basketball performance as indicated by a composite of such quantities as points scored, rebounds, assists, blocked shots, forced turnovers, and steals. Discriminant validity is the ability of a test to distinguish between two different constructs and is evidenced by a low correlation between the results of the test and those of tests of a different construct. It is best if tests in a battery measure relatively independent ability components (e.g., flexibility, speed, aerobic endurance). Good discriminant validity of tests in a battery avoids unnecessary expenditures of time, energy, and resources in administering tests that correlate very highly with each other.
summary: This chunk explains predictive and discriminant validity, emphasizing their importance in test selection and battery design.

content: Reliability is a measure of the degree of consistency or repeatability of a test. If an athlete whose ability does not change is measured two times with a perfectly reliable test, the same score is obtained both times. On an unreliable test, an individual could obtain a high score on one day and a low score on another. A test must be reliable to be valid, because highly variable results have little meaning. The most obvious way to determine the reliability of a test is to administer the same test twice to the same group of athletes. Statistical correlation of the scores from the two administrations provides a measure of test-retest reliability. Any difference between the two sets of scores represents measurement error, which can arise from any of the following factors: Intrasubject (within subjects) variability, Lack of interrater (between raters) reliability or agreement, Intrarater (within raters) variability, Failure of the test itself to provide consistent results.
summary: This chunk defines reliability, its importance for test validity, and methods for assessing it.

content: On an unreliable test, an individual could obtain a high score on one day and a low score on another. A test must be reliable to be valid, because highly variable results have little meaning. The most obvious way to determine the reliability of a test is to administer the same test twice to the same group of athletes. Statistical correlation of the scores from the two administrations provides a measure of test-retest reliability.
summary: Reliability refers to the consistency of test results, which is essential for validity.

content: Any difference between the two sets of scores represents measurement error, which can arise from any of the following factors: Intrasubject (within subjects) variability, Lack of interrater (between raters) reliability or agreement, Intrarater (within raters) variability, Failure of the test itself to provide consistent results.
summary: Measurement error can stem from various sources, including individual variability, rater inconsistency, and test limitations.

content: Intrasubject variability is a lack of consistent performance by the person tested. Interrater reliability, also referred to as objectivity or interrater agreement, is the degree to which different raters agree. A clearly defined scoring system and competent scorers who are trained and experienced with the test are essential to enhance interrater reliability.
summary: Intrasubject variability refers to inconsistent performance by individuals, while interrater reliability assesses agreement among different raters.

content: Even a test that appears simple, such as timing a 40-yard (37 m) dash with a stopwatch, can exhibit both random and systematic error if the timer is not trained and experienced. Sprint times obtained using handheld stopwatches are typically shorter than those obtained using automatic timers because raters using stopwatches exhibit  reaction-time delay can occur when pressing the start button in response to the gun.
summary: Even seemingly straightforward tests can be susceptible to errors due to human factors and equipment limitations.

content: Different scorers administering tests to different subgroups of athletes can lead to interrater reliability issues. To accurately measure improvement, the same scorer should test a group at the beginning and end of a training period. Interrater differences can arise from variations in calibrating testing devices, preparing athletes, and running the test.
summary: Consistent scoring by the same individual is crucial for accurate measurement of progress.

content: Different testers may motivate athletes to varying degrees based on factors like personality, status, physical appearance, demeanor, and sex. A common scenario increasing interrater variability is when the coach tests some athletes while an assistant tests others.
summary: Rater bias and differences in testing procedures can contribute to interrater variability.

content: Intrarater variability is the lack of consistent scores by a given tester. A coach eager to see improvement may unintentionally be more lenient on a posttest than on a pretest. Other causes of intrarater variability include inadequate training, inattentiveness, lack of concentration, or failure to follow standardized procedures.
summary: Intrarater variability refers to inconsistent scoring by the same individual, which can be influenced by various factors.

content: Accurate and consistent athletic testing should be a priority for all strength and conditioning professionals. Sometimes, the test itself might fail to provide consistent results due to a technique the athlete has not mastered. More technique-intensive tests generally exhibit greater variability in results and require more pretest practice.
summary: Accurate testing is essential for strength and conditioning professionals, and technique-intensive tests may require additional considerations.

content: For a measure to be valid, it must be reliable. A reliable test may not be valid because it may not measure what it is supposed to measure. The 60 m (66-yard) dash and the 1.5-mile (2.4 km) run are reliable field tests, but only the 1.5-mile run is considered a valid field test for cardiovascular fitness.
summary: Reliability is a prerequisite for validity, but a reliable test may not accurately measure the intended construct.

content: A test can be highly reliable for one group but only moderately reliable for another group due to differences in physical or emotional maturity and skill level. When evaluating tests for high levels of validity and reliability, the strength and conditioning professional must rely on their knowledge base and practical experience in the sport.
summary: Test reliability can vary across different groups, and expertise is crucial for selecting appropriate tests.

content: The strength and conditioning professional must consider sport specificity, athlete experience, training status, age, sex, and environmental factors when selecting tests. A valid test must emulate the energy requirements of the sport for which ability is being assessed. The strength and conditioning professional should have a thorough understanding of the three basic energy systems (phosphagen, glycolytic, and oxidative) and their interrelationships.
summary: Test selection should be guided by various factors, including sport specificity and athlete characteristics, and a deep understanding of energy systems is essential.

content: In choosing an appropriate test for running ability in basketball, the strength and conditioning professional must understand that basketball is predominantly an anaerobic running sport. All else being equal, the more similar the test is to an important movement in the sport, the better.
summary: Test selection should align with the energy demands and key movements of the specific sport.

content: Linemen need to push strength to move linemen out of the way and 5- to 15-yard (5 to 14 m) sprint speed to reach the opposing quarterback. Wide receivers depend less on pushing strength but must be able to sprint 30 to 100 yards (27-91 m) quickly. The bench press and 10-yard (9 m) sprint test would be more relevant to the lineman. Sprint tests of 30 to 100 yards (27-91 m) would be more relevant to the wide receiver.
summary: Different positions within a sport may require different athletic qualities, influencing test selection.

content: For a test to be valid, it must emulate the energy requirements and important movements of the sport for which ability is being tested. For a well-trained, experienced athlete, a technique-intensive test may be appropriate because it can be very sport specific, and one can assume that poor technique will not impair performance of the test.
summary: Test validity requires alignment with the sport's demands, and experience levels can influence test appropriateness.

content: The number of one-leg hops needed to travel 27 yards (25 m) may represent a valid and reliable test of plyometric strength for an experienced long jumper but not for a novice. It would not be fair, for example, to ask a baseball player to perform a 3-mile (4.8 km) run test a week before the beginning of fall practice, because the player has probably been doing interval training and relatively short runs.
summary: Test selection should consider athlete experience and training history.

content: For a test to be valid, it must emulate the energy requirements and important movements of the sport for which ability is being tested. Sprint tests of 30 to 100 yards (27-91 m) would be more relevant to the wide receiver.  The number of one-leg hops needed to travel 27 yards (25 m) may represent a valid and reliable test of plyometric strength for an experienced long jumper but not for a novice.  It would not be fair, for example, to ask a baseball player to perform a 3-mile (4.8 km) run test a week before the beginning of fall practice, because the player has probably been doing interval training and relatively short runs.
summary: A valid test should mimic the sport's demands. Examples include sprint tests for wide receivers and plyometric tests for long jumpers, avoiding mismatched tests like a 3-mile run for a baseball player.

content: A strength test using free weight equipment may not be fair to an athlete who has been training exclusively with isokinetic equipment. A lower body strength test using the parallel squat would not be a fair test for an athlete who has trained using the leg press exclusively.
summary: Strength tests should consider an athlete's training equipment, as familiarity with specific equipment influences performance.

content: Both age and sex can affect the validity and reliability of a test. The 1.5-mile (2.4 km) run may be a valid and reliable field test of aerobic power for college-aged men and women. The 1.5-mile (2.4 km) run may not be appropriate for preadolescents because of their probable lack of experience and interest in sustained running. A test of the maximum number of chin-ups that can be performed may be a valid test of elbow flexion muscular endurance for male wrestlers, but it is not valid for females because many females lack sufficient upper body strength to complete even one chin-up. A test of maximum pulldown repetitions with a percentage of body weight would be more appropriate for females.
summary: Test selection should consider age, sex, and experience, as appropriate tests vary based on these factors.

content: High ambient temperature, especially in combination with high humidity, can impair endurance exercise performance, pose health risks, and lower the validity of an aerobic endurance exercise test. Aerobic endurance performance may be impaired when the temperature approaches 80 °F, especially if the humidity exceeds 50%. The effects of temperature and humidity on aerobic endurance performance can create problems for comparing the results of tests administered at different times of year, on different days, and even at different times of day. The maximal oxygen uptake of an athlete impaired by the heat is underestimated by the 1.5-mile (2.4 km) run test. Run times can also be impaired by cold temperatures. Outdoor aerobic endurance tests may be inappropriate at locations characterized by wide fluctuations in temperature.
summary: Environmental factors like temperature and humidity significantly impact test results and should be standardized for accurate comparisons.

content: Aerobic endurance tests can be administered on an indoor track, if available, or with a treadmill or stationary cycle.
summary: Indoor alternatives exist for aerobic endurance tests when outdoor conditions are unsuitable.

content: Altitude can also impair performance on aerobic endurance tests, although not on tests of strength and power. Norms on aerobic endurance tests should be adjusted when testing at altitudes exceeding 1,900 feet (580 m). Up to about 9,000 feet (2,740 m), maximal oxygen uptake declines by approximately 5% for each 3,000 feet (910 m) of elevation. At even higher altitudes, maximal oxygen uptake declines more sharply. Athletes who arrive at a relatively high altitude after living near sea level for an extended period of time should be given at least 10 days to acclimatize before undergoing aerobic endurance tests.
summary: Altitude affects aerobic endurance performance, requiring adjusted norms and acclimatization periods for accurate testing.

content: Athletes’ experience, training status, age, and sex can affect test performance, so these factors should be considered in test selection. Environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, and altitude can also influence test performance, so testers should try to standardize environmental conditions as much as possible.
summary: Test selection should consider athlete characteristics and environmental factors to ensure accurate and fair results.

content: To achieve accurate test results, tests must be administered safely, correctly, and in an organized manner. Staff should ensure the health and safety of athletes, testers should be carefully selected and trained, tests should be well organized and administered efficiently, and athletes should be properly prepared and instructed.
summary: Safe, organized, and well-instructed testing procedures are crucial for accurate results.

content: Even though all athletes should be medically cleared before being permitted to physically train and compete, the strength and conditioning professional must be aware of testing conditions that can threaten the health of athletes and be observant of signs and symptoms of health problems that warrant exclusion from testing. The strength and conditioning professional must remain attentive to the health status of athletes, especially before, during, and after maximal exertions that occur during training, testing, and competition.
summary: Strength and conditioning professionals must prioritize athlete health and safety during testing, recognizing potential risks and monitoring for health concerns.

content: Strenuous exercise, such as maximal runs or 1-repetition maximum (1RM) tests, can uncover or worsen existing heart problems, such as impaired blood flow to the heart muscle and irregular heartbeats. Standard medical screening cannot always reveal hidden heart problems, which occasionally result in fatality among young athletes. Heat injury is also a risk during heavy physical exertion in hot environments, especially when humidity is high. Athletes should wear light clothing in warm weather and should be encouraged to drink ample water in the hours before heavy physical exertion in the heat.
summary: Testing protocols should consider potential cardiovascular risks and heat injury, emphasizing athlete safety and preparedness.

content: Musculoskeletal injuries can also be a problem. If symptoms are ignored, recovery can be greatly delayed. Medical referral may be warranted for an athlete who persistently has any of the following symptoms: chest pressure, pain, or discomfort; listlessness; light-headedness; dizziness; confusion; headache; deeply reddened or cold and clammy skin; irregular pulse; bone or joint pain; blurred vision; nausea; or shortness of breath, rapid pulse, or weakness either not commensurate with the level of exertion or unresponsive to rest. Such symptoms can occur long after exercise is terminated. Even symptoms that occur only once, if severe (such as loss of consciousness), call for immediate medical attention.
summary: Athletes should be monitored for musculoskeletal and other symptoms, with prompt medical attention sought for concerning signs.

content: When aerobic endurance exercise tests are being administered in a hot environment, caution must be observed to protect both the health and safety of the athlete and the validity of the test. Test administrators should be well trained and should have a thorough understanding of all testing procedures and protocols.
summary: Testing in hot environments requires extra precautions to ensure athlete safety and test validity, with well-trained administrators crucial.

content: Medical referral may be warranted for an athlete who persistently has any of the following symptoms: chest pressure, pain, or discomfort; listlessness; light-headedness; dizziness; confusion; headache; deeply reddened or cold and clammy skin; irregular pulse; bone or joint pain; blurred vision; nausea; or shortness of breath, rapid pulse, or weakness either not commensurate with the level of exertion or unresponsive to rest. Such symptoms can occur long after exercise is terminated. Even symptoms that occur only once, if severe (such as loss of consciousness), call for immediate medical attention.
summary: This chunk outlines medical symptoms that warrant attention, including those occurring during or after exercise, emphasizing the need for immediate medical care for severe symptoms.

content: When aerobic endurance exercise tests are being administered in a hot environment, caution must be observed to protect both the health and safety of the athlete and the validity of the test. Test administrators should be well trained and should have a thorough understanding of all testing procedures and protocols. The testing supervisor should make sure that all novice personnel perform and score all tests correctly, as in timing sprint speed with a stopwatch or determining a 1RM back squat. It is essential that all testers have sufficient practice so that the scores they obtain correlate closely with those produced by experienced and reliable personnel. The testers should be trained to explain and administer the tests as consistently as possible.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of proper training and procedures for administering aerobic endurance tests, especially in hot environments, to ensure both athlete safety and test validity.

content: Test reliability is impaired, for example, if one test administrator provides considerable verbal encouragement to a group of athletes while another tester provides no verbal encouragement to another group. Administrators should have a checklist of materials needed for testing and written test protocols to refer to if questions arise during the testing process. Scoring forms should be developed before the testing session and should have space for all test results and comments. This allows test time to be used more efficiently and reduces the incidence of recording errors. A well-organized testing session in which athletes are aware of testing purpose and procedures usually enhances the reliability of test measures.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of standardized procedures, training, and organization during testing to ensure reliable and consistent results.

content: Reliable measures obtained from valid tests are a great asset in assessing fitness levels and evaluating changes over a period of time. Test planning must address issues such as whether athletes will be tested all at once or in groups and whether the same person will administer a given test to all athletes. It is preferable for the same person to administer a given test to all athletes if time and schedules permit because it eliminates the issue of interrater reliability. If it is not feasible for the same person to administer a given test to all athletes, the test supervisor can allow simple, well-defined tests (such as counting correct chin-ups) to be administered by different testers and tests requiring schooled judgment (as of proper form in the squat) to be scored by the most skilled personnel.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of test planning, including considerations for consistency in test administration and the selection of appropriate testers based on test complexity.

content: As a rule, each tester should administer only one test at a time, especially when the tests require complex judgments. It is permissible to have one tester alternate between two testing stations to avoid wasting time as athletes get ready. During the weeks prior to the test, athletes should engage in enough training to establish a baseline of fitness in the activity being tested. Avoid testing under extreme combinations of heat and humidity. Table 11.1 lists the combinations of temperature and humidity at which heat injury risk is present. Using temperature limits at least 5 °F (3 °C) below those listed is recommended, especially on sunny days, to provide a safety margin and enable better test performance. On days when the temperature is expected to or has exceeded the recommended limits, indoor facilities should be used if available, or testing should be conducted during morning or early evening hours when temperatures are acceptable.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on test administration logistics, including timing, tester roles, pre-test training, and considerations for testing in hot environments.

content: The athletes, especially those coming from cool climates, should be acclimatized to the heat and humidity for at least one week prior to testing. Start with short workouts and progress to workouts of longer duration. Athletes should make sure they are well hydrated in the 24-hour period preceding aerobic endurance testing in the heat. A good indication of adequate hydration is a plentiful volume of clear urine. Fluid intake should continue until a half hour before testing, at which time athletes should consume about 500 ml (17 fluid ounces). Salt tablets should generally be avoided. Athletes should be encouraged to drink during exercise in the heat, ideally 150 to 250 ml (5-9 fluid ounces) every 15 minutes. Plain water is most appropriate for exercise up to 1 hour in duration, but glucose-electrolyte sport drinks are preferable for longer exercise.
summary: This chunk details the importance of athlete acclimatization, hydration strategies, and fluid intake recommendations for aerobic endurance testing in hot environments.

content: Athletes should wear a light-colored, loose-fitting tank top and shorts, preferably of a mesh material. Male athletes may be allowed to go shirtless. Heart rate may be monitored to detect extreme reactions to the heat. Be attentive to possible symptoms of heat-stroke or heat exhaustion: cramps, nausea, dizziness, difficulty in walking or standing, faintness, garbled speech, lack of sweat, red or ashen skin, and goose bumps.
summary: This chunk provides recommendations for athlete attire and monitoring for potential heat-related issues during testing.

content: Be aware of the symptoms of hyponatremia or water intoxication, a potentially fatal condition in which excess water intake reduces blood sodium to dangerously low levels. Symptoms may include extremely dilute urine in combination with bloated skin, altered consciousness, or loss of consciousness, with no increase in body temperature. A victim of hyponatremia should never be given fluid and should be treated by a physician. Athletes should be encouraged to eat foods high in magnesium and potassium, such as cantaloupe, watermelon, tomatoes, carrots, and cucumbers, which are preferable to supplements.
summary: This chunk warns about the dangers of hyponatremia, its symptoms, and the importance of proper dietary intake for electrolyte balance.

content: Be attentive to possible symptoms of heat-stroke or heat exhaustion: cramps, nausea, dizziness, difficulty in walking or standing, faintness, garbled speech, lack of sweat, red or ashen skin, and goose bumps. Be aware of the symptoms of hyponatremia or water intoxication, a potentially fatal condition in which excess water intake reduces blood sodium to dangerously low levels. Symptoms may include extremely dilute urine in combination with bloated skin, altered consciousness, or loss of consciousness, with no increase in body temperature. A victim of hyponatremia should never be given fluid and should be treated by a physician.
summary: This chunk outlines potential health risks during athletic testing, including heat-stroke, heat exhaustion, and hyponatremia, and provides guidance on recognizing and responding to these conditions.

content: Athletes should be encouraged to eat foods high in magnesium and potassium, such as cantaloupe, watermelon, tomatoes, carrots, and cucumbers, which are preferable to supplements. Proficient medical coverage should be readily available so that an athlete encountering a test-related health problem can be very rapidly treated or evacuated (or both).
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of proper nutrition and readily available medical support for athletes during testing.

content: Nonfatiguing tests include height, weight, flexibility, skinfold and girth measurements, and vertical jump. Agility tests include the T-test and pro agility test. Maximum power and strength tests include the 1RM power clean and 1RM bench press. Sprint tests include the 40-yard [37 m] sprint. Local muscular endurance tests include the partial curl-up test.
summary: This chunk categorizes different types of athletic tests based on their nature: nonfatiguing, agility, power/strength, sprint, and local muscular endurance.

content: Fatiguing anaerobic capacity tests include the 400 m [437-yard] run and the 300-yard [275 m] shuttle. Aerobic capacity tests include the 1.5-mile [2.4 km] run and the 12-minute run. Aerobic tests should be performed last, after an hour-long rest period, if performed on the same day as other tests.
summary: This chunk describes tests that assess anaerobic and aerobic capacity, noting the importance of scheduling aerobic tests last to avoid fatigue interference.

content: The date, time, and purpose of a test battery should be announced in advance to allow athletes to prepare physically and mentally. Athletes should be familiar with test content and procedures to maximize test reliability. A short, supervised pretest practice session one to three days before the test, in which athletes exert themselves at somewhat less than full intensity, is often beneficial.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of advance notice, familiarity with test procedures, and a pretest practice session to ensure reliable test results.

content: Instructions should cover the purpose of the test, how it is to be performed, the amount of warm-up recommended, the number of practice attempts allowed, the number of trials, test scoring, criteria for disallowing attempts, and recommendations for maximizing performance. Viewing a video of the test being performed properly can help athletes receive consistent instruction before performing a test. The test administrator or a competent assistant should demonstrate proper test performance when possible.
summary: This chunk details the essential components of clear and comprehensive test instructions, including demonstration and visual aids.

content: Athletes should be given opportunities to ask questions before and after the demonstration. The test administrator should anticipate questions and have answers prepared. The test administrator should motivate all athletes equally rather than giving special encouragement to only some.
summary: This chunk focuses on effective communication and motivation strategies employed by the test administrator.

content: Athletes should be told their test scores immediately after each trial to motivate them to perform better on subsequent trials. The tester must focus on only one test at a time. Duplicate test setups may be employed to make efficient use of testing time when time is limited and there is a large group of athletes. A tester can administer up to two nonfatiguing tests in sequence to an athlete as long as test reliability can be maintained.
summary: This chunk outlines best practices for test administration, including immediate feedback, single-test focus, efficient setup, and sequential administration of nonfatiguing tests.

content: When multiple trials of a test or a battery of tests are performed, allow complete recovery between trials. There should be at least 2 to 3 minutes of rest between attempts that are not close to the athlete’s maximum and 3 to 5 minutes between attempts that are close to the maximum, as judged by the relative difficulty of the previous trial or testing set. When administering a test battery, tests should be separated by at least 5 minutes to prevent the effects of fatigue from confounding test results.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of adequate rest periods between test attempts and across different tests to prevent fatigue interference.

content: There should be at least 2 to 3 minutes of rest between attempts that are not close to the athlete’s maximum and 3 to 5 minutes between attempts that are close to the maximum, as judged by the relative difficulty of the previous trial or testing set. When administering a test battery, tests should be separated by at least 5 minutes to prevent the effects of fatigue from confounding test results.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of adequate rest periods between test attempts and across different tests to prevent the effects of fatigue from confounding test results.

content: Knowledge of exercise science can help determine the proper order of tests and the duration of rest periods between tests to ensure test reliability. A test that maximally taxes the phosphagen energy system requires 3 to 5 minutes of rest for complete recovery. A maximal test of the lactic acid energy system requires at least 1 hour for complete recovery. Tests requiring high-skill movements, such as reaction and coordination tests, should be administered before tests that are likely to produce fatigue and confound the results of subsequent tests.
summary: This chunk highlights the role of exercise science knowledge in test sequencing and rest periods, emphasizing the recovery time needed for different energy systems and the importance of test order.

content: A logical sequence for administering tests is to start with tests requiring high-skill movements, followed by tests that maximally tax the phosphagen energy system, and then tests that maximally tax the lactic acid energy system. Reliability improves with a pretest warm-up. An appropriately organized warm-up consists of a general warm-up followed by a specific warm-up. Both types of warm-ups include body movements similar to those involved in the test. An organized, instructor-led general warm-up ensures uniformity.
summary: This chunk provides a recommended sequence for administering tests, emphasizing the importance of a structured warm-up for improved reliability.

content: It is acceptable to allow two to three activity-specific warm-up trials, depending on the test protocol, and have subsequent trials actually count toward the score.
summary: This chunk clarifies the acceptable practice of incorporating warm-up trials into the official test score.

content: Reliability improves with a pretest warm-up. An appropriately organized warm-up consists of a general warm-up followed by a specific warm-up. Both types of warm-ups include body movements similar to those involved in the test. General and specific warm-ups performed before a test can increase the test’s reliability.
summary: Warm-ups, consisting of general and specific components, enhance test reliability.

content: It is acceptable to allow two to three activity-specific warm-up trials, depending on the test protocol, and have subsequent trials actually count toward the score. Depending on the test protocol, the score can be the best or the average of the post-warm-up trials. Administer a supervised cool-down period to athletes following tests that dramatically increase heart rate and at the completion of the test battery. For example, after the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle, the athlete should not sit or lie down; low-intensity movement and light stretching enhance the recovery process.
summary: Warm-up trials can contribute to the score, and a cool-down period is recommended after strenuous tests.

content: Tests and measurements can be used to assess athletic talent, identify physical abilities in need of improvement, provide reference values to evaluate the effectiveness of a training program, and set realistic training goals. To evaluate test quality, testers must understand and consider validity and reliability. Test selection involves consideration of the physiological energy systems required by the sport; movement specificity; and the athletes’ experience, training status, age, and sex. Testers must also consider environmental factors such as temperature, humidity, and altitude before administering tests.
summary: Tests and measurements are valuable tools for assessing athletic performance, and careful selection and administration are crucial.

content: Strength and conditioning professionals must always remain conscious of potential health risks during testing and attentive to signs and symptoms of possible health problems that require medical referral. Testers must be carefully selected and well trained, and the testing session must be well planned and organized using the appropriate testing sequence. Consistent and effective preparation of athletes for testing is essential. The strength and conditioning professional, often referred to as the tester in this chapter, can effectively choose and utilize tests and measurements to make training program decisions that help athletes achieve their goals and maximize their potential.
summary: Safety, proper training, and meticulous planning are essential for effective athletic testing.

content: This chapter covers these basic aspects of testing performance-related parameters and provides comprehensive age- and sport-specific descriptive and normative data for selected tests. Athleticism incorporates many physical abilities, some of which are much more amenable to training than others. Such abilities may be called components of athletic performance, that is, the ability to respond effectively to various physical challenges. This section focuses on how each component can be tested and highlights specific relevant issues.
summary: This chapter provides a foundation for understanding and applying athletic testing principles.

content: Muscular strength is related to the force a muscle or muscle group can exert in one maximal effort, and it can be quantified by the maximum weight that can be lifted once (the 1-repetition maximum [1RM]) in exercises such as the bench press or back squat, the maximum force exerted isometrically (against an immovable object) as measured with a transducer, or the maximum force that can be exerted at a particular isokinetic speed (2, 12, 16, 19, 21, 27, 28, 29, 39, 41, 45, 52, 57, 60, 65, 66, 69). Because 1RM tests do not require expensive equipment and reflect the kind of dynamic ability necessary in sport, they are the maximal strength tests of choice for most strength and conditioning professionals.
summary: Muscular strength can be measured in various ways, with 1RM tests being common in strength and conditioning.

content: In general, 1RM tests are administered after the athlete has warmed up by performing a few sets of the test exercise with submaximal loads, beginning with a relatively light one. The first attempt is usually with about 50% of the athlete’s estimated 1RM weight. After the athlete has rested enough to feel recovered from the previous attempt (1-5 minutes, depending on the difficulty of the attempt), the strength and conditioning professional increases the weight somewhat, based on the ease with which the previous trial was performed. A skilled strength and conditioning professional should, within three to five attempts following warm-up, be able to find the athlete’s 1RM load to within a few percentage points of the true value.
summary: 1RM tests are typically conducted after a warm-up, gradually increasing weight until the athlete reaches their maximum.

content: Maximal strength tests usually involve relatively low movement speeds and therefore reflect low-speed muscular strength. High-speed muscular strength or maximal anaerobic muscular power (or anaerobic power) is related to the ability of muscle tissue to exert high force while contracting at a high speed. Tests of such strength and power are of very short duration, are performed at speeds that are much faster than those used in maximal strength tests. High-speed maximal muscular power tests are often called (maximal) anaerobic power tests. Scores on high-speed muscular strength tests include the 1RM of explosive exercises (e.g., the power clean, snatch, or push jerk), the height of a vertical jump, and the time to sprint up a staircase.
summary: Maximal strength tests assess low-speed strength, while high-speed tests measure anaerobic power.

content: As explosive exercise tests take about 1 second while low-speed maximal strength tests generally require 2 to 4 seconds to complete, ATP stored in the active muscle(s) is the primary energy source for both types of tests. Most maximal muscular strength tests use relatively slow movement speeds and therefore reflect low-speed strength. Conversely, assessment of high-speed muscular strength can involve measuring the 1RM of explosive resistance training exercises, the height of a vertical jump, or the time to sprint up a staircase. ATP is the primary energy source for both low-speed and high-speed muscular strength tests. Power output reflects both force and velocity. The height of a jump is a function of the velocity at which the athlete leaves the ground.
summary: Both low-speed and high-speed strength tests primarily utilize ATP, and power output is a combination of force and velocity.

content: An athlete may not improve in jump height after gaining body weight during a resistance training cycle, making it appear that power output is unchanged.
summary: Body weight gain may not always translate to improved jump height, highlighting the complexity of power output.

content: Power output reflects both force and velocity. The height of a jump is a function of the velocity at which the athlete leaves the ground.
summary: Power output is determined by both force and velocity, and jump height is directly related to the takeoff velocity.

content: An athlete may not improve in jump height after gaining body weight during a resistance training cycle, making it appear that power output is unchanged. However, because the athlete is heavier and propels the body to the same height, indicating the same takeoff velocity, an increase in power output is evident. This applies to any test in which body weight is manipulated (e.g., running up stairs). Moving a heavier body at the same speed requires a higher power output.
summary: While body weight gain may not appear to affect jump height, it actually increases power output due to the increased force required to achieve the same jump height.

content: Conversely, assessment of high-speed muscular strength can involve measuring the 1RM of explosive resistance training exercises, the height of a vertical jump, or the time to sprint up a staircase.
summary: High-speed muscular strength can be assessed using various methods, including 1RM tests, vertical jump height, and staircase sprint times.

content: ATP is the primary energy source for both low-speed and high-speed muscular strength tests.
summary: ATP is the main energy source used during both low-speed and high-speed muscular strength tests.

content: An alternative class of anaerobic power tests involves the use of a cycle ergometer. This type of test can be advantageous for the strength and conditioning professional in some injury situations in which running is restricted or when the athlete participates in a non–body weight support sport such as rowing or cycling.
summary: Cycle ergometer tests provide an alternative method for assessing anaerobic power, particularly beneficial for athletes with injuries or those participating in non-bodyweight sports.

content: The most commonly used test of this type is the Wingate anaerobic test. A field test protocol involves use of a cycle ergometer with mechanical means of adjusting resistance and measuring pedal revolutions and rate (rpm). In a laboratory setting, an electronically instrumented ergometer can simplify parameter measurement and improve accuracy.
summary: The Wingate anaerobic test is a common cycle ergometer-based test for assessing anaerobic power, with field and laboratory protocols available.

content: Typical protocols involve a basic warm-up followed by a 30-second test interval (9). In this test, resistance is applied quickly after the individual reaches a near-maximal pedaling rate (typically 90 to 110 rpm). The resistance applied is proportional to body weight; the percentage is greater for trained athletes than for individuals with less training. Work performed is determined from the resistance value and number of pedal revolutions. Power is generally calculated as work divided by time for each 5-second time interval during the 30-second test. Parameters typically calculated include peak power, average power, and a fatigue index such as a ratio of maximum to minimum interval power.
summary: The Wingate anaerobic test protocol involves a warm-up, followed by a 30-second high-resistance cycling effort, with power and fatigue indices calculated from the data.

content: Norms for cycle ergometer tests are available (32). Anaerobic capacity is the maximal rate of energy production by the combined phosphagen and lactic acid energy systems for moderate-duration activities. It is typically quantified as the maximal power output during muscular activity between 30 and 90 seconds using a variety of tests for the upper and lower body (2, 26, 27, 38, 41, 45, 57, 65, 67), as opposed to maximal anaerobic power tests, which last no longer than a few seconds.
summary: Anaerobic capacity refers to the maximum power output during 30-90 second activities, measured using various tests, distinct from maximal anaerobic power tests lasting only a few seconds.

content: Local muscular endurance is the ability of certain muscles or muscle groups to perform repeated contractions against a submaximal resistance (2, 10, 11, 16). A test of local muscular endurance should be performed in a continuous manner for several seconds to several minutes without the advantage of rest periods or extraneous body movements. Examples include performing a maximal number of repetitions in the chin-up, parallel bar dip, or push-up exercises or a resistance training exercise using a fixed load (e.g., a percentage of an athlete’s 1RM or body weight) (2, 28, 41, 45).
summary: Local muscular endurance is the ability of muscles to repeatedly contract against a submaximal resistance for extended periods, tested through exercises like chin-ups, dips, push-ups, or resistance training with a fixed load.

content: Aerobic capacity, also called aerobic power, is the maximum rate at which an athlete can produce energy through oxidation of energy resources (carbohydrates, fats, and proteins) and is usually expressed as a volume of oxygen consumed per kg of body weight per minute (i.e., ml · kg–1 · min–1) (10). Few strength and conditioning professionals have the equipment to measure oxygen consumption directly, so aerobic capacity is generally estimated by performance in aerobic endurance Activities such as running 1 mile (1.6 km) or more are examples of agility.
summary: Aerobic capacity, the maximum rate of energy production through oxygen, is often estimated by performance in endurance activities like running.

content: Agility is the ability to stop, start, and change the direction of the body or body parts rapidly and in a controlled manner. Tests of agility require proper footwear and a nonslip surface.
summary: Agility involves the ability to quickly and controllably change direction, requiring proper footwear and a safe surface for testing.

content: Electronic timing devices are becoming more accessible to strength and conditioning professionals due to increased ease of use and lower prices. Many tests of agility and speed are administered using hand timing with a stopwatch. Stopwatch-measured sprint times are up to 0.24 seconds faster than electronically measured times because of the tester’s reaction-time delay in pressing the stopwatch button at the gun and tendency to anticipate and press the button early as the athlete approaches the finish line.
summary: Electronic timing devices are increasingly used for agility and speed tests, but hand-timed stopwatches can be more accurate due to the tester's reaction time.

content: Speed is movement distance per unit time. Speed is typically quantified as the time taken to cover a fixed distance. Tests of speed are not usually conducted over distances greater than 200 m because longer distances reflect anaerobic or aerobic capacity more than absolute ability to move the body at maximal speed.
summary: Speed is measured as distance covered per unit time, typically assessed over short distances (up to 200m) to avoid confounding factors like anaerobic or aerobic capacity.

content: Flexibility can be defined as the range of motion about a body joint. Typical devices for measuring flexibility include manual and electric goniometers, which measure joint angle, and the sit-and-reach box, which is used to evaluate the combined flexibility of the lower back and hips. Flexibility measurements are more reliable when standardized warm-up and static stretching precede the flexibility assessment.
summary: Flexibility is the range of motion around a joint, measured using tools like goniometers and sit-and-reach boxes. Standardized warm-up and stretching improve measurement reliability.

content: Speed is typically quantified as the time taken to cover a fixed distance. Tests of speed are not usually conducted over distances greater than 200 m because longer distances reflect anaerobic or aerobic capacity more than absolute ability to move the body at maximal speed.
summary: Speed is measured as time to cover a distance, typically up to 200m, as longer distances reflect endurance rather than pure speed.

content: Flexibility can be defined as the range of motion about a body joint. Typical devices for measuring flexibility include manual and electric goniometers, which measure joint angle, and the sit-and-reach box, which is used to evaluate the combined flexibility of the lower back and hips. Flexibility measurements are more reliable when standardized warm-up and static stretching precede the flexibility assessment. During a flexibility test, the athlete should move slowly into the fully stretched position and hold this position. Ballistic stretching should be prohibited during warm-up and cannot be allowed during any flexibility testing.
summary: Flexibility is the range of motion at a joint, measured using tools like goniometers and sit-and-reach boxes. Standardized warm-ups and static stretching improve measurement reliability.

content: Body composition usually refers to the relative proportions by weight of fat and lean tissue. The body composition procedures typically performed by strength and conditioning professionals use the basic two-compartment (fat and lean) model. With a trained and competent tester, the skinfold measurement technique provides the most valid and reliable (r = 0.99) means for assessing body fatness that is generally available to the strength and conditioning professional and is preferable to body circumference methods.
summary: Body composition refers to the ratio of fat to lean tissue. Skinfold measurements are the most reliable method for assessing body fatness.

content: The skinfold method uses a pincher device that measures the thickness of a double layer of finger-pinched skin and subcutaneous fat. A good skinfold measurement device should squeeze the fold of skin and fat with constant pressure regardless of the amount of tissue being measured.
summary: Skinfold measurements assess body fatness by measuring the thickness of a skin fold using a specialized device.

content: Anthropometry, which is the science of measurement applied to the human body, generally includes measurements of height, weight, and selected body girths. Measurement of height requires a flat wall against which the athlete stands, a measuring tape attached or unattached to the wall, and a rectangular object placed concurrently against both the athlete’s head and the wall. Height is usually measured without shoes to the nearest quarter-inch or half-centimeter. The most accurate body mass or body weight measurement is performed with a certified balance scale, which is generally more reliable than a spring scale and should be calibrated on a regular basis. A calibrated electronic scale is an acceptable alternative. Athletes should be weighed while wearing minimal dry clothing (e.g., gym shorts and T-shirt, no shoes). For comparison measurements at a later date, they should dress similarly and be weighed at the same time of day. Athletes should be encouraged to avoid eating salty food the day before weighing and to go to bed normally hydrated.
summary: Anthropometry involves measuring various body dimensions, including height, weight, and girths. Accurate measurements require standardized procedures and equipment.

content: The most reliable girth measurements are usually obtained with the aid of a flexible measuring tape equipped with a spring-loaded attachment at the end that, when pulled out to a specified mark, exerts a fixed amount of tension on the tape—for example, a Gulich tape (65). Girth measurements should be made at the beginning of a training period for comparison with subsequent measurements (2, 28).
summary: Girth measurements, using a specialized tape, are most reliable when taken consistently at the start of a training period.

content: In order to maximize the reliability of tests, it is essential that testing conditions be as similar as possible for all the athletes tested and from test to retest of the same athlete. The temperature and humidity should not differ radically from test to test. For any particular test conducted on the ground, the surface—whether gym floor, grass, rubber, or dirt—should always be the same and should not be wet for one test and dry for another. Maximum strength tests should use the same type of racks with the supports set at the same height for a given athlete. For jumping tests, the type of equipment used should be constant.
summary: Reliable testing requires consistent conditions, including temperature, humidity, surface, equipment, and rack setup.

content: Athletes should never be tested after fatiguing sport activities or workouts. They should arrive for testing normally hydrated and neither glycogen depleted nor full from eating a large meal. It is best to perform tests and retests at roughly the same time of day. Warm-up for the tests should be standardized and should include both a general warm-up such as jogging or light calisthenics and a specific warm-up that involves movements like those required by the test, such as practice of the test at submaximal intensity. Stretching is appropriate for any test requiring flexibility.
summary: Testing protocols should consider athlete preparation, including hydration, meal timing, and standardized warm-up routines.

content: As with any maximal strength test, the athlete first does a specific warm-up of 5 to 10 repetitions with a light-to-moderate load. Usually, at least two heavier warm-up sets of two to five repetitions each are completed before the first actual 1RM attempt. Generally, it is desirable to measure the 1RM within three to five attempts after the warm-up; otherwise fatigue may detract from the final result. A more detailed step-by-step method for the 1RM protocol is shown in figure 15.1 on page 396. Note: Normative data for the 1RM bench press are presented in tables 12.1, 12.2, and 12.3 on pages 275-277.
summary: The 1RM protocol involves a specific warm-up routine, progressing to heavier weights, and aiming to measure the maximum weight lifted within a few attempts to minimize fatigue.

content: Equipment: A barbell, weight plates, and two safety locks; enough total weight to accommodate the maximum load of the strongest athlete; and a variety of plate sizes to allow for 5-pound (2.5 kg) gradations in weight. A sturdy bench press bench with integral bar rack (preferably of adjustable height)
summary: Equipment required for 1RM testing includes a barbell, weight plates, safety locks, a sturdy bench press bench, and a variety of weight plates.

content: Personnel: One spotter, one recorder
summary: Personnel needed for 1RM testing include a spotter for safety and a recorder to track weight and repetitions.

content: A more detailed step-by-step method for the 1RM protocol is shown in figure 15.1 on page 396. Note: Normative data for the 1RM bench press are presented in tables 12.1, 12.2, and 12.3 on pages 275-277. Equipment: A barbell, weight plates, and two safety locks; enough total weight to accommodate the maximum load of the strongest athlete; and a variety of plate sizes to allow for 5-pound (2.5 kg) gradations in weight. A sturdy bench press bench with integral bar rack (preferably of adjustable height). Personnel: One spotter, one recorder. Instruct the athlete in proper technique for the flat barbell bench press as described in chapter 14 (pp. 342-343). The spotter stands at the head end of the bench throughout the test to help in raising the bar on a failed attempt and to help the athlete place the bar back on the rack.
summary: Details on the 1RM bench press protocol, equipment, personnel, and technique instruction.

content: Equipment: A barbell, weight plates, and two safety locks; enough total weight to accommodate the maximum load of the strongest athlete; and a variety of plate sizes to allow for 5-pound (2.5 kg) gradations in weight. A sturdy squat rack with adjustable spotting bars to support the weight of the bar if the athlete is unable to rise (as an alternative, one spotter can be used at each end of the bar) A flat and solid surface to stand on. Personnel: Two spotters, one recorder. Instruct the athlete in proper technique for the back squat as described in chapter 14 (pp. 350-351). Warm-up sets are performed as in the 1RM bench press test. However, the loads lifted are typically heavier than in the 1RM bench press test so the load increments will be greater than those of the 1RM bench press. Refer to figure 15.1 (p. 396) for a 1RM testing protocol. Note: Normative data for the 1RM back squat are presented in tables 12.1, 12.2, and 12.3 on pages 275-277.
summary: Equipment, personnel, and technique instructions for the 1RM back squat test.

content: The power clean exercise is very technique intensive. Two athletes with the same muscular power capacity can differ greatly in their tested 1RM. The power clean test lessens the value of predicting athletic performance. Equipment needed for the power clean test includes an Olympic-style barbell with a revolving sleeve, weight plates, two safety locks, enough total weight to accommodate the maximum load of the strongest athlete, and a variety of plate sizes to allow for 5-pound (2.5 kg) gradations in weight. A lifting platform or designated area set apart from the rest of the facility for safety is needed for the power clean test. One tester/recorder is needed for the power clean test. Athletes should be instructed in proper technique for the power clean as described in chapter 14 (pp. 372-373). Warm-up sets are performed and load increments are selected as in the 1RM bench press test for the power clean. Normative data for the 1RM power clean are presented in tables 12.1, 12.2, and 12.3 on pages 275-277.
summary: Considerations and equipment for the power clean test, emphasizing its technical complexity and limitations in predicting athletic performance.

content: A flat jumping area at least 20 feet (6 m) in length, which can be a gym floor, artificial turf, grass field, or a track is needed for the maximum muscular power test. A tape measure at least 10 feet (3 m) long, duct tape or masking tape, and a permissible alternative of a commercial jumping mat premarked in half-inch (1 cm) increments are needed for the maximum muscular power test. One distance judge and one recorder are needed for the maximum muscular power test. To perform the standing long jump, the athlete places a 2- to 3-foot (0.6-0.9 m) length of tape on the floor to serve as a starting line. The athlete stands with the toes just behind the starting line for the standing long jump. The athlete performs a countermovement and jumps forward as far as possible for the standing long jump. The athlete must land on the feet for the jump to be scored in the standing long jump. A marker is placed at the back edge of the athlete’s rearmost heel, and the tape measure determines the distance between the starting line and the mark for the standing long jump. The best of three trials is recorded to the nearest 0.5 inch or 1 cm for the standing long jump.
summary: Equipment and setup for the standing long jump test, including distance measurement and scoring.

content: To perform the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat, the athlete stands with the toes just behind the starting line. The athlete performs a countermovement and jumps forward as far as possible for the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat. The athlete must land on the feet for the jump to be scored in the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat. The imprinted mat line closest to the back edge of the athlete’s rearmost heel indicates the jump distance for the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat. The best of three trials is recorded to the nearest 0.5 inch or 1 cm for the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat. Normative data for the standing long jump are presented in tables 12.6 and 12.7 on page 279.
summary: Procedure for the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat, including distance measurement and scoring.

content: Equipment needed for the standing long jump includes a smooth wall with a ceiling higher than the highest jumper’s jump height, a flat floor with good traction, chalk of a different color than the wall, and a measuring tape or stick.
summary: Equipment required for the standing long jump test.

content: The athlete must land on the feet for the jump to be scored in the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat. The imprinted mat line closest to the back edge of the athlete’s rearmost heel indicates the jump distance for the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat. The best of three trials is recorded to the nearest 0.5 inch or 1 cm for the standing long jump using a commercial jump mat. Normative data for the standing long jump are presented in tables 12.6 and 12.7 on page 279. Equipment needed for the standing long jump includes a smooth wall with a ceiling higher than the highest jumper’s jump height, a flat floor with good traction, chalk of a different color than the wall, and a measuring tape or stick. A permissible alternative for the standing long jump is a commercial device for vertical jump testing (e.g., Vertec). One tester/recorder is needed for the standing long jump.
summary: This chunk describes the standing long jump test, including scoring, equipment, and normative data.

content: The tester rubs chalk on the fingertips of the athlete’s dominant hand for the standing long jump. The athlete stands with the dominant shoulder about 6 inches (15 cm) from the wall and, with both feet flat on the floor, reaches as high as possible with the dominant hand and makes a chalk mark on the wall for the standing long jump. The athlete then lowers the dominant hand and, without a preparatory or stutter step, performs a countermovement by quickly flexing the knees and hips, moving the trunk forward and downward, and swinging the arms backward (figure 12.1a) during the standing long jump. During the jump, the dominant arm reaches upward while the nondominant arm moves downward relative to the body for the standing long jump. At the highest point in the jump, the athlete places a second chalk mark on the wall with the fingers of the dominant hand using a swiping motion of the fingers for the standing long jump. The score is the vertical distance between the two chalk marks for the standing long jump.
summary: This chunk details the step-by-step procedure for performing the standing long jump.

content: To perform the vertical jump using a commercial Vertec device, the tester adjusts the height of the stack of movable color-coded horizontal plastic vanes to be within the athlete’s standing reach height. The highest vane that can be reached and pushed forward with the dominant hand while the athlete stands flat-footed determines the standing touch height for the vertical jump. The vane stack is then raised by a measured distance (marked on the shaft holding the vanes) so that the athlete will not jump higher or lower than the set of vanes for the vertical jump. Without a preparatory or stutter step, the athlete performs a countermovement by quickly flexing the knees and hips, moving the trunk forward and downward, and swinging the arms backward (figure 12.1a) during the vertical jump. During the jump, the dominant arm reaches upward while the nondominant arm moves downward relative to the body for the vertical jump. At the highest point in the jump, the athlete taps the highest possible vane with the fingers of the dominant hand for the vertical jump. The score is the vertical distance between the height of the highest vane tapped during the standing vertical reach and the vane tapped at the highest point of the jump for the vertical jump. The best of three trials is recorded to the nearest 0.5 inches (the distance between adjacent vanes) for the vertical jump. Normative data for the vertical jump are presented in table 12.4 on page 278.
summary: This chunk describes the procedure for performing the vertical jump using a commercial Vertec device.

content: The Margaria-Kalamen test measures maximum muscular power (high-speed strength). The athlete's weight is measured in newtons (pounds 3 4.45 or kg 3 9.807). The height of each step is measured with a ruler or tape measure. The elevation from the third step to the ninth step is calculated (6 3 step height). The timer start switch mechanism is placed on the third step, and the stop switch mechanism is placed on the ninth step. The athlete sprints toward the stairs from a standing start 20 feet (6 m) from the base of the stairs and then up the staircase three steps at a time (third step to sixth step to ninth step) as fast as possible. The time from third- to ninth-step contact is determined to the nearest 0.01 second using the timing system. Power in watts is calculated as the athlete’s weight (w) in newtons (pounds 3 4.45 or kg 3 9.807) times height (h) in meters (inches 3 0.0254) from the third step to the ninth step divided by the measured time interval (t) in seconds; P (watts) = (w 3 h) / t. Normative data for the Margaria-Kalamen test are presented in table 12.5 on page 279. Equipment needed for the Margaria-Kalamen test includes a staircase with nine or more steps, each about 7 inches (18 cm) high, and a straight and flat lead-up area 20 feet (6 m) or more in length. Equipment needed for the Margaria-Kalamen test also includes a measuring tape or stick, an electronic timing system with both a start and a stop switch mechanism, and a scale. Personnel needed for the Margaria-Kalamen test includes one tester/recorder.
summary: This chunk describes the Margaria-Kalamen test, including its purpose, procedure, calculations, and equipment.

content: The 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test measures anaerobic capacity. Equipment needed for the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test includes a stopwatch with at least 0.1-second resolution and two parallel lines 25 yards (22.86 m) apart on a flat surface. Personnel needed for the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test includes one timer and two line judges. Athletes are paired off based on similar ability for the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test. Athletes sprint to the line 25 yards (22.86 m) away, make foot contact with it, then immediately sprint back to the first line for six round trips.
summary: This chunk describes the 300-yard shuttle test, including its purpose, equipment, personnel, and procedure.

content: The 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test measures anaerobic capacity. Equipment needed for the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test includes a stopwatch with at least 0.1-second resolution and two parallel lines 25 yards (22.86 m) apart on a flat surface. Personnel needed for the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test includes one timer and two line judges. Athletes are paired off based on similar ability for the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test. Athletes sprint to the line 25 yards (22.86 m) away, make foot contact with it, then immediately sprint back to the first line for six round trips. The average of two trials of the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle test is recorded to the nearest 1.0 second. Normative data for the 300-yard (274 m) shuttle are presented in table 12.4 on page 278.
summary: The 300-yard shuttle test assesses anaerobic capacity and involves sprinting between two lines for a set number of repetitions.

content: The partial curl-up test measures the muscular endurance of the abdominal muscles. The partial curl-up test is often favored over the sit-up test because it eliminates the use of the hip flexor muscles. Equipment needed for the partial curl-up test includes a metronome, ruler, masking tape, and a mat. Personnel needed for the partial curl-up test includes one recorder/technique judge. The athlete assumes a supine position on a mat with a 90° knee angle for the partial curl-up test. The arms are at the sides, resting on the floor, with the fingers touching a 4-inch (10 cm)-long piece of masking tape positioned perpendicular to the fingers. A second piece of masking tape is situated parallel to the first tape at a distance determined by the age of the athlete (4.7 inches [12 cm] for those younger than 45 and 3.1 inches [8 cm] for those 45 or older). The metronome is set to 40 beats per minute and the individual does slow, controlled curl-ups to lift the shoulder blades off the mat (trunk makes a 30° angle with the mat) in time with the metronome (20 curl-ups per minute). The upper back must touch the floor before each curl-up. The athlete should avoid flexing the neck to bring the chin close to the chest during the partial curl-up test. The athlete performs as many curl-ups as possible without pausing, to a maximum of 75. Normative data for the partial curl-up are presented in table 12.8 on page 280.
summary: The partial curl-up test assesses abdominal muscular endurance by measuring the number of repetitions performed with controlled movements.

content: For both the Army and American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) standards, men assume the standard push-up starting position with hands shoulder-width apart and elbows and body straight. For the Army standards, women assume the same position as the men. For the ACSM standards, women start similarly except that the knees rather than the feet contact the ground, with the knees flexed at 90° and the ankles crossed. For the Army standards, the push-up low position is when the upper arms are parallel to the ground. For the ACSM standards, the low position for males is when the chest makes contact with the recorder’s fist held vertically against the ground. There is no standard criterion for the female low position. It has been suggested that females make torso contact with a foam roller on the ground rather than a fist. For either standard, repetitions that do not achieve the required low position are not counted. For the Army standard, as many repetitions as possible are done within a timed 2-minute period. The athlete may pause only in the up position. For the ACSM standard, as many repetitions as possible are done continuously until failure.
summary: The push-up test, with variations for Army and ACSM standards, assesses upper body strength and endurance.

content: The resistance for the YMCA bench press test is set at 80 pounds (36 kg) for males and 35 pounds (16 kg) for females. The metronome cadence for the YMCA bench press test is set at 60 beats per minute to establish a rate of 30 repetitions per minute. The athlete grips the bar at shoulder width, lifts the bar off the rack, and extends the elbows. Then, in time with the metronome, the bar is repeatedly lowered to the chest and raised up again, so that the elbows are extended, until the athlete can no longer keep up with the metronome. The movement should be smooth and controlled, with the bar reaching its highest and lowest position with each beat of the metronome.
summary: The YMCA bench press test measures upper body strength by recording the number of repetitions performed with a set weight and metronome cadence.

content: Athletes line up at the starting line for the 12-Minute Run. On an auditory signal, the athletes travel by foot as far as possible in 12 minutes, preferably by running, but if necessary by walking part or all of the time. At 12 minutes, on an auditory signal, all the athletes stop in place. The distance run by each athlete is calculated and recorded. Each athlete should be recognizable to the scorer at the finish line for the 1.5-Mile Run.
summary: The 12-Minute Run and 1.5-Mile Run tests assess cardiovascular endurance by measuring the distance covered within a set time.

content: Athletes line up at the starting line for the 12-Minute Run. On an auditory signal, the athletes travel by foot as far as possible in 12 minutes, preferably by running, but if necessary by walking part or all of the time. At 12 minutes, on an auditory signal, all the athletes stop in place. The distance run by each athlete is calculated and recorded.
summary: Description of the 12-Minute Run test: athletes run as far as possible in 12 minutes, with distance recorded.

content: Each athlete should be recognizable to the scorer at the finish line for the 1.5-Mile Run. If that is not possible, numbers should be pinned to the athletes’ shirts. At the start, all runners should line up behind the starting line for the 1.5-Mile Run. Instruct the athletes to complete the run as quickly as possible at a steady pace that they can barely maintain over the distance.
summary: Instructions for the 1.5-Mile Run: athlete identification, starting procedure, and pacing guidance.

content: On an auditory signal, the athletes start running and cover the course as quickly as possible. As the runners cross the finish line, each runner’s time is recorded on a form as a timer calls off the time in minutes and seconds.
summary: Start and finish procedures for the 1.5-Mile Run: auditory signal, time recording.

content: The T-test requires four cones arranged as seen in figure 12.7. The athlete warms up and stretches prior to the T-test. The T-test begins with the athlete standing at point A. On an auditory signal, the athlete sprints forward to point B and touches the base of the cone with the right hand. The athlete shuffles to the left 5 yards (4.6 m) and touches the base of the cone at point C with the left hand. The athlete shuffles to the right 10 yards (9.1 m) and touches the base of the cone at point D with the right hand. The athlete shuffles to the left 5 yards and touches the base of the cone at point B with the left hand, and next runs backward past point A, at which time the watch is stopped. For safety, a spotter and gym mat should be positioned several feet behind point A to catch an athlete who falls while running backward.
summary: Description of the T-test: cone setup, warm-up, procedure, and safety precautions.

content: The best time of two trials is recorded to the nearest 0.1 second. Reasons for disqualification of a T-test trial include: failing to touch the base of any cone, crossing one foot in front of the other instead of shuffling the feet, or failing to face forward for the entire test. Normative data for the T-test are presented in table 12.4 on page 278.
summary: T-test scoring: best time, disqualification criteria, and normative data.

content: The hexagon test requires a hexagon on the floor with 24-inch (61 cm) sides meeting to form 120° angles. The athlete warms up and practices performance of the hexagon test at submaximal speed. The hexagon test begins with the athlete standing in the middle of the hexagon. On an auditory signal, the athlete begins double-leg hopping from the center of the hexagon over each side and back to the center, starting with the side directly in front of the athlete, in a continuous clockwise sequence until all six sides are covered three times (three revolutions around the hexagon for a total of 18 jumps) and the athlete is again standing at the center. The athlete remains facing the same direction throughout the hexagon test.
summary: Description of the hexagon test: setup, warm-up, procedure, and direction.

content: If the athlete lands on a side of the hexagon rather than over it, or loses balance and takes an extra step or changes the direction he or she is facing, the trial is stopped and restarted after the athlete is allowed time for full recovery. The best time of three trials is recorded to the nearest 0.1 second. Normative data for the hexagon test are presented in table 12.4 on page 278.
summary: Hexagon test scoring: trial restarts, best time, and normative data.

content: The pro agility test requires an American football field or other field marked with three parallel lines 5 yards (4.6 m) apart. The athlete straddles the centermost of the three parallel lines using a three-point stance.
