On an auditory signal, the athlete sprints 5 yards (4.6 m) to the line on the left, then changes direction and sprints 10 yards (9.1 m) to the line on the right, then again changes direction and sprints 5 yards (4.6 m) to the center line. Foot contact must be made with all indicated lines.
summary: Description of the pro agility test: field setup, starting position, and procedure.

content: The best time of two trials is recorded to the nearest 0.01 second. Normative data for the pro agility test are presented in table 12.18 on page 287.
summary: Pro agility test scoring: best time and normative data.

content: The 40-yard (37 m) sprint requires a flat running surface with start and finish lines 40 yards (37 m) apart, with at least 20 yards (18 m) after the finish line for deceleration. The athlete warms up and stretches for several minutes. The athlete assumes a starting position using a three- or four-point stance. On an auditory signal, the athlete sprints 40 yards (37 m) at maximal speed. The average of two trials is recorded to the nearest 0.1 second.
summary: Description of the 40-yard sprint: setup, warm-up, starting position, procedure, and time recording.

content: The 40-yard (37 m) sprint requires a flat running surface with start and finish lines 40 yards (37 m) apart, with at least 20 yards (18 m) after the finish line for deceleration. The athlete warms up and stretches for several minutes. The athlete assumes a starting position using a three- or four-point stance. On an auditory signal, the athlete sprints 40 yards (37 m) at maximal speed. The average of two trials is recorded to the nearest 0.1 second. Normative data for the 40-yard (37 m) sprint are presented in table 12.4 on page 278.
summary: This chunk describes the procedure for administering the 40-yard sprint test, including setup, warm-up, execution, and data recording.

content: A consistent method for the sit-and-reach test should be maintained if the test is done periodically. If the test is performed with a measuring tape or stick during initial testing of the athlete, all subsequent testing of the athlete should be performed with a measuring tape or stick. A standard sit-and-reach box is a permissible alternative to a measuring tape or stick. One tester/recorder is required for the sit-and-reach test.
summary: This chunk outlines the standardized procedures for administering the sit-and-reach test, emphasizing consistency and the acceptable tools for measurement.

content: The measuring stick or tape measure should be taped to the floor. A piece of tape should be placed about 24 inches (61 cm) long across the measuring stick and at a right angle to it at the 15-inch (38 cm) mark. The athlete should warm up with nonballistic exercises involving the hamstrings and lower back for 3 to 5 minutes. Examples of warm-up exercises include walking rapidly, flexing forward from a standing, knees-straight position, reaching toward the toes, then reaching upward toward the ceiling, jogging in place while trying to kick the heels into the upper thighs from behind, and finishing with standing toe-touching or similar stretching on the floor.
summary: This chunk details the specific setup and warm-up instructions for the sit-and-reach test, ensuring proper positioning and preparation.

content: The athlete should sit shoeless with the measuring stick between the legs with its zero end toward the body. The athlete's feet should be 12 inches (30 cm) apart, toes pointed upward, and heels nearly touching the edge of the taped line at the 15-inch (38-cm) mark. The athlete should slowly reach forward with both hands as far as possible on the measuring stick, holding this position momentarily. To get the best stretch, the athlete should exhale and drop the head between the arms when reaching. The athlete should keep the hands adjacent to each other and not lead with one hand. The fingertips should remain in contact with the measuring stick. The tester may hold the athlete’s knees down, if necessary, to keep them straight.
summary: This chunk provides detailed instructions on the athlete's posture and movement during the sit-and-reach test, emphasizing proper form and technique.

content: A score of less than 15 inches (38 cm) indicates that the athlete could not reach the bottom of the feet. The best of three trials is recorded to the nearest 0.25 inch or 1 cm.
summary: This chunk explains the scoring criteria for the sit-and-reach test, including the minimum score and the level of precision for recording results.

content: Skinfold measurements should be made on dry skin, prior to exercise, to ensure maximum validity and reliability. Grasp the skin firmly with the thumb and index finger to form a fold of skin and subcutaneous fat. Place the caliper prongs perpendicular to the fold 0.5 inch to 1 inch (approximately 1 to 2 cm) from the thumb and index finger. Release the caliper grip so that its spring tension is exerted on the skinfold. Read the dial on the caliper to the nearest 0.5 mm between 1 and 2 seconds after the grip on the caliper has been released.
summary: This chunk outlines the proper technique for taking skinfold measurements, emphasizing the importance of preparation and accuracy.

content: Obtain one measurement from each test site, and then repeat all test sites for a second trial. If the measurements do not differ by more than 10%, average the two measurements to the nearest 0.5 mm. Otherwise, take one or more additional measurements until two of the measurements are within 10%, and average those two measurements to the nearest 0.5 mm.
summary: This chunk describes the procedure for obtaining reliable skinfold measurements, including repetition and averaging techniques.

content: There are specific equations for estimating body density (Db) (then, in turn, percent body fat [%BF]) for different populations. Select the equation appropriate for the athlete from table 12.19 on page 288. Mark the skin at the appropriate anatomical sites: Chest—a diagonal fold one-half the distance between the anterior axillary line and the nipple for men, Thigh—a vertical fold on the anterior aspect of the thigh, midway between the hip and knee joints, Abdomen—a vertical fold 1 inch (2.5 cm) to the right (relative to the athlete) of the umbilicus, Triceps—a vertical fold on the posterior midline of the upper arm (over the triceps muscle), halfway between the acromion and the olecranon processes (the arm should be in anatomical position with the elbow extended and relaxed). Suprailium is a diagonal fold above the crest of the ilium at the spot where an imaginary line would come down from the anterior axillary line. Some prefer the suprailium measure to be taken more laterally, at the midaxillary line. Midaxilla is a vertical fold on the midaxillary line at the level of the xiphoid process of the sternum. Subscapula is a fold taken on a diagonal line that extends from the vertebral border to a point 0.5 inches to 1 inch (1 to 2 cm) from the inferior angle of the scapula. Calf is a vertical fold along the medial side of the calf, at the level of maximum calf circumference.
summary: This chunk details the anatomical sites for skinfold measurements and provides guidance on selecting the appropriate equation for body density estimation.

content: Once the proper test or tests have been chosen and administered and the scores collected, the next step may include any or all of the following: (1) analysis of the data to determine the change in performance of the group over the training period (weeks, months, or years); (2) analysis of the group’s performance relative to that of similar groups tested in the past; (3) analysis of the relationship of each athlete’s scores to those of the group; and (4) comparison of individual scores to local, state, national, or international norms.
summary: This chunk outlines the potential next steps after administering fitness tests, including data analysis and comparison to benchmarks.

content: Midaxilla is a vertical fold on the midaxillary line at the level of the xiphoid process of the sternum. Some prefer the suprailium measure to be taken more laterally, at the midaxillary line.
summary: Describes the location and variations in taking the midaxilla and suprailium body composition measurements.

content: Subscapula is a fold taken on a diagonal line that extends from the vertebral border to a point 0.5 inches to 1 inch (1 to 2 cm) from the inferior angle of the scapula. Calf is a vertical fold along the medial side of the calf, at the level of maximum calf circumference.
summary: Defines the measurement techniques for the subscapula and calf body composition folds.

content: Once the proper test or tests have been chosen and administered and the scores collected, the next step may include any or all of the following: (1) analysis of the data to determine the change in performance of the group over the training period (weeks, months, or years); (2) analysis of the group’s performance relative to that of similar groups tested in the past; (3) analysis of the relationship of each athlete’s scores to those of the group; and (4) comparison of individual scores to local, state, national, or international norms.
summary: Outlines the steps involved in analyzing performance test data, including comparing individual and group performance over time and against established norms.

content: An important outcome of repeated performance testing is evaluation of both the improvement of individual athletes and the overall effectiveness of the physical conditioning program as determined by changes in test scores (10). A difference score is the difference between an athlete’s score at the beginning and end of a training period or between any two separate testing times.
summary: Emphasizes the importance of repeated performance testing for evaluating individual and program effectiveness, defining a difference score as a key metric.

content: Judging the effectiveness of a training program merely by degree of improvement has two major limitations. First, athletes who begin the training period at a higher training status will not improve as much as untrained athletes who perform poorly at the beginning of training. Second, athletes may deliberately fail to give maximal effort on pretraining tests to inflate their pre- to posttraining improvement scores.
summary: Discusses limitations of solely relying on improvement scores to assess training program effectiveness, highlighting potential biases due to initial training status and athlete motivation.

content: It is important to encourage athletes to give maximal effort on both the pre- and posttraining tests.
summary: Stresses the importance of athlete effort during both pre- and post-training tests for accurate evaluation.

content: Statistics is the science of collecting, classifying, analyzing, and interpreting numerical data (33, 68). A working knowledge of statistics is helpful in making sound evaluations of test results.
summary: Introduces statistics as a crucial tool for analyzing and interpreting test results, emphasizing its importance in performance evaluation.

content: There are two main branches of statistics—descriptive and inferential. Descriptive statistics summarizes or describes a large group of data. It is used when all the information about a population is known.
summary: Defines the two branches of statistics: descriptive and inferential, explaining that descriptive statistics focuses on summarizing and describing data from a known population.

content: There are three categories of numerical measurement in descriptive statistics: central tendency, variability, and percentile rank. Measures of central tendency are values about which the data tend to cluster. The three most common measures of central tendency (2, 37, 68) are as follows: Mean—the average of the scores (i.e., the sum of the scores divided by the number of scores). This is the most commonly used measure of central tendency. Median—the middlemost score when a set of scores is arranged in order of magnitude. With an even number of scores, the median is the average of the two middlemost scores. Half a group of scores falls above the median and half falls below the median. Depending on the distribution of scores, the median can be a better measure of central tendency than the mean. This is particularly true when very high or very low scores of one or a few members of the group tested raise or lower the group mean to the extent that it does not adequately describe the ability of most group members. Mode—the score that occurs with the greatest frequency. If each numerical score appears only once, there is no mode. If two or more scores are “tied” for greatest frequency, then all of the similar scores are modes. The mode is generally regarded as the least useful measure of central tendency.
summary: Explains the concept of descriptive statistics, focusing on measures of central tendency (mean, median, mode) and their applications in describing data distribution.

content: The degree of dispersion of scores within a group is called variability. Two common measures of variability are the range and the standard deviation. The range is the interval from the lowest to the highest score. The advantage of the range is that it is easy to understand; the disadvantage is that it uses only the two extreme scores and so may not be an accurate measure of variability (2, 68). For example, the range could be the same for a group of widely dispersed scores as for a group of scores that are narrowly dispersed except for one deviant score. The standard deviation is a measure of the variability of a set of scores about the mean. The formula for the standard deviation of a sample is as follows: sxxn= −()−•21 The standard deviation is most useful when the group of scores is normally distributed, forming the bell-shaped curve shown in figure 12.12 (2, 37, 62, 68).
summary: Defines variability and its two common measures: range and standard deviation, highlighting the limitations of the range and the importance of normal distribution for standard deviation interpretation.

content: The z score can be used to express the distance of any individual score in standard deviation units from the mean: z = (x – x–) / s  (12.2). If an athlete runs the 40-yard (37 m) sprint in 4.6 seconds and the mean and standard deviation for the group tested are 5.0 and 0.33 seconds, respectively, the z score for that athlete is –1.2.
summary: Explains the concept of z-score as a measure of individual score deviation from the mean in standard deviation units, providing an example calculation.

content: An individual’s percentile rank is the percentage of test takers scoring below that individual. If an athlete is ranked in the 75th percentile, 75% of the group produced scores below that athlete’s score.
summary: Defines percentile rank as a measure of individual performance relative to the group, explaining its interpretation.

content: Norms based on large samples are sometimes expressed in evenly spaced percentiles, as in tables 12.12-12.15 on pages 282-284 (2). Several percentile rank tables have been developed for males and females of different age groups for various sports, including American football (10, 20, 42, 45, 57, 58, 70), basketball (31, 56, 57), and volleyball (10, 56), as well as health-related fitness (3, 16, 53, 70).
summary: Discusses the availability of percentile rank tables for various sports and age groups, highlighting their use in comparing individual performance to established norms.

content: Inferential statistics allows one to draw general conclusions about a population from information collected in a population sample. If a boys’ 9th-grade gym class is put through a battery of tests and it is assumed that the class (sample) is representative of all the 9th-grade boys in the school (the population), then the results of these tests can be used to make inferences about the population as a whole.
summary: Explains inferential statistics as a method for drawing conclusions about a population based on a representative sample, providing an example scenario.

content: A basic assumption of inferential statistics is that the sample is truly representative of the population (61).
summary: Emphasizes the crucial assumption of representative sampling in inferential statistics.

content: To determine the sport-specific training status of an athlete, the strength and conditioning professional can combine the results of selected tests to generate an athletic profile, which is a group of test results related to sport-specific abilities that are important for quality performance in a sport or sport position.
summary: Describes the concept of an athletic profile, which combines test results to assess an athlete's sport-specific training status.

content: When evaluating athletes, the strength and conditioning professional should follow these five steps: 1. Select tests that will measure the specific parameters most closely related to the characteristics of the sport or sports in question. For example, a testing battery for wrestlers should include tests for pulling strength, pushing strength, and local muscular endurance. 2. Choose valid and reliable tests to measure these parameters, and arrange the testing battery in an appropriate order with sufficient rest between tests to promote test reliability. For example, appropriate tests for wrestling might include push-ups and sit-ups for maximum repetitions in a given time interval. These two tests should be separated by at least 10 minutes of rest to allow recovery from fatigue and thus promote accurate scores. 3. Administer the test battery to as many athletes as possible. 4. Calculate percentile ranks to present a visual profile. 5. Evaluate the athlete based on percentile rank within the group and against the individual’s best performances over previous years, if possible.
summary: Outlines a five-step process for evaluating athletes, emphasizing test selection, reliability, administration, percentile ranking, and comparison to previous performance.

content: Motor abilities and body composition variables that can be improved through strength and conditioning programs include maximum muscular strength, maximum muscular power, anaerobic capacity, local muscular endurance, aerobic capacity, agility, speed, flexibility, girths, percent body fat, and lean body mass.
summary: Lists various motor abilities and body composition variables that can be enhanced through strength and conditioning programs.

content: Performance testing can be used to evaluate basic motor abilities, as well as the improvement of individual athletes over time and the overall effectiveness of a physical conditioning program.
summary: Highlights the multifaceted applications of performance testing, including evaluating motor abilities, individual progress, and program effectiveness.

content: Numerous tests are available to measure sport-specific physical capabilities and training status.
summary: Emphasizes the availability of various tests for assessing sport-specific physical attributes and training progress.

content: To determine the sport-specific training status of an athlete, the strength and conditioning professional can combine the results of selected tests to generate an athletic profile, which is a group of test results related to sport-specific abilities that are important for quality performance in a sport or sport position. When evaluating athletes, the strength and conditioning professional should follow these five steps: 1. Select tests that will measure the specific parameters most closely related to the characteristics of the sport or sports in question. For example, a testing battery for wrestlers should include tests for pulling strength, pushing strength, and local muscular endurance. 2. Choose valid and reliable tests to measure these parameters, and arrange the testing battery in an appropriate order with sufficient rest between tests to promote test reliability. For example, appropriate tests for wrestling might include push-ups and sit-ups for maximum repetitions in a given time interval. These two tests should be separated by at least 10 minutes of rest to allow recovery from fatigue and thus promote accurate scores. 3. Administer the test battery to as many athletes as possible. 4. Calculate percentile ranks to present a visual profile. 5. Evaluate the athlete based on percentile rank within the group and against the individual’s best performances over previous years, if possible.
summary: This chunk outlines the process of evaluating athletes using performance testing, emphasizing the importance of selecting relevant tests, ensuring reliability, and interpreting results within a group and individual context.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body density (Db) from skinfold measurements among Black or Hispanic women aged 18-55 years is: Db = 1.0970 – 0.00046971 (S7SKF) + 0.00000056 (S7SKF)<sup>2</sup> – 0.00012828 (Age). The equation for calculating estimated body density (Db) from skinfold measurements among Black or Hispanic athletes aged 18-61 years is: Db = 1.1120 – 0.00043499 (S7SKF) + 0.00000055 (S7SKF)<sup>2</sup> – 0.00028826 (Age).
summary: Equations for estimating body density (Db) from skinfolds in Black or Hispanic women and athletes.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body density (Db) from skinfold measurements among athletes aged 18-29 years is: Db = 1.096095 – 0.0006952 (S4SKF) – 0.0000011 (S4SKF)<sup>2</sup> – 0.0000714 (Age). The equation for calculating estimated body density (Db) from skinfold measurements among White or anorexic women aged 18-55 years is: Db = 1.0994921 – 0.0009929 (S3SKF) + 0.0000023 (S3SKF)<sup>2</sup> – 0.0001392 (Age).
summary: Equations for estimating body density (Db) from skinfolds in athletes and White or anorexic women.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body density (Db) from skinfold measurements among White men aged 18-61 years is: Db = 1.109380 – 0.0008267 (S3SKF) + 0.0000016 (S3SKF)<sup>2</sup> – 0.0002574 (Age).

content: The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from skinfold measurements among Black or White boys aged 6-17 years is: %BF = 0.735 (S2SKF) + 1.0. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from skinfold measurements among Black or White girls aged 6-17 years is: %BF = 0.610 (S2SKF) + 5.1.
summary: Equations for estimating body fat percentage (%BF) from skinfolds in Black or White children.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body density (Db) from suprailiac and triceps skinfolds among athletes of both sexes in high school and college age is: Db = 1.0764 – (0.000813 suprailiac) – (0.000883 triceps). The equation for calculating estimated body density (Db) from thigh and subscapular skinfolds among athletes of both sexes in high school and college age is: Db = 1.1043 – (0.001333 thigh) – (0.001313 subscapular).
summary: Equations for estimating body density (Db) from specific skinfolds in athletes.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for American Indian females aged 18-60 years is: %BF = (4.81/Db) – 4.34. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for Black males aged 18-32 years is: %BF = (4.37/Db) – 3.93. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for Black females aged 24-79 years is: %BF = (4.85/Db) – 4.39. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for Hispanic females aged 20-40 years is: %BF = (4.87/Db) – 4.41.
summary: Equations for estimating body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) in various populations.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for Japanese native males aged 18-48 years is: %BF = (4.97/Db) – 4.52. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for Japanese native females aged 18-48 years is: %BF = (4.76/Db) – 4.28. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for Japanese native males aged 61-78 years is: %BF = (4.87/Db) – 4.41. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for Japanese native females aged 61-78 years is: %BF = (4.95/Db) – 4.50.
summary: Equations for estimating body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) in Japanese native populations.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White males aged 7-12 years is: %BF = (5.30/Db) – 4.89. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White females aged 7-12 years is: %BF = (5.35/Db) – 4.95. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White males aged 13-16 years is: %BF = (5.07/Db) – 4.64. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White females aged 13-16 years is: %BF = (5.10/Db) – 4.66. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White males aged 17-19 years is: %BF = (4.99/Db) – 4.55. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White females aged 17-19 years is: %BF = (5.05/Db) – 4.62. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White males aged 20-80 years is: %BF = (4.95/Db) – 4.50. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for White females aged 20-80 years is: %BF = (5.01/Db) – 4.57. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for anorexic females aged 15-30 years is: %BF = (5.26/Db) – 4.83. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for obese females aged 17-62 years is: %BF = (5.00/Db) – 4.56. content: Males with less than 7% body fat are considered extremely lean in gymnastics. Females with less than 15% body fat are considered extremely lean in bodybuilding (at contest) and wrestling (at contest).
summary: Body fat percentage thresholds for 'extremely lean' in specific sports.

content: The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for anorexic females aged 15-30 years is: %BF = (5.26/Db) – 4.83. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for obese females aged 17-62 years is: %BF = (5.00/Db) – 4.56. The equation for calculating estimated body fat percentage (%BF) from body density (Db) for athletes aged 18-62 years is: %BF = (4.57/Db) – 4.142.
summary: Equations for estimating body fat percentage (%BF) based on body density (Db) are provided for different female populations (anorexic, obese, athletes).

content: Males with less than 7% body fat are considered extremely lean in gymnastics. Females with less than 15% body fat are considered extremely lean in bodybuilding (at contest) and wrestling (at contest). Females with less than 15% body fat are considered extremely lean in cross-country. Males with 8-10% body fat are considered very lean in men’s basketball. Females with 16-18% body fat are considered very lean in racquetball, rowing, and soccer. Females with 16-18% body fat are considered very lean in track and field decathlon (women) and track and field heptathlon (women).
summary: Criterion scores for extremely lean and very lean body fat percentages are provided for males and females across various sports.

content: Males with 11-13% body fat are considered leaner than average in men’s baseball. Females with 19-20% body fat are considered leaner than average in canoeing, downhill skiing, speed skating, Olympic-style weightlifting. Males with 14-17% body fat are considered average in women’s basketball. Females with 21-25% body fat are considered average in American football quarterbacks, kickers, linebackers, hockey, horse racing (jockey), tennis, discus throw, volleyball, women’s softball, and powerlifting. Males with 18-22% body fat are considered fatter than average in American football (linemen). Females with 26-30% body fat are considered fatter than average in shot put.
summary: Criterion scores for leaner than average, average, and fatter than average body fat percentages are provided for males and females across various sports.

content: The table presents criterion scores and normative values for percent body fat for males and females. The table includes data for ages 6-17 and 18-66+ for males and females. Data for male and female ratings (criterion scores), ages 18-66+, are adapted from Morrow et al., 2005 (47). Data for male and female ratings (criterion scores), ages 6-17, are from Lohman, Houtkooper, and Going, 1997 (43). Data for male and female percentiles (normative references) are reprinted from ACSM, 2000 (5).
summary: This table presents criterion scores and normative values for body fat percentages in males and females across different age groups, citing the sources for the data.

content: The minimum SEE for population-specific skinfold equations is ±3% to ±5%. If a 25-year-old male athlete’s body fat is measured at 24%, there is a minimum of a 6% range (21-27%) that suggests a criterion reference score of “fat.” Reporting an athlete’s body fat percentage with an SEE range can also cover any gaps and overlaps in the criterion-referenced norms. For example, what is the criterion score for a 30-year-old male with 29% body fat? The minimum SEE of ±3% places this athlete between 26% and 32% and therefore would suggest a criterion reference score of “fat-overfat” or “borderline overfat.”
summary: The standard error of estimate (SEE) for body fat measurements is discussed, along with how to interpret body fat percentages within the SEE range for accurate criterion-referenced scoring.

content: This chapter is devoted to two key areas, warm-up and flexibility. Warm-up is designed to prepare an athlete for training or competition and can improve subsequent performance and lessen the risk of injury. Flexibility training, through the use of different forms of stretching, aims to increase the range of motion around a joint.
summary: This chapter focuses on the importance of warm-up and flexibility training for athletes.

content: A warm-up period is important before any athletic performance, the goal being to prepare the athlete mentally and physically for exercise or competition. A well-designed warm-up can increase muscle temperature, core temperature, and blood flow. A well-designed warm-up can disrupt transient connective tissue bonds.
summary: The importance of a warm-up before athletic performance is emphasized, highlighting its benefits for physical preparation.

content: A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Faster muscle contraction and relaxation of both agonist and antagonist muscles. A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Improvements in the rate of force development and reaction time. A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Improvements in muscle strength and power. A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Lowered viscous resistance in muscles. A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Improved oxygen delivery due to the Bohr effect, whereby higher temperatures facilitate oxygen release from hemoglobin and myoglobin. A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Increased blood flow to active muscles. A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Enhanced metabolic reactions.
summary: The positive impacts of a warm-up on athletic performance are detailed, encompassing various physiological benefits.

content: The influence of a warm-up on injury prevention is unclear, but the evidence suggests a positive effect. Increased muscle temperature can increase the resistance to muscle tear. Static stretching has long been used in a warm-up, with the aim of enhancing performance and reducing the risk of injury.
summary: While the link between warm-up and injury prevention is not definitive, there is some evidence to suggest a positive effect. Static stretching is commonly incorporated into warm-ups for performance and injury reduction.

content: A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Increased blood flow to active muscles. A warm-up can have the following positive impacts on performance: Enhanced metabolic reactions. The influence of a warm-up on injury prevention is unclear, but the evidence suggests a positive effect. Increased muscle temperature can increase the resistance to muscle tear.
summary: A warm-up improves performance by increasing blood flow, enhancing metabolic reactions, and potentially reducing injury risk.

content: Static stretching has long been used in a warm-up, with the aim of enhancing performance and reducing the risk of injury. There is little, if any, evidence that stretching pre- or postparticipation prevents injury. Although static stretching before activity might increase performance in sports that require an increased range of motion, such as gymnastics, static stretching can compromise muscle performance. It is important that the strength and conditioning professional perform a benefit–risk analysis when choosing whether or not to include static stretching in a warm-up.
summary: The role of static stretching in warm-ups is debated, with potential benefits for range of motion but possible negative impacts on performance.

content: Some studies demonstrated that static stretching had no effect on subsequent performance. Static stretching has also been shown to lead to a decrease in force production. Static stretching has also been shown to lead to a decrease in power performance. Static stretching has also been shown to lead to a decrease in running speed. Static stretching has also been shown to lead to a decrease in reaction and movement time. Static stretching has also been shown to lead to a decrease in strength endurance.
summary: Research suggests that static stretching can negatively affect various performance metrics.

content: Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) stretching and ballistic stretching have been shown to be detrimental to subsequent performance.
summary: PNF and ballistic stretching are not recommended for warm-ups due to their potential to hinder performance.

content: Dynamic stretching does not seem to elicit the performance reduction effects of static and PNF stretching. Stretching has been shown to improve subsequent running performance. Dynamic stretching would be the preferred option for stretching during a warm-up.
summary: Dynamic stretching is a more effective warm-up strategy compared to static or PNF stretching.

content: Sports in which increased flexibility is needed, such as gymnastics or diving, require a greater degree of stretching. Those with high demands for a stretch-shortening cycle of high intensity, as in sprinting and American football, are likely to require more stretching than those with low or medium stretch-shortening cycle activity, as in jogging or cycling.
summary: Stretching needs vary depending on the sport's demands for flexibility and stretch-shortening cycles.

content: Strength and conditioning professionals should look at the specific range of motion and stretch-shortening cycle requirements of the sport or activity and use this information when designing an appropriate warm-up regimen.
summary: Warm-up design should be tailored to the specific demands of the sport or activity.

content: A general warm-up period may consist of 5 to 10 minutes of slow activity such as jogging or skipping. Low-intensity sport-specific actions such as dribbling a soccer ball can be productive during the general warm-up period. The aim of the general warm-up period is to increase heart rate, blood flow, deep muscle temperature, respiration rate, and perspiration and to decrease viscosity of joint fluids.
summary: A general warm-up involves light activity to elevate heart rate, blood flow, and muscle temperature.

content: A specific warm-up period incorporates movements similar to the movements of the athlete’s sport. The specific warm-up period involves 8 to 12 minutes of dynamic stretching focusing on movements that work through the range of motion required for the sport. The specific warm-up period is followed by sport-specific movements of increasing intensity such as sprint drills, bounding activities, or jumping.
summary: A specific warm-up focuses on sport-specific movements and progressively increases intensity.

content: The more power necessary for the sport or activity, the more important the warm-up becomes. Including high-intensity dynamic exercises can facilitate subsequent performance. This phase should also include rehearsal of the skill to be performed.
summary: The importance of a warm-up increases with the power demands of the activity, and it should include skill rehearsal.

content: The warm-up should progress gradually and provide sufficient intensity to increase muscle and core temperatures without causing fatigue or reducing energy stores.
summary: Warm-up progression should be gradual, aiming to increase temperature without causing fatigue.

content: The degree of movement that occurs at a joint is called the range of motion (ROM). The ROM of a particular joint is determined by a number of factors including connective tissue structure, activity level, age, and sex. Range of motion is specific to each joint’s anatomy and the movements required at that joint.
summary: Range of motion (ROM) is influenced by various factors and is specific to each joint's structure and function.

content: Flexibility is a measure of ROM and has static and dynamic components. Static flexibility is the range of possible movement about a joint and its surrounding muscles during a passive movement. Static flexibility requires no voluntary muscular activity; an external force such as gravity, a partner, or a machine provides the force for a stretch.
summary: Flexibility encompasses both static and dynamic components, with static flexibility referring to passive range of motion.

content: Dynamic flexibility refers to the available ROM during active movements and therefore requires voluntary muscular actions. Dynamic ROM is generally greater than static ROM.
summary: Dynamic flexibility involves active movement and typically exceeds static flexibility.

content: Sports and activities have specific requirements for ROM, and it is likely that optimal levels of flexibility exist for each activity.
summary: Optimal flexibility levels vary depending on the specific demands of each sport or activity.

content: Static flexibility is the range of possible movement about a joint and its surrounding muscles during a passive movement. Static flexibility requires no voluntary muscular activity; an external force such as gravity, a partner, or a machine provides the force for a stretch.
summary: Static flexibility is the range of motion achievable passively, without muscle contraction.

content: Dynamic flexibility refers to the available ROM during active movements and therefore requires voluntary muscular actions. Dynamic ROM is generally greater than static ROM.
summary: Dynamic flexibility is the range of motion achievable during active movements, typically exceeding static ROM.

content: Sports and activities have specific requirements for ROM, and it is likely that optimal levels of flexibility exist for each activity. Therefore there are optimal ranges of flexibility for different sports and activities, and injury risk may be increased when an athlete is outside of this range. It is important to note that both inflexibility and hyperflexibility can result in higher risks of injury. An imbalance in flexibility could predispose the individual to an increased risk of injury.
summary: Optimal flexibility varies by sport, and both inflexibility and hyperflexibility increase injury risk.

content: A number of anatomical and training-related factors affect flexibility. Some factors affecting flexibility, such as joint structure, age, and sex, cannot be significantly altered by training. Activities related to training, including activity level, resistance training, and stretching exercises, can alter flexibility.
summary: Flexibility is influenced by both inherent factors (e.g., joint structure, age) and training variables (e.g., activity level, stretching).

content: The structure of a joint determines its range of motion (ROM). Ball-and-socket joints, such as the hip and shoulder, move in all anatomical planes and have the greatest ROM of all joints. The wrist is an ellipsoidal joint, primarily allowing movement in the sagittal and frontal planes; its ROM is significantly less than that of the shoulder or hip. The knee is a modified hinge joint, with movement primarily in the sagittal plane; its ROM is less than that of either a ball-and-socket joint or the ellipsoidal wrist joint.
summary: Joint structure dictates range of motion, with ball-and-socket joints offering the greatest flexibility, followed by ellipsoidal and hinge joints.

content: The type of joint, the shapes of the joints’ articulating surfaces, and the soft tissues surrounding the joint all affect its ROM.
summary: Joint structure, including articulating surfaces and surrounding soft tissues, influences range of motion.

content: Young people tend to be more flexible than older people. Females tend to be more flexible than males. Differences in flexibility between young men and women may be due in part to structural and anatomical differences and the type and extent of activities performed.
summary: Age and sex influence flexibility, with younger individuals and females generally exhibiting greater flexibility.

content: Older people undergo a process called fibrosis, in which fibrous connective tissue replaces degenerating muscle fibers. This is likely due to inactivity and a tendency to use less of the available ROM during movement. Just as older people can improve strength, however, they can improve flexibility with appropriate exercise.
summary: Age-related decline in flexibility is partly due to fibrosis, but it can be improved with exercise.

content: Tendons, ligaments, fascial sheaths, joint capsules, and skin may limit ROM. Elasticity (the ability to return to original resting length after a passive stretch) and plasticity (the tendency to assume a new and greater length after a passive stretch) of connective tissue are other factors that determine ROM.
summary: Connective tissues, including tendons, ligaments, and fascia, influence range of motion through their elasticity and plasticity.

content: Stretching exercises can positively affect connective tissues by taking advantage of their plastic potential.
summary: Stretching can improve flexibility by enhancing the plasticity of connective tissues.

content: Although a comprehensive and proper resistance training program may increase flexibility, heavy resistance training with limited ROM during the exercises may decrease ROM. To prevent loss of ROM, an athlete should perform exercises that develop both agonist and antagonist muscles and should exercise through the full available ROM of the involved joints.
summary: Resistance training can both enhance and hinder flexibility; full range of motion exercises are crucial for maintaining flexibility.

content: A large increase in muscle bulk may adversely affect ROM by impeding joint movement. An athlete with large biceps and deltoids, for example, may experience difficulty in stretching the triceps, racking a power clean, or holding a bar while performing the front squat.
summary: Excessive muscle mass can limit range of motion due to joint restrictions.

content: Although altering the training program can decrease the amount of muscle bulk, this may not be advisable for large power athletes such as shot-putters or American football offensive linemen. Strength and conditioning professionals should keep the requirements of the athlete’s sport in mind; the need for large muscles may supersede the need for extreme joint mobility.
summary: Balancing muscle mass and flexibility is crucial, with sport-specific needs often prioritizing muscle size over extreme mobility.

content: An active person tends to be more flexible than an inactive one. This is particularly true if the activity includes flexibility exercises but is also true if the person performs other activities, such as resistance training and functional activities.
summary: Regular physical activity, including stretching, enhances flexibility.

content: Both men and women have successfully increased their flexibility as an outcome of a properly designed resistance training program. It is also important to understand that activity level alone will not improve flexibility; stretching exercises are essential if joint flexibility is to be maintained or increased.
summary: Resistance training and stretching are both essential for improving and maintaining flexibility.

content: As with all forms of training, frequency, duration, and intensity are important issues in program design.
summary: Program design for flexibility training should consider frequency, duration, and intensity.

content: Both static and PNF stretching have been shown to increase joint flexibility around the knee, hip, trunk, shoulder, and ankle joints.
summary: Both static and PNF stretching techniques effectively improve joint flexibility.

content: The exact mechanisms responsible for increased flexibility are still unclear.
summary: The precise mechanisms underlying flexibility improvements require further research.

content: The acute effects of stretching on range of motion (ROM) are transient and are greatest immediately after the stretching session.
summary: The immediate benefits of stretching on range of motion are temporary.

content: It is also important to understand that activity level alone will not improve flexibility; stretching exercises are essential if joint flexibility is to be maintained or increased. A stretching program is required for longer-lasting effects. Stretching twice per week for a minimum of five weeks has been shown to significantly improve flexibility.
summary: Flexibility requires stretching exercises, not just activity. Consistent stretching programs are needed for lasting improvements.

content: Both static and PNF stretching have been shown to increase joint flexibility around the knee, hip, trunk, shoulder, and ankle joints. The exact mechanisms responsible for increased flexibility are still unclear.
summary: Both static and PNF stretching improve joint flexibility, but the underlying mechanisms are not fully understood.

content: The acute effects of stretching on range of motion (ROM) are transient and are greatest immediately after the stretching session. The duration of significant improvements in flexibility ranges from 3 minutes to 24 hours.
summary: Stretching's immediate effects on range of motion are temporary, with lasting improvements lasting several hours.

content: As with all forms of training, frequency, duration, and intensity are important issues in program design. 15 to 30 seconds is generally recommended for the duration of a static stretch. 30 seconds has been shown to be more effective than shorter durations for static stretches. Another consideration is the total stretch time throughout a day, which may be as important as the duration of a single stretch.
summary: Frequency, duration, and intensity are crucial factors in designing a stretching program. Optimal static stretch duration is 30 seconds, and total daily stretch time is also important.

content: Athletes should hold stretches at a position of mild discomfort (not pain) during static stretches. Joint integrity should never be compromised in order to increase ROM. All stretching sessions should be preceded by a period of general warm-up to raise muscle temperature.
summary: Stretching should be performed comfortably, without pain, and always preceded by a warm-up to protect joints and prepare muscles.

content: Athletes should be monitored for a loss of sensation or radiating pain during stretching because neural and vascular structures are stretched during flexibility exercises. Stretching should be performed following practice and competition.
summary: Monitor athletes for pain or numbness during stretching, as it can indicate injury. Stretching is beneficial after practice and competition.

content: Post-practice stretching facilitates ROM improvements because of increased muscle temperature. Post-practice stretching should be performed within 5 to 10 minutes after practice.
summary: Post-practice stretching is effective due to elevated muscle temperature, ideally performed within 5-10 minutes after exercise.

content: Increased body temperature increases the elastic properties of collagen within muscles and tendons, which allows for a greater stretch magnitude. Post-practice stretching may also decrease muscle soreness although the evidence on this is ambiguous.
summary: Elevated body temperature improves flexibility by increasing collagen elasticity. Post-practice stretching may also reduce muscle soreness, though research is inconclusive.

content: If increased levels of flexibility are required, additional stretching sessions may be needed. Stretching should be preceded by a thorough warm-up to allow for the increase in muscle temperature necessary for effective stretching. This type of stretching session can be especially useful as a recovery session on the day after a competition.
summary: Additional stretching sessions may be necessary for significant flexibility gains. A thorough warm-up is essential for effective stretching, and it can be beneficial as a recovery tool.

content: Muscle spindles, located within intrafusal muscle fibers that run parallel to extrafusal muscle fibers, monitor changes in muscle length. During a rapid stretching movement, a sensory neuron from the muscle spindle innervates a motor neuron in the spine. The motor neuron then causes a muscle action of the previously stretched extrafusal muscle fibers; this is the stretch reflex.
summary: Muscle spindles sense muscle length changes. Rapid stretching triggers the stretch reflex, causing muscle contraction.

content: Stimulation of the muscle spindle and the subsequent activation of the stretch reflex should be avoided during stretching, as motion will be limited by the reflexive muscle action. If the muscle spindles are not stimulated, the muscle relaxes and allows greater stretch.
summary: Avoid stimulating muscle spindles during stretching to prevent the stretch reflex, which limits flexibility.

content: Because of the very slow movement during static stretching, the stretch reflex is not invoked. Rapid (ballistic and dynamic) stretching movements may stimulate the muscle spindles, causing a stretch reflex.
summary: Static stretching avoids the stretch reflex due to slow movements, while rapid stretching can trigger it.

content: The Golgi tendon organ (GTO), a mechanoreceptor located near the musculotendinous junction, is sensitive to increases in muscular tension. When stimulated, the GTO causes a muscle to reflexively relax.
summary: The Golgi tendon organ (GTO) detects muscle tension and triggers relaxation.

content: Relaxation that occurs in the same muscle that is experiencing increased tension is called autogenic inhibition. Autogenic inhibition is accomplished via active contraction of a muscle immediately before a passive stretch of that same muscle. Tension built up during the active contraction stimulates the GTO, causing a reflexive relaxation of the muscle during the subsequent passive stretch.
summary: Autogenic inhibition involves active muscle contraction followed by passive stretching, leading to muscle relaxation.

content: Relaxation that occurs in the muscle opposing the muscle experiencing the increased tension is called reciprocal inhibition. This is accomplished when one simultaneously contracts the muscle opposing the muscle that is being passively stretched. Here the tension in the contracting muscle stimulates the GTO and causes a simultaneous reflexive relaxation of the stretched muscle.
summary: Reciprocal inhibition involves contracting the opposing muscle while passively stretching another, leading to relaxation in the stretched muscle.

content: An active stretch occurs when the person stretching supplies the force of the stretch.
summary: An active stretch involves the person generating the force for the stretch.

content: Tension built up during the active contraction stimulates the GTO, causing a reflexive relaxation of the muscle during the subsequent passive stretch. This is accomplished when one simultaneously contracts the muscle opposing the muscle that is being passively stretched. Here the tension in the contracting muscle stimulates the GTO and causes a simultaneous reflexive relaxation of the stretched muscle.
summary: This chunk explains the mechanism of reciprocal inhibition, where contracting a muscle opposite to the stretched one relaxes the stretched muscle.

content: An active stretch occurs when the person stretching supplies the force of the stretch. A passive stretch occurs when a partner or stretching machine provides external force to cause or enhance a stretch.
summary: This chunk differentiates between active and passive stretching.

content: A static stretch is slow and constant, with the end position held for 30 seconds. A static stretch includes the relaxation and concurrent elongation of the stretched muscle. Because it is performed slowly, static stretching does not elicit the stretch reflex of the stretched muscle. The likelihood of injury is less than during ballistic stretching. Static stretching is easy to learn and has been shown to effectively improve ROM. Injury to muscles or connective tissue may result if the static stretch is too intense. Static stretching is appropriate for all athletes in a variety of sports for increasing flexibility.
summary: This chunk describes the characteristics, benefits, and potential risks of static stretching.

content: The sitting toe touch is a good example of a static stretch. To perform this stretch statically, the athlete sits on the ground with the lower extremities together and knees extended, leans forward from the waist, and slowly reaches toward the ankles. The athlete gradually increases the intensity of the stretch by leaning forward until he or she feels mild discomfort in the hamstrings or lower back. The athlete holds this position for 30 seconds and then slowly returns to an upright sitting position. The stretch is static because it is performed slowly and the end position is held without movement.
summary: This chunk provides a detailed example of a static stretch, the sitting toe touch.

content: A ballistic stretch typically involves active muscular effort and uses a bouncing-type movement in which the end position is not held. Ballistic stretching is often used in the preexercise warm-up; however, it may injure muscles or connective tissues, especially when there has been a previous injury. Ballistic stretching usually triggers the stretch reflex that does not allow the involved muscles to relax and defeats the purpose of stretching. As an example, consider the sitting toe touch performed as a ballistic stretch rather than a static stretch. The athlete sits on the ground with knees extended, lower extremities together, and upper body perpendicular to the legs. The athlete reaches quickly toward the ankles, bounces at the end position, and immediately returns to a near-vertical upper body position. With each repetition, the end position extends farther than in the preceding repetition. The ballistic stretch is normally not the preferred technique and should not be used by those with low back or hamstring injuries.
summary: This chunk describes ballistic stretching, its potential risks, and a specific example.

content: A dynamic stretch is a type of functionally based stretching exercise that uses sport-specific movements to prepare the body for activity. Dynamic stretching—also called mobility drills—places an emphasis on the movement requirements of the sport or activity rather than on individual muscles. This type of exercise can closely duplicate the movement requirements of a sport or activity. Essentially, one can think of dynamic stretching as actively moving a joint through the ROM required for a sport.
summary: This chunk defines dynamic stretching and its key characteristics.

content: Dynamic and ballistic stretches may appear similar; however, a number of key differences significantly alter the effects of these activities such that dynamic stretching avoids the negative effects associated with ballistic stretching. Dynamic stretching avoids bouncing and is performed in a more controlled manner than ballistic stretching. The result is a controlled ROM that is often smaller than that produced by ballistic stretching. The ability to actively move a joint through a ROM is generally far more sport specific than the ability to statically hold a stretch.
summary: This chunk differentiates dynamic stretching from ballistic stretching, highlighting the advantages of dynamic stretching.

content: Advantages of dynamic stretching include its ability to promote dynamic flexibility and to replicate the movement patterns and ROM required for sport activities. As a consequence, dynamic stretches are increasingly the preferred method of stretching during warm-up, and ideally match the requirements of the specific warm-up. The use of dynamic stretches during the specific part of the warm-up provides a number of key advantages. Dynamic stretching helps promote the temperature-related benefits of the general warm-up, unlike static stretching, which can lead to a decrease in performance. Dynamic stretches are time efficient, which can be important where training time is limited (28, 43).
summary: This chunk outlines the benefits of dynamic stretching, particularly in the context of warm-up routines.

content: Advantages of dynamic stretching include its ability to promote dynamic flexibility and to replicate the movement patterns and ROM required for sport activities. As a consequence, dynamic stretches are increasingly the preferred method of stretching during warm-up, and ideally match the requirements of the specific warm-up. The use of dynamic stretches during the specific part of the warm-up provides a number of key advantages. Dynamic stretching helps promote the temperature-related benefits of the general warm-up, unlike static stretching, which can lead to a decrease in performance. Dynamic stretches are time efficient, which can be important where training time is limited (28, 43). In dynamic stretching, unlike static stretching, the muscle does not relax during the stretch but instead is active through the ROM; this is also more specific to the movements that occur in sport. Even though it is an ideal warm-up activity, dynamic stretching may be less effective than static or PNF stretching at increasing static ROM (6); in situations in which an increased static ROM is needed, static or PNF methods may be preferred.
summary: Dynamic stretching is preferred during warm-ups as it promotes dynamic flexibility, mimics sport movements, and avoids performance decrements associated with static stretching.

content: When one is designing a dynamic stretching program, the starting point should be a careful analysis of the major movement patterns within the given sport and the ROM required for those movements. One can then select exercises that replicate those movements via a series of dynamic stretches. In this way it is possible to achieve a highly specific stretching program. Dynamic stretching provides the opportunity to combine movements (28). This gives the strength and conditioning professional a large number of combinations that can be used to provide variety in the warm-up. Athletes can perform dynamic stretching exercises either for a series of repetitions in the same place (e.g., 10 lunges) or for a series of repetitions to cover a given distance (e.g., lunge for 15 m). Regardless of the method chosen, each drill should start slowly and gradually increase the ROM, the speed, or both during subsequent repetitions or sets. For example, athletes can perform the knee lift exercise over a distance of 15 m, starting at a walk, and build to a skip over subsequent repetitions. This progression provides for an increase in both speed and ROM. An effective warm-up utilizing dynamic stretching can be achieved in 10 to 15 minutes (55).
summary: Dynamic stretching programs should be tailored to the specific sport, incorporating exercises that mimic movement patterns and gradually increasing range of motion and speed.

content: In a dynamic stretch that mirrors a sport skill—such as a sprinter’s knee lift drill—it is important that the stretch also emphasize the key skill factors required for the movement so that the most important mechanics of the drill are reinforced. For example, if the knee lift drill is used in the warm-up, effective body mechanics should be emphasized along with key joint positions such as dorsiflexion of the ankle of the lifted foot. The use of dynamic stretches must always be coordinated with appropriate sport techniques and never compromise proper technique.
summary: Dynamic stretches should reinforce key sport skills and proper technique.

content: Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation (PNF) stretching was originally developed as part of a neuromuscular rehabilitation program designed to relax muscles with increased tone or activity (80). It has since been expanded to athletics as a method of increasing flexibility. Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation techniques are usually performed with a partner and involve both passive movement and active (concentric and isometric) muscle actions. Proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation stretching may be superior to other stretching methods because it facilitates muscular inhibition (18, 30, 47, 64, 70, 76), although evidence for this has not been consistently shown (24). However, PNF stretching is often impractical because most of the stretches require a partner and some expertise. This section serves as an introduction to PNF stretching.
summary: PNF stretching, involving partner-assisted active and passive movements, may enhance flexibility by facilitating muscular inhibition, but its practicality can be limited.

content: During a PNF stretch, three specific muscle actions are used to facilitate the passive stretch. Both isometric and concentric muscle actions of the antagonist (the muscle being stretched) are used before a passive stretch of the antagonist to achieve autogenic inhibition. The isometric muscle action is referred to as hold and the concentric muscle action as contract. A concentric muscle action of the agonist, called agonist contraction, is used during a passive stretch of the antagonist to achieve reciprocal inhibition. Each technique also involves passive, static stretches that are referred to as relax. There are three basic types of PNF stretching techniques: Hold-relax (13, 16, 18, 70, 76) ° Contract-relax (13, 18) ° Hold-relax with agonist contraction (16, 61) ° The PNF techniques are completed in three phases. With each of the three techniques, the first phase incorporates a passive prestretch of 10 seconds. The muscle actions used in the second and third phases differ for the three techniques; the second and third phases give each technique its name. A stretch to improve hamstring flexibility provides an illustration (see figures 13.1 through 13.11).
summary: PNF stretching utilizes three muscle actions (hold, contract, relax) in various combinations to facilitate passive stretching and achieve autogenic and reciprocal inhibition.

content: The hold-relax technique begins with a passive prestretch that is held at the point of mild discomfort for 10 seconds. The partner applies a hip flexion force and instructs the athlete, ‘Hold and don’t let me move the leg’; the athlete ‘holds’ and resists the movement so that an isometric muscle action occurs and is held for 6 seconds. The athlete then relaxes, and a passive stretch is performed and held for 30 seconds. The contract-relax technique also begins with a passive prestretch of the hamstrings that is held at the point of mild discomfort for 10 seconds. The athlete then extends the hip against resistance from the partner so that a concentric muscle action through the full ROM occurs. The athlete then relaxes, and a passive hip flexion stretch is applied and held for 30 seconds. The hold-relax with agonist contraction technique is identical to hold-relax in the first two phases. During the third phase, a concentric action of the agonist is used in addition to the passive stretch to add to the stretch force. Following the isometric hold, the athlete flexes the hip, thereby moving further into the new ROM. The hold-relax with agonist contraction is the most effective PNF stretching technique due to facilitation via both reciprocal and autogenic inhibition.
summary: Three main PNF stretching techniques (hold-relax, contract-relax, and hold-relax with agonist contraction) involve specific muscle actions and phases to enhance flexibility.

content: A warm-up can provide benefits that enhance subsequent performance. Warm-up should be geared toward the particular sport or activity and should consist of both general and specific phases. During warm-up, dynamic stretching techniques are generally more appropriate than static stretching methods. The warm-up should prepare the athlete for the upcoming activity but should not induce undue fatigue. Strength and conditioning professionals should consider each athlete’s unique combination of joint structure, age, sex, and sport requirements when recommending stretching protocols. Optimal flexibility for performance varies from sport to sport. For athletes who need to increase flexibility, static and PNF stretching techniques will allow for an effective increase in range of motion (ROM). These techniques are a key component of an extended training program.
summary: A warm-up should be tailored to the specific sport, incorporate both general and specific phases, and utilize dynamic stretching to prepare the athlete without inducing fatigue.

content: The Side Bend With Straight Arms stretch is located on page 314. The Side Bend With Bent Arm stretch is located on page 314.
summary: Locations of two specific stretches are provided.

content: Optimal flexibility for performance varies from sport to sport. For athletes who need to increase flexibility, static and PNF stretching techniques will allow for an effective increase in range of motion (ROM). These techniques are a key component of an extended training program.
summary: Flexibility needs vary by sport, but static and PNF stretching techniques are effective for increasing range of motion and are essential for long-term training.

content: The Side Bend With Straight Arms stretch is located on page 314. The Side Bend With Bent Arm stretch is located on page 314. The Side Quadriceps Stretch is located on page 315. The Sitting Toe Touch stretch is located on page 316. The Semistraddle (Figure Four) stretch is located on page 316. The Straddle (Spread Eagle) stretch is located on page 317. The Butterfly stretch is located on page 317. The Wall Stretch is located on page 318. The Step Stretch is located on page 318.
summary: This chunk lists various stretches and their corresponding page numbers in a resource.

content: To perform static stretching, get into a position that facilitates relaxation. Move to the point in the ROM where you experience a sensation of mild discomfort. Hold stretches for 30 seconds. Repeat unilateral stretches on both sides.
summary: Instructions for performing static stretches: find a relaxed position, stretch to a mild discomfort point, hold for 30 seconds, and repeat on both sides.

content: If performing partner-assisted PNF stretching, communicate clearly with your partner.
summary: Communication is crucial when performing partner-assisted PNF stretching.

content: The Look Right and Left stretch is located on page 308. The Flexion and Extension stretch is located on page 308. The Straight Arms Behind Back stretch is located on page 309. The Seated Lean-Back stretch is located on page 309. The Behind-Neck Stretch (Chicken Wing) stretch is located on page 310. The Cross Arm in Front of Chest stretch is located on page 311. The Arms Straight Up Above Head (Pillar) stretch is located on page 311.
summary: This chunk lists various stretches and their corresponding page numbers in a resource.

content: The Spinal Twist (Pretzel) stretch is located on page 312. The Semi–Leg Straddle stretch is located on page 312. The Forward Lunge (Fencer) stretch is located on page 313. The Supine Knee Flex stretch is located on page 313.
summary: This chunk lists various stretches and their corresponding page numbers in a resource.

content: Decrease stretch intensity if you experience pain, radiating symptoms, or loss of sensation. Use caution when stretching a hypermobile joint. Avoid combination movements that involve the spine (e.g., extension and lateral flexion). Stabilizing muscles should be active to protect other joints and prevent unwanted movements.
summary: Safety precautions for stretching: reduce intensity if pain occurs, be cautious with hypermobile joints, avoid spine combination movements, and engage stabilizing muscles.

content: Standing, place both arms behind the back. Interlock fingers with palms facing each other. Straighten the elbows fully. Slowly raise the arms, keeping the elbows straight. Keep head upright and neck relaxed. Shoulders and Chest STr AighT Arm S Behind B ACk muscles Affected Anterior deltoid, pectoralis major
summary: Instructions for performing a specific stretch: Straight Arms Behind Back.

content: Stretching the shoulder joints—standing SeATed leAn-B ACk 1. Sitting with the legs straight and arms extended, place palms on the floor about 12 inches (30 cm) behind the hips. 2. Point the fingers away from the body (backward).
summary: Instructions for performing a specific stretch: Seated Lean-Back.

content: Slowly raise the arms, keeping the elbows straight. Keep head upright and neck relaxed. Shoulders and Chest STr AighT Arm S Behind B ACk muscles Affected Anterior deltoid, pectoralis major
summary: This describes a shoulder and chest stretch, focusing on keeping the arms straight and the head and neck relaxed.

content: Stretching the shoulder joints—standing SeATed leAn-B ACk 1. Sitting with the legs straight and arms extended, place palms on the floor about 12 inches (30 cm) behind the hips. 2. Point the fingers away from the body (backward). 3. Slide the hands backward and lean backward. muscles Affected deltoids, pectoralis major
summary: Instructions for a seated shoulder stretch, emphasizing backward leaning and hand placement.

content: Stretching the shoulder joints—seated
summary: This is a general statement indicating a seated shoulder stretch.

content: Stretching the triceps posterior of upper arm Behind-n eCk S TreTC h (Chi Cken Wing) 1. Standing or sitting, abduct the right shoulder and flex the elbow. 2. Reach the right hand down toward the left scapula. 3. Grasp the right elbow with the left hand. 4. Pull the elbow behind the head with the left hand to increase shoulder abduction. muscles Affected Triceps brachii, latissimus dorsi
summary: Instructions for a 'Chicken Wing' stretch targeting the triceps and latissimus dorsi.

content: Stretching the upper back 1. Stand or sit with the left elbow slightly flexed (15°-30°) and the arm across the body (i.e., shoulder in horizontal adduction). 2. Grasp the upper arm just above the elbow, placing the right hand on the posterior side of the upper arm. 3. Pull the left arm across the chest (toward the right) with the right hand.
summary: Instructions for an upper back stretch involving pulling the arm across the chest.

Slowly straighten the arms above the head with the palms up. 3. Continue to reach upward with the hands and arms. 4. While continuing to reach upward, slowly reach slightly backward. muscles Affected posterior deltoid, rhomboids, middle trapezius
summary: Instructions for a 'Pillar' stretch, focusing on reaching arms straight up and slightly backward.

content: The exercise 'SpinAl T WiST (preT zel)' involves pushing the right knee to the left with the left elbow while turning the shoulders and head to the right. The muscles affected by the 'SpinAl T WiST (preT zel)' exercise are the internal oblique, external oblique, piriformis, and erector spinae.
summary: Description of the 'Spinal Twist' exercise and the muscles it targets.

content: To perform the 'Stretching the low back and sides' exercise, sit with the legs straight and the upper body nearly vertical, place the right foot to the left side of the left knee, place the back of the left elbow on the right side of the right knee, and place the right palm on the floor 12 to 16 inches (30-40 cm) behind the hips. The 'Stretching the low back and sides' exercise affects the erector spinae muscle.
summary: Instructions and muscle targeted for the 'Stretching the low back and sides' exercise.

content: To perform the 'Stretching the low back from a seated position' exercise, sit with knees flexed 30° to 50°, let the legs totally relax, point the knees outward, and lean forward from the waist and reach forward with extended arms. Bending the knees and relaxing the legs during the 'Stretching the low back from a seated position' exercise decreases hamstring involvement and increases lower back stretch.
summary: Instructions for a seated low back stretch, emphasizing knee flexion and relaxation.

content: The exercise 'f or WArd lU nge (f en Cer)' stretches the iliopsoas and rectus femoris muscles. To perform the 'f or WArd lU nge (f en Cer)' exercise, stand, take a long step forward with the right leg and flex the right knee until it is directly over the right foot, keep the right foot flat on the floor, keep the back leg straight, keep the back foot pointed in the same direction as the front foot, keep the torso upright, and rest the hands on the hips or the front leg.
summary: Description of the 'Forward Lunge' exercise and the muscles it stretches.

content: To perform the 'SUpine k nee f lex' exercise, lie on the back with legs straight, flex the right knee and hip, bringing the thigh toward the chest, and place both hands behind the thigh and continue to pull the thigh toward the chest. The 'SUpine k nee f lex' exercise affects the hip extensors (gluteus maximus and hamstrings) muscles.
summary: Instructions for the 'Supine Knee Flex' exercise and the muscles it targets.

content: To perform the 'Side Bend WiTh S Tr AighT Arm S' exercise, stand with feet approximately 16 inches (40 cm) apart, interlace the fingers with the palms away from the torso and facing outward, reach upward with straight arms, and keeping the arms straight, lean from the waist to the left side. The 'Side Bend WiTh S Tr AighT Arm S' exercise stretches the external oblique, latissimus dorsi, and serratus anterior muscles.
summary: Instructions for the 'Side Bend with Straight Arms' exercise and the muscles it stretches.

content: To perform the 'Side Bend WiTh BenT Arm' exercise, stand with feet approximately 16 inches (40 cm) apart, flex the right elbow and raise the elbow above the head, reach the right hand down toward the left shoulder, grasp the right elbow with the left hand, pull the elbow behind the head, keeping the arm bent, lean from the waist to the left side. The 'Side Bend WiTh BenT Arm' exercise stretches the external oblique, latissimus dorsi, serratus anterior, and triceps brachii muscles.
summary: Instructions for the 'Side Bend with Bent Arm' exercise and the muscles it stretches.

content: The stretch targets the anterior of the thigh and hip flexor. The Side Quadriceps Stretch targets the quadriceps and iliopsoas. To perform the Side Quadriceps Stretch, lie on the left side with both legs straight. Place the left forearm flat on the floor and the upper arm perpendicular to the floor.
summary: Description of two stretches: one targeting the anterior thigh and hip flexor, and the other targeting the quadriceps and iliopsoas.

content: The 'Side Bend WiTh BenT Arm' exercise stretches the external oblique, latissimus dorsi, serratus anterior, and triceps brachii muscles.
summary: The 'Side Bend WiTh BenT Arm' exercise targets multiple muscles in the back and arm.

content: The Side Quadriceps Stretch targets the quadriceps and iliopsoas. To perform the Side Quadriceps Stretch, lie on the left side with both legs straight. Place the left forearm flat on the floor and the upper arm perpendicular to the floor. Place the left forearm at a 45° angle to the torso. Flex the right leg (knee) with the heel of the right foot moving toward the buttocks. Grasp the front of the ankle with the right hand and pull toward the buttocks. The stretch occurs as a result of knee flexion and hip extension.
summary: The Side Quadriceps Stretch targets the quadriceps and iliopsoas muscles.

content: The Sitting Toe to Touch targets the hamstrings, erector spinae, and gastrocnemius. To perform the Sitting Toe to Touch, sit with the upper body nearly vertical and legs straight. Lean forward using hip flexion and grasp the toes with each hand. Slightly pull the toes toward the upper body and pull the chest toward the legs. If you have limited flexibility, try to grasp the ankles.
summary: The Sitting Toe to Touch targets the hamstrings, erector spinae, and gastrocnemius.

content: The Semi-Stddle targets the gastrocnemius, hamstrings, and erector spinae. To perform the Semi-Stddle, sit with the upper body nearly vertical and legs straight. Place the sole of the left foot on the inner side of the right knee. The outer side of the left leg should be resting on the floor. Lean forward using hip flexion and grasp the toes of the right foot with the right hand. Slightly pull the toes toward the upper body as the chest is also pulled toward the right leg.
summary: The Semi-Stddle targets the gastrocnemius, hamstrings, and erector spinae.

content: The Stretch targets the gastrocnemius, hamstrings, erector spinae, hip adductors, and sartorius. To perform the Stretch, sit with the upper body nearly vertical and legs straight. Abduct the hips, spreading the legs as far as possible. With both hands, grasp the toes of the right foot and pull on the toes slightly while pulling the chest toward the right leg. Repeat toward the center by grasping the right toes with the right hand and the left toes with the left hand. Pull the torso forward and toward the ground.
summary: The Stretch targets multiple muscle groups, including the gastrocnemius, hamstrings, and hip adductors.

content: The Butterfly targets the hip adductors and sartorius. To perform the Butterfly, sit with the upper body nearly vertical and legs straight.
