Flex both knees, bringing the soles of the feet together. Pull the feet toward the body. Place the hands on the feet and the elbows on the legs. Pull the torso slightly forward as the elbows push down, causing hip abduction.
summary: The Butterfly targets the hip adductors and sartorius.

content: The calf wall stretch involves standing facing a wall with feet shoulder-width apart and toes approximately 2 feet (0.6 m) from the wall. In the calf wall stretch, lean forward, placing the hands on the wall. For the calf wall stretch, step back approximately 2 feet (0.6 m) with the stretch leg while flexing the opposite knee. Extend the knee of the stretch leg and lower the heel to the floor to apply the stretch in the calf wall stretch.
summary: The calf wall stretch targets the gastrocnemius, soleus, and Achilles tendon.

content: The step stretch involves placing the ball of one foot on the edge of a step or board 3 to 4 inches (8-10 cm) high, with the other foot flat on the step. In the step stretch, lower the heel of the foot on the edge of the step as far as possible with straight legs. Repeat the step stretch with the other leg.
summary: The step stretch targets the gastrocnemius and soleus.

content: The calf wall stretch targets the gastrocnemius, soleus, and Achilles tendon. Extend the knee of the stretch leg and lower the heel to the floor to apply the stretch in the calf wall stretch. The step stretch involves placing the ball of one foot on the edge of a step or board 3 to 4 inches (8-10 cm) high, with the other foot flat on the step. In the step stretch, lower the heel of the foot on the edge of the step as far as possible with straight legs. Repeat the step stretch with the other leg. To stretch the Achilles tendon, complete the same stretch with 10° of knee flexion. The step stretch targets the gastrocnemius, soleus, and Achilles tendon.
summary: This chunk describes calf stretches: the calf wall stretch and the step stretch, targeting the gastrocnemius, soleus, and Achilles tendon.

content: The walking knee lift targets the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, and quadriceps. The forward lunge with elbow to instep targets the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, and quadriceps. The heel-to-toe walk targets the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, and quadriceps. The walking over and under targets the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, and quadriceps. The lunge walk targets the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, and quadriceps. The lunge with twist targets the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, and quadriceps.
summary: This chunk lists various exercises that target the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, and quadriceps: walking knee lift, forward lunge with elbow to instep, heel-to-toe walk, walking over and under, lunge walk, and lunge with twist.

content: The arm swings target the latissimus dorsi, teres major, anterior and posterior deltoids, and pectoralis major. To perform arm swings, stand erect and raise the arms in front of the body until they are parallel to the floor. While walking over a prescribed distance, swing the arms in unison to the right so the left arm is in front of the chest, the fingers of the left hand are pointing directly lateral to the left shoulder, and the right arm is behind the body. Immediately reverse the direction of movement to swing the arms in unison to the left. Movement should occur only at the shoulder joints (i.e., keep torso and head facing forward). Alternate swinging the arms in unison to the right and left.
summary: This chunk describes the arm swing exercise, which targets the latissimus dorsi, teres major, anterior and posterior deltoids, and pectoralis major.

content: To perform an inchworm, stand erect with the feet placed shoulder-width apart. While slightly flexing the knees, bend forward at the waist and place hands shoulder-width apart flat on the floor. The weight of the body should be shifted back (i.e., not directly over the hands), with the buttocks high in the air—imagine making an inverted V with the body. Move the hands alternately forward, as if taking short steps with the hands, until the body is in the push-up position. Walk the legs to the hands using small steps while keeping the knees slightly flexed. Repeat the motion over a prescribed distance.
summary: This chunk describes the inchworm exercise, targeting the erector spinae, gastrocnemius, gluteus maximus, hamstrings, soleus, and anterior tibialis.

content: The forward lunge involves standing erect with the feet parallel to each other and shoulder-width apart. Take an exaggerated step directly forward with the left leg, planting the left foot flat on the floor pointing straight ahead. Allow the left hip and knee to slowly flex, keeping the left knee directly over the left foot. Slightly flex the right knee and lower it until it is 1 to 2 inches (3-5 cm) above the floor; the right foot should be pointed straight ahead. Balance the weight evenly between the ball of the right foot and the entire left foot. Keep the torso perpendicular to the floor by “sitting back” on the right leg. Forcefully push off of the floor by extending the left hip and knee. Pick up the right foot and place it next to the left foot; do not stutter-step forward. Stand erect, pause, and then step forward with the right leg, progressing forward with each step.
summary: This chunk describes the forward lunge exercise, detailing the steps and form.

content: The Walking Knee Lift exercise begins with standing erect with feet parallel and shoulder-width apart. In the Walking Knee Lift, step forward with the left leg and flex the right hip and knee to move the right thigh upward toward the chest. Grasp the front of the right knee/upper shin and use the arms to pull the right knee up further and to squeeze the thigh against the chest during the Walking Knee Lift. Dorsiflex the left foot as the right hip and knee are flexed during the Walking Knee Lift. Keeping the torso erect, pause for a moment, then proceed to step down with the right leg during the Walking Knee Lift.
summary: This chunk describes the Walking Knee Lift exercise, outlining the steps and form.

content: The Walking Knee Lift exercise begins with standing erect with feet parallel and shoulder-width apart. In the Walking Knee Lift, step forward with the left leg and flex the right hip and knee to move the right thigh upward toward the chest. Grasp the front of the right knee/upper shin and use the arms to pull the right knee up further and to squeeze the thigh against the chest during the Walking Knee Lift. Dorsiflex the left foot as the right hip and knee are flexed during the Walking Knee Lift. Keeping the torso erect, pause for a moment, then proceed to step down with the right leg during the Walking Knee Lift. Shift the body weight to the right leg and repeat the motion with the left leg during the Walking Knee Lift. Progress forward with each step, increasing the ROM and speed on subsequent steps during the Walking Knee Lift. The Walking Knee Lift primarily targets the gluteus maximus and hamstrings.
summary: The Walking Knee Lift is a dynamic exercise targeting the glutes and hamstrings, performed by stepping forward and lifting the knee towards the chest.

content: The Lunge With Twist exercise begins with standing erect with feet parallel and shoulder-width apart. Take an exaggerated step directly forward with the right leg, planting the right foot flat on the floor pointing straight ahead during the Lunge With Twist. Allow the right hip and knee to slowly flex, keeping the right knee directly over the right foot during the Lunge With Twist. Slightly flex the left knee and lower it until it is 1 to 2 inches (3-5 cm) above the floor; the left foot should be pointed straight ahead during the Lunge With Twist. Reach up high with the left arm and bend the torso laterally toward the right leg during the Lunge With Twist. Return to an erect torso position, and then forcefully push off the floor by extending the right hip and knee during the Lunge With Twist. Pick up the left foot and place it next to the right foot; do not stutter-step forward during the Lunge With Twist. Stand erect, pause, and then step forward with the left leg, progressing forward with each step during the Lunge With Twist. The Lunge With Twist primarily targets the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, latissimus dorsi, internal and external oblique, and rectus femoris.
summary: The Lunge With Twist is a compound exercise that combines a lunge with a torso twist, engaging multiple muscle groups.

content: The Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep exercise begins with standing erect with feet parallel and shoulder-width apart. Take an exaggerated step directly forward with the left leg, planting the left foot flat on the floor pointing straight ahead during the Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep. Allow the left hip and knee to slowly flex, keeping the left knee directly over the left foot during the Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep. Slightly flex the right knee and lower it until it is 1 to 2 inches (3-5 cm) above the floor; the right foot should be pointed straight ahead during the Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep. Lean forward, bringing the left arm forward and touching the left elbow to the instep of the left foot; the right hand may be placed on the floor to maintain balance during the Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep. Lean back to return to an erect torso position, and then forcefully push off the floor by extending the left hip and knee during the Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep. Pick up the right foot and place it next to the left foot; do not stutter-step forward during the Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep. Stand erect, pause, and then step forward with the right leg, progressing forward with each step during the Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep. The Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep primarily targets the biceps femoris, erector spinae, gastrocnemius, gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, latissimus dorsi, internal and external oblique, quadriceps, rectus femoris, and soleus.
summary: The Forward Lunge With Elbows To Instep is a lunge variation that emphasizes hip flexor and hamstring engagement, incorporating a forward lean and elbow touch.

content: The Heel-To-Toe Walk exercise begins with standing erect with feet parallel and shoulder-width apart. Take a small step forward with the right leg; place the heel of the right foot on the ground first and then continue to dorsiflex the foot during the Heel-To-Toe Walk. Immediately roll forward and rise up as high as possible onto the ball of the right foot during the Heel-To-Toe Walk. Swing the left leg forward in order to take another small step during the Heel-To-Toe Walk. Repeat with the left leg, progressing forward with each step during the Heel-To-Toe Walk. The Heel-To-Toe Walk primarily targets the gastrocnemius, soleus, and anterior tibialis.
summary: The Heel-To-Toe Walk is a gait exercise that focuses on ankle mobility and strength, targeting the calf muscles and tibialis anterior.

content: The Walking Over And Under exercise mimics the motion of trying to step laterally over and then under a hurdle. The Walking Over And Under exercise begins with standing erect with feet parallel and shoulder-width apart. Flex the left hip and knee and then abduct the left thigh until it is parallel to the floor during the Walking Over And Under. Step laterally to the left as if stepping laterally over an imaginary hurdle during the Walking Over And Under. Place the left foot firmly on the ground, shift the body weight to the left leg, and then proceed to lift the right leg over the imaginary hurdle during the Walking Over And Under. After lifting the right leg over the hurdle and placing the right foot firmly on the ground, stand erect, pause, and then flex the hips and knees and dorsiflex the ankles to assume a full squat position during the Walking Over And Under. Extend the left leg laterally, as if performing a lateral lunge during the Walking Over And Under. Keeping the body weight low, move the body laterally as if ducking under an imaginary hurdle during the Walking Over And Under.
summary: The Walking Over And Under exercise simulates stepping over and under an obstacle, engaging multiple leg muscles and promoting coordination.

content: Step laterally to the left as if stepping laterally over an imaginary hurdle during the Walking Over And Under. Place the left foot firmly on the ground, shift the body weight to the left leg, and then proceed to lift the right leg over the imaginary hurdle during the Walking Over And Under. After lifting the right leg over the hurdle and placing the right foot firmly on the ground, stand erect, pause, and then flex the hips and knees and dorsiflex the ankles to assume a full squat position during the Walking Over And Under. Extend the left leg laterally, as if performing a lateral lunge during the Walking Over And Under. Keeping the body weight low, move the body laterally as if ducking under an imaginary hurdle during the Walking Over And Under. Stand erect, pause, and repeat the motion in the opposite direction during the Walking Over And Under.
summary: The Walking Over And Under is a complex exercise involving lateral steps, squats, and lunges, targeting multiple muscle groups.

content: The Walking Over And Under primarily targets the hip abductors, hip adductors, gastrocnemius, gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, rectus femoris, and soleus.
summary: The Walking Over And Under primarily targets the hip abductors, hip adductors, gastrocnemius, gluteus maximus, hamstrings, iliopsoas, rectus femoris, and soleus.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat is a compound exercise primarily targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, with deeper squats potentially increasing glute activation.

content: Seated or supine exercises performed on a bench require a specific posture. The athlete should position the body to achieve a five-point body contact position. The five-point body contact position consists of the head placed firmly on the bench or back pad, shoulders and upper back placed firmly and evenly on the bench or back pad, buttocks placed evenly on the bench or seat, right foot flat on the floor, and left foot flat on the floor. Establishing and maintaining this five-point body contact position at the beginning and throughout the movement phases promotes maximal stability and spinal support.
summary: Proper posture during seated or supine bench exercises involves a five-point body contact position for stability and spinal support.

content: Exercises performed while standing typically require that the feet be positioned slightly wider than hip-width with the heels and balls of the feet in contact with the floor. Seated or supine exercises performed on a bench usually require a five-point body contact position.
summary: Foot placement for standing exercises is slightly wider than hip-width, while seated or supine exercises often require a five-point body contact position.

content: Cam-, pulley-, or lever-based exercise machines that have an axis of rotation require specific positioning of the athlete’s body, arms, or legs for reasons of safety and optimal execution. To align the primary joint of the body involved in the exercise with the axis of the machine, it may be necessary to move the seat; the ankle or arm roller pad; or the thigh, chest, or back pad. Before performing machine exercises, adjust seat and pads to position the body joint primarily involved in the exercise in alignment with the machine’s axis of rotation.
summary: Machine exercises require proper positioning to align the body joint with the machine's axis for safety and effectiveness.

content: Ideally, an exercise’s full ROM should mimic the full ROM of the involved joint or joints in order for the greatest improvements to occur, but sometimes this is not possible (trailing leg knee joint during a lunge) or recommended (using intervertebral joints during a squat). Repetitions performed in a slow, controlled manner increase the likelihood that full ROM can be reached. However, when power or quick-lift exercises (e.g., power clean, push jerk, and snatch) are performed, an effort should be made to accelerate the bar to a maximal speed while still maintaining control.
summary: Exercises should ideally cover the full range of motion, but modifications may be necessary depending on the exercise. Slow, controlled repetitions enhance full ROM, while power exercises prioritize speed.

content: The most strenuous movement of a repetition—typically soon after the transition from the eccentric phase to the concentric phase—is referred to as the sticking point. Professionals should typically instruct athletes to exhale through the sticking point and inhale during the less stressful phase of the repetition. The sticking point of the biceps curl exercise occurs about midway through the upward movement phase (concentric elbow flexion). This breathing strategy applies to most resistance training exercises.
summary: The sticking point, the most strenuous part of a repetition, is best addressed with exhalation, while the less stressful phase involves inhalation. This breathing pattern applies to most exercises.

content: For experienced and well-resistance-trained athletes performing structural exercises (those that load the vertebral column and therefore place stress on it) with high loads, the Valsalva maneuver can be helpful for maintaining proper vertebral alignment and support. The Valsalva maneuver involves expiring against a closed glottis, which, when combined with contracting the abdomen and rib cage muscles, creates rigid compartments of fluid in the lower torso and air in the upper torso (i.e., the 'fluid ball'). The advantage of the Valsalva maneuver is that it increases the rigidity of the entire torso to aid in supporting the vertebral column, which in turn reduces the associated compressive forces on the disks during lifting. The Valsalva maneuver also helps to establish the 'flat-back' and erect upper torso position described in the technique checklists for certain exercises.
summary: The Valsalva maneuver, involving breath-holding and abdominal contraction, can assist experienced athletes during heavy structural exercises by increasing torso rigidity and supporting the spine.

content: The resulting increase in intra-abdominal pressure from the Valsalva maneuver has potentially detrimental side effects, such as dizziness, disorientation, excessively high blood pressure, and blackouts. The breath-holding phase during the Valsalva maneuver should be transient, only about 1 to 2 seconds (at most). Even a very well-trained individual should not extend the length of the breath-holding phase, as blood pressure can quickly rise to triple resting levels. Strength and conditioning professionals involved in conducting 1-repetition maximum (1RM) tests in, for example, the squat, deadlift, hip sled, leg press, shoulder press, or power clean, need to be aware of the advantages and disadvantages of coaching athletes in the Valsalva maneuver. It is recommended that an athlete not extend the breath-holding period during the Valsalva maneuver.
summary: While the Valsalva maneuver can be beneficial, it carries risks like dizziness, high blood pressure, and blackouts. Breath-holding should be brief (1-2 seconds) and professionals should be aware of its potential downsides.

content: For most exercises, exhale through the sticking point of the concentric phase and inhale during the eccentric phase. Experienced and well-trained athletes may want to use the Valsalva maneuver when performing structural exercises to assist in maintaining proper vertebral alignment and support. The use of a weight belt can contribute to injury-free training. It is recommended that a weight belt be worn for exercises that place stress on the lower back and during sets that use near-maximal or maximal loads.
summary: General breathing guidelines involve exhaling during the sticking point and inhaling during the eccentric phase. Experienced athletes may use the Valsalva maneuver for structural exercises, and weight belts are recommended for heavy, lower back-loading exercises.

content: It is recommended that an athlete not extend the breath-holding period during the Valsalva maneuver. Experienced and well-trained athletes may want to use the Valsalva maneuver when performing structural exercises to assist in maintaining proper vertebral alignment and support.
summary: Guidelines for Valsalva maneuver use during exercise, emphasizing safety and potential benefits for experienced athletes.

content: For most exercises, exhale through the sticking point of the concentric phase and inhale during the eccentric phase.
summary: Breathing technique recommendations for most exercises, emphasizing exhalation during the exertion phase and inhalation during the lowering phase.

content: The use of a weight belt can contribute to injury-free training. It is recommended that a weight belt be worn for exercises that place stress on the lower back and during sets that use near-maximal or maximal loads. Adopting the strategy of wearing a weight belt for exercises that place stress on the lower back and during sets that use near-maximal or maximal loads may reduce the risk of lower back injury when combined with proper lifting and spotting techniques. A weight belt is not needed for exercises that do not stress the lower back or for those that do stress the lower back but involve light loads. Typically an athlete should wear a weight belt when performing exercises that place stress on the lower back and during sets that involve near-maximal or maximal loads.
summary: Guidelines for weight belt usage, emphasizing its benefits for exercises stressing the lower back and heavy loads, while not being necessary for lighter exercises.

content: Often an athlete needs to lift a barbell or dumbbells off the floor, either while performing an exercise or in order to move the bar to a different location in the strength and conditioning facility. The position of the feet and back shown in figure 14.3 enables the leg muscles to make a major contribution as the bar is lifted off the floor. Keeping the bar close to the body and the back flat during the upward pull of the bar helps avoid excessive strain on the lower back.
summary: Technique for lifting barbells and dumbbells off the floor, emphasizing proper foot placement, back position, and minimizing lower back strain.

content: A spotter is someone who assists in the execution of an exercise to help protect the athlete from injury. A spotter may also serve to motivate the athlete and help in the completion of forced repetitions (also referred to as partner-assisted reps), but the spotter’s primary responsibility is the safety of the athlete being spotted. The spotter must realize that poor execution of this responsibility may result in serious injury, not only to the athlete being spotted, but also to the spotter. Although partner-assisted actions are valuable in helping an athlete benefit from training, the importance of promoting safety cannot be overemphasized.
summary: Role of a spotter, emphasizing safety as the primary responsibility, and the importance of proper technique to prevent injuries.

content: Free weight exercises performed over the head (e.g., barbell shoulder press) or with the bar on the back (e.g., back squat), racked anteriorly on the shoulders or on the clavicles (e.g., front squat), or over the face (e.g., bench press, lying triceps extension) are more challenging for the athlete to correctly execute than those in which the bar or dumbbells are held or raised at the sides or in front (e.g., lateral shoulder raise or barbell biceps curl, respectively) and therefore should involve one or more spotters. The overhead, bar-on-the-back or bar-on-the-front-shoulders, and over-the-face exercises (especially using dumbbells) also require more skill on the part of the spotter and are potentially the most dangerous to the athlete.
summary: Exercises requiring spotters due to increased risk of injury, particularly those involving the bar over the head, back, or face.

content: Spotting dumbbell exercises typically requires more skill than spotting barbell exercises because there is an additional piece of equipment to observe and spot. Power exercises should not be spotted. With the exception of power exercises, free weight exercises performed with a bar moving over the head, positioned on the back, racked on the front of the shoulders, or passing over the face typically require one or more spotters. Ideally, to promote the safety of the lifter, the spotters, and others nearby, overhead exercises and those involving the bar on the back or front shoulders should be performed inside a power rack with the crossbars in place at an appropriate height.
summary: Guidelines for spotting different types of exercises, emphasizing the increased difficulty of spotting dumbbells and the avoidance of spotting power exercises.

content: All plates, bars, locks, and weight-plate trees must be cleared from the lifting area so that they cannot be tripped over or run into. Athletes who are not lifting should be instructed to stay clear of the lifting area. Since the loads lifted in these exercises can be considerable, to exert sufficient leverage the spotter or spotters should be at least as strong and at least as tall as the athlete who is lifting.
summary: Safety precautions for the lifting area, including clearing obstacles, keeping non-lifters away, and ensuring spotters are strong and tall enough.

content: Out-of-the-rack exercises (e.g., forward step lunge or step-up) with heavy weights can result in serious injury. These exercises should be executed only by well-trained and skilled athletes and spotted by experienced professionals. When spotting over-the-face barbell exercises, it is important for the spotter to grasp the bar with an alternated grip, usually narrower than the athlete’s grip. Because of the bar’s curved trajectory in some exercises (e.g. lying triceps extension, barbell pull-over), the spotter will use an alternated grip to pick up the bar and return it to the floor but a supinated grip to spot the bar. This helps ensure that the bar does not roll out of the spotter’s hands and onto the athlete’s face or neck.
summary: Safety considerations for out-of-the-rack exercises and spotting over-the-face barbell exercises, emphasizing the need for skilled athletes and spotters, and proper grip techniques.

content: For dumbbell exercises, it is important to spot as close to the dumbbells as possible or, in a few exercises, to spot the dumbbell itself. Although some individuals advocate spotting dumbbell movements by placing the hands on the athlete’s upper arms or elbows (figure 14.4a), this technique may lead to injury. If the athlete’s elbows “collapse” (i.e., flex), the spotter will not be in a position to stop the dumbbells from striking the athlete’s face or chest. If the athlete’s elbows “collapse” (i.e., flex), the spotter will not be in a position to stop the dumbbells from striking the athlete’s face or chest. Spotting at the forearms near the wrists (figure 14.4b) provides a safer technique. Note that for some exercises (e.g., dumbbell pullover and overhead dumbbell triceps extension), it is necessary to spot with hands on the dumbbell itself.
summary: Specific techniques for spotting dumbbell exercises, emphasizing the importance of proximity to the dumbbells and avoiding spotting on the athlete's arms.

content: Spotting is recommended in exercises previously described but not in power exercises. Instead of spotting power exercises, the strength and conditioning professional should teach athletes how to get away from an unmanageable bar. Athletes should push the bar away or drop it if they miss the bar in front. Athletes should release the bar and jump forward if they lose the bar behind the head.
summary: Guidelines for spotting, emphasizing the avoidance of spotting power exercises and teaching athletes safe escape techniques.

content: Spotting is recommended in exercises previously described but not in power exercises. Instead of spotting power exercises, the strength and conditioning professional should teach athletes how to get away from an unmanageable bar. Athletes should push the bar away or drop it if they miss the bar in front. Athletes should release the bar and jump forward if they lose the bar behind the head.
summary: Guidelines for spotting exercises, emphasizing that power exercises require different techniques than traditional lifts.

content: The surrounding area or platform should be cleared of other athletes and equipment before power exercises are performed. With heavier loads, the likelihood of injury increases, as does the severity of the injury should an accident occur. Another spotter must become involved once the load exceeds the spotter’s ability to effectively protect the athlete and themselves.
summary: Safety precautions for power exercises, highlighting the importance of a clear area and adequate spotting based on load.

content: One spotter is preferred if they can easily handle the load because two or more spotters must coordinate their actions with those of the athlete. As the number of spotters increases, so does the chance that an error in timing or technique may occur. Communication is the responsibility of both the spotter and the athlete.
summary: Considerations for the number of spotters, emphasizing the importance of coordination and communication.

content: Before beginning a set, the athlete should tell the spotter how the bar will initially be handled, how many repetitions will be performed, and when they are ready to move the bar into position. If spotters do not have this information, they may take control of the bar improperly, too soon, or too late and consequently disrupt the exercise or injure the athlete.
summary: Communication protocols between athletes and spotters before starting a set.

content: A liftoff refers to moving the bar from the upright supports to a position in which the athlete can begin the exercise. Usually the spotter helps place the barbell or dumbbells into the athlete’s hands while the elbows are extended and helps to move the barbell or dumbbells to the proper starting position. If a liftoff is needed or requested, the athlete and spotter need to agree in advance on a verbal signal (e.g., the command ‘up’ or ‘I will give it to you on the count of three’) Typically, the athlete signals that they are ready; the spotter says, ‘One, two, three,’ and on ‘three’ the bar is moved into position.
summary: Procedure for a liftoff, emphasizing pre-agreed signals and coordination between athlete and spotter.

content: Liftoffs are normally used in the bench press (off the supports), shoulder press (off the supports or the shoulders), and squatting exercises if the supports are too low and nonadjustable. When two spotters are involved and the athlete wants a liftoff, as with a bench press, one spotter should assist with the liftoff and then quickly move to the end of the bar to spot (the other spotter is already at the other end). The spotter providing the liftoff must be sure that the athlete has complete control of the bar at the conclusion of the liftoff.
summary: Specific exercises where liftoffs are commonly used and the proper technique for two-spotter liftoffs.

content: If two spotters are used, both should help the athlete place the bar back onto the supports on completion of the exercise. Knowing how much and when to help an athlete is an important aspect of spotting and requires experience. Most athletes typically need just enough help to successfully complete a repetition (i.e., a partner-assisted action); other times they might need the spotter to handle the entire load.
summary: Spotting procedures at the end of a set and the importance of experience in determining the level of assistance needed.

content: At the first indication that a repetition will be missed, the athlete should quickly ask or signal the spotter for help, and the spotter needs to immediately attempt to provide the amount of assistance needed. If the athlete cannot contribute anything to the completion of the repetition, the athlete should immediately tell the spotter to ‘take it’ or use some other phrase agreed upon before the lift.
summary: Spotting techniques when a repetition is in danger of being missed, emphasizing clear communication and immediate assistance.

content: Regardless of when or why the spotter is needed to assist, they should take the bar—if possible—from the athlete quickly and smoothly, trying to avoid abrupt changes in the amount of load being handled by the athlete. The athlete should try to stay with the bar until it is racked or placed safely on the floor. This helps to protect both the spotter and athlete from injury.
summary: Proper technique for spotters to take over the bar and the importance of athlete staying with the bar for safety.

content: The spotting guidelines provided in the exercise technique checklists are appropriate for a typical training environment. Spotting procedures may vary when excessively heavy loads are being used—such as 1RM attempts—because more spotters are typically needed.
summary: General guidelines for spotting and adjustments for heavier loads like 1RM attempts.

content: Note that for some exercises (e.g., dumbbell pullover and overhead dumbbell triceps extension), it is necessary to spot with hands on the dumbbell itself.
summary: Specific spotting considerations for exercises requiring hand-on dumbbell spotting.

content: The Hip Sled (Machine) exercise is found on page 349. The Back Squat exercise is found on page 350. The Front Squat exercise is found on page 352. The Forward Step Lunge exercise is found on page 354. The Step-Up exercise is found on page 356. The Good Morning exercise is found on page 358. The Deadlift exercise is found on page 359. The Stiff-Leg Deadlift exercise is found on page 360. The Leg (Knee) Extension (Machine) exercise is found on page 361. The Leg (Knee) Curl (Machine) exercise is found on page 362.
summary: List of exercises and their corresponding page numbers in a manual.

content: The Good Morning exercise is found on page 358. The Deadlift exercise is found on page 359. The Stiff-Leg Deadlift exercise is found on page 360.
summary: Locations of three exercises targeting the posterior chain: Good Morning, Deadlift, and Stiff-Leg Deadlift.

content: The Leg (Knee) Extension (Machine) exercise is found on page 361. The Leg (Knee) Curl (Machine) exercise is found on page 362.
summary: Locations of two isolation exercises targeting the quadriceps and hamstrings: Leg Extension and Leg Curl.

content: The Shoulder Press (Machine) exercise is found on page 363. The Seated Barbell Shoulder Press (and Dumbbell Variation) exercise is found on page 364.
summary: Locations of two exercises targeting the shoulders: Shoulder Press and Seated Barbell Shoulder Press.

content: The Upright Row exercise is found on page 366. The Lateral Shoulder Raise exercise is found on page 367.
summary: Locations of two exercises targeting the shoulders: Upright Row and Lateral Shoulder Raise.

content: The Lying Barbell Triceps Extension exercise is found on page 368. The Triceps Pushdown (Machine) exercise is found on page 369.
summary: Locations of two exercises targeting the triceps: Lying Barbell Triceps Extension and Triceps Pushdown.

content: The Push Press (and Push Jerk Variation) exercise is found on page 370. The Power Clean (and Hang Clean Variation) exercise is found on page 372. The Snatch exercise is found on page 374.
summary: Locations of three compound exercises: Push Press, Power Clean, and Snatch.

content: The Bent-Knee Sit-Up exercise is found on page 333. The Abdominal Crunch exercise is found on page 333.
summary: Locations of two core exercises: Bent-Knee Sit-Up and Abdominal Crunch.

content: The Bent-Over Row exercise is found on page 334. The Lat Pulldown (Machine) exercise is found on page 335. The Seated Row (Machine) exercise is found on page 336. The Low-Pulley Seated Row (Machine) exercise is found on page 337.
summary: Locations of four exercises targeting the back: Bent-Over Row, Lat Pulldown, Seated Row, and Low-Pulley Seated Row.

content: The Barbell Biceps Curl exercise is found on page 338. The Hammer Curl exercise is found on page 339.
summary: Locations of two exercises targeting the biceps: Barbell Biceps Curl and Hammer Curl.

content: The Standing Calf (Heel) Raise (Machine) exercise is found on page 340. The Seated Calf (Heel) Raise (Machine) exercise is found on page 341.
summary: Locations of two exercises targeting the calves: Standing Calf Raise and Seated Calf Raise.

content: The Flat Barbell Bench Press (and Dumbbell Variation) exercise is found on page 342. The Incline Dumbbell Bench Press (and Barbell Variation) exercise is found on page 344. The Flat Dumbbell Fly (and Incline Variation) exercise is found on page 345. The Vertical Chest Press (Machine) exercise is found on page 346.
summary: Locations of four exercises targeting the chest: Flat Barbell Bench Press, Incline Dumbbell Bench Press, Flat Dumbbell Fly, and Vertical Chest Press.

content: The Wrist Curl exercise is found on page 347. The Wrist Extension exercise is found on page 348.
summary: Locations of two exercises targeting the forearms: Wrist Curl and Wrist Extension.

content: It is critically important that the strength and conditioning professional provide athletes with proper instruction in resistance training exercise technique, including the fundamentals of breathing and weight belt use. The strength and conditioning professional needs to teach athletes how and when exercises should be spotted. Combined with quality supervision and ongoing feedback, this attention to proper instruction results in a safe training environment and an effective and appropriate training stimulus.
summary: Importance of proper instruction, spotting, and supervision in resistance training for athlete safety and effectiveness.

content: The elbows should be slowly extended back to the starting position. The torso should be kept in the same position. At the end of the set, stand up and return the bar to its resting position.
summary: Instructions for performing a set of exercises, emphasizing controlled movement and proper form.

content: The major muscles involved in the lat pulldown (machine) are the latissimus dorsi, teres major, middle trapezius, rhomboids, and posterior deltoids.
summary: Muscles primarily targeted during the Lat Pulldown exercise.

content: Grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. The grip should be wider than shoulder-width. Sit down on the seat facing the machine.
summary: Instructions for proper grip and positioning on the Lat Pulldown machine.

content: The Good Morning exercise is found on page 358. The Deadlift exercise is found on page 359. The Stiff-Leg Deadlift exercise is found on page 360.
summary: Locations of three exercises targeting the posterior chain: Good Morning, Deadlift, and Stiff-Leg Deadlift.

content: The major muscles involved in the lat pulldown (machine) are the latissimus dorsi, teres major, middle trapezius, rhomboids, and posterior deltoids. Sit down on the seat facing the machine. Position the thighs under the pads with the feet flat on the floor. If necessary, adjust the seat and thigh pad. Lean the torso slightly backward. Grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. The grip should be wider than shoulder-width. Extend the elbows fully. All repetitions begin from this position. Pull the bar down and toward the upper chest.
summary: This describes the lat pulldown exercise, including muscle targets, setup, and movement.

content: The major muscles involved in the seated row (machine) are the latissimus dorsi, teres major, middle trapezius, rhomboids, and posterior deltoids. Sit erect with the feet flat on the floor and press the torso against the chest pad. Grasp the handles with a closed grip, either pronated or neutral. If necessary, adjust the seat height to position the arms approximately parallel to the floor. Allow the elbows to extend fully. All repetitions begin from this position. Pull the handles toward the chest or upper abdomen. Maintain an erect torso position and keep the elbows next to the torso. Pull the handles as far back as possible. Do not jerk the torso backward. Allow the handles to move forward, back to the starting position. Keep the torso in the same position.
summary: This describes the seated row exercise, including muscle targets, setup, and movement.

content: Starting position. Sit on the floor (or on the long seat pad, if available) and place the feet on the machine frame or foot supports. Grasp the handles with a closed grip, either neutral or pronated. Sit in an erect position with the torso perpendicular to the floor, knees slightly flexed, and the feet and legs parallel to each other. Allow the elbows to fully extend with the arms approximately parallel to the floor. All repetitions begin from this position. Pull the handles toward the abdomen. Maintain an erect torso position with the knees in the same slightly flexed position. Do not jerk the upper body or lean back. Continue pulling until the handles touch the abdomen. Allow the elbows to slowly extend back to the starting position. Maintain an erect torso position with the knees in the same slightly flexed position. At the end of the set, flex the knees and hips to return the weight to its resting position.
summary: This describes the seated barbell curl exercise, including muscle targets, setup, and movement.

content: The barbell biceps curl targets the biceps brachii, brachialis, and brachioradialis.
summary: This states the muscle groups targeted by the barbell biceps curl.

content: The barbell should rest on the front of the thighs with the elbows fully extended in the starting position for the barbell biceps curl. In the starting position for the barbell biceps curl, grasp the bar with a closed, supinated grip that is shoulder-width apart. The starting position for the barbell biceps curl requires the individual to stand erect with feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly flexed. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for the barbell biceps curl.

content: During the upward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl, flex the elbows until the bar is near the anterior deltoids. The torso should be kept erect and the upper arms stationary during the upward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl. Do not jerk the body or swing the bar upward during the upward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl.
summary: Outlines the upward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl.

content: Lower the bar until the elbows are fully extended during the downward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl. Keep the torso and knees in the same position during the downward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl. Do not bounce the bar on the thighs between repetitions during the downward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl.
summary: Describes the downward movement phase of the barbell biceps curl.

content: The barbell biceps curl targets the biceps brachii, brachialis, and brachioradialis.
summary: States the muscle groups targeted by the barbell biceps curl.

content: In the starting position for the dumbbell hammer curl, grasp two dumbbells using a closed, neutral grip. The starting position for the dumbbell hammer curl requires the individual to stand erect with feet shoulder-width apart and knees slightly flexed. Position the dumbbells alongside the thighs with the elbows fully extended in the starting position for the dumbbell hammer curl.
summary: Describes the starting position for the dumbbell hammer curl.

content: Keeping the dumbbell in a neutral grip, flex the elbow of one arm until the dumbbell is near the anterior deltoid during the upward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl. The other arm should be kept stationary at the side of the thigh during the upward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl. Keep the torso erect and the upper arm stationary during the upward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl. Do not jerk the body or swing the dumbbell upward during the upward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl.
summary: Outlines the upward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl.

content: Lower the dumbbell until the elbow is fully extended during the downward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl. Keep the dumbbell in a neutral grip position during the downward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl. Keep the torso and knees in the same position during the downward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl. Repeat the upward and downward movement phases with the other arm (alternate arms) during the dumbbell hammer curl.
summary: Describes the downward movement phase of the dumbbell hammer curl.

content: The dumbbell hammer curl targets the brachialis, biceps brachii, and brachioradialis.
summary: States the muscle groups targeted by the dumbbell hammer curl.

content: In the starting position for the standing calf raise, position the body evenly under the shoulder pads. Grasp the handles and place the balls of the feet on the nearest edge of the step with the legs and feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other in the starting position for the standing calf raise. Stand erect with the knees fully extended but not forcefully locked out in the starting position for the standing calf raise. Allow the heels to lower to a comfortable, stretched position in the starting position for the standing calf raise.
summary: Describes the starting position for the standing calf raise.

content: Keeping the torso erect and the legs and feet parallel, push up as high as possible on the toes during the upward movement phase of the standing calf raise. Push up off the step; do not invert or evert the ankles during the upward movement phase of the standing calf raise. Keep the knees extended but not locked out during the upward movement phase of the standing calf raise.
summary: Outlines the upward movement phase of the standing calf raise.

content: Allow the heels to lower slowly back to the starting position during the downward movement phase of the standing calf raise. Maintain the same body position during the downward movement phase of the standing calf raise.
summary: Describes the downward movement phase of the standing calf raise.

content: The standing calf raise targets the gastrocnemius and soleus.
summary: States the muscle groups targeted by the standing calf raise.

content: The seated calf raise machine exercise begins with the user sitting erect on the seat with feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other. The user places the balls of their feet on the nearest edge of the step and lowers the thigh/knee pad so it firmly presses against the knees and front of the lower-thigh area. The user then plantar-flexes their ankles to remove the supports and allows the heels to lower to a comfortable, stretched position. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for the seated calf raise machine exercise.

content: The seated calf raise machine exercise begins with the user sitting erect on the seat with feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other. The user places the balls of their feet on the nearest edge of the step and lowers the thigh/knee pad so it firmly presses against the knees and front of the lower-thigh area. The user then plantar-flexes their ankles to remove the supports and allows the heels to lower to a comfortable, stretched position. All repetitions begin from this position. During the upward movement phase, the user keeps their torso erect and legs and feet parallel, pushing up on their toes as high as possible. The user pushes up off the step, avoiding inversion or eversion of the ankles. During the downward movement phase, the user allows their heels to lower slowly back to the starting position, maintaining the same body position. At the end of the set, the user replaces the supports and removes their feet. The major muscles involved in the seated calf raise are the soleus and gastrocnemius.
summary: Description of the seated calf raise exercise, including setup, movement, and target muscles.

content: The flat barbell bench press can also be performed with two dumbbells, using a closed, pronated grip. The athlete lies in a supine position on a bench in the five-point body contact position. The athlete places their body on the bench so that their eyes are below the racked bar. The athlete grasps the bar with a closed, pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. The athlete signals the spotter for assistance in moving the bar off the supports. The spotter stands erect and very close to the head of the bench, but does not distract the athlete. The spotter places their feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. The spotter grasps the bar with a closed, alternated grip inside the athlete’s hands. At the athlete’s signal, the spotter assists with moving the bar off the supports and guides the bar to a position over the athlete’s chest. The spotter releases the bar smoothly. The athlete lowers the bar to touch their chest at approximately nipple level. The athlete keeps their wrists stiff and forearms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The spotter keeps their hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends. The spotter slightly flexes their knees, hips, and torso and keeps their back flat when following the bar. The athlete pushes the bar upward until their elbows are fully extended. The athlete keeps their wrists stiff and forearms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position and does not arch their back or raise their chest to meet the bar. At the end of the set, the athlete signals the spotter for assistance in racking the bar and keeps a grip on the bar until it is racked. The spotter keeps their hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. The spotter slightly extends their knees, hips, and torso and keeps their back flat when following the bar. At the athlete’s signal, the spotter grasps the bar with an alternated grip inside the athlete’s hands. The spotter guides the bar back onto the supports and keeps a grip on the bar until it is racked. The major muscles involved in the flat barbell bench press are the pectoralis major, anterior deltoids, and triceps brachii.
summary: Detailed description of the flat barbell bench press, including setup, movement, spotting technique, and target muscles.

content: The incline dumbbell bench press can also be performed with a barbell using a closed, pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. The athlete grasps two dumbbells using a closed, pronated grip. The athlete lies in a supine position on an inclined bench in the five-point body contact position. The spotter stands erect and very close to the head of the bench (but does not distract the athlete).
summary: Description of the incline dumbbell bench press, including variations and setup.

content: The incline dumbbell bench press can also be performed with a barbell using a closed, pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. The athlete grasps two dumbbells using a closed, pronated grip. The athlete lies in a supine position on an inclined bench in the five-point body contact position. The athlete keeps the wrists stiff and directly above the elbows, with the dumbbell handles aligned with each other. The athlete lowers the dumbbells down and slightly out to be near the armpits and in line with the upper one-third area of the chest (between the clavicles and the nipples). The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The athlete does not arch the back or raise the chest to meet the dumbbells. The athlete pushes the dumbbells upward until the elbows are fully extended. The athlete keeps the wrists stiff and directly above the elbows, with the dumbbell handles aligned with each other. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position.
summary: Instructions for performing the incline dumbbell bench press, emphasizing proper form and technique.

content: The spotter assists by spotting the bar instead of the athlete’s forearms. The spotter stands erect and very close to the head of the bench (but does not distract the athlete). The spotter places the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. The spotter grasps the athlete’s forearms near the wrists. At the athlete’s signal, the spotter assists with moving the dumbbells to a position over the athlete’s head and face. The spotter releases the athlete’s forearms smoothly. The spotter keeps the hands near—but not touching—the athlete’s forearms as the dumbbells descend. The spotter slightly flexes the knees, hips, and torso and keeps the back flat when following the dumbbells. The spotter keeps the hands near—but not touching—the athlete’s forearms as the dumbbells ascend. The spotter slightly extends the knees, hips, and torso and keeps the back flat when following the dumbbells.
summary: Guidelines for a spotter during the incline dumbbell bench press, ensuring safety and assistance.

content: The flat dumbbell fly can also be performed on an incline bench. The athlete grasps two dumbbells using a closed, neutral grip. The athlete lies in a supine position on a bench in the five-point body contact position. The spotter positions one knee on the floor with the foot of the other leg forward and flat on the floor (or kneels on both knees). The spotter grasps the athlete’s forearms near the wrists. At the athlete’s signal, the spotter assists with moving the dumbbells to a position over the athlete’s chest. The spotter releases the athlete’s forearms smoothly. The athlete lowers the dumbbells in a wide arc until they are level with the shoulders or chest. The athlete keeps the dumbbell handles parallel to each other as the elbows move downward. The athlete keeps the wrists stiff and the elbows held in a slightly flexed position. The athlete keeps the hands, wrists, forearms, elbows, upper arms, and shoulders in the same vertical plane. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The spotter keeps the hands near—but not touching—the athlete’s forearms as the dumbbells descend. The athlete raises the dumbbells up toward each other in a wide arc back to the starting position. The athlete keeps the wrists stiff and the elbows held in a slightly flexed position. The athlete keeps the hands, wrists, forearms, elbows, upper arms, and shoulders in the same vertical plane. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The spotter keeps the hands near—but not touching—the athlete’s forearms as the dumbbells ascend.
summary: Instructions for performing the flat dumbbell fly, including variations and spotter assistance.

content: The vertical chest press machine exercise involves a backward and forward movement.
summary: Description of the vertical chest press machine exercise.

content: The athlete keeps the wrists stiff and the elbows held in a slightly flexed position. The athlete keeps the hands, wrists, forearms, elbows, upper arms, and shoulders in the same vertical plane. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The spotter keeps the hands near—but not touching—the athlete’s forearms as the dumbbells ascend.
summary: Describes the proper form and spotting technique for a dumbbell exercise.

content: The vertical chest press machine exercise involves a backward and forward movement. The athlete allows the handles to slowly move backward so that they are level with the chest. The athlete pushes the handles away from the chest back to the starting position. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The athlete does not arch the lower back or forcefully lock out the elbows.
summary: Details the execution of a vertical chest press machine exercise.

content: The starting position for wrist curl is to sit on the end of a bench. In the wrist curl starting position, grasp the bar with a closed, supinated grip 8 to 12 inches (20-30 cm) wide. Position the feet and legs parallel to each other with the toes pointing straight ahead in the wrist curl starting position. Lean the torso forward and place the elbows and forearms on the top of the thighs in the wrist curl starting position. Move the wrists forward until they extend slightly beyond the patellae in the wrist curl starting position. Allow the wrists to extend and the hands to open so that the fingertips hold the bar in the wrist curl starting position.
summary: Outlines the starting position for a wrist curl exercise.

content: The upward movement phase of the wrist curl involves raising the bar by flexing the fingers and then the wrists. Flex the wrists as far as possible without moving the elbows or forearms during the upward movement phase of the wrist curl. Do not jerk the shoulders backward or swing the bar upward during the upward movement phase of the wrist curl. The downward movement phase of the wrist curl involves allowing the wrists and fingers to slowly extend back to the starting position. Keep the torso and arms in the same position during the downward movement phase of the wrist curl. The major muscles involved in the wrist curl are the flexor carpi ulnaris, flexor carpi radialis, and palmaris longus.
summary: Describes the execution of a wrist curl exercise, including the upward and downward phases and the primary muscles involved.

content: The starting position for wrist extension is to sit on the end of a bench. In the wrist extension starting position, grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip 8 to 12 inches (20-30 cm) wide. Position the feet and legs parallel to each other with the toes pointing straight ahead in the wrist extension starting position. Lean the torso forward and place the elbows and forearms on the top of the thighs in the wrist extension starting position. Move the wrists forward until they extend slightly beyond the patellae in the wrist extension starting position. Keep a closed grip on the bar but allow the wrists to flex toward the floor in the wrist extension starting position.
summary: Describes the starting position for a wrist extension exercise.

content: The upward movement phase of the wrist extension involves raising the bar by extending the wrists. Extend the wrists as far as possible without moving the elbows or forearms during the upward movement phase of the wrist extension. Do not jerk the body backward or swing the bar upward during the upward movement phase of the wrist extension. The downward movement phase of the wrist extension involves allowing the wrists to slowly flex back to the starting position. Keep the torso and arms in the same position during the downward movement phase of the wrist extension. Maintain a closed grip during the downward movement phase of the wrist extension. The major muscles involved in the wrist extension are the extensor carpi ulnaris, extensor carpi radialis brevis (and longus).
summary: Describes the execution of a wrist extension exercise, including the upward and downward phases and the primary muscles involved.

content: The downward movement phase of the wrist extension involves allowing the wrists to slowly flex back to the starting position. Keep the torso and arms in the same position during the downward movement phase of the wrist extension. Maintain a closed grip during the downward movement phase of the wrist extension.
summary: Describes the proper form for the downward phase of wrist extension.

content: The starting position for the hip sled involves sitting in the machine with the lower back, hips, and buttocks pressed against their pads. Place the feet flat on the platform hip-width apart with the toes slightly angled out in the hip sled starting position. Position the legs parallel to each other in the hip sled starting position. Grasp the handles or the sides of the seat and move the hips and knees to a fully extended position, but do not forcefully lock the knees in the hip sled starting position. Keep the hips on the seat and the back against the back pad in the hip sled starting position. Remove the support mechanism from the foot platform and grasp the handles or the seat again in the hip sled starting position. All repetitions begin from this position in the hip sled.
summary: Details the correct starting position for the hip sled exercise.

content: The downward movement phase of the hip sled involves allowing the hips and knees to slowly flex to lower the platform. Do not allow the platform to lower rapidly during the downward movement phase of the hip sled. Keep the hips and buttocks on the seat and the back flat against the back pad during the downward movement phase of the hip sled. Keep the knees aligned over the feet as they flex during the downward movement phase of the hip sled. Allow the hips and knees to flex until the thighs are parallel to the foot platform, the buttocks lose contact with the seat, the hips roll off of the back pad, or the heels rise off the foot platform during the downward movement phase of the hip sled.
summary: Explains the proper form for the downward phase of the hip sled.

content: The upward movement phase of the hip sled involves pushing the platform up by extending the hips and knees. Push to a fully extended position, but do not forcefully lock out the knees during the upward movement phase of the hip sled. Maintain the same hip and back position; do not allow the buttocks to rise during the upward movement phase of the hip sled. Keep the knees aligned over the feet as they extend during the upward movement phase of the hip sled.
summary: Describes the correct form for the upward phase of the hip sled.

content: The major muscles involved in the wrist extension are the extensor carpi ulnaris, extensor carpi radialis brevis (and longus).
summary: Lists the primary muscles used in wrist extension.

content: The major muscles involved in the hip sled are the gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, and rectus femoris.
summary: Identifies the key muscle groups engaged during the hip sled exercise.

content: The back squat starting position requires the athlete to grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip, with the width depending on the bar position. The athlete should step under the bar and position the feet parallel to each other. The bar should be placed on the upper back and shoulders in one of two locations: low bar position across the posterior deltoids at the middle of the trapezius (using a handgrip wider than shoulder-width) or high bar position above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck (using a handgrip only slightly wider than shoulder-width). The athlete should lift the elbows up to create a 'shelf' for the bar using the upper back and shoulder muscles. The athlete should hold the chest up and out and tilt the head slightly up. Once in position, the athlete should signal the spotters for assistance in moving the bar off the supports. The athlete should extend the hips and knees to lift the bar and take one or two steps backward. The athlete should position the feet shoulder-width apart (or wider), even with each other, with the toes pointed slightly outward. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Provides a detailed description of the starting position for the back squat.

content: The starting position for two spotters requires them to stand erect at opposite ends of the bar with the feet shoulder-width apart and the knees slightly flexed. The spotters should grasp the end of the bar by cupping the hands together with the palms facing upward.
summary: Outlines the proper starting position for spotters during a back squat.

content: During the upward movement phase, the athlete should maintain a position with a flat back, high elbows, and the chest up and out. The athlete should extend the hips and knees at the same rate to keep the torso-to-floor angle constant. The athlete should keep the heels on the floor and the knees aligned over the feet. The athlete should not flex the torso forward or round the back. The athlete should continue extending the hips and knees to reach the starting position.
summary: Describes the correct form for the upward movement phase of the back squat.

content: At the end of the set, the athlete should step forward toward the rack.
summary: Specifies the final action after completing a set of back squats.

content: Do not jerk the body backward or swing the bar upward during the upward movement phase of the wrist extension.
summary: Provides a warning about improper form during wrist extension.

content: At the end of the set, replace the supports, remove the feet, and exit the machine.
summary: Describes the final steps after completing a set on the hip sled.

content: The athlete should extend the hips and knees at the same rate to keep the torso-to-floor angle constant. The athlete should keep the heels on the floor and the knees aligned over the feet. The athlete should not flex the torso forward or round the back. The athlete should continue extending the hips and knees to reach the starting position. At the end of the set, the athlete should step forward toward the rack. The athlete should squat down until the bar rests on the supports. The athlete maintains a position with the back flat, elbows high, and the chest up and out during the upward movement phase. The athlete extends the hips and knees at the same rate during the upward movement phase to keep the torso-to-floor angle constant. The athlete keeps the heels on the floor and the knees aligned over the feet during the upward movement phase. The athlete does not flex the torso forward or round the back during the upward movement phase. The athlete continues extending the hips and knees to reach the starting position during the upward movement phase. At the end of the set, the athlete steps forward toward the rack during the upward movement phase. The athlete squats down until the bar rests on the supports during the upward movement phase.
summary: Instructions for performing a back squat, emphasizing proper form and technique.

content: The major muscles involved in the back squat are the gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, and rectus femoris.
summary: Muscles primarily engaged during a back squat.

content: During the downward movement phase, the athlete should allow the hips and knees to slowly flex while keeping the torso-to-floor angle relatively constant. The athlete should keep the heels on the floor and the knees aligned over the feet. The athlete should continue flexing the hips and knees until the thighs are parallel to the floor, the trunk begins to round or flex forward, or the heels rise off the floor. During the downward movement phase, the spotters should keep the cupped hands close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends. The spotters should slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso to keep a flat-back position when following the bar. At the athlete’s signal, the two spotters assist with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved off the supports during the downward movement phase. The two spotters release the bar smoothly during the downward movement phase. The two spotters hold their hands 2 to 3 inches (5-8 cm) below the ends of the bar during the downward movement phase. The two spotters move sideways in unison with the athlete as the athlete moves backward during the downward movement phase.
summary: Detailed instructions for the downward phase of a back squat, including the role of spotters.

content: During the upward movement phase, the spotters should keep the cupped hands close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. The spotters should slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso and keep the back flat when following the bar. At the end of the set, the spotters should move sideways in unison with the athlete back to the rack. Simultaneously, the spotters should grasp the bar and assist with balancing the bar as it is racked. The spotters should release the bar smoothly.
summary: Instructions for spotters during the upward phase of a back squat.

content: The major muscles involved are gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, and rectus femoris.
summary: The primary muscles engaged during the exercise are the gluteus maximus, hamstrings, and quadriceps.

content: At the athlete’s signal, the two spotters assist with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved off the supports during the downward movement phase. The two spotters release the bar smoothly during the downward movement phase. The two spotters hold their hands 2 to 3 inches (5-8 cm) below the ends of the bar during the downward movement phase. The two spotters move sideways in unison with the athlete as the athlete moves backward during the downward movement phase. Once the athlete is in position, the two spotters get into a hip-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect during the downward movement phase. The two spotters keep their cupped hands close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends during the downward movement phase. The two spotters slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso and keep the back flat when following the bar during the downward movement phase.
summary: Spotters assist the athlete during the downward movement phase of the exercise, providing support and ensuring safety.

content: The athlete maintains a position with the back flat, elbows high, and the chest up and out during the downward movement phase. The athlete allows the hips and knees to slowly flex while keeping the torso-to-floor angle relatively constant during the downward movement phase. The athlete keeps the heels on the floor and the knees aligned over the feet during the downward movement phase. The athlete does not flex the torso forward or round the back during the downward movement phase. The athlete continues flexing the hips and knees until the thighs are parallel to the floor, the trunk begins to round or flex forward, or the heels rise off the floor during the downward movement phase.
summary: The athlete's form during the downward movement phase emphasizes controlled movement, maintaining a neutral spine, and proper alignment.

content: The athlete steps under the bar and positions the feet parallel to each other during the starting position. The athlete grasps the bar with a closed, pronated grip during the starting position. The athlete places the bar in a balanced position on the upper back and shoulders above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck using a handgrip only slightly wider than shoulder-width during the starting position. The athlete lifts the elbows up to create a ‘shelf’ for the bar using the upper back and shoulder muscles during the starting position. The athlete holds the chest up and out during the starting position. The athlete tilts the head slightly up during the starting position. Once in position, the athlete signals the spotter for assistance in moving the bar off the supports during the starting position. The athlete extends the hips and knees to lift the bar during the starting position. The athlete takes two or three steps backward during the starting position. All repetitions begin from this position during the starting position.
summary: The starting position involves precise bar placement, grip, and body alignment, preparing the athlete for the lift.

content: The spotter stands erect and very close to the athlete (but does not distract the athlete) during the starting position. The spotter places the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed during the starting position. At the athlete’s signal, the spotter assists with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved out of the rack during the starting position. The spotter moves in unison with the athlete as the athlete moves backward to the starting position during the starting position. Once the athlete is in position, the spotter gets into a hip-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect during the starting position. The spotter positions the hands near the athlete’s hips, waist, or torso during the starting position.
summary: The spotter's role in the starting position involves providing support, maintaining proximity, and ensuring a safe lift initiation.

content: The athlete takes one exaggerated step directly forward with one leg (the lead leg) during the forward movement phase. The athlete keeps the torso erect as the lead foot moves forward and contacts the floor during the forward movement phase. The athlete keeps the trailing foot in the starting position, but allows the trailing knee to slightly flex during the forward movement phase. Plant the lead foot flat on the floor pointing straight ahead or slightly inward. Allow the lead hip and knee to slowly flex. Keep the lead knee directly over the lead foot. Continue to flex the trailing knee until it is 1 to 2 inches (3-5 cm) above the floor. Balance the weight evenly between the ball of the trailing foot and the whole lead foot. Keep the torso perpendicular to the floor by ‘sitting back’ on the trailing leg. Step forward with the same foot as the athlete. Keep the lead knee and foot aligned with the athlete’s lead foot.
summary: The forward movement phase involves a controlled, staggered step, maintaining balance, and proper alignment of the legs and torso.

content: Stand erect in the starting position, pause, and then alternate lead legs. At the end of the set, step toward the rack and place the bar in the supports. Stand erect in the starting position, pause to wait for the athlete, and alternate lead legs. At the end of the set, help the athlete rack the bar.
summary: Instructions for performing a barbell exercise, alternating lead legs and safely racking the bar.

content: Grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. Step under the bar and position the feet parallel to each other. Place the bar in a balanced position on the upper back and shoulders above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck (using a handgrip only slightly wider than shoulder-width). Lift the elbows up to create a ‘shelf’ for the bar using the upper back and shoulder muscles. Hold the chest up and out. Tilt the head slightly up. Once in position, signal the spotter for assistance in moving the bar off the supports.
summary: Steps for setting up under a barbell, ensuring proper grip, bar placement, and posture.

content: Extend the hips and knees to lift the bar. Walk toward the box and stand in a position near the box that is as far away from the box as the box is tall. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Instructions for lifting the barbell and positioning for box jumps.

content: Keep the torso erect; do not lean forward. Keep the trailing foot in the starting position, but shift the body weight to the lead leg.
summary: Maintaining proper form during a box jump.

content: Step forward with the same foot as the athlete. Plant the foot 12 to 18 inches (30-46 cm) behind the athlete’s foot. Flex the lead knee as the athlete’s lead knee flexes. Keep the lead knee and foot aligned with the athlete’s lead foot. Balance the weight evenly between the ball of the trailing foot and the whole lead foot. Keep the torso perpendicular to the floor by ‘sitting back’ on the trailing leg. Continue to flex the trailing knee until it is 1 to 2 inches (3-5 cm) above the floor.
summary: Steps for performing a step-up exercise, emphasizing proper foot placement and balance.

content: Step up with one leg (the lead leg) to place the entire foot on the top of the box.
summary: Initiating a box jump step-up.

content: Assist only when necessary to keep the athlete balanced. Keep hands near the athlete’s hips, waist, or torso. Move in unison with the athlete as the athlete moves to the starting position. Once the athlete is in position, get into a hip-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect. Position the hands near the athlete’s hips, waist, or torso.
summary: Instructions for a spotter assisting an athlete during a barbell exercise.

content: Forcefully push off the floor by extending the lead hip and knee. Maintain the same erect torso position; do not jerk the upper body backward. Bring the lead foot back to a position next to the trailing foot; do not stutter-step backward. Push backward with the lead leg in unison with the athlete. Bring the lead foot back to a position next to the trailing foot; do not stutter-step backward.
summary: Instructions for the downward phase of a barbell exercise.

content: Place the feet shoulder-width apart with the knees slightly flexed. Stand erect and very close to the athlete (but do not distract the athlete).
summary: Positioning for assisting an athlete during a barbell exercise.

content: At athlete’s signal, assist with lifting and balancing the bar as it is moved out of the rack.
summary: Spotter's role in assisting an athlete during the initial lift.

content: Once the athlete is in position, get into a hip-width stance with the knees slightly flexed and the torso erect. Position the hands near the athlete’s hips, waist, or torso. Step up with one leg (the lead leg) to place the entire foot on the top of the box. Keep the torso erect; do not lean forward. Keep the trailing foot in the starting position, but shift the body weight to the lead leg. Forcefully extend the lead hip and knee to move the body to a standing position on top of the box. Do not push off or hop up with the trailing leg or foot. At the highest position, stand erect and pause before beginning the downward movement phase.
summary: Instructions for performing a box step-up, emphasizing proper form and technique.

content: Lean the torso forward and reach with the arms (but do not take a step) toward the athlete as the athlete steps up on the box. Keep the hands as near as possible to the athlete’s hips, waist, or torso (since the athlete is stepping away). Assist only when necessary to keep the athlete balanced. Shift the body weight to the same lead leg. Step off the box with the same trailing leg. Maintain an erect torso position. Place the trailing foot on the floor 12 to 18 inches (30-46 cm) away from the box. When the trailing foot is in full contact with the floor, shift the body weight to the trailing leg. Step off the box with the lead leg. Bring the lead foot back to a position next to the trailing foot. Stand erect in the starting position, pause, and then alternate lead legs.
