summary: Detailed instructions for assisting an athlete during a box step-up, focusing on balance and support.

content: At the end of the set, step toward the rack and place the bar in the supports. Follow the athlete with the arms as the athlete steps back down to the floor. Keep the hands near the athlete’s hips, waist, or torso. Stand erect in the starting position and pause to wait for the athlete. Assist only when necessary to keep the athlete balanced. At the end of the set, help the athlete rack the bar.
summary: Instructions for assisting an athlete after completing a set of exercises, emphasizing safety and support.

content: The buttocks should move straight back during the descent. Maintain a flat back and high elbow position during the descent. The bar should be slightly behind the toes during the descent. Keep the knees slightly flexed during the descent. Continue the downward movement until the torso is approximately parallel to the floor.
summary: Proper form guidelines for the descent phase of a barbell exercise, likely a deadlift.

content: Raise the bar by extending the hips. Keep the back flat and the knees slightly flexed during the ascent. Continue extending the hips to reach the starting position. The major muscles involved are gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, and erector spinae.
summary: Instructions for the ascent phase of a barbell exercise, likely a deadlift, and the primary muscle groups engaged.

content: Grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. Step under the bar and position the feet parallel to each other. Place the bar in a balanced position on the upper back and shoulders above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck (using a handgrip only slightly wider than shoulder-width). Lift the elbows up to create a ‘shelf’ for the bar using the upper back and shoulder muscles. Hold the chest up and out. Tilt the head slightly up.
summary: Starting position and grip instructions for a barbell exercise, likely a deadlift.

content: Stand with the feet flat and placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders, and grasp the bar with a closed, alternated grip. Place the hands on the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, outside of the knees, with the elbows fully extended. Place the feet flat on the floor and position the bar approximately 1 inch (3 cm) in front of the shins and over the balls of the feet. Position the body with the back flat or slightly arched, trapezius relaxed and slightly stretched, chest held up and out, head in line with the vertebral column or slightly hyperextended, heels in contact with the floor, shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar, and eyes focused straight ahead or slightly upward. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for a squat.

content: Lift the bar off the floor by extending the hips and knees. Keep the torso-to-floor angle constant; do not let the hips rise before the shoulders. Maintain a flat-back position. Keep the elbows fully extended and the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar. As the bar is raised, keep it as close to the shins as possible. As the bar rises just above the knees, move the hips forward to move the thighs against and the knees under the bar. Continue to extend the hips and knees until the body reaches a fully erect torso position. Allow the hips and knees to flex to slowly lower the bar to the floor. Maintain the flat-back body position; do not flex the torso forward.
summary: Describes the movement pattern for a conventional deadlift.

content: Step under the bar and position the feet parallel to each other. Place the bar in a balanced position on the upper back and shoulders above the posterior deltoids at the base of the neck (using a handgrip only slightly wider than shoulder-width). Lift the elbows up to create a ‘shelf’ for the bar using the upper back and shoulder muscles. Hold the chest up and out. Tilt the head slightly up.
summary: Describes the setup for a barbell squat.

content: The major muscles involved are gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, and rectus femoris.
summary: Lists the major muscles involved in the squat.

content: The major muscles involved are gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, and erector spinae.
summary: Lists the major muscles involved in the deadlift.

content: After performing the deadlift exercise to lift the bar off the floor, slightly to moderately flex the knees and keep them in this position throughout this exercise. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for a good morning exercise.

content: Begin the exercise by forming a flat back, then flex the torso forward at the hips slowly and under full control toward the floor. Keep the knees in the same slightly or moderately flexed position with the back flat or slightly arched and the elbows fully extended during the descent. Lower the bar until the plates touch the floor, the back cannot be held in the flat position, the knees fully extend, or the heels rise off of the floor.
summary: Describes the movement pattern for a good morning exercise.

content: The starting position for the leg extension machine exercise involves sitting down in the machine and pressing the back firmly against the back pad. The starting position for the leg extension machine exercise involves placing the feet behind and in contact with the roller pad. The starting position for the leg extension machine exercise involves positioning the legs parallel to each other.
summary: Describes the starting position for a leg extension machine exercise.

content: The downward movement phase of the leg extension machine exercise involves allowing the knees to slowly flex back to the starting position. The downward movement phase of the leg extension machine exercise requires keeping the torso erect and the back firmly pressed against the back pad. The downward movement phase of the leg extension machine exercise requires keeping the thighs, lower legs, and feet parallel to each other. The downward movement phase of the leg extension machine exercise requires not allowing the buttocks to lift off the seat.
summary: Describes the downward movement phase of a leg extension machine exercise.

content: The leg extension machine exercise involves maintaining a tight grip on the handles or the sides of the seat.
summary: Describes a general aspect of performing a leg extension machine exercise.

content: The starting position for the leg extension machine exercise involves sitting down in the machine and pressing the back firmly against the back pad. It also involves placing the feet behind and in contact with the roller pad, positioning the legs parallel to each other, aligning the knees with the axis of the machine, and grasping the handles or the sides of the seat.
summary: Describes the setup for the leg extension machine exercise.

content: The downward movement phase of the leg extension machine exercise requires keeping the thighs, lower legs, and feet parallel to each other and not allowing the buttocks to lift off the seat. The upward movement phase involves raising the roller pad by fully extending the knees, keeping the torso erect and the back firmly pressed against the back pad, and maintaining parallelism of the thighs, lower legs, and feet.
summary: Details the leg extension machine exercise's movement phases.

content: The major muscles involved in the leg extension machine exercise are the vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, and rectus femoris.
summary: Identifies the primary muscles targeted during the leg extension machine exercise.

content: The starting position for the leg curl machine exercise involves lying in a prone position on the machine and pressing the hips and torso firmly against the pads. It also involves placing the ankles behind and in contact with the roller pad, positioning the legs parallel to each other, positioning the knees slightly off the bottom edge of the thigh pad, aligning the knees with the axis of the machine, and grasping the handles or the sides of the chest pad.
summary: Describes the setup for the leg curl machine exercise.

content: The upward movement phase of the leg curl machine exercise involves raising the roller pad by fully flexing the knees, keeping the torso stationary and the hips and torso firmly pressed against the pads, and maintaining a tight grip on the handles or the sides of the chest pad. The downward movement phase involves allowing the knees to slowly extend back to the starting position, keeping the torso stationary and the hips and torso firmly pressed against the pads, maintaining a tight grip on the handles or the sides of the chest pad, and not forcefully locking out the knees.
summary: Details the leg curl machine exercise's movement phases.

content: The major muscles involved in the leg curl machine exercise are the semimembranosus, semitendinosus, and biceps femoris.
summary: Identifies the primary muscles targeted during the leg curl machine exercise.

content: The starting position for the shoulder press machine exercise involves sitting down and leaning back to place the body in the five-point body contact position. It also involves grasping the handles with a closed, pronated grip, aligning handles with the top of the shoulders, and adjusting the seat height to position the handles correctly.
summary: Describes the setup for the shoulder press machine exercise.

content: The upward movement phase of the shoulder press machine exercise involves pushing the handles upward until the elbows are fully extended, maintaining the five-point body contact position, and not arching the lower back or forcefully locking out the elbows. The downward movement phase involves allowing the elbows to slowly flex to lower the handles to the starting position, maintaining the five-point body contact position.
summary: Details the shoulder press machine exercise's movement phases.

content: The major muscles involved in the shoulder press machine exercise are the anterior and medial deltoids and triceps brachii.
summary: Identifies the primary muscles targeted during the shoulder press machine exercise.

content: The seated barbell shoulder press can also be performed with two dumbbells using a closed, pronated grip. The athlete sits on a vertical shoulder press bench and leans back to place the body in the five-point body contact position. The athlete grasps the bar with a closed, pronated grip that is slightly wider than shoulder-width. The spotter assists the athlete by spotting their forearms near the wrists instead of the bar.
summary: Provides alternative variations and safety considerations for the seated barbell shoulder press.

content: The major muscles involved in the shoulder press machine exercise are the anterior and medial deltoids and triceps brachii. The major muscles involved in the seated barbell shoulder press are the anterior and medial deltoids and triceps brachii.
summary: The shoulder press exercises target the anterior and medial deltoids and triceps brachii.

content: The seated barbell shoulder press can also be performed with two dumbbells using a closed, pronated grip. The athlete grasps the bar with a closed, pronated grip that is slightly wider than shoulder-width. The athlete starts in the five-point body contact position, sitting on a vertical shoulder press bench and leaning back. The athlete extends the neck slightly to allow the bar to pass by the face as it is lowered to touch the clavicles and anterior deltoids.
summary: The seated barbell shoulder press technique involves a specific grip, starting position, and movement pattern.

content: The athlete signals the spotter for assistance in moving the bar off the supports. At the athlete’s signal, the spotter assists with moving the bar off the supports. The spotter guides the bar to a position over the athlete’s head. The spotter releases the bar smoothly. The athlete allows the elbows to slowly flex to lower the bar. The athlete keeps the wrists stiff and the forearms parallel to each other. The athlete extends the neck slightly to allow the bar to pass by the face as it is lowered to touch the clavicles and anterior deltoids. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The spotter keeps the hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends. The spotter keeps the knees slightly flexed and the back flat when following the bar.
summary: The spotter assists the athlete during the barbell shoulder press, guiding the bar and ensuring safety.

content: The athlete pushes the bar upward until the elbows are fully extended. The athlete extends the neck slightly to allow the bar to pass by the face as it is raised. The athlete keeps the wrists stiff and the forearms parallel to each other. The athlete maintains the five-point body contact position. The athlete does not arch the back or rise off the seat. At the end of the set, the athlete signals the spotter for assistance in racking the bar. The athlete keeps a grip on the bar until it is racked. The spotter keeps the hands in the alternated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. The spotter slightly extends the knees, hips, and torso and keeps the back flat when following the bar. At the athlete’s signal after the set is completed, the spotter grasps the bar with an alternated grip inside the athlete’s hands. The spotter guides the bar back onto the supports. The spotter keeps a grip on the bar until it is racked.
summary: The upward phase of the barbell shoulder press and the racking procedure are described.

content: The starting position for the lateral raise is to grasp two dumbbells with a closed, neutral grip. Position the feet shoulder- or hip-width apart, with the knees slightly flexed, torso erect, shoulders back, and eyes focused ahead. Move the dumbbells to the front of the thighs, positioning them with the palms facing each other. Slightly flex the elbows and hold this flexed position throughout the exercise. Raise the dumbbells up and out to the sides; the elbows and upper arms should rise together and ahead of the forearms and hands and dumbbells. Maintain an erect upper body position with the knees slightly flexed and feet flat. Do not jerk the body or swing the dumbbells upward. Continue raising the dumbbells until the arms are approximately parallel to the floor or nearly level with the shoulders.
summary: The lateral raise exercise involves a specific starting position, grip, and movement pattern.

content: The starting position for the lying barbell triceps extension is to lie in a supine position on a bench in the five-point body contact position. Grasp the bar from the spotter with a closed, pronated grip about 12 inches (30 cm) wide. Position the bar over the chest with the elbows fully extended and the arms parallel. Point the elbows toward the knees (not out to the sides). All repetitions begin from this position. The spotter should stand erect and very close to the head of the bench (but do not distract the athlete).
summary: Describes the setup and starting position for the lying barbell triceps extension.

content: Keeping the upper arms stationary, allow the elbows to slowly flex to lower the bar toward the face. Keep the wrists stiff and the upper arms perpendicular to the floor and parallel to each other. Lower the bar until it almost touches the head or face. Maintain the five-point body contact position. Keep the hands in a supinated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it descends. Slightly flex the knees, hips, and torso and keep the back flat when following the bar.
summary: Details the eccentric phase of the lying barbell triceps extension.

content: Push the bar upward by extending the elbows back to the starting position. Keep the wrists stiff and the elbows pointed toward the knees. Keep the upper arms parallel to each other and perpendicular to the floor. Maintain the five-point body contact position. At the end of the set, signal the spotter to take the bar. Keep a grip on the bar until the spotter removes it. Keep the hands in a supinated grip position close to—but not touching—the bar as it ascends. Slightly extend the knees, hips, and torso and keep the back flat when following the bar.
summary: Describes the concentric phase of the lying barbell triceps extension.

content: The starting position for the triceps pushdown (machine) is to grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip 6 to 12 inches (15-30 cm) wide. Stand erect with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly flexed. Place the body close enough to the machine to allow the cable to hang straight down when it is held in the starting position. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Outlines the starting position for the triceps pushdown machine exercise.

content: Pull the bar down to position the upper arms against the sides of the torso. Flex the elbows to position the forearms parallel to the floor or slightly above.
summary: Details the movement pattern for the triceps pushdown machine exercise.

content: Slightly flex the elbows and hold this flexed position throughout the exercise. Raise the dumbbells up and out to the sides; the elbows and upper arms should rise together and ahead of the forearms and hands and dumbbells. Maintain an erect upper body position with the knees slightly flexed and feet flat. Do not jerk the body or swing the dumbbells upward. Continue raising the dumbbells until the arms are approximately parallel to the floor or nearly level with the shoulders. Allow the dumbbells to descend slowly back to the starting position. Keep the torso and knees in the same position.
summary: Provides step-by-step instructions for performing the lateral raise exercise.

content: The major muscles involved in the lateral raise are the deltoids.
summary: Identifies the primary muscle group targeted during the lateral raise.

content: The major muscles involved in the lying barbell triceps extension are the triceps brachii.
summary: Specifies the primary muscle group worked during the lying barbell triceps extension.

content: The starting position for the triceps pushdown (machine) is to grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip 6 to 12 inches (15-30 cm) wide. Stand erect with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly flexed. Place the body close enough to the machine to allow the cable to hang straight down when it is held in the starting position. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for the triceps pushdown exercise.

content: Pull the bar down to position the upper arms against the sides of the torso. Flex the elbows to position the forearms parallel to the floor or slightly above. Push the bar down until the elbows are fully extended. Keep the torso erect and the upper arms stationary. Do not forcefully lock out the elbows. Allow the elbows to slowly flex back to the starting position. Keep the torso, arms, and knees in the same position. At the end of the set, return the bar to its resting position.
summary: Outlines the execution of the triceps pushdown exercise.

content: The major muscles involved in the triceps pushdown (machine) are the triceps brachii.
summary: Identifies the primary muscle group targeted by the triceps pushdown.

content: This exercise consists of quickly and forcefully pushing the bar from the shoulders to over the head. The upward movement of the bar occurs in one continuous motion without interruption.
summary: Describes the basic movement pattern of the push press and push jerk exercises.

content: Both the push press and the push jerk exercises involve a rapid hip and knee extension that accelerates the bar off the shoulders, followed immediately by movements that position the bar overhead. The technique used to attain this final bar position varies.
summary: Explains the common elements and variations in the push press and push jerk exercises.

content: In the push press, the hip and knee extension thrust is only forceful enough to drive the bar one-half to one-third the distance overhead. From this height, the bar is ‘pressed out’ to the overhead position, with the hips and knees remaining fully extended after the thrust.
summary: Details the specific technique of the push press exercise.

content: The push jerk involves a more forceful hip and knee thrust so that the bar is actually ‘thrown’ upward and caught with extended elbows in the overhead position with the hips and knees slightly flexed.
summary: Describes the technique of the push jerk exercise.

content: The athlete can begin with a bar taken from shoulder-height supports outside a power rack or by lifting a bar from the floor to the shoulders (via a repetition of the power clean exercise).
summary: Explains the starting points for the push press and push jerk exercises.

content: Grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. Grip should be slightly wider than shoulder-width.
summary: Describes the grip used for the push press and push jerk exercises.

content: Step under the bar and position the feet hip-width apart and parallel to each other. Move up to the bar to place it on top of the anterior deltoids and clavicles.
summary: Outlines the initial setup for the push press and push jerk exercises.

content: Extend the hips and knees to lift the bar off the supports.
summary: Describes the initial lift phase of the push press and push jerk exercises.

content: Stand in the middle of the lifting platform. Position the feet shoulder-width apart (or wider) and even with each other with the toes pointed slightly outward. All repetitions begin from this position.
summary: Describes the starting position for the dip exercise.

content: Flex the hips and knees at a slow to moderate speed to move the bar in a straight path downward. Continue the dip to a depth not to exceed a quarter-squat, the catch position of the power clean, or 10% of the athlete’s height.
summary: Outlines the lowering phase of the dip exercise.

content: Keep the feet flat on the floor, the torso erect, and the upper arms parallel to the floor.
summary: Describes the body positioning during the dip exercise.

content: Immediately upon reaching the lowest position of the dip, reverse the movement by forcefully and quickly extending the hips and knees and then the elbows to move the bar overhead. After the hips and knees are fully extended and the bar is overhead from the drive phase, press it up the rest of the way until the elbows are fully extended.
summary: Describes the upward movement of the dip exercise.

content: In this position the torso is erect, the head is in a neutral position, the feet are flat on the floor, and the bar is slightly behind the head.
summary: Describes the final position at the top of the dip exercise.

content: After the hips and knees are fully extended and the bar is overhead from the drive phase, quickly re-flex the hips and knees to approximately a quarter-squat position and simultaneously extend the elbows fully to catch the bar overhead at the same moment as the bar reaches its highest position. Catch the bar with the torso erect, the head in a neutral position, the feet flat on the floor, and the bar slightly behind the head.
summary: Describes the technique for catching the bar overhead in the dip exercise.

content: Lower the bar by gradually reducing the muscular tension of the arms to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the shoulders. Simultaneously flex the hips and knees to cushion the impact of the bar on the shoulders.
summary: Describes the lowering phase of the dip exercise.

content: At the end of the set, step toward the rack and place the bar in the supports.
summary: Describes the final action after completing a set of dips.

content: Major muscles involved: gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, rectus femoris, soleus, gastrocnemius, deltoids, trapezius
summary: Lists the major muscle groups involved in the dip exercise.

content: The power clean exercise consists of quickly and forcefully pulling the bar from the floor to the front of the shoulders in one continuous motion. The hang clean is similar to the power clean exercise, except that the bar begins positioned at the thighs just above the knees and does not return to the floor between repetitions.
summary: Describes the power clean and hang clean exercises, emphasizing their similarities and differences.

content: Stand with the feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with the toes pointed slightly outward. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. Place the hands on the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width apart, outside of the knees, with the elbows fully extended. Place the feet flat on the floor and position the bar approximately 1 inch (3 cm) in front of the shins and over the balls of the feet. Position the body with the back flat or slightly arched, trapezius relaxed and slightly stretched, chest held up and out, head in line with the vertebral column or slightly hyperextended, shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar, and eyes focused straight ahead or slightly upward.
summary: Provides a detailed description of the starting position for both the power clean and hang clean.

content: Lift the bar off the floor by forcefully extending the hips and knees. Keep the torso-to-floor angle constant; do not let the hips rise before the shoulders. Maintain a flat-back position. Keep the elbows fully extended and the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar. As the bar is raised, keep it as close to the shins as possible. As the bar rises just above the knees, thrust the hips forward and slightly re-flex the knees to move the thighs against and the knees under the bar. Keep the back flat or slightly arched and the elbows fully extended and pointing out to the sides.
summary: Outlines the initial upward movement of the bar during the power clean and hang clean.

content: Forcefully jump upward by quickly extending the hips and knees and plantar-flexing the ankles. Keep the bar as close to the body as possible. Keep the back flat and the elbows pointing out to the sides. Keep the shoulders over the bar and the elbows extended as long as possible. When the lower body joints reach full extension, rapidly shrug the shoulders upward with the elbows still fully extended. As the shoulders reach their highest elevation, flex the elbows to begin pulling the body under the bar. Continue to pull with the arms as high and as long as possible. Due to the explosive nature of this phase, the torso is erect or slightly hyperextended, the head is tilted slightly back, and the feet may lose contact with the floor.
summary: Describes the explosive upward phase of the power clean and hang clean.

content: After the lower body has fully extended, pull the body under the bar and rotate the arms around and under the bar. Simultaneously, flex the hips and knees to a quarter-squat position. Once the arms are under the bar, lift the elbows to position the upper arms parallel to the floor. Rack the bar across the front of the clavicles and anterior deltoids. Catch the bar with a nearly erect torso, the shoulders slightly ahead of the buttocks, a neutral head position, and flat feet. After gaining control and balance, stand up by extending the hips and knees to a fully erect position.
summary: Details the final stages of the power clean and hang clean, including catching the bar and standing up.

content: Lower the elbows to unrack the bar from the anterior deltoids and clavicles then slowly lower the bar down to the thighs. Simultaneously flex the hips and knees to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs. Squat down with the elbows fully extended until the bar touches the floor. Major muscles involved: gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, rectus femoris, soleus, gastrocnemius, deltoids, trapezius.
summary: Describes the lowering phase of the power clean and hang clean, emphasizing the controlled descent and muscle groups involved.

content: The Snatch is an exercise that consists of quickly and forcefully pulling the bar from the floor to over the head with the elbows fully extended in one continuous motion. To start the Snatch, stand with feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with toes pointed slightly outward. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip.
summary: Introduces the Snatch exercise and its starting position.

content: The Snatch is an exercise that consists of quickly and forcefully pulling the bar from the floor to over the head with the elbows fully extended in one continuous motion. To start the Snatch, stand with feet placed between hip- and shoulder-width apart with toes pointed slightly outward. Squat down with the hips lower than the shoulders and grasp the bar with a closed, pronated grip. The grip width for the Snatch is wider than for other exercises and can be estimated using one of these distances: the distance from the edge of the clenched fist of one hand to the opposite shoulder when the arm is straight out at the side, or the elbow-to-elbow distance when the arms are straight out at the sides. Extend the elbows fully, place the feet flat on the floor, and position the bar approximately 1 inch (3 cm) in front of the shins and over the balls of the feet. Position the body with the back flat or slightly arched, trapezius relaxed and slightly stretched, chest held up and out, head in line with the vertebral column or slightly hyperextended, heels in contact with the floor, shoulders over or slightly in front of the bar, and eyes focused straight ahead or slightly upward. All repetitions begin from this starting position.
summary: The Snatch is a complex exercise involving a full-body movement from the floor to overhead, starting with a specific stance and grip.

content: To begin the upward movement phase of the Snatch, lift the bar off the floor by forcefully extending the hips and knees. Keep the torso-to-floor angle constant during the first pull and do not let the hips rise before the shoulders. Maintain a flat-back position, keep the elbows fully extended and the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar. As the bar is raised, keep it as close to the shins as possible. As the bar rises just above the knees during the transition phase, thrust the hips forward and slightly re-flex the knees to move the thighs against and the knees under the bar. Keep the back flat or slightly arched and the elbows fully extended and pointing out to the sides during the transition phase.
summary: The upward phase of the Snatch involves a controlled pull, maintaining proper form and keeping the bar close to the body.

content: Forcefully jump upward by quickly extending the hips and knees and plantar-flexing the ankles during the second pull phase. Keep the bar as close to the body as possible, keep the back flat, and the elbows pointing out to the sides during the second pull phase. Keep the shoulders over the bar and the elbows extended as long as possible during the second pull phase. When the lower body joints reach full extension, rapidly shrug the shoulders upward with the elbows still fully extended. Continue to pull with the arms as high and as long as possible during the second pull phase. Due to the explosive nature of this phase, the torso is erect or slightly hyperextended, the head is tilted slightly back, and the feet may lose contact with the floor during the second pull phase.
summary: The second pull phase of the Snatch is explosive, requiring full body extension and maintaining proper form.

content: After the lower body has fully extended, pull the body under the bar and rotate the hands around and under the bar during the catch phase. Simultaneously flex the hips and knees to a quarter-squat position during the catch phase. Once the body is under the bar, catch the bar over and slightly behind the head with fully extended elbows, a neutral head position, and flat feet during the catch phase. After gaining control and balance, stand up by extending the hips and knees to a fully erect position. Stabilize the bar overhead after standing up.
summary: The catch phase involves controlling the bar overhead, transitioning into a standing position, and stabilizing the lift.

content: To lower the bar from the overhead position, gradually reduce the muscular tension of the shoulders to allow a controlled descent of the bar to the thighs during the downward movement phase. Simultaneously flex the hips and knees to cushion the impact of the bar on the thighs during the downward movement phase. Squat down with the elbows fully extended until the bar touches the floor during the downward movement phase. The major muscles involved in the Snatch are the gluteus maximus, semimembranosus, semitendinosus, biceps femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus intermedius, vastus medialis, rectus femoris, soleus, gastrocnemius, deltoids, and trapezius.
summary: The Snatch's descent phase involves controlled lowering of the bar, followed by a controlled squat to the starting position.

content: Well-constructed strength and conditioning programs are based on the application of sound principles during each step of a process called program design. This section discusses those training principles, highlights pertinent recent research, and provides recommendations on how to design different types of training programs. The information in part I of this section is grouped into three content areas: Resistance training (chapter 15), Plyometric training (chapter 16), and Speed, agility, and speed-endurance development (chapter 17). The application of training principles is presented in each chapter; the most comprehensive discussion occurs in chapter 15, where the seven steps or variables involved in designing safe and effective resistance training programs are presented in detail. A unique feature of chapter 15 is the use of scenarios to illustrate how the variables can be manipulated to develop resistance training programs. Regardless of the type of training program (resistance, plyometric, speed, etc.), there are three foundational principles that always apply: specificity, overload, and progression.
summary: This section discusses the principles of program design for strength and conditioning, highlighting specificity, overload, and progression as foundational principles.

content: One of the most basic concepts to incorporate in all training programs is specificity. The term, first suggested by DeLorme in 1945 (3), refers to the method whereby an athlete is trained in a specific manner to produce a specific adaptation or training outcome. For example, to design a resistance training program that is specific to strengthening the chest muscles, the strength and conditioning professional must give an athlete an exercise like the bench press that recruits the pectoralis major. Sometimes used interchangeably with specificity is the acronym SAID, which stands for specific adaptation to imposed demands.
summary: Specificity, or SAID (Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demands), is a fundamental principle in training, emphasizing that training should be tailored to the desired outcome.

content: Regardless of the type of training program (resistance, plyometric, speed, etc.), there are three foundational principles that always apply: specificity, overload, and progression.  One of the most basic concepts to incorporate in all training programs is specificity. The term, first suggested by DeLorme in 1945 (3), refers to the method whereby an athlete is trained in a specific manner to produce a specific adaptation or training outcome. For example, to design a resistance training program that is specific to strengthening the chest muscles, the strength and conditioning professional must give an athlete an exercise like the bench press that recruits the pectoralis major. Sometimes used interchangeably with specificity is the acronym SAID, which stands for specific adaptation to imposed demands. The underlying principle is that the type of demand placed on the body dictates the type of adaptation that will occur. For instance, athletes training for power in high-speed movements (e.g., baseball pitch, tennis serve) should attempt to activate or recruit the same motor units required by their sport at the highest velocity possible (5). Incorporating resistance training exercises that mimic the movement patterns of the athlete’s sport increases the likelihood that muscles involved in the sport will be recruited. For instance, if a high jumper performs depth jumps as part of a plyometric exercise program, he or she should experience an improvement in performance in high jumping because the force production and muscular recruitment at the ankle, knee, and hip are similar to those with the high jump. Specificity also relates to the athlete’s sport season. As an athlete progresses through the preseason, in-season, and postseason, all forms of training should gradually progress in an organized manner from generalized to sport specific (1, 2, 4, 5). Although participation in the sport itself provides the greatest opportunity to improve performance in the sport, the proper application of the specificity principle certainly increases the likelihood that other training will also positively contribute to performance.
summary: This chunk outlines the principle of specificity in training, emphasizing the importance of tailoring training to specific adaptations and sport demands.

content: Overload refers to assigning a workout or training regime of greater intensity than the athlete is accustomed to. Without the stimulus of overload, even an otherwise well-designed program greatly limits an athlete’s ability to make improvements. The obvious application of this principle in the design of resistance training programs involves increasing the loads assigned in the exercises. Other more subtle changes include increasing the number of sessions per week (or per day in some instances), adding exercises or sets, emphasizing complex over simple exercises, decreasing the length of the rest periods between sets and exercises, or any combination of these or other changes. Applying the overload principle, for instance, in a sprint training program may be accomplished through the use of gravity-assisted and wind-resisted training (e.g., downhill running and parachute sprinting, respectively). Another application is to advance an athlete’s plyometric program from single- to multiple-jump drills or to raise the box height for depth jumps. The intent is to stress the body at a higher level than it is used to. When the overload principle is properly applied, overtraining is avoided and the desired training adaptation will occur.
summary: This chunk explains the overload principle in training, highlighting the need for progressively increasing training intensity to stimulate adaptation.

content: If a training program is to continue producing higher levels of performance, the intensity of the training must become progressively greater. Progression, when applied properly, promotes long-term training benefits. Although it is customary to focus only on the resistance used, one can progressively increase training intensity by raising the number of weekly training sessions, adding more drills or exercises to each session, changing the type or difficulty of the drills or exercises, or increasing the training stimulus. The issue of importance is that progression is based on the athlete’s training status and is introduced systematically and gradually.
summary: This chunk focuses on the principle of progression in training, emphasizing the need for gradual and systematic increases in training intensity over time.

content: Designing a resistance training program is a complex process that requires the recognition and manipulation of seven program design variables. This chapter discusses each variable in the context of three scenarios. The three scenarios include a basketball center, an American football offensive lineman, and a cross-country runner. It is understood that, in each scenario, the athlete is well conditioned for his or her sport, has no musculoskeletal dysfunction, and has been cleared for training and competition by the sports medicine staff. The athletes in scenarios A (basketball center) and B (American football lineman) have been resistance training since high school, are accustomed to lifting heavy loads, and are skilled in machine and free weight exercises. The high school cross-country runner in scenario C, in contrast, began a resistance training program in the preseason only four weeks ago, so his training is limited and his exercise technique skills are not well developed.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of program design variables and outlines three athlete scenarios with varying training backgrounds.

content: The first task in a needs analysis is to determine the unique characteristics of the sport. This information enables the strength and conditioning professional to design a program specific to those requirements and characteristics. Although this task can be approached in several ways, it should at least include consideration of the following attributes of the sport: body and limb movement patterns and muscular involvement (movement analysis). The text describes three athlete scenarios: Scenario A, Scenario B, and Scenario C. Scenario A is a female athlete aged 20 years old who plays collegiate basketball. Scenario B is a male athlete aged 28 years old who plays professional American football. Scenario C is a male athlete aged 17 years old who plays high school cross-country running. The text focuses on the physiological outcomes of resistance training: strength, power, hypertrophy, and muscular endurance. A movement analysis of the shot put field event reveals that it is an all-body movement that begins with the athlete in a semicrouched stance with many joints flexed and adducted, and culminates in an upright stance with many joints extended and abducted.
summary: This chunk details the process of needs analysis in program design, emphasizing the importance of understanding the sport's demands and the athlete's characteristics.

content: Scenario A is a female athlete aged 20 years old who plays collegiate basketball. The sport in Scenario A is running and jumping, ball handling, shooting, blocking, and rebounding. Muscular involvement in Scenario A includes all major muscle areas, especially the hips, thighs, and shoulders. The primary physiological requirement for Scenario A is strength/power. The athlete in Scenario A has resistance trained regularly since high school. The athlete in Scenario A possesses excellent skill in performing free weight and machine exercises.
summary: Scenario A describes a 20-year-old female collegiate basketball player with a focus on strength/power development and a history of regular resistance training.

content: Scenario B is a male athlete aged 28 years old who plays professional American football.
summary: Scenario B describes a 28-year-old male professional American football player.

content: Scenario C is a male athlete aged 17 years old who plays high school cross-country running.
summary: Scenario C describes a 17-year-old male high school cross-country runner.

content: The text focuses on the physiological outcomes of resistance training: strength, power, hypertrophy, and muscular endurance.
summary: The text centers on the physiological benefits of resistance training, including strength, power, muscle growth, and endurance.

content: A movement analysis of the shot put field event reveals that it is an all-body movement that begins with the athlete in a semicrouched stance with many joints flexed and adducted, and culminates in an upright stance with many joints extended and abducted.
summary: Shot putting involves a full-body movement, transitioning from a flexed to an extended posture.

content: The most heavily recruited muscles in shot putting (not in order) are the elbow extensors (triceps brachii), shoulder abductors (deltoids), hip extensors (gluteals, hamstrings), knee extensors (quadriceps), and ankle plantarflexors (soleus, gastrocnemius).
summary: Shot putting primarily engages the triceps, deltoids, glutes, hamstrings, quadriceps, and calf muscles.

content: Physiologically, shot putting requires high levels of strength and power for a successful performance. Enhanced muscular hypertrophy is advantageous for shot putting because the muscle’s ability to produce force increases as its cross-sectional area becomes greater.
summary: Shot putting demands high strength and power, with muscle hypertrophy benefiting performance.

content: The muscular endurance requirement for shot putting is minimal.
summary: Shot putting places minimal demands on muscular endurance.

content: Reports from the sports medicine community indicate that the muscles and tendons surrounding the shoulder and elbow joints tend to be injured due to overuse.
summary: Overuse injuries commonly affect the shoulder and elbow in shot putters.

content: The second task in resistance training program design is to profile the athlete’s needs and goals by evaluating training (and injury) status, conducting a variety of tests, evaluating the results, and determining the primary goal of training.
summary: The second step in program design involves assessing the athlete's needs, goals, training history, and potential injuries.

content: The more individualized the assessment process, the more specific the resistance training program for each athlete can be. An athlete’s current condition or level of preparedness to begin a new or revised program (training status) is an important consideration when designing training programs. An evaluation by a sports medicine professional of any current or previous injuries that may affect training is important.
summary: Individualized assessments, considering training status and injury history, are crucial for tailoring resistance training programs.

content: An athlete’s training background or exercise history is important because it helps the strength and conditioning professional better understand the athlete’s training capabilities. An assessment of the athlete’s training background should examine the type of training program (sprint, plyometric, resistance, etc.), length of recent regular participation in previous training program(s), level of intensity involved in previous training program(s), and degree of exercise technique experience (i.e., the knowledge and skill to perform resistance training exercises properly).
summary: Understanding an athlete's past training experience, including program type, duration, intensity, and technique proficiency, is essential for program design.

content: Table 15.1 provides an example of how information might be used to classify athletes’ training status as beginner, intermediate, or advanced. The strength and conditioning professional should realize that the three classifications exist on a continuum and cannot be definitively described.
summary: Athletes' training status is categorized as beginner, intermediate, or advanced, recognizing that these classifications are fluid and interconnected.

content: This chapter focuses on assessing maximal muscular strength. Strength and conditioning professionals should refer to step 5 (training load and repetitions), page 392 and chapters 11 and 12 for a complete discussion of these procedures.
summary: This chapter focuses on assessing maximal muscular strength, with further details on training load and repetitions provided in other chapters.

content: To yield pertinent and reliable data that can be used effectively to develop a resistance training program, the tests selected should be related to the athlete’s sport, consistent with the athlete’s level of skill, and realistically based on the equipment available.
summary: Test selection should align with the athlete's sport, skill level, and available equipment to ensure relevant and reliable data for program development.

content: The result of the movement analysis discussed previously provides direction in selecting tests. Typically, major upper body exercises (e.g., bench press and shoulder press) and exercises that mimic jumping movements in varying degrees (e.g., power clean, squat, hip sled) are used in testing batteries.
summary: Movement analysis informs test selection, typically including upper body exercises and those mimicking jumping movements.

content: Refer to step 2, page 386, for a discussion of exercise selection. After testing is completed, the results should be compared with normative or descriptive data (refer to chapter 12) to determine the athlete’s strengths and weaknesses.
summary: Exercise selection is guided by step 2 on page 386, and test results are compared to normative data to identify strengths and weaknesses.

content: Based on this evaluation and the needs analysis of the sport, a training program can be developed to improve deficiencies, maintain strengths, or further develop physiological qualities that will enable the athlete to better meet the demands of the sport.
summary: Training programs are tailored based on the athlete's test results, sport-specific needs, and goals to address weaknesses, maintain strengths, and enhance performance.

content: The athlete’s test results, the movement and physiological analysis of the sport, and the priorities of the athlete’s sport season determine the primary goal or outcome for the resistance training program. Typically, this goal is to improve strength, power, hypertrophy, or muscular endurance. Despite a potential desire or need to make improvements in two different areas (e.g., strength and muscular endurance), an effort should be made to concentrate on only one training outcome per season.
summary: The primary training goal is determined by the athlete's test results, sport demands, and season priorities, typically focusing on one outcome per season (e.g., strength, power, hypertrophy, or muscular endurance).

content: An example of how the strength and conditioning professional may prioritize the resistance training emphases during the four main sport seasons is shown in table 15.2. In this example, “training stress” refers to the degree of physical demand or stimulus of the resistance training program.
summary: Training emphasis can be prioritized across different sport seasons, with 'training stress' referring to the intensity of the program.

content: The sport in Scenario A is running and jumping, ball handling, shooting, blocking, and rebounding. The primary physiological requirement for Scenario A is strength/power. Muscular involvement in Scenario A includes all major muscle areas, especially the hips, thighs, and shoulders. The athlete in Scenario A has resistance trained regularly since high school. The athlete in Scenario A possesses excellent skill in performing free weight and machine exercises. The athlete in Scenario A just completed a 4 days/week resistance training program in the off-season consisting of upper body exercises (23/week) and lower body exercises (23/week). Upper body exercises in Scenario A consist of 6 exercises (2 core, 4 assistance), 3 sets of 10RM-12RM loads. Lower body exercises in Scenario A consist of 6 exercises (2 core, 4 assistance), 3 sets of 10RM-12RM loads. The athlete in Scenario A's resistance training status is advanced. The primary preseason resistance training goal for Scenario A is strength/power.
summary: Scenario A describes an athlete in a sport requiring strength and power, with a focus on training for preseason goals.

content: The sport in Scenario B is grabbing, pushing, repelling, and deflecting opponents. The primary physiological requirement for Scenario B is hypertrophy. Muscular involvement in Scenario B includes all major muscle areas, especially the hips, thighs, chest, arms, and low back. The athlete in Scenario B has resistance trained regularly throughout high school, college, and his professional career. The athlete in Scenario B possesses excellent skill in performing free weight and machine exercises. The athlete in Scenario B just completed a 2 days/week resistance training program in the postseason consisting of 8 exercises (3 core, 5 assistance; 2 lower body, 6 upper body), 2-3 sets of 12RM-15RM loads. The athlete in Scenario B's resistance training status is advanced. The primary off-season resistance training goal for Scenario B is hypertrophy.
summary: Scenario B describes an athlete in a grappling sport, prioritizing hypertrophy through resistance training.

content: The sport in Scenario C is running, repetitive leg and arm movements. The primary physiological requirement for Scenario C is muscular endurance. The athlete in Scenario C just began resistance training in the preseason. The athlete in Scenario C has only limited skill in performing free weight and machine exercises. The athlete in Scenario C just completed a 2 days/week resistance training program in the preseason consisting of 7 exercises (3 core, 4 assistance; 3 lower body, 4 upper body), 1-2 sets of 15RM loads. The athlete in Scenario C's resistance training status is beginner. The primary in-season resistance training goal for Scenario C is muscular endurance.
summary: Scenario C describes a beginner athlete in a sport requiring muscular endurance, focusing on building a foundation through resistance training.

content: Exercise selection involves choosing exercises for a resistance training program. To make informed exercise selections, the strength and conditioning professional must understand the nature of various types of resistance training exercises, the movement and muscular requirements of the sport, the athlete’s exercise technique experience, the equipment available, and the amount of training time available. Although there are literally hundreds of resistance training exercises to select from when one is designing a program, most involve primary muscle groups or body areas and fall into categories based on their relative importance to the athlete’s sport.
summary: This chunk explains the importance of exercise selection in resistance training programs, considering various factors like sport demands and athlete experience.

content: Exercises can be classified as either core or assistance based on the size of the muscle areas involved and their level of contribution to a particular sport movement. Core exercises recruit one or more large muscle areas (i.e., chest, shoulder, back, hip, or thigh), involve two or more primary joints (multijoint exercises), and receive priority when one is selecting exercises because of their direct application to the sport. Assistance exercises usually recruit smaller muscle areas (i.e., upper arm, abdominals, calf, neck, forearm, lower back, or anterior lower leg), involve only one primary joint (single-joint exercises), and are considered less important to improving sport performance.
summary: This chunk defines core and assistance exercises based on their muscle group involvement and contribution to sport performance.

content: Generally, all the joints at the shoulder—the glenohumeral and shoulder girdle articulations—are considered one primary joint when resistance training exercises are categorized as core or assistance. The spine is similarly considered a single primary joint (as in the abdominal crunch and back extension exercises). A common application of assistance exercises is for injury prevention and rehabilitation, as these exercises often isolate a specific muscle or muscle group. The muscles that are predisposed to injury from the unique demands of a sport skill (e.g., the shoulder external rotators for overhand pitching) or those that require reconditioning after an injury (e.g., a quadriceps contusion) can be specifically conditioned by an assistance exercise.
summary: This chunk clarifies the categorization of shoulder and spine joints and highlights the role of assistance exercises in injury prevention and rehabilitation.

content: A core exercise that emphasizes loading the spine directly (e.g., back squat) or indirectly (e.g., power clean) can be further described as a structural exercise. More specifically, a structural exercise involves muscular stabilization of posture during performance of the lifting movement (e.g., maintaining a rigid torso and a flat back during the back squat). A structural exercise that is performed very quickly or explosively is considered a power exercise. Typically, power exercises are assigned to athletes when they are appropriate for the athlete’s sport-specific training priorities.
summary: This chunk defines structural and power exercises, emphasizing their role in posture stabilization and explosive movements.

content: The muscles that are predisposed to injury from the unique demands of a sport skill (e.g., the shoulder external rotators for overhand pitching) or those that require reconditioning after an injury (e.g., a quadriceps contusion) can be specifically conditioned by an assistance exercise.
summary: Assistance exercises target specific muscles vulnerable to injury or needing reconditioning.

content: A core exercise that emphasizes loading the spine directly (e.g., back squat) or indirectly (e.g., power clean) can be further described as a structural exercise. More specifically, a structural exercise involves muscular stabilization of posture during performance of the lifting movement (e.g., maintaining a rigid torso and a flat back during the back squat).
summary: Structural exercises involve stabilizing the spine during lifting movements.

content: A structural exercise that is performed very quickly or explosively is considered a power exercise. Typically, power exercises are assigned to athletes when they are appropriate for the athlete’s sport-specific training priorities.
summary: Power exercises are explosive structural exercises tailored to an athlete's sport.

content: In the needs analysis step (step 1), the strength and conditioning professional has identified the unique requirements and characteristics of the sport. The exercises selected for a resistance training program that focus on conditioning for a particular sport need to be similar to the activities of that sport in their body and limb movement patterns, joint ranges of motion, and muscular involvement. The exercises should also create muscular balance to reduce risk of injury from disproportionate training.
summary: Exercise selection should mirror the sport's demands and promote muscular balance.

content: The more similar the training activity is to the actual sport movement, the greater the likelihood that there will be a positive transfer to that sport. This is the specificity concept, also called the specific adaptation to imposed demands (SAID) principle.
summary: Training specificity, or the SAID principle, emphasizes the importance of exercises resembling the target sport's movements.

content: The over the back squat should be chosen over the front squat if time constraints require choosing only one exercise. Exercises selected for a sport should maintain a balance of muscular strength across joints and between opposing muscle groups. A resistance training program should not increase the risk of injury due to a disparity between the strength of the agonist and the antagonist. If an imbalance is created or discovered, exercises to restore an appropriate strength balance need to be selected.
summary: Exercise selection should prioritize time efficiency and maintain muscular balance across joints and opposing muscle groups.

content: Muscle balance does not always mean equal strength, just a proper ratio of strength, power, or muscular endurance of one muscle or muscle group relative to another muscle or muscle group. If there is any question whether an athlete can perform an exercise with proper technique, the strength and conditioning professional should ask the athlete to demonstrate the exercise. If the athlete uses incorrect technique, the strength and conditioning professional should provide complete instruction.
summary: Muscle balance emphasizes proper strength ratios, and proper technique instruction is crucial.

content: Unskilled individuals are often introduced to machines and free weight assistance exercises because they are considered easier to perform than free weight core exercises. The availability of training equipment must be considered in the selection of exercises. A lack of certain equipment may necessitate selecting exercises that are not as sport specific. The absence of Olympic-type barbells with revolving sleeves would preclude exercises such as the power clean. An insufficient supply of barbell plates may result in substituting exercises that do not require as much resistance; for example, the back squat could be replaced by the front squat.
summary: Exercise selection is influenced by equipment availability and athlete skill level.

content: The strength and conditioning professional should weigh the value of certain exercises against the time available per session. Some exercises take longer to complete than others. If time for a training session is limited, exercises that are more time efficient may need to be given priority over others. The machine leg press could be selected instead of the free weight lunge to train the hips and thighs of a 100 m sprinter. The time required to move the machine pin to the correct slot in a weight stack and perform 10 repetitions of a machine leg press is much less than the time required for the lunge exercise. For the lunge exercise, the athlete has to load both ends of a bar, attach the locks, back out of the power rack, establish a stable starting position, perform 10 repetitions of each leg, and rerack the bar. Although the machine leg press is less sport specific, the time saved may permit including other exercises or performing more sets. The benefit of including the more sport-specific lunge exercise, on the other hand, may be worth the additional time needed, although this depends on the goals of the training season and time available.
summary: Time constraints influence exercise selection, balancing efficiency with sport specificity.

content: Training frequency refers to the number of training sessions completed in a given time period. For a resistance training program, a common time period is one week. When determining training frequency, the strength and conditioning professional should consider the athlete’s training status, sport season, projected exercise loads, types of exercises, and other concurrent training or activities. The athlete’s level of preparedness for training, which was determined during the needs analysis (step 1), is an influential factor in determining training frequency because it affects the number of rest days needed between training sessions. Traditionally, three workouts per week are recommended for many athletes, as the intervening days allow sufficient recovery between sessions. As an athlete adapts to training and becomes better conditioned, it is appropriate to consider increasing the number of training days to four and, with additional training, maybe five, six, or seven. The general guideline is to schedule training sessions so that there is at least one rest or recovery day—but not more than three—between sessions that stress the same muscle groups. If a strength and conditioning professional wants a beginner athlete to perform a total body resistance training program two times per week, the sessions should be spaced out evenly (e.g., Monday and Thursday or Tuesday and Friday). If the athlete trains only on Monday and Wednesday, the absence of a training stimulus between Wednesday and the following Monday may result in a decrease in the athlete’s training status. For a short time in well-trained athletes, one session a week can maintain strength.
summary: Training frequency depends on athlete preparedness, training goals, and recovery needs.

content: As an athlete adapts to training and becomes better conditioned, it is appropriate to consider increasing the number of training days to four and, with additional training, maybe five, six, or seven.  More highly resistance-trained (intermediate or advanced) athletes can augment their training by using a split routine in which different muscle groups are trained on different days.  Training nearly every day may seem to violate the recommended guidelines for recovery, but grouping exercises that train a portion of the body (e.g., upper body or lower body) or certain muscle areas (e.g., chest, shoulder, and triceps) gives the trained athlete an opportunity to adequately recover between similar training sessions.
summary: This chunk discusses how training frequency can increase with athlete adaptation and the use of split routines for more advanced athletes.

content: The general guideline is to schedule training sessions so that there is at least one rest or recovery day—but not more than three—between sessions that stress the same muscle groups. If a strength and conditioning professional wants a beginner athlete to perform a total body resistance training program two times per week, the sessions should be spaced out evenly (e.g., Monday and Thursday or Tuesday and Friday). If the athlete trains only on Monday and Wednesday, the absence of a training stimulus between Wednesday and the following Monday may result in a decrease in the athlete’s training status.
summary: This chunk outlines general guidelines for scheduling training sessions, emphasizing the importance of rest days and avoiding excessive gaps between training.

content: For a short time in well-trained athletes, one session a week can maintain strength. Athletes who train with maximal or near-maximal loads require more recovery time prior to their next training session. The ability to train more frequently may be enhanced by alternating lighter and heavier training days. There is also evidence that upper body muscles can recover more quickly from heavy loading sessions than lower body muscles. The same is true regarding an athlete’s ability to recover faster from single-joint exercises compared to multijoint exercises. These research findings may explain why, for example, powerlifters may schedule only one very heavy deadlift or squat training session per week.
summary: This chunk explores factors influencing training frequency, including athlete experience, training intensity, and muscle group recovery.

content: Another influence on resistance training frequency is the sport season. For example, the increased emphasis on practicing the sport skill during the in-season necessitates a decrease in the time spent in the weight room and, consequently, reduces the frequency of resistance training. Table 15.6 shows resistance training frequency based on the sport season for a trained athlete. During the off-season, resistance training frequency guidelines are 4-6 sessions per week. During the preseason, resistance training frequency guidelines are 3-4 sessions per week. During the in-season, resistance training frequency guidelines are 1-3 sessions per week. During the postseason (active rest), resistance training frequency guidelines are 0-3 sessions per week. Data for Table 15.6 is from references 27, 100, and 104.
summary: This chunk focuses on how the sport season influences resistance training frequency, providing specific guidelines for different phases.

content: Exercise frequency is also influenced by the overall amount of physical stress. The strength and conditioning professional must consider the effects of all forms of exercise on exercise frequency. If an athlete’s program includes aerobic or anaerobic training, sport skill practice, or any combination of these components, the frequency of resistance training may need to be reduced. Athletes who work in manual labor jobs, instruct or assist others in physical activities, or are on their feet all day may not be able to withstand the same training frequency as athletes who are less active outside of their sport-related pursuits.
summary: This chunk highlights the impact of overall physical stress and individual factors on exercise frequency.

content: Exercise order refers to a sequence of resistance exercises performed during one training session. Decisions on exercise order are based on how one exercise affects the quality of effort or the technique of another exercise. Exercises are usually arranged so that an athlete’s maximal force capabilities are available to complete a set with proper exercise technique. Four common methods of ordering resistance exercises are described in the following paragraphs. Strength and conditioning professionals may consult the work of Fleck and Kraemer for detailed information on other exercise arrangement strategies.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of exercise order and its importance in training sessions.

content: Power exercises such as the snatch, hang clean, power clean, and push jerk should be performed first in a training session, followed by other nonpower core exercises, and then assistance exercises. The literature also refers to the exercise arrangement of power exercises followed by other core exercises and then assistance exercises as multijoint exercises and then single-joint exercises or large muscle areas and then small muscle areas.
summary: This chunk provides a specific example of exercise order, prioritizing power exercises followed by core and assistance exercises.

content: Table 15.5 provides examples of common split routines. A sample training week for a lower body split routine is shown in Table 15.5. The lower body split routine in Table 15.5 is trained 4 times per week. The upper body split routine in Table 15.5 is trained 5 times per week. The lower body split routine in Table 15.5 is trained 6 times per week. Frequency of training varies between 5 times per week and 6 times per week depending on the day of the week that is the first training day.
summary: This chunk presents examples of common split routines and their training frequencies.

content: Exercises are usually arranged so that an athlete’s maximal force capabilities are available to complete a set with proper exercise technique. Power exercises such as the snatch, hang clean, power clean, and push jerk should be performed first in a training session, followed by other nonpower core exercises, and then assistance exercises. The literature also refers to the exercise arrangement of power exercises followed by other core exercises and then assistance exercises as multijoint exercises and then single-joint exercises or large muscle areas and then small muscle areas.
summary: This chunk outlines the general principle of exercise arrangement, emphasizing the prioritization of power exercises followed by core and assistance exercises.

content: Power exercises require the highest level of skill and concentration of all exercises and are most affected by fatigue. Athletes who become fatigued are prone to using poor technique and consequently are at higher risk of injury. The explosive movements and extensive muscular involvement of power exercises also result in a significant energy expenditure. If power exercises are not selected in step 2 (exercise selection), then the recommended order of exercises is core exercises and then assistance exercises.
summary: This chunk explains the rationale behind prioritizing power exercises, highlighting their demanding nature and the potential risks of fatigue.

content: The ‘reverse’ exercise arrangement, called preexhaustion, is a viable ordering method that purposely fatigues a large muscle group as a result of the application of the Training Frequency Guidelines. Athletes can perform the leg (knee) curl exercise before the back squat exercise to pre-exhaust the hamstring muscle group.
summary: This chunk introduces the preexhaustion method, where a muscle group is fatigued before a compound exercise targeting that muscle.

content: One method of providing the opportunity for athletes to recover more fully between exercises is to alternate upper body exercises with lower body exercises. This arrangement is especially helpful for untrained individuals who find that completing several upper or lower body exercises in succession is too strenuous. If training time is limited, this method of arranging exercises minimizes the length of the rest periods required between exercises and maximizes the rest between body areas. The result is a decrease in overall training time, because the athlete can perform an upper body exercise and then immediately go to a lower body exercise without having to wait for the upper body to rest.
summary: This chunk describes the benefits of alternating upper and lower body exercises for recovery and time efficiency.

content: If the exercises are performed with minimal rest periods (20-30 seconds), this method is also referred to as circuit training. Resistance training can sometimes be used to improve cardiorespiratory endurance, although to a lesser extent than conventional aerobic exercise training.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the concept of circuit training and its potential for improving cardiorespiratory fitness.

content: Another method of improving recovery and recruitment between exercises is to alternate pushing exercises with pulling exercises. A push-pull arrangement ensures that the same muscle group will not be used in two exercises (or sets, in some cases) in succession, thus reducing fatigue in the involved muscles. Arranging several pulling exercises one after the other, even with a rest period between each, will compromise the number of repetitions performed because the biceps brachii muscle will become less responsive due to fatigue. The same result would occur if several pushing exercises were sequentially arranged.
summary: This chunk explains the push-pull training method, emphasizing its benefits for muscle recovery and preventing fatigue.

content: There are also push-pull arrangements for the lower body, for example, hip sled and back squat as 'push' and stiff-leg deadlift and leg (knee) curl as 'pull'. The classification of some exercises as 'push' or 'pull' is not as clear (e.g., leg [knee] extension). The alternation of push and pull exercises is also used in circuit training programs and is an ideal arrangement for athletes beginning or returning to a resistance training program.
summary: This chunk provides examples of push-pull arrangements for both upper and lower body exercises.

content: Other methods of arranging exercises involve having athletes perform one set of a pair of exercises with little to no rest between them. A superset involves two sequentially performed exercises that stress two opposing muscles or muscle areas (i.e., an agonist and its antagonist). For example, an athlete performs 10 repetitions of the barbell biceps curl exercise, sets the bar down, then goes over to the triceps pushdown station and performs 10 repetitions. A compound set involves sequentially performing two different exercises for the same muscle group. For instance, an athlete completes a set of the barbell biceps curl exercise, then switches to dumbbells and immediately performs a set of the hammer curl exercise. Both methods of arranging and performing pairs of exercises are time efficient and purposely more demanding—and consequently may not be appropriate for unconditioned athletes. Note, however, that sometimes the meanings of superset and compound set are interchanged.
summary: This chunk describes supersets and compound sets, highlighting their time efficiency and intensity.

content: Load is most simply referred to as the amount of weight assigned to an exercise set and is often characterized as the most critical aspect of a resistance training program. Mechanical work can be defined as the product of force and displacement (sometimes labeled distance). An athlete can perform (external) mechanical work via 'demands' made upon the body to generate (internal) metabolic energy. Thus, it is important to quantify the amount of mechanical work or degree of metabolic demand in order to plan variation in the training program and to avoid the exhaustion phase of Selye’s General Adaptation Syndrome (see chapter 19) associated with overtraining (see chapters 5 and 6). A quantity measure for resistance training 'work' is needed. Traditionally, at least in the sport of Olympic weightlifting, this 'work' is called the 'load' and it can be calculated by multiplying each weight lifted by the number of times it is lifted and summing all such values over a training session. However, load-volume or volume-load may be a better term than just 'load'. This quantity is highly related to mechanical work and the associated metabolic energy demands and physiological stress, and also is distinguished from repetition- (rep-) volume (i.e., the total number of repetitions performed). The text describes exercise order strategies for three different athlete scenarios.
summary: This chunk delves into the concept of load, mechanical work, and its importance in training program design.

content: A quantity measure for resistance training 'work' is needed. Traditionally, at least in the sport of Olympic weightlifting, this 'work' is called the 'load' and it can be calculated by multiplying each weight lifted by the number of times it is lifted and summing all such values over a training session. However, load-volume or volume-load may be a better term than just 'load'. This quantity is highly related to mechanical work and the associated metabolic energy demands and physiological stress, and also is distinguished from repetition- (rep-) volume (i.e., the total number of repetitions performed).
summary: This chunk defines 'load' as a measure of resistance training work, exploring alternative terms and its relationship to mechanical work, metabolic stress, and repetition volume.

content: The text describes exercise order strategies for three different athlete scenarios. Scenario A is a female collegiate basketball player in the preseason. Scenario B is a male professional American football lineman in the off-season. Scenario C is a male high school cross-country runner in the in-season.
summary: This chunk introduces three athlete scenarios used to illustrate different exercise order strategies.

content: The first exercise order strategy is power, other core, then assistance exercises. The first strategy also alternates 'push' and 'pull' exercises on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. Exercises in the first strategy include hang clean, push jerk, front squat, incline bench press, seated row, dumbbell alternating curl, triceps pushdown, and abdominal crunch.
summary: This chunk describes the first exercise order strategy, focusing on power exercises, core work, and assistance exercises, with a push-pull alternation.
