content: The second exercise order strategy is core and then assistance exercises. The second strategy also alternates 'push' and 'pull' exercises. Lower body exercises are performed on Monday and Thursday, and upper body exercises are performed on Tuesday and Friday. Lower body exercises in the second strategy include deadlift, back squat, step-up, leg (knee) curl, and seated calf (heel) raise. Upper body exercises in the second strategy include bench press, bent-over row, shoulder press, barbell biceps curl, shoulder shrug, and lying triceps extension.
summary: This chunk outlines the second exercise order strategy, prioritizing core work followed by assistance exercises, with a push-pull alternation and specific lower and upper body days.

content: The third exercise order strategy is core and then assistance exercises. The third strategy alternates upper and lower body exercises on Wednesday and Saturday. Exercises in the third strategy include lunge, vertical chest press, leg (knee) curl, one-arm dumbbell row, toe raise (dorsiflexion), lateral shoulder raise, machine back extension, and abdominal crunch. In the third strategy, complete one set of each exercise, then repeat.
summary: This chunk describes the third exercise order strategy, focusing on core work, assistance exercises, alternating upper and lower body days, and completing one set of each exercise before repeating.

content: Exercises that concentrically train the lower back muscles should be performed after exercises that require an erect torso or a flat-back position. Fatigue of the lower back muscles can result in incorrect and potentially injurious exercise technique in structural or standing exercises.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of exercise order, suggesting lower back exercises should follow those requiring an erect torso to prevent fatigue-induced improper technique.

content: Mechanical and metabolic power output is a good approximation for intensity or quality of work parameters.
summary: This chunk highlights the use of mechanical and metabolic power output as a measure of exercise intensity and quality.

content: The number of repetitions an exercise can be performed is inversely related to the load lifted. Training for muscular strength involves lifting heavy loads for few repetitions.
summary: This chunk explains the inverse relationship between load and repetitions, emphasizing the use of heavy loads for strength training.

content: Load is commonly described as either a certain percentage of a 1-repetition maximum (1RM) or the most weight lifted for a specified number of repetitions, a repetition maximum (RM). If an athlete can perform 10 repetitions with 60 kg in the back squat exercise, her 10RM is 60 kg. By definition, 100% of the 1RM allows the athlete to perform one repetition.
summary: This chunk defines 'load' as a percentage of 1RM or a repetition maximum (RM), providing an example of calculating a 10RM.

content: As the percentage of the 1RM (i.e., the load lifted) decreases, the athlete will be able to successfully complete more repetitions. Other %1RM–repetition tables with slightly different %1RM values can be found in the literature, but they vary by only about 0.5 to 2 percentage points from those provided in table 15.7. Table 15.7 assumes there is a linear association between the loads lifted and the repetitions performed; however, several studies have reported a curvilinear relationship.
summary: This chunk explains the inverse relationship between load and repetitions, referencing a table (15.7) that provides estimated repetitions for different percentages of 1RM, acknowledging potential curvilinear relationships.

content: Resistance-trained athletes may be able to exceed the number of repetitions listed in the table at any given percentage of their 1RM, especially in lower body core exercises. The number of repetitions that can be performed at a certain percent of the 1RM is based on a single set. When an athlete performs multiple sets, the loads may need to be reduced so that the desired number of repetitions can be completed in all of the sets.
summary: This chunk acknowledges that athletes may exceed predicted repetitions, particularly in lower body core exercises, and emphasizes the need to adjust loads for multiple sets.

content: Despite the prevalence of 1RM research, athletes may not always perform the predicted number of repetitions at a specified percentage of a 1RM. Studies conducted by Hoeger and colleagues showed that subjects were able to perform two to three times more repetitions than are listed in table 15.7. A certain percentage of the 1RM assigned to a machine exercise can result in more repetitions at the same percentage of the 1RM than with a similar free weight exercise. Exercises involving smaller muscle areas may not produce as many repetitions as seen in table 15.7, and exercises recruiting large muscle areas are likely to result in more repetitions performed.
summary: This chunk highlights the variability in actual repetitions compared to predicted values, citing studies showing higher performance, and discusses factors influencing repetitions based on exercise type and muscle group.

content: The most accurate relationship between percentages of the 1RM and the maximum repetitions possible is for loads ≥75% of the 1RM and ≤10 repetitions. Empirical evidence suggests that as the percentage of the 1RM decreases, the variability in the number of repetitions that can be completed increases. Loads calculated from the %1RM in table 15.7 should be used only as a guideline for estimating a particular RM load for a resistance training exercise.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the most accurate relationship between 1RM percentages and repetitions, acknowledging increased variability at lower percentages, and reminding users that the provided table is a guideline.

content: A certain percentage of the 1RM assigned to a machine exercise can result in more repetitions at the same percentage of the 1RM than with a similar free weight exercise. Exercises involving smaller muscle areas may not produce as many repetitions as seen in table 15.7, and exercises recruiting large muscle areas are likely to result in more repetitions performed.
summary: Repetitions vary based on exercise type and muscle group size, with machine exercises potentially allowing for more reps at the same percentage of 1RM compared to free weights.

content: The most accurate relationship between percentages of the 1RM and the maximum repetitions possible is for loads ≥75% of the 1RM and ≤10 repetitions. Empirical evidence suggests that as the percentage of the 1RM decreases, the variability in the number of repetitions that can be completed increases.
summary: The relationship between 1RM percentage and maximum repetitions is most accurate for loads between 75% and 10 reps, with variability increasing as the percentage decreases.

content: Loads calculated from the %1RM in table 15.7 should be used only as a guideline for estimating a particular RM load for a resistance training exercise. It appears that it is still more accurate to assign loads based on a percentage of a test-established 1RM than it is to estimate a 1RM from a submaximal load.
summary: While table 15.7 provides guidance, estimating 1RM from submaximal loads is less accurate than using a tested 1RM for load assignment.

content: To gather information needed to assign a training load, the strength and conditioning professional has the option of determining the athlete’s actual 1RM (directly tested), estimated 1RM from a multiple-RM test (e.g., a 10RM), or multiple RM based on the number of repetitions planned for that exercise (the “goal” repetitions; e.g., five repetitions per set). Once the actual 1RM is measured or estimated, the athlete’s training load is calculated as a percentage of the 1RM.
summary: Strength and conditioning professionals can determine training loads using actual 1RM, estimated 1RM from multiple-RM tests, or goal repetitions, with the load calculated as a percentage of the chosen 1RM.

content: Alternatively, a multiple-RM test may be performed based on goal repetitions, thereby eliminating computations or estimations. In many cases, the strength and conditioning professional will use a variety of testing options depending on the exercises selected and the athlete’s training background.
summary: Multiple-RM tests based on goal repetitions offer an alternative to direct 1RM testing, and professionals often choose testing methods based on the athlete's experience and exercise selection.

content: A common strategy for testing sufficiently conditioned athletes is to conduct a 1RM test in several core exercises and use multiple-RM testing for assistance exercises. To assign training loads based on a percentage of the 1RM, the strength and conditioning professional must first determine the athlete’s 1RM.
summary: Experienced athletes often undergo 1RM testing for core exercises and multiple-RM testing for assistance exercises, with 1RM determination being crucial for load assignment.

content: This method of assessment is typically reserved for resistance-trained athletes who are classified as intermediate or advanced and have exercise technique experience in the exercises being tested. Individuals who are untrained, inexperienced, injured, or medically supervised may not be appropriate participants for 1RM testing.
summary: 1RM testing is suitable for experienced, resistance-trained athletes with proper technique, but not for untrained, inexperienced, injured, or medically supervised individuals.

content: 1RM testing requires an adequate training status and lifting experience, as the assessment of maximal strength places significant stress on the involved muscles, connective tissues, and joints. Thus, it has been suggested that a 3RM test could be used instead of a maximal 1RM test.
summary: 1RM testing demands adequate training and lifting experience due to the stress placed on the body, suggesting a 3RM test as a safer alternative for some individuals.

content: Ignoring an athlete’s training status and exercise technique experience will diminish the safety and accuracy of 1RM test results. When selecting exercises for 1RM testing, the strength and conditioning professional should choose core exercises because the large muscle groups and multiple joints are better able to handle the heavy loads.
summary: Safety and accuracy of 1RM testing rely on considering the athlete's training status and technique, with core exercises being preferred due to their ability to handle heavy loads.

content: An exercise should not be selected for 1RM testing if it cannot provide valid and reliable data (i.e., does not accurately and consistently assess maximal muscular strength). For instance, the large upper back musculature and multiple joints involved in the bent-over row exercise can probably tolerate the loads from a 1RM test, but maintaining a correct body position throughout testing would be extremely difficult.
summary: Exercises chosen for 1RM testing must accurately assess maximal strength, considering factors like the ability to maintain proper form under heavy loads.

content: The weaker stabilizing muscles of the lower back might become very fatigued after several testing sets, resulting in a loss of proper exercise technique and invalid and potentially unreliable test data.
summary: Exercise selection for 1RM testing should consider the potential for fatigue in stabilizing muscles, which can compromise test validity.

content: A variety of procedures can be used to accurately determine a 1RM; one method is described in figure 15.1. When maximal strength testing is not warranted, testing with a 10RM load (and then estimating or predicting the 1RM) can be a suitable secondary option.
summary: Various methods exist for determining 1RM, with a 10RM test offering a suitable alternative when maximal strength testing is not necessary.

content: This approach is appropriate for nearly all athletes, provided they can demonstrate the proper technique in the exercise tested. Core and assistance exercises can be selected for 10RM testing, but excessive warm-up and testing sets may fatigue the athlete and compromise the accuracy of the test.
summary: 10RM testing is suitable for most athletes with proper technique, but excessive warm-up and testing sets can lead to fatigue and compromised accuracy.

content: The protocol for 10RM testing is similar to that for 1RM testing, but each set requires 10 repetitions, not one. After the completion of warm-up sets, the athlete’s sequential load changes for the 10RM test are smaller than those listed in figure 15.1 (approximately one-half). An experienced strength and conditioning professional will be able to adjust the loads so that the 10RM can be measured within three to five testing sets.
summary: 10RM testing follows a similar protocol to 1RM testing, but with 10 repetitions per set and smaller load increments, requiring an experienced professional to adjust loads for accurate measurement.

content: To estimate the athlete’s 1RM, consult table 15.8. In the “Max reps (RM) = 10” column, first find the tested 10RM load; then read across the row to the “Max reps (RM) = 1” (%1RM = 100) column to discover the athlete’s projected 1RM.
summary: Table 15.8 allows estimation of 1RM based on a tested 10RM load, providing a projected 1RM value.

content: Data from references 5, 15, 16, 25, 62, 68, 73, and 110. Resistance training has developed the neuromuscular attributes that will make testing with heavier loads (e.g., 1RM-5RM) safe and effective. Equations are available to predict the 1RM from multiple-RM loads. Researchers report that as the loads used in multiple-RM testing become heavier (i.e., bringing the loads closer to the actual 1RM), the accuracy of the 1RM estimation increases. Predictions are more accurate when the equations are based on loads equal to or less than a 10RM. The results obtained from lower multiple-RM testing (and subsequent predictions of the 1RM) are generally more accurate when an athlete has been consistently training with low multiple-RM resistances (i.e., heavy loads) for a few months prior to testing.
summary: This chunk discusses the safety and accuracy of predicting 1RM from multiple-RM loads, emphasizing the importance of consistent heavy training.

content: A third option for determining training loads requires the strength and conditioning professional to first decide the number of repetitions (i.e., the goal repetitions) the athlete will perform in the actual program for the exercise being tested. If the strength and conditioning professional decides that the athlete should perform six repetitions for the bench press exercise in the training program, the multiple-RM testing protocol should have the athlete perform the exercise with a load that will result in six repetitions (6RM).
summary: This chunk explains an alternative method for determining training loads based on the desired number of repetitions for a specific exercise.

content: Core and assistance exercises can be selected for multiple-RM testing, but, as previously mentioned, high-repetition testing sets can create significant fatigue and may compromise the accuracy of the tested multiple RM. This effect seems to be more problematic for exercises that involve multiple joints and large muscle areas due to their high metabolic demand. Further, multiple-RM testing (and subsequent load assignments) for assistance exercises should be at or above an 8RM to minimize the isolative stress on the involved joint and connective tissue. Even if an athlete is following a muscular strength training program that involves 2RM loads for the core exercises, the heaviest load the assistance exercises should be assigned is an 8RM.
summary: This chunk provides guidelines for multiple-RM testing of core and assistance exercises, emphasizing the importance of minimizing fatigue and joint stress.

content: If the strength and conditioning professional decides that the athlete should perform six repetitions for the bench press exercise in the training program, the multiple-RM testing protocol should have the athlete perform the exercise with a load that will result in six repetitions (6RM).  The options for testing and assigning training loads and repetitions are summarized in figure 15.2.  The continuum concept effectively illustrates that a certain RM emphasizes a specific outcome, but the training benefits are blended at any given RM.  Despite the physiological blend of training effects, the specificity principle still dictates the dominant outcome that is attained and enhanced with a particular training load.
summary: This chunk explains how to determine the appropriate load for a given repetition range during multiple-RM testing, emphasizing the relationship between RM and training outcomes.

content: During the needs analysis (step 1, p. 382), the strength and conditioning professional is challenged to choose the primary goal of the resistance training program based on the athlete’s testing results, the movement and physiological analysis of the sport, and the priorities of the athlete’s sport season. Once decided on, the training goal can be applied to determine specific load and repetition assignments via the RM continuum, a percentage of the 1RM (either directly tested or estimated), or the results of multiple-RM testing.
summary: This chunk outlines the process of determining the primary training goal for an athlete based on various factors and how that goal influences load and repetition assignments.

content: The relationship between the percentage of the 1RM and the estimated number of repetitions that can be performed at that load (table 15.7) allows the strength and conditioning professional to assign a specific resistance to be used for an exercise in a training session. The training goal is attained when the athlete lifts a load of a certain percentage of the 1RM for a specific number of repetitions (table 15.9). For example, suppose an athlete’s training goal is muscular strength and the tested 1RM in the bench press exercise is 220 pounds (100 kg). To increase strength, the athlete needs to handle loads of at least 85% of the 1RM (after warm-up) that typically allow performance of up to six repetitions per set (table 15.9). Multiply the 1RM by the percent of the 1RM expressed as a decimal. Determine the percent of the 1RM associated with the number of goal repetitions. Choose the GOAL REPETITIONS. Consider the influence of the training goal on the load and repetition assignments.
summary: This chunk explains how to determine the appropriate load and repetitions for a given training goal using the 1RM and percentage-based guidelines.

content: Relatively heavy loads should be used if the goal is strength or power, moderate loads for hypertrophy, and light loads for muscular endurance (as indicated by the larger font sizes). Low multiple RMs appear to have the greatest effect on strength and maximum power training, and high multiple RMs seem to result in better muscular endurance improvements.
summary: This chunk outlines the general relationship between training load and specific training goals (strength, power, hypertrophy, muscular endurance).

content: The force–velocity curve illustrates that the greater the amount of concentric muscular force generated, the slower the muscle shortening and corresponding movement velocity and vice versa. Maximal power is produced at intermediate velocities with the lifting of moderate, not maximal, loads. Performing a 1RM involves slower movement velocities; maximum force is generated, but with reduced power output. Seldom is an athlete required to demonstrate a singular, maximal, slow-speed muscular strength effort in a sport (except in powerlifting, for example). Most sport movements are faster and involve higher power outputs than those produced during a 1RM test. An athlete’s power capabilities are unaffected by maximal muscular strength training. Because speed- or power-related sport movements often begin from zero or near-zero velocities, slow-velocity strength gains have direct application to power production. For these reasons, the load and repetition assignments for power training overlap the guidelines for strength training.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between force, velocity, and power, highlighting the importance of considering movement speed in training.

content: Single-joint muscle action data reveal that peak power is reached with the lifting of very light loads—30% of the 1RM. Figure 15.3 shows how RM ranges are associated with various training goals.
summary: This chunk provides specific information about the optimal load for power development and illustrates the relationship between RM ranges and training goals.

content: Core and assistance exercises can be selected for multiple-RM testing, but, as previously mentioned, high-repetition testing sets can create significant fatigue and may compromise the accuracy of the tested multiple RM. This effect seems to be more problematic for exercises that involve multiple joints and large muscle areas due to their high metabolic demand. Further, multiple-RM testing (and subsequent load assignments) for assistance exercises should be at or above an 8RM to minimize the isolative stress on the involved joint and connective tissue. Even if an athlete is following a muscular strength training program that involves 2RM loads for the core exercises, the heaviest load the assistance exercises should be assigned is an 8RM.
summary: This chunk provides guidelines for selecting and testing exercises for multiple-RM, emphasizing the importance of considering fatigue and joint stress.

content: Perform a 1RM test. Perform a 10RM test. Perform a multiple-RM test.  Determine the 1RM. Estimate the 1RM. Determine the multiple RM. If the strength and conditioning professional assigns four repetitions per set for this exercise, the corresponding load will be approximately 90% of the 1RM or approximately 200 pounds (90 kg). The strength and conditioning professional should make adjustments to assigned loads based on observation of the ease or difficulty an athlete experiences in lifting the load for the required repetitions.
summary: This chunk outlines the different methods for testing and determining 1RM and multiple-RM values, emphasizing the importance of individualization.

content: An athlete’s power capabilities are unaffected by maximal muscular strength training. Because speed- or power-related sport movements often begin from zero or near-zero velocities, slow-velocity strength gains have direct application to power production. For these reasons, the load and repetition assignments for power training overlap the guidelines for strength training.
summary: Power development is distinct from maximal strength, but slow-velocity strength gains still contribute to power. Power training loads and reps overlap with strength training guidelines.

content: Single-joint muscle action data reveal that peak power is reached with the lifting of very light loads—30% of the 1RM. Figure 15.3 shows how RM ranges are associated with various training goals. The repetition ranges shown for power in Figure 15.3 are not consistent with the %1RM–repetition relationship.
summary: Peak power is achieved with light loads (30% of 1RM), but power training repetition ranges don't perfectly align with the typical %1RM-repetition relationship.

content: On average, loads equaling about 80% of the 1RM apply to the two- to five-repetition range. The load and repetition assignments shown for power in Table 15.9 are not consistent with the %1RM–repetition relationship. On average, loads equaling about 80% of the 1RM apply to the two- to five-repetition range. Data from references 11, 26, 27, 30, 42, 44, 58, 65, 78, 99, 105, 106, and 107 were used to create Table 15.9.
summary: Power training often uses loads around 80% of 1RM for 2-5 reps, but this doesn't perfectly align with the typical %1RM-repetition relationship. This data is based on multiple research references.

content: The load and repetition assignments shown for power in Table 15.9 are not consistent with the %1RM–repetition relationship. On average, loads equaling about 80% of the 1RM apply to the two- to five-repetition range. For athletes training for single-effort power events (e.g., shot put, high jump, weightlifting), Garhammer suggests assigning sets of one to two repetitions using loads that equal 80% to 90% of the 1RM, especially on heavy training days. For sports with multiple maximum power efforts (e.g., the frequent maximum vertical jumping motions of a volleyball blocker), three to five repetitions per set with loads at 75% to 85% of the 1RM are recommended.
summary: Power training load and reps don't perfectly align with the %1RM-repetition relationship.  Specific recommendations vary based on the sport, with single-effort events using heavier loads (80-90% 1RM) for 1-2 reps, and multiple-effort sports using 75-85% 1RM for 3-5 reps.

content: The primary preseason resistance training goal is strength/power. Power exercises should be performed at 75-85% of the 1RM for 3-5 repetitions. Other core exercises should be performed at ≥85% of the 1RM for ≤6 repetitions. Assistance exercises should be limited to loads not heavier than an 8RM.
summary: Preseason training prioritizes strength and power. Power exercises use 75-85% 1RM for 3-5 reps, core exercises use ≥85% 1RM for ≤6 reps, and assistance exercises are limited to ≤8RM.

content: The primary off-season resistance training goal is hypertrophy. Exercises for hypertrophy should be performed at 67-85% of the 1RM for 6-12 repetitions.
summary: Off-season training focuses on hypertrophy. Exercises are performed at 67-85% 1RM for 6-12 reps.

content: The primary in-season resistance training goal is muscular endurance. Exercises for muscular endurance should be performed at ≤67% of the 1RM for ≤12 repetitions.
summary: In-season training emphasizes muscular endurance. Exercises are performed at ≤67% 1RM for ≤12 reps.

content: 1RM testing is used for core exercises. Deadlift, back squat, bench press, and shoulder press are examples of core exercises. 10RM testing is used for new assistance exercises. Step-up, seated calf (heel) raise, bent-over row, and shoulder shrug are examples of new assistance exercises.

On Mondays, the full load assignments calculated under 'Training loads' should be used. On Wednesdays, only 80% of Monday's 'heavy day' load should be assigned. On Fridays, only 90% of Monday's 'heavy day' load should be assigned.

content: The load and repetition assignments for power exercises are based on basketball, a multiple-effort event, and may not align with the %1RM–repetition relationship. To assess power, a multiple-RM (3RM) protocol is used, and the 1RM is estimated from the result. Load assignments for power exercises are calculated as a percentage of the estimated 1RM.
summary: This chunk describes the load and repetition assignments for power exercises.

content: The athlete will be tested to determine their current 1RM for more accurate preseason load assignments. The athlete's preseason goal repetitions for power exercises are 10, rather than 12 from the previous season. Estimate the 1RM using table 15.8, pages 397-398.

Calculate 90% of the loads lifted on Monday and perform the same number of goal repetitions (power–5; other core–6).

summary: This chunk describes the in-season testing and load assignments for exercises.

content: Three to five repetitions are typically associated with loads 93% to 87% of the 1RM. Power exercises cannot be maximally loaded at any repetition scheme because the quality of the movement technique will decline before momentary muscle fatigue defines a true multiple-RM set. Power exercises are usually limited to five repetitions per set, but with loads up to and equal to a 10RM (i.e., approximately 75% of the 1RM).
summary: This chunk describes the typical load and repetition schemes for power exercises.

content: Power training can be emphasized across the range of five repetitions or fewer, but the strength and conditioning professional should realize that these loads are not true repetition maximums. Training for muscular strength and power places a high physiological stress on an athlete’s body. Intermediate and advanced resistance-trained athletes are accustomed to lifting heavy loads and possess the experience and motivation to make nearly every set an RM set.
summary: This chunk describes the physiological stress of power training and the experience of advanced athletes.

content: An athlete may resistance train three days a week with muscular strength as the goal (e.g., Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays).
summary: This chunk describes a possible training schedule for muscular strength.

content: Power exercises are usually limited to five repetitions per set, but with loads up to and equal to a 10RM (i.e., approximately 75% of the 1RM). Power training can be emphasized across the range of five repetitions or fewer, but the strength and conditioning professional should realize that these loads are not true repetition maximums.
summary: Power exercises typically involve loads up to 75% of 1RM for 5 repetitions or fewer.

content: Training for muscular strength and power places a high physiological stress on an athlete’s body. Intermediate and advanced resistance-trained athletes are accustomed to lifting heavy loads and possess the experience and motivation to make nearly every set an RM set.
summary: Strength and power training are physiologically demanding, especially for experienced athletes accustomed to heavy lifting.

content: One strategy to counterbalance the overtraining associated with the heavy loads is to alter the loads (%1RMs) for the power and other core exercises so that only one training day each week (e.g., Monday) is a heavy day. These ‘heavy day’ loads are designed to be full repetition maximums, the greatest resistance that can be successfully lifted for the goal number of repetitions.
summary: To mitigate overtraining, a weekly training schedule can alternate between heavy lifting days (targeting 1RM) and lighter days.

content: For the light day, calculate 80% of the loads lifted in the power and other core exercises on the heavy day (Monday) and instruct the athlete to complete the same number of goal repetitions. Similarly, calculate 90% of the loads lifted in the power and other core exercises from Monday’s training session for the ‘medium’ day and instruct the athlete to perform only the assigned number of goal repetitions (5, 6, 99, 110).
summary: Training days can be structured with varying load percentages (80% for light, 90% for medium) compared to the heavy day.

content: This approach can be used for any training frequency. A two-days-a-week program could have a heavy day and a light day, or an upper body–lower body split routine could consist of two heavy days (one upper body day and one lower body day) followed by two light days. Varying the training loads also works well with an athlete’s other training, in that heavy lifting days can fall on light sport conditioning days, and light lifting days on heavy sport conditioning days. The strength and conditioning professional needs to monitor this schedule, so that it does not lead to heavy training every day.
summary: Training frequency and load variation can be tailored to individual programs and integrated with other training modalities.

content: As the athlete adapts to the training stimulus, the strength and conditioning professional needs to have a strategy for advancing exercise loads so that improvements will continue over time (progression). Monitoring each athlete’s training and charting his or her response to the prescribed workouts enable the strength and conditioning professional to know when and to what extent the loads should be increased.
summary: Progressive overload is essential for continued training adaptations, requiring monitoring and adjustments based on individual athlete progress.

content: A conservative method that can be used to increase an athlete’s training loads is called the 2-for-2 rule. If the athlete can perform two or more repetitions over his or her assigned repetition goal for a given exercise in the last set in two consecutive workouts, weight should be added to that exercise for the next training session. For example, a strength and conditioning professional assigns three sets of 10 repetitions in the bench press exercise, and the athlete performs all 10 repetitions in all sets. After several workout sessions (the specific number depends on many factors), the athlete is able to complete 12 repetitions in the third (last) set for two consecutive workouts.
summary: The 2-for-2 rule is a conservative method for progressive overload, increasing weight when an athlete consistently exceeds their repetition goal for two consecutive workouts.

content: The decision as to the size of the load increase can be difficult to make, but table 15.10 provides general recommendations based on the athlete’s condition (stronger or weaker) and body area (upper or lower body). Despite these guidelines, the significant variation in training status, load-volumes, and exercises (type and muscular involvement) greatly influences the appropriate load increases. To contend with this variability, relative load increases of 2.5% to 10% can be used instead of the absolute values shown in table 15.10.
summary: Load increases should be individualized based on athlete factors and training variables, with general guidelines and relative increases offering flexibility.

content: Volume relates to the total amount of weight lifted in a training session. A set is a group of repetitions sequentially performed before the athlete stops to rest. Repetition-volume is the total number of repetitions performed during a workout session. Load-volume is the total number of sets multiplied by the number of repetitions per set then multiplied by the weight lifted per repetition. For example, the load-volume for two sets of 10 repetitions with 50 pounds would be expressed as 2 3 10 3 50 pounds or 1,000 pounds. If different sets are performed with different amounts of weight, the volumes per set are calculated and then added to obtain the total training session volume.
summary: Volume in training can be expressed as repetition-volume, load-volume, or total weight lifted, depending on the specific metric used.

content: In the example just given (a load-volume of 1,000 pounds), multiplying each repetition by the additional factor of vertical displacement of the weight during that repetition would yield the concentric work performed. The displacement factor is fairly constant for a given athlete, so it is not used, but the resulting load-volume is still directly proportional to concentric work. As previously stated, load-volume divided by repetition-volume results in the average weight lifted per repetition, which is related to intensity or the quality of work.
summary: Load-volume is directly proportional to concentric work, and dividing load-volume by repetition-volume provides an indication of training intensity.

content: In running exercise, the common (rep) volume measure is distance. If an intensity value is known or measured (such as running pace, which relates to percent V.O2max), then total metabolic energy cost (which is proportional to mechanical work done) can be calculated. This value is comparable to load-volume in resistance exercise. The same concepts are applicable to the number of foot or hand contacts (volume) in plyometric exercise, the number of strokes (volume) in swimming or rowing, or the number of throws or jumps (volume) for various sport activities.
summary: Volume concepts can be applied to various training modalities, such as running, plyometrics, swimming, and rowing, using relevant metrics like distance, contacts, strokes, or repetitions.

content: Some have advocated that one set of 8 to 12 repetitions (after warm-up) performed to volitional muscular failure is sufficient to maximize gains in muscular strength and hypertrophy. Additionally, others have reported increases in maximum strength after the performance of only one set per exercise per session.
summary: Research suggests that even a single set of repetitions to volitional failure can be effective for strength and hypertrophy gains.

content: If an intensity value is known or measured (such as running pace, which relates to percent V.O2max), then total metabolic energy cost (which is proportional to mechanical work done) can be calculated. This value is comparable to load-volume in resistance exercise. The same concepts are applicable to the number of foot or hand contacts (volume) in plyometric exercise, the number of strokes (volume) in swimming or rowing, or the number of throws or jumps (volume) for various sport activities.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of metabolic energy cost and its relationship to training volume across various exercise types.

content: Some have advocated that one set of 8 to 12 repetitions (after warm-up) performed to volitional muscular failure is sufficient to maximize gains in muscular strength and hypertrophy. Additionally, others have reported increases in maximum strength after the performance of only one set per exercise per session. Single-set training may be appropriate for untrained individuals or during the first several months of training, but many studies indicate that higher volumes are necessary to promote further gains in strength, especially for intermediate and advanced resistance-trained athletes. Further, the musculoskeletal system will eventually adapt to the stimulus of one set to failure and require the added stimulus of multiple sets for continued strength gains. Moreover, performing three sets of 10 repetitions without going to failure enhances strength better than one set to failure in 8 to 12 repetitions, although the higher training volume with use of three sets is a contributing factor.
summary: This chunk discusses the effectiveness of single-set training versus multiple-set training for strength gains, highlighting the importance of progressive overload.

content: Therefore, an athlete who performs multiple sets from the initiation of his or her resistance training program will increase muscular strength faster than single-set training. Strength and conditioning professionals should not expect athletes to complete multiple sets with full RM loads at fixed repetition schemes for every exercise in each training session. Fatigue will affect the number of repetitions that can be performed in later sets. The training status of athletes affects the volume they will be able to tolerate. Beginners should perform only one or two sets, and as they become better trained, sets can be added. Training volume is directly based on the athlete’s resistance training goal.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of multiple sets for optimal strength gains and provides guidance on adjusting training volume based on athlete experience and goals.

content: Sets of 10 repetitions were recommended by DeLorme (19) and DeLorme and Watkins (20) to increase muscular strength. Berger (10, 11) determined that three sets of six repetitions created maximal strength gains in the bench press and back squat exercises. Berger's subsequent research (9) showed no significant difference among six sets of a 2RM load, three sets of a 6RM load, and three sets of a 10RM load despite the differences in volume. Many other studies have also been unable to support an exact set and repetition scheme to promote maximal increases in strength (22, 34, 35, 43, 79, 90, 98, 118). Most inconclusive reports on set and repetition schemes for strength training involved relatively untrained subjects. Nearly any type of program will cause improvements in strength for relatively untrained individuals.
summary: This chunk explores historical recommendations and research findings regarding optimal set and repetition schemes for strength training, noting the variability in results and the influence of training experience.

content: When training an athlete for strength, assigning volume begins with an examination of the optimal number of repetitions for maximal strength gains. Sets of six or fewer repetitions (at the corresponding RM load) for core exercises appear to be optimal for maximal strength gains (12, 26, 27, 30, 42, 44, 58, 65, 78, 99, 100, 105, 106, 107). Fleck and Kraemer (27) and Tan (104) concluded that a range of two to five sets or three to six sets, respectively, promotes the greatest increases in strength. Specific set guidelines based on exercise type suggest that only one to three sets may be appropriate or necessary for assistance exercises (6, 58).
summary: This chunk provides specific recommendations for set and repetition schemes based on exercise type and training goals, emphasizing the importance of considering individual needs.

content: Volume assignments for power training are typically lower than those for strength training in order to maximize the quality of exercise. The common guideline is three to five sets (after warm-up) for power exercises included in a trained athlete’s program (29, 44, 99, 100). Higher training volumes are associated with increases in muscular size (41, 71). This is the result of both a moderate to higher number of repetitions per set (6 to 12) and the commonly recommended number of sets. Resistance training programs typically involve three to six sets per exercise.
summary: This chunk outlines volume recommendations for power training and explains the relationship between training volume and muscle hypertrophy.

content: Research suggests that performing three or more exercises per muscle group is the most effective strategy for increasing muscle size. content: Resistance training programs that emphasize muscular endurance involve performing many repetitions—12 or more—per set.

content: The eight exercises are performed one set at a time, one immediately after the other (i.e., in a “circuit”) for Scenario B. Once two sets/circuits are completed, the athlete performs the final sets of the lunge, vertical chest press, and abdominal crunch exercises in that order for Scenario B.

content: Resistance training programs that emphasize muscular endurance involve performing many repetitions—12 or more—per set. Despite this relatively high repetition assignment, the overall volume-load is not necessarily overly inflated since the loads lifted are lighter and fewer sets are performed, commonly two to three per exercise.
summary: Muscular endurance training involves high repetitions (12+) per set with lighter loads and fewer sets (2-3).

content: The time dedicated to recovery between sets and exercises is called the rest period or interset rest. The length of the rest period between sets and exercises is highly dependent on the goal of training, the relative load lifted, and the athlete’s training status.
summary: Rest periods between sets and exercises vary based on training goals, load, and athlete status.

content: The amount of rest between sets is strongly related to load; the heavier the loads lifted, the longer the rest periods the athlete will need between sets. Training for muscular strength with 4RM loads requires significantly longer rest periods between sets than training for muscular endurance in which lighter 15RM loads are lifted.
summary: Heavier loads require longer rest periods; strength training needs more rest than muscular endurance training.

content: It is important that the strength and conditioning professional allocate rest periods based on the relative load lifted and the amount of muscle mass involved in each exercise. For example, an assistance exercise as part of a muscular strength training program may involve a 12RM load and therefore require only a 1-minute rest period.
summary: Rest periods should be tailored to the exercise load and muscle mass involved, with lighter assistance exercises requiring shorter rest.

content: Strength and Power Training may enhance an athlete’s ability to exercise with less rest. Athletes who seek to perform maximal or near-maximal repetitions with a heavy load usually need long rest periods, especially for lower body or all-body structural exercises. Common guidelines for rest period length are at least 2 minutes or a range of 2 to 5 minutes or 3 to 5 minutes. These recovery intervals appear to apply equally to resistance training programs designed to improve maximal strength and those that focus on muscular power.
summary: Strength and power training can improve recovery, but heavy lifts still require long rest periods (2-5 minutes). This applies to both maximal strength and power training.

content: Athletes who are interested in gaining muscular size often use a short to moderate interset rest period. Some reviews of hypertrophy training programs support a limited rest period because they recommend that the athlete begin the next set before full recovery has been achieved. Typical strategies for the length of rest periods are less than 1.5 minutes or a span of 30 seconds to 1 minute or 30 seconds to 1.5 minutes.
summary: Hypertrophy training often uses short to moderate rest periods (30 seconds to 1.5 minutes) to promote muscle growth.

content: A muscular endurance training program has very short rest periods, often less than 30 seconds. Short rest periods are characteristic of circuit training programs in which it is common to alternate exercises and limit rest period lengths to 30 seconds or less.
summary: Muscular endurance training uses very short rest periods (less than 30 seconds), common in circuit training.

content: The rest period for power and core exercises is 3 minutes. The rest period for assistance exercises is 60 seconds to 1.5 minutes. The rest period for core exercises is 1.5 minutes. The rest period for assistance exercises is 60 seconds. The rest period for core exercises is 30 seconds. The rest period for assistance exercises is 20 seconds.
summary: Rest periods are specified for different exercise types: power/core (3 minutes), assistance (60 seconds-1.5 minutes), core (1.5 minutes, 30 seconds), and assistance (20 seconds).
