content: Rest periods are 3 minutes for hang cleans, 1.5 minutes for back squats, 20 seconds for abdominal crunches, 60 seconds for triceps pushdowns, and 30 seconds for all other exercises.
summary: Rest periods vary depending on the exercise, ranging from 20 seconds for abdominal crunches to 3 minutes for hang cleans.

content: Plyometric exercise refers to activities that enable a muscle to reach maximal force in the shortest possible time. Plyometric exercise is a quick, powerful movement using a prestretch, or counter-movement, that involves the stretch-shortening cycle (SSC). The purpose of plyometric exercise is to increase the power of subsequent movements by using both the natural elastic components of muscle and tendon and the stretch reflex.
summary: Plyometric exercise involves quick, powerful movements using a prestretch to maximize force production and power output.

content: Functional movements and athletic success depend on both the proper function of all active muscles and the speed at which these muscular forces are used. When used correctly, plyometric training has consistently been shown to improve the production of muscle force and power. The mechanical model of plyometric exercise proposes that elastic energy in the musculotendinous components is increased with a rapid stretch and then stored.
summary: Plyometric training enhances muscle power and force production, which are crucial for functional movements and athletic performance.

content: The athlete completes one set of each exercise before repeating the circuit. The athlete completes two sets/circuits before performing the final sets of the lunge, vertical chest press, and abdominal crunch exercises in that order.
summary: The workout structure involves completing one set of each exercise per circuit, with two circuits performed before finishing with lunges, chest presses, and abdominal crunches.

content: Plyometric exercise is a quick, powerful movement using a prestretch, or counter-movement, that involves the stretch-shortening cycle (SSC). The purpose of plyometric exercise is to increase the power of subsequent movements by using both the natural elastic components of muscle and tendon and the stretch reflex. When used correctly, plyometric training has consistently been shown to improve the production of muscle force and power.
summary: Plyometric exercise utilizes the stretch-shortening cycle to enhance power output by leveraging muscle elasticity and the stretch reflex.

content: The mechanical model of plyometric exercise proposes that elastic energy in the musculotendinous components is increased with a rapid stretch and then stored. When this movement is immediately followed by a concentric muscle action, the stored elastic energy is released, increasing the total force production. It is likely that both the mechanical and neurophysiological models contribute to the increased production of force seen during plyometric exercise. The degree to which each model contributes remains uncertain. Further research is needed to improve our understanding of both models and their respective roles in plyometric exercise.
summary: Plyometric exercise benefits from both mechanical (elastic energy storage) and neurophysiological (stretch reflex potentiation) mechanisms.

content: The neurophysiological model of plyometric exercise involves potentiation of the concentric muscle action by use of the stretch reflex. The stretch reflex is the body’s involuntary response to an external stimulus that stretches the muscles. This reflexive component of plyometric exercise is primarily composed of muscle spindle activity. Muscle spindles are proprioceptive organs that are sensitive to the rate and magnitude of a stretch; when a quick stretch is detected, muscular activity reflexively increases. During plyometric exercises, the muscle spindles are stimulated by a rapid stretch, causing a reflexive muscle action. This reflexive response potentiates, or increases, the activity in the agonist muscle, thereby increasing the force the muscle produces. If a concentric muscle action does not immediately follow a stretch (i.e., there is too long a time between stretch and concentric action or movement over too large a range), the potentiating ability of the stretch reflex is negated.
summary: Plyometric exercise relies on the stretch reflex, triggered by muscle spindles, to enhance muscle activation and force production.

content: The SSC employs the energy storage capabilities of the SEC and stimulation of the stretch reflex to facilitate a maximal increase in muscle recruitment over a minimal amount of time. The SSC involves three distinct phases as shown in table 16.1. Phase I is the eccentric phase, which involves preloading the agonist muscle group(s). During this phase, the SEC stores elastic energy, and the muscle spindles are stimulated. As the muscle spindles are stretched, they send a signal to the ventral root of the spinal cord via the Type Ia afferent nerve fibers. Phase II is the time between the eccentric and concentric phases and is termed the amortization—or transition— phase. This is the time from the end of the eccentric phase to the initiation of the concentric muscle action. There is a delay between the eccentric and concentric muscle actions during which Type Ia afferent nerves synapse with the alpha motor neurons in the ventral root of the spinal cord. The alpha motor neurons then transmit signals to the agonist muscle group. This phase of the SSC is perhaps the most crucial in allowing greater power production; its duration must be kept short. If the amortization phase lasts too long, the energy stored during the eccentric phase dissipates as heat, and the stretch reflex will not increase muscle activity during the concentric phase. The concentric phase, phase III, is the body’s response to the eccentric and amortization phases. During this phase, the energy stored in the SEC during the eccentric phase either is used to increase the force of the subsequent movement or is dissipated as heat. This stored elastic energy increases the force produced during the concentric phase.
summary: The stretch-shortening cycle (SSC) involves three phases: eccentric (preload), amortization (delay), and concentric (power output), maximizing force production through elastic energy storage and the stretch reflex.

content: Movement beyond that of an isolated concentric muscle action is essential to the proper performance of plyometric exercises. As soon as movement begins in an upward direction, the concentric phase of the SSC has begun and the amortization phase has ended. One of the agonist muscles is the gastrocnemius. Upon touchdown, the gastrocnemius undergoes a rapid stretch (eccentric phase), there is a delay in movement (amortization phase), and then the muscle concentrically plantarflexes the ankle, allowing the athlete to push off the ground (concentric phase). The rate of musculotendinous stretch is vital to plyometric exercise. A high stretch rate results in greater muscle recruitment and activity during the SSC concentric phase. As the rate of stretch increases, an athlete’s absolute performance in these tests improves; the static squat jump results in the lowest jump height, and the approach jump in the highest.
summary: Plyometric exercises require a rapid stretch-shortening cycle, with the rate of stretch influencing performance.

content: One of the agonist muscles is the gastrocnemius. Upon touchdown, the gastrocnemius undergoes a rapid stretch (eccentric phase), there is a delay in movement (amortization phase), and then the muscle concentrically plantarflexes the ankle, allowing the athlete to push off the ground (concentric phase).
summary: Describes the gastrocnemius muscle's role in the stretch-shortening cycle (SSC) during landing and push-off.

content: The rate of musculotendinous stretch is vital to plyometric exercise. A high stretch rate results in greater muscle recruitment and activity during the SSC concentric phase. As the rate of stretch increases, an athlete’s absolute performance in these tests improves; the static squat jump results in the lowest jump height, and the approach jump in the highest.
summary: Explains the importance of stretch rate in plyometric exercise and how it influences jump performance.

content: The static squat jump requires the athlete to get into a squatting position (i.e., 90° hip flexion and 90° knee flexion) followed by a jump up. This jump does not use stored elastic energy and is too slow to allow potentiation from the stretch reflex because there is essentially no eccentric phase.
summary: Describes the static squat jump and its lack of elastic energy storage and stretch reflex potentiation.

content: The countermovement jump uses a rapid eccentric element (i.e., partial squat) followed immediately by rapid concentric muscle activity (i.e., jump up). The rapid eccentric phase allows the athlete to store (and use) elastic energy in the stretched musculotendinous unit and stimulates the stretch reflex, thereby potentiating muscle activity.
summary: Explains the countermovement jump, highlighting its eccentric phase, elastic energy storage, and stretch reflex activation.

content: The approach jump uses an even quicker, more forceful eccentric phase than the countermovement jump; the increased rate of stretch during the eccentric phase allows a further increase in vertical jump height.
summary: Describes the approach jump, emphasizing its even faster eccentric phase and resulting higher jump height.

content: The stretch-shortening cycle combines mechanical and neurophysiological mechanisms and is the basis of plyometric exercise. A rapid eccentric muscle action stimulates the stretch reflex and storage of elastic energy, which increase the force produced during the subsequent concentric action.
summary: Defines the stretch-shortening cycle (SSC) and its role in plyometric exercise, explaining the mechanical and neurophysiological components.

content: Plyometric exercise prescription is similar to resistance and aerobic exercise prescriptions—mode, intensity, frequency, duration, recovery, progression, and a warm-up period must all be included in the design of a sound plyometric training program. There is little research demarcat-ing optimal program variables for the design of plyometric exercise programs. When prescribing plyometric exercise, practitioners must rely on the available research, practical experience, and the methodology used for designing resistance and aerobic training programs.
summary: Discusses the principles of plyometric exercise prescription, emphasizing the need for a structured program and the limited research on optimal variables.

content: The guidelines that follow are largely based on Chu’s work and the National Strength and Conditioning Association’s position statement.
summary: States the basis for the provided plyometric exercise guidelines.

content: The mode of plyometric training is determined by the body region performing the given exercise. Lower body plyometrics are appropriate for virtually any athlete and any sport, including track and field throwing and sprinting, soccer, volleyball, basketball, American football, and baseball. These sports require athletes to produce a maximal amount of muscular force in a short amount of time.
summary: Explains the concept of plyometric mode and highlights the benefits of lower body plyometrics for various sports.

content: American football, baseball, and sprinting generally require horizontal or lateral movement during competition, while volleyball involves primarily horizontal and vertical movements. Soccer and basketball players must make quick, powerful movements and changes of direction in all planes to compete successfully.
summary: Provides examples of sports and their specific movement demands, emphasizing the need for power and agility.

content: A basketball center is an example of an athlete who would benefit greatly from a plyometric training program, as the center is required to jump repeatedly for rebounds. To be successful, the center must be able to outjump the opposing center in order to rebound more loose balls. Lower body plyometric training allows the player the ability to produce more force in a shorter amount of time, thereby allowing a higher jump.
summary: Illustrates the benefits of plyometric training for a basketball center, focusing on improved jumping ability for rebounding.

content: There are a wide variety of lower body plyometric drills with various intensity levels and directional movements. Types of lower body plyometric drills include jumps in place, standing jumps, multiple hops and jumps, bounds, box drills, and depth jumps.
summary: Lists various types of lower body plyometric drills, highlighting their diversity and intensity levels.

content: Rapid, powerful upper body movements are requirements for several sports and activities, including baseball, softball, tennis, golf, and throws in track and field (i.e., the shot put, discus, and javelin. As an example, a baseball pitcher routinely throws a baseball at 80 to 100 miles per hour (129-161 km/hour). To reach velocities of this magnitude, the pitcher’s shoulder joint must move at more than 6,000°/s (19, 22, 23, 45). Plyometric training of the shoulder joint would not only increase pitching velocity, it may also prevent injury to the shoulder and elbow joints, although further research is needed to substantiate the role of plyometrics in injury prevention.
summary: Discusses the importance of upper body power for various sports and activities, using baseball pitching as an example, and suggests potential benefits of plyometric training for shoulder health.

content: Plyometric drills for the upper body are not used as often as those for the lower body and have been studied less extensively, but they are nonetheless essential to athletes who require upper body power. Plyometrics for the upper body include medicine ball throws, catches, and several types of push-ups.
summary: Acknowledges the limited research on upper body plyometrics but emphasizes their importance for athletes requiring upper body power and provides examples of drills.

content: In general, it is difficult to perform true plyometric drills that directly target trunk musculature because all the requisite plyometric elements may not be present. Plyometric exercise uses stored elastic energy (mechanical model) and potentiates muscle activity through stimulation of the stretch reflex (neurophysiological model). Following the eccentric phase of the SSC, there is likely some storage of elastic energy during “plyometric” trunk drills.
summary: Explains the challenges of applying plyometric principles to trunk training due to the lack of essential elements and discusses the potential for elastic energy storage during trunk drills.

content: Research supports the notion that the stretch reflex is not sufficiently involved during many trunk exercises to potentiate muscle activity. Stretch reflex latencies (time from reflex stimulation to the beginning of agonist muscle activity) largely depend on nerve conduction velocities and therefore increase with greater distances from the spinal cord (i.e., longer nerves). Quadriceps and gastrocnemius stretch reflexes typically range from 20 to 30 ms and from 30 to 45 ms, respectively. Although no research has addressed abdominal stretch reflexes, it may be assumed that they are likely to be longer than those of the quadriceps and gastrocnemius.
summary: Discusses the role of the stretch reflex in trunk exercises and its potential limitations, comparing stretch reflex latencies in different muscle groups.

content: Muscles closer to the spinal cord have shorter latencies.
summary: States the relationship between muscle location and stretch reflex latency.

content: Research supports the notion that the stretch reflex is not sufficiently involved during many trunk exercises to potentiate muscle activity. Trunk exercises can be performed plyometrically with modifications. Plyometric trunk exercises require shorter and quicker movements to allow for stimulation and use of the stretch reflex. The medicine ball sit-up depicted in Figure 16.4 has a large range of motion and time needed to complete the movement, which prevents reflexive potentiation of the abdominal muscles. The medicine ball sit-up can be modified to decrease both the range of motion and time, allowing for potentiation of the agonist muscles and making it more like a plyometric exercise (Figure 16.5).
summary: Trunk exercises can be modified to utilize the stretch reflex for potentiation.

content: Stretch reflex latencies (time from reflex stimulation to the beginning of agonist muscle activity) largely depend on nerve conduction velocities and therefore increase with greater distances from the spinal cord (i.e., longer nerves). Muscles closer to the spinal cord have shorter latencies. Quadriceps and gastrocnemius stretch reflexes typically range from 20 to 30 ms and from 30 to 45 ms, respectively. Although no research has addressed abdominal stretch reflexes, it may be assumed that they are likely to be longer than those of the quadriceps and gastrocnemius.
summary: Stretch reflex latency depends on nerve conduction velocity and distance from the spinal cord.

content: Plyometric intensity refers to the amount of stress placed on involved muscles, connective tissues, and joints. The type of plyometric drill primarily controls intensity. Skipping is relatively low in intensity, while depth jumps place high stress on muscles and joints. Factors affecting plyometric intensity include points of contact, speed, height of the drill, body weight, and external weight.
summary: Plyometric intensity is determined by drill type and factors like speed and weight.

content: As intensity increases, volume should decrease. Plyometric training frequency ranges from one to three sessions per week. Strength and conditioning professionals often rely on practical experience when determining plyometric frequency. A typical recovery time guideline for prescribing plyometrics is 48 to 72 hours between sessions. Athletes commonly perform two to four plyometric sessions per week. Plyometric frequency may vary depending on the sport, intensity and volume of daily workouts, and time of the training cycle. During the season, one plyometric session per week is appropriate for American football players, while two or three sessions per week are common for track and field athletes. During the off-season, plyometric training frequency may increase to two or three sessions per week for American football players and to three to four sessions per week for track and field athletes.
summary: Plyometric frequency and volume are adjusted based on intensity, sport, and training cycle.

content: Plyometric drills involve maximal efforts to improve anaerobic power, requiring complete and adequate recovery. Recovery for depth jumps may consist of 5 to 10 seconds of rest between repetitions and 2 to 3 minutes between sets. The time between sets is determined by a proper work-to-rest ratio (i.e., 1:5 to 1:10) and is specific to the volume and type of drill being performed. Drills should not be thought of as cardiorespiratory conditioning exercises but as power training. Recovery between workouts must be adequate to prevent overtraining (two to four days of recovery, depending on the sport and time of year). Drills for a given body area should not be performed two days in succession.
summary: Plyometric training requires adequate recovery to prevent overtraining and optimize power development.

content: Plyometric volume is typically expressed as the number of repetitions and sets performed during a given training session. Lower body plyometric volume is normally given as the number of foot contacts per workout but may also be expressed as distance. An athlete beginning a plyometric training program may start with a single-leg bound for 98 feet (30 m) per repetition but may progress. Recommended lower body plyometric volumes vary for athletes of different levels of experience. Suggested lower body plyometric volumes are provided in table 16.4. Upper body plyometric volume is typically expressed as the number of throws or catches per workout.
summary: Plyometric volume is expressed as repetitions, sets, foot contacts, or distance and varies based on experience level.

content: Research has yet to determine an optimal plyometric training program length. Most plyometric programs range from 6 to 10 weeks. Vertical jump height improves as quickly as four weeks after the start of a plyometric training program. Plyometric training should be prescribed similarly to both resistance and aerobic training. For those sports requiring quick, powerful movements, it is beneficial to perform plyometric exercise throughout the training cycle (macrocycle). The intensity and volume of the chosen drills should vary with the sport and the season (i.e., off-season, preseason, or in-season). Plyometrics is a form of resistance training and thus must follow the principles of progressive overload.
summary: Plyometric training programs typically last 6-10 weeks and should be tailored to the sport and training cycle.

content: Plyometric training should be prescribed similarly to both resistance and aerobic training. Plyometrics is a form of resistance training and thus must follow the principles of progressive overload. Progressive overload is the systematic increase in training frequency, volume, and intensity in various combinations. Typically, as intensity increases, volume decreases.
summary: Plyometric training should follow the principles of progressive overload, similar to resistance and aerobic training, with volume adjustments based on intensity.

content: For those sports requiring quick, powerful movements, it is beneficial to perform plyometric exercise throughout the training cycle (macrocycle). The intensity and volume of the chosen drills should vary with the sport and the season (i.e., off-season, preseason, or in-season). An off-season plyometric program for American football, for example, may be performed twice a week. The program’s intensity should progress from low to moderate volumes of low-intensity plyometrics, to low to moderate volumes of moderate intensity, to low to moderate volumes of moderate to high intensity.
summary: Plyometric training should be integrated throughout the training cycle, with intensity and volume adjusted based on the sport and training phase.

content: Vertical jump height improves as quickly as four weeks after the start of a plyometric training program.  As in any training program, the plyometric exercise session must begin with a general warm-up, stretching, and a specific warm-up. The specific warm-up for plyometric training should consist of low-intensity, dynamic movements.
summary: Plyometric training can lead to rapid improvements in vertical jump height, and sessions should begin with a proper warm-up.

content: It is becoming more common for younger and older individuals to want to augment the training programs for their sport with plyometric exercise. When these exercises are applied appropriately, these populations can experience the same positive outcomes with minimal risk of injury. Although plyometrics have commonly been viewed as appropriate only for conditioning elite adult athletes, prepubescent and adolescent children may also benefit from training with plyometric and plyometric-like exercises. Besides providing the well-documented muscular power and bone strength adaptations, regular participation in an appropriately designed plyometric training program can better prepare young athletes for the demands of sport practice and competition (17) by enhancing neuromuscular control and performance.
summary: Plyometric training can be beneficial for a wide range of ages, including children and older adults, when implemented safely and appropriately.

content: Research has yet to determine a universal age at which people are physically able to begin participating in a plyometric training program. An analysis of the body’s development provides some insight into the issue. Because the epiphyseal plates of the bones of prepubescent children have yet to close (33, 40), depth jumps and other high-intensity lower body drills are contraindicated (2, 32, 39). While the growth plates are open, highly intense activity and injury may cause them to close prematurely, resulting in limb length discrepancies (32). Further, and as with all forms of exercise, boys and girls should have the emotional maturity to accept and follow directions and should be able to appreciate the benefits and concerns associated with this method of training. Empirically, 7- and 8-year-olds have been trained in progressive plyometric programs, and they continue to lead active lives as teenagers and adults (17).
summary: The optimal age to begin plyometric training is not universally established, with considerations for growth plate development and emotional maturity.

content: Plyometric exercise programs for children should be used to develop the neuromuscular control and the anaerobic skills that will carry over to participation in sport and athletics, both during childhood and as they advance to higher levels of competition. For these reasons, it is extremely important to gradually progress from relatively simple to more complex drills. It is important to focus on the quality of the movements (e.g., proper body alignment and speed of movement) to develop techniques that will be essential for more advanced exercises.
summary: Plyometric programs for children should focus on developing neuromuscular control and anaerobic skills, progressing gradually from simpler to more complex drills.

content: As with adults, recovery between workouts must be adequate to prevent overtraining. While the optimal amount of recovery needed between plyometric workouts is unknown, it should vary based on the intensity of the training program and the athlete’s skills, abilities, and tolerances as well as on the time of year (i.e., off-season, preseason, or in-season). Therefore, a minimum of two to three days between plyometric workouts should be considered essential to optimize adaptations to the training program and minimize the athlete’s risk of injury (17).
summary: Adequate recovery between plyometric workouts is crucial, with the duration varying based on factors like intensity, athlete abilities, and training phase.

content: Under proper supervision and with an appropriate program, prepubescent and adolescent children may perform plyometric exercises. Depth jumps and high-intensity lower body plyometrics are contraindicated for this population.
summary: Children can participate in plyometric training, but high-intensity exercises like depth jumps should be avoided.

content: Masters athletes are finding that they can maintain their physical capabilities late into life and are looking for additional training insights. When designing a plyometric training program for a masters athlete, the strength and conditioning professional needs to be specific in deciding on the goal or goals of the program. Some primary issues to consider are any preexisting orthopedic conditions (such as osteoarthritis or any sort of surgical joint intervention) or joint degeneration. These call for even greater caution and a more careful use of plyometric exercise.
summary: Plyometric training can be beneficial for older adults, but programs should be tailored to individual needs and considerations, especially for those with pre-existing conditions.

content: For example, a healthy masters athlete without surgical history who wants to improve his or her running performance should use depth jumps and single-leg exercises cautiously, so alternate-leg bounding and the double-leg hop would be better choices. Similarly, a masters runner with a history of knee surgery such as partial meniscus removal, or with significant joint degeneration, should consider depth jumps and single-leg plyometric exercises contraindicated and should use other forms of plyometrics cautiously.
summary: Plyometric exercise selection for older adults should be individualized based on their health history and goals, with modifications for potential joint issues.

content: After consideration of the predispositions of the masters athlete’s physical condition, a plyometric program should be designed according to the same guidelines as outlined for adult athletes, with the following changes. The plyometric program should include no more than five low- to moderate-intensity exercises; the volume should be lower, that is, should include fewer total foot contacts than a standard plyometric training program; and the recovery time between plyometric workouts should be three to
summary: Plyometric programs for older adults should be modified to include fewer exercises, lower volume, and longer recovery periods.

content: Plyometric exercise is only one part of an athlete’s overall training program. Many sports and activities use multiple energy systems or require other forms of exercise to properly prepare athletes for competition. Each energy system and sport-specific need must be included in a well-designed training program.
summary: Plyometric training should be integrated into a comprehensive training program that addresses various energy systems and sport-specific demands.

content: Plyometric exercise is only one part of an athlete’s overall training program. Many sports and activities use multiple energy systems or require other forms of exercise to properly prepare athletes for competition. Each energy system and sport-specific need must be included in a well-designed training program.
summary: Plyometrics are just one component of a comprehensive training program, which should also address various energy systems and sport-specific demands.

content: A combination of plyometrics and resistance training during a training cycle should be structured to allow maximal efficiency and physical improvement. Combine lower body resistance training with upper body plyometrics, and upper body resistance training with lower body plyometrics. Performing heavy resistance training and plyometrics on the same day is not usually recommended.
summary: Plyometrics and resistance training should be strategically combined, alternating upper and lower body focus, and avoiding training on the same day.

content: Some athletes may benefit from complex training, a combination of high-intensity resistance training followed by plyometrics. If athletes perform this type of training, adequate recovery is needed between plyometrics and other high-intensity training.
summary: Complex training, involving high-intensity resistance followed by plyometrics, may be beneficial for some athletes, but requires sufficient recovery.

content: Traditional resistance training exercises may be combined with plyometric movements to further enhance gains in muscular power. For example, performing a squat jump with approximately 30% of one’s squat 1-repetition maximum (1RM) as an external resistance further increases performance. This is an advanced form of complex training that is appropriate only for athletes who have previously participated in high-intensity plyometric training programs.
summary: Combining resistance exercises with plyometric movements can amplify power gains, but this advanced technique is suitable only for experienced athletes.

content: Many sports—such as basketball and soccer—have both an anaerobic (i.e., power) and an aerobic component. Therefore, multiple types of training must be combined to best prepare athletes for these types of sports. Because aerobic exercise may have a negative effect on power production, it is advisable to perform plyometric exercise before aerobic endurance training.
summary: Sports requiring both anaerobic and aerobic capacity necessitate a combination of training types, prioritizing plyometrics before aerobic exercise.

content: Plyometric exercise is not inherently dangerous; however, as with all modes of exercise, the risk of injury exists. Injuries can occur simply due to an accident, but they more typically occur when proper training procedures are violated and may be the result of an insufficient strength and conditioning base, inadequate warm-up, improper progression of lead-up drills, inappropriate volume or intensity for the phase of training, poor shoes or surface, or a simple lack of skill.
summary: While plyometrics are generally safe, injuries can occur due to improper technique, insufficient preparation, or other training errors.

content: To reduce the risk of injury and facilitate the performance of plyometric exercises, the athlete must understand proper plyometric technique and possess a sufficient base of strength, speed, and balance. In addition, the athlete must be sufficiently mature, both physically and psychologically, to participate in a plyometric training program.
summary: Successful plyometric training requires proper technique, adequate strength, speed, balance, and psychological maturity.

content: Before adding any drill to an athlete’s plyometric program, the strength and conditioning professional must demonstrate proper technique to the athlete in order to maximize the drill’s effectiveness and minimize the risk of injury. For lower body plyometrics, proper landing technique is essential, particularly for depth jumps. If the center of gravity is offset from the base of support during a landing, performance is hindered and injury may occur. The shoulders should be over the knees during the landing, which the jumper accomplishes through flexion of the ankles, knees, and hips.
summary: Proper technique instruction is crucial for plyometric drills, especially landing mechanics, to ensure effectiveness and minimize injury risk.

content: Consideration of the athlete’s level of strength is necessary before he or she performs plyometrics. For lower body plyometrics, the athlete’s 1RM squat should be at least 1.5 times his or her body weight. For upper body plyometrics, the bench press 1RM should be at least the athlete’s body weight for larger athletes (those weighing over 220 pounds, or 100 kg) and at least 1.5 times the body weight for smaller athletes (those weighing less than 220 pounds). An alternative measure of prerequisite upper body strength is the ability to perform five clap push-ups in a row. These should be considered minimal standards for performing high-intensity plyometrics.
summary: Minimum strength requirements, based on 1RM or clap push-ups, are essential before engaging in high-intensity plyometrics.

content: For athletes who do not possess sufficient muscular strength or a sufficient fitness level, plyometrics should be delayed until they meet minimum standards. Because plyometric exercise involves quick movements, the ability to move rapidly is essential before one begins a plyometric program. For lower body plyometrics, the athlete should be able to perform five repetitions of the squat with 60% body weight in 5 seconds or less. To meet this speed prerequisite, participation in sprint training and speed-specific resistance training programs is helpful.
summary: Plyometrics should be delayed until athletes meet minimum strength and speed standards, which can be developed through specific training.

content: To satisfy the speed requirement for upper body plyometrics, the athlete should be able to perform five repetitions of the bench press with 60% body weight in 5 seconds or less. A less obvious lower body plyometric requirement is balance. Balance is the maintenance of a position without moving for a given period of time. Many lower body plyometric drills require the athlete to move in nontraditional patterns (e.g., double-leg zigzag hop and backward skip) or on a single leg (e.g., single-leg tuck jump and single-leg hop). These types of drills necessitate a solid, stable base of support upon which the athlete can safely and correctly perform the exercises.
summary: Upper body plyometrics require similar speed benchmarks as lower body, and balance is crucial for executing various plyometric drills.

content: Three balance tests are provided in table 16.7, listed in order of difficulty; each test position must be held for 30 seconds. For example, an athlete beginning plyometric training for the first time would be required to stand on one leg for 30 seconds without falling.
summary: Balance assessments, increasing in difficulty, are used to gauge an athlete's readiness for plyometric training.

content: Balance is the maintenance of a position without moving for a given period of time. Many lower body plyometric drills require the athlete to move in nontraditional patterns (e.g., double-leg zigzag hop and backward skip) or on a single leg (e.g., single-leg tuck jump and single-leg hop). These types of drills necessitate a solid, stable base of support upon which the athlete can safely and correctly perform the exercises.
summary: Balance is crucial for plyometric drills, which often involve complex movements and require a stable base of support.

content: Three balance tests are provided in table 16.7, listed in order of difficulty; each test position must be held for 30 seconds. For example, an athlete beginning plyometric training for the first time would be required to stand on one leg for 30 seconds without falling. An experienced athlete beginning an advanced plyometric training program must maintain a single-leg half squat for 30 seconds without falling.
summary: Balance is assessed using three tests of increasing difficulty, with the goal of maintaining a stable position for 30 seconds.

content: The surface on which the balance testing is performed must be the same as that used in the plyometric drills. Plyometric training utilizes shock-absorbing surfaces. Grass fields, suspended floors, or rubber mats are good surfaces for plyometric training (32). Concrete, tile, and hardwood are not recommended surfaces for plyometric training because they lack effective shock-absorbing properties (32).
summary: Plyometric training requires shock-absorbing surfaces, such as grass, suspended floors, or rubber mats, to minimize impact stress.

content: Excessively thick exercise mats (6 inches [15 cm] or thicker) may extend the amortization phase and thus not allow efficient use of the stretch reflex. Mini-trampolines are commonly used for beginning plyometric and balance training in rehabilitation (28). Mini-trampolines, like thick exercise mats, are not effective for plyometric training of uninjured athletes because the amortization phase is extended while the athlete is in contact with the elastic surface.
summary: The landing surface should not be too thick or elastic, as it can interfere with the stretch reflex and the effectiveness of plyometric exercises.

content: The amount of space needed for plyometric training depends on the drill. Most bounding and running drills require at least 30 m (33 yards) of straightaway, though some drills may require a straightaway of 100 m (109 yards). Most standing, box, and depth jumps only require a minimal surface area, but the ceiling height must be 3 to 4 m (9.8-13.1 feet) in order to be adequate.
summary: The required space for plyometric training varies depending on the specific drill, with some drills needing a long straightaway and others requiring adequate ceiling height.

content: Boxes used for box jumps and depth jumps must be sturdy and have a nonslip top. Boxes should range in height from 6 to 42 inches (15 to 107 cm) and should have landing surfaces of at least 18 by 24 inches (46 by 61 cm) (16). Boxes should be constructed of sturdy wood (e.g., 3/4-inch [1.9 cm] plywood) or heavy-gauge metal. Ways to make the landing surface nonslip include adding nonslip treads, mixing sand into the paint used to cover the box, or affixing rubberized flooring to the top (16).
summary: Boxes used in plyometric exercises should be sturdy, non-slip, and appropriately sized, with a range of heights from 6 to 42 inches.

content: Participants must use footwear with good ankle and foot support; good lateral stability; and a wide, nonslip sole (43). Shoes with a narrow sole and poor upper support (e.g., running shoes) may invite ankle problems, especially with lateral movements. Shoes with insufficient foot support may lead to arch and/or lower leg injuries, while footwear without enough cushioning might lead to damage of more proximal joints (e.g., knee and hip joints).
summary: Appropriate footwear is essential for plyometric training, providing ankle support, lateral stability, and a non-slip sole to prevent injuries.

content: Athletes who weigh more than 220 pounds (100 kg) may be at an increased risk for injury when performing plyometric exercises (43, 51). Greater weight increases the compressive force on joints during plyometric exercises, thereby predisposing these joints to injury. Athletes weighing over 220 pounds (100 kg) should avoid high-volume, high-intensity plyometric exercises. Athletes weighing over 220 pounds should not perform depth jumps from heights greater than 18 inches (46 cm) (43, 51).
summary: Athletes weighing over 220 pounds should exercise caution during plyometric training, avoiding high-volume, high-intensity exercises and limiting depth jump heights.

content: An athlete’s joint structure and previous injuries must be examined before he or she begins a plyometric training program. Previous injuries or abnormalities of the spine, lower extremities, or upper extremities may increase an athlete’s risk of injury during plyometric exercise. Athletes with a history of muscle strains, pathological joint laxity, or spinal dysfunction—including vertebral disk dysfunction or compression—should use caution when beginning a plyometric training program (24, 25, 32, 47).
summary: Pre-existing injuries or joint abnormalities can increase the risk of plyometric exercise injuries, requiring careful consideration and potential modifications to the training program.

content: The landing surface used for lower body plyometrics must possess adequate shock-absorbing properties to prevent injuries. Close monitoring of athletes is necessary to ensure proper technique during plyometric exercise. Plyometric exercise is not intrinsically dangerous when performed correctly; but as with other forms of training, poor technique may unnecessarily predispose an athlete to injury.
summary: Proper landing surfaces and close monitoring are crucial for plyometric safety, as incorrect technique can increase the risk of injury.

content: There is a limit to the maximal height at which a depth jump can be effectively and safely performed. A height of 48 inches (1.2 m) would provide a significant overload on the muscles, but the resistance may be too great for many athletes to overcome while maintaining correct technique (40). Jumping from a height of 48 inches (1.2 m) increases the possibility of injury; furthermore, the amount of force to be overcome is so great that the amortization phase is extended and thus the purpose of the exercise defeated. The recommended height for depth jumps ranges from 16 to 42 inches (41 to 107 cm), with 30 to 32 inches (76 to 81 cm) being the norm (4, 18, 26, 37, 38, 41). Depth jump box height for athletes who weigh over 220 pounds (100 kg) should be 18 inches (46 cm) or less.
summary: Depth jump height should be carefully considered, with a recommended range of 16 to 42 inches, and adjustments made based on athlete weight and ability.

content: The major goal of plyometric training is to rapidly apply force to provide an overload to the agonist muscles. Plyometric exercise has been repeatedly shown to increase muscular power for participants in a formal training program (30, 46, 53, 54).
summary: Plyometric training aims to overload muscles with rapid force application, resulting in increased muscular power.

content: Jumping from a height of 48 inches (1.2 m) increases the possibility of injury; furthermore, the amount of force to be overcome is so great that the amortization phase is extended and thus the purpose of the exercise defeated. The recommended height for depth jumps ranges from 16 to 42 inches (41 to 107 cm), with 30 to 32 inches (76 to 81 cm) being the norm (4, 18, 26, 37, 38, 41). Depth jump box height for athletes who weigh over 220 pounds (100 kg) should be 18 inches (46 cm) or less.
summary: Optimal depth jump height depends on athlete weight and should be within a specific range to avoid injury and maximize training benefits.

content: The major goal of plyometric training is to rapidly apply force to provide an overload to the agonist muscles. Plyometric exercise has been repeatedly shown to increase muscular power for participants in a formal training program (30, 46, 53, 54). Research has yet to determine whether mechanical or neurophysiological adaptations account for the improvement in muscular power from plyometric exercise.
summary: Plyometric training aims to overload muscles with rapid force application, proven to increase power, but the underlying mechanisms are still being researched.

content: Plyometrics should be considered not an end in itself, but part of an overall program that includes strength, speed, aerobic and flexibility training, and proper nutrition. After the athlete has begun a proper strength and conditioning program, plyometric training may be used to further develop power. The steps for implementing a plyometric program are: 1. Evaluate the athlete. 2. Ensure that facilities and equipment are safe. 3. Establish sport-specific goals. 4. Determine program design variables. 5. Teach the athlete proper technique. 6. Properly progress the program.
summary: Plyometric training should be integrated into a comprehensive program, implemented with careful planning and progression.

content: Plyometric Training is on page 427.
summary: Information about plyometric training can be found on page 427.

content: Two-Foot Ankle Hop is a lower body plyometric drill. The intensity level of the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is low. The direction of the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is vertical. The starting position for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is none or double arm. The preparatory movement for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is a slight countermovement. The upward movement for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is hopping up with primary motion at the ankle joint. The downward movement for the Two-Foot Ankle Hop is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the hop. The Two-Foot Ankle Hop should be performed with little horizontal or lateral movement.
summary: Description of the Two-Foot Ankle Hop plyometric drill, including its intensity, direction, starting position, arm action, and movement pattern.

content: The Squat Jump is a lower body plyometric drill. The intensity level of the Squat Jump is low. The direction of the Squat Jump is vertical. The starting position for the Squat Jump is a squat position (thighs slightly above parallel with the ground) with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the Squat Jump is none. The preparatory movement for the Squat Jump is none. The upward movement for the Squat Jump is explosively jumping up to a maximum height. The downward movement for the Squat Jump is landing in the squat position and immediately repeating the jump.
summary: Description of the Squat Jump plyometric drill, including its intensity, direction, starting position, arm action, and movement pattern.

content: The Jump and Reach is a lower body plyometric drill. The intensity level of the Jump and Reach is low. The direction of the Jump and Reach is vertical. The starting position for the Jump and Reach is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the Jump and Reach is double arm with reach at the top of the jump. The preparatory movement for the Jump and Reach is a countermovement. The upward movement for the Jump and Reach is explosively jumping up and reaching for an object or target. The downward movement for the Jump and Reach is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the jump. Emphasis is on vertical height with minimal delay between jumps. The Jump and Reach should be performed with little horizontal or lateral movement.
summary: Description of the Jump and Reach plyometric drill, including its intensity, direction, starting position, arm action, and movement pattern.

content: The Double-Leg Tuck Jump is a lower body plyometric drill. The intensity level of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is medium. The direction of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is vertical.
summary: Description of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump plyometric drill, including its intensity and direction.

content: The Double-Leg Tuck Jump is a lower body plyometric drill. The intensity level of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is medium. The direction of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is vertical. The starting position for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is a countermovement. The upward movement for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is explosively jumping up. Pulling the knees to the chest, quickly grasping the knees with both hands, and releasing before landing. The downward movement for the Double-Leg Tuck Jump is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the jump.
summary: Description of the Double-Leg Tuck Jump, a medium-intensity, vertical plyometric drill.

content: The Jump and Reach should be performed with little horizontal or lateral movement. Emphasis is on vertical height with minimal delay between jumps.
summary: Instructions for performing the Jump and Reach, emphasizing vertical height and minimal lateral movement.

content: SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a jump exercise with a medium intensity level. The direction of the jump in SPlIT Squ AT JumP is vertical. The starting position for SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a lunge position with one leg forward (hip and knee joints flexed approximately 90°) and the other behind the midline of the body. Arm action for SPlIT Squ AT JumP is double arm or none. The preparatory movement for SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a countermovement. During the upward movement of SPlIT Squ AT JumP, explosively jump up, using the arms to assist as needed. Maximum height and power should be emphasized. During the downward movement of SPlIT Squ AT JumP, maintain the lunge position (same leg forward) and immediately repeat the jump. After completing a set of SPlIT Squ AT JumP, rest and switch front legs.
summary: Description of the SPlIT Squ AT JumP, a medium-intensity, vertical jump exercise.

content: c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a jump exercise with a high intensity level. The direction of the jump in c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is vertical. The starting position for c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a lunge position with one leg forward (hip and knee joints flexed approximately 90°) and the other behind the midline of the body. Arm action for c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is double arm or none. The preparatory movement for c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP is a countermovement. During the upward movement of c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP, explosively jump up, using the arms to assist as needed. While off the ground, switch the position of the legs. Maximum height and power should be emphasized. During the downward movement of c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP, maintain the lunge position (opposite leg forward) and immediately repeat the jump. Be sure the lunge is not too deep (as in the far right photo) in c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP, as the SSC may not be able to effectively contribute to subsequent jumps.
summary: Description of the c ycle D SPlIT Squ AT JumP, a high-intensity, vertical jump exercise involving leg switching mid-air.

content: SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is a jump exercise with a high intensity level. The direction of the jump in SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is vertical. The starting position for SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is a comfortable, upright stance on one foot. The nonjumping leg is held in a stationary position with the knee flexed during the exercise. Arm action for SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is double arm. The preparatory movement for SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P is a countermovement. During the upward movement of SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P, explosively jump up. Pull the knee of the jumping leg to the chest, grasp the knee with both hands, and release before landing. During the downward movement of SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P, land in the starting position and immediately repeat the jump using the same leg. Repeat with the opposite leg after a brief rest in SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P.
summary: Description of the SIngle-leg Tuck Jum P, a high-intensity, single-leg, vertical jump exercise.

content: PIke Jum P is a jump exercise with a high intensity level. The direction of the jump in PIke Jum P is vertical. The starting position for PIke Jum P is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Arm action for PIke Jum P is double arm. The preparatory movement for PIke Jum P is a countermovement. During the upward movement of PIke Jum P, explosively jump up. Keeping the legs straight and together, try to lift them to the front and try to touch the toes with the hands.
summary: Description of the PIke Jum P, a high-intensity, vertical jump exercise involving bringing the legs up towards the chest.

content: The direction of the jump in Pike Jump is vertical. The starting position for Pike Jump is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Arm action for Pike Jump is double arm. The preparatory movement for Pike Jump is a countermovement. During the upward movement of Pike Jump, explosively jump up. Keeping the legs straight and together, try to lift them to the front and try to touch the toes with the hands. During the downward movement of Pike Jump, land in the starting position and immediately repeat the jump.
summary: Pike Jump is a vertical jump with a countermovement, focusing on extending the legs upward and touching the toes.

content: Double-leg Vertical Jump is a jump exercise with a low intensity level. The direction of the jump in Double-leg Vertical Jump is vertical. The starting position for Double-leg Vertical Jump is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Arm action for Double-leg Vertical Jump is double arm. The preparatory movement for Double-leg Vertical Jump is a countermovement. During the upward movement of Double-leg Vertical Jump, explosively jump up, using both arms to assist, and reach for a target. During the downward movement of Double-leg Vertical Jump, land in the starting position and repeat the jump. Allow recovery time between jumps.
summary: Double-leg Vertical Jump is a low-intensity jump with a countermovement, using both arms for assistance.

content: Jump Over Barrier is a jump exercise with a medium intensity level. The direction of the jump in Jump Over Barrier is horizontal and vertical. Equipment for Jump Over Barrier is a barrier such as a cone or hurdle. The starting position for Jump Over Barrier is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. Arm action for Jump Over Barrier is double arm. The preparatory movement for Jump Over Barrier is a countermovement. During the upward movement of Jump Over Barrier, jump over a barrier with both legs, using primarily hip and knee flexion to clear the barrier. Keep the knees and feet together without lateral deviation. During the downward movement of Jump Over Barrier, land in the starting position and repeat the jump. Allow recovery time between jumps. The height of the barrier should be progressively increased (e.g., from a cone to a hurdle) in Jump Over Barrier.
summary: Jump Over Barrier is a medium-intensity jump exercise that involves clearing a progressively increasing barrier.

content: Single-leg Vertical Jump has a high intensity level. The direction of the Single-leg Vertical Jump is vertical. The starting position for the Single-leg Vertical Jump is a comfortable, upright stance on one foot. The nonjumping leg in the Single-leg Vertical Jump is held stationary with the knee flexed. The arm action for the Single-leg Vertical Jump is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Single-leg Vertical Jump is a countermovement. The upward movement of the Single-leg Vertical Jump involves explosively jumping up using both arms to assist and reaching for a target. The downward movement of the Single-leg Vertical Jump is landing in the starting position and repeating the jump using the same leg. Recovery time is allowed between jumps in the Single-leg Vertical Jump. The opposite leg is used after a brief rest in the Single-leg Vertical Jump.
summary: Single-leg Vertical Jump is a high-intensity jump performed on one leg, alternating legs after rest.

content: Double-leg Hop has a medium intensity level. The direction of the Double-leg Hop is horizontal and vertical. The starting position for the Double-leg Hop is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the Double-leg Hop is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Double-leg Hop is a countermovement. The upward movement of the Double-leg Hop involves jumping as far forward as possible. The downward movement of the Double-leg Hop is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the hop.
summary: Double-leg Hop is a medium-intensity jump with a countermovement, focusing on horizontal distance.

content: Double-leg Zigzag Hop has a high intensity level.
summary: Double-leg Zigzag Hop is a high-intensity jump involving a zigzag pattern.

content: The arm action for the Double-leg Hop is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Double-leg Hop is a countermovement. The upward movement of the Double-leg Hop involves jumping as far forward as possible. The downward movement of the Double-leg Hop is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the hop.
summary: The Double-leg Hop involves a double arm action, a countermovement, jumping forward, and immediately repeating the hop upon landing.

content: Double-leg Zigzag Hop has a high intensity level. The direction of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is diagonal. Equipment for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop includes about 10 hurdles placed 18 to 24 inches (45-60 cm) apart in a zigzag pattern. The starting position for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart, standing on the outside of the first hurdle. Elbows should be flexed at 90° and held at the sides of the body in the Double-leg Zigzag Hop. The arm action for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is a countermovement. The upward movement of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop involves jumping from the outside of the first hurdle to the outside of the second hurdle, keeping the shoulders perpendicular to an imaginary line through the center of all hurdles. The downward movement of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop is immediately upon landing on the outside of the second hurdle, changing direction and jumping diagonally over the second hurdle to the outside of the third hurdle. The Double-leg Zigzag Hop involves continuing to hop over all the hurdles. A less intense version of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop involves setting the hurdles in a straight line and hopping over one hurdle at a time. The intensity of the Double-leg Zigzag Hop can be increased by performing the hops with one leg only.
summary: The Double-leg Zigzag Hop is a high-intensity exercise involving hopping diagonally over a series of hurdles. It can be modified for different intensity levels and performed with one leg.

content: Single-leg Hop has a high intensity level. The direction of the Single-leg Hop is horizontal and vertical. The starting position for the Single-leg Hop is a comfortable, upright stance on one foot. The nonjumping leg in the Single-leg Hop is held stationary with the knee flexed. The arm action for the Single-leg Hop is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Single-leg Hop is a countermovement. The upward movement of the Single-leg Hop involves explosively jumping forward using both arms to assist.
summary: The Single-leg Hop is a high-intensity exercise involving jumping forward on one leg, using both arms for assistance.

content: Front Barrier Hop has a medium intensity level. The direction of the Front Barrier Hop is horizontal and vertical. Equipment for the Front Barrier Hop includes two barriers such as two cones or two hurdles. The starting position for the Front Barrier Hop is facing the first barrier, getting into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the Front Barrier Hop is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Front Barrier Hop is a countermovement. The upward movement of the Front Barrier Hop involves jumping over the first barrier with both legs, using primarily hip and knee flexion to clear the barrier. The knees and feet should be kept together without lateral deviation during the Front Barrier Hop. The downward movement of the Front Barrier Hop is landing in the starting position and immediately repeating the jump over a second barrier. The intensity level of the Front Barrier Hop can be increased from medium to high by progressively increasing the height of the barrier (e.g., from a cone to a hurdle) or by performing the hops with one leg only.
summary: The Front Barrier Hop is a medium-intensity exercise involving jumping over two barriers, with the intensity adjustable by barrier height or single-leg variations.

content: The lateral barrier hop has a medium intensity level. The direction of the lateral barrier hop is lateral and vertical. Equipment for the lateral barrier hop includes a barrier such as a cone or hurdle. The starting position for the lateral barrier hop is with the barrier to one side, in a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the lateral barrier hop is double arm. The preparatory movement for the lateral barrier hop is a countermovement. The upward movement of the lateral barrier hop involves jumping over the barrier with both legs, using primarily hip and knee flexion to clear the barrier.
summary: The Lateral Barrier Hop is a medium-intensity exercise involving jumping laterally over a barrier.

content: Equipment for the lateral barrier hop includes a barrier such as a cone or hurdle. The starting position for the lateral barrier hop is with the barrier to one side, in a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the lateral barrier hop is double arm. The preparatory movement for the lateral barrier hop is a countermovement. During the lateral barrier hop, the knees and feet should be kept together. The downward movement of the lateral barrier hop involves landing on the opposite side of the barrier and immediately repeating the jump to the starting side. The intensity level of the lateral barrier hop can be increased from medium to high by progressively increasing the height of the barrier (e.g., from a cone to a hurdle) or by performing the hops with one leg only.
summary: The lateral barrier hop is a plyometric exercise involving jumping over a barrier, with variations in intensity and leg usage.

content: The skip has a low intensity level. The direction of the skip is horizontal and vertical. The starting position for the skip is with one leg lifted to approximately 90° of hip and knee flexion. The arm action for the skip is reciprocal (as one leg is lifted, the opposite arm is lifted). The preparatory movement for the skip is a countermovement on one leg. The upward movement of the skip involves jumping up and forward on one leg. The opposite leg should remain in the starting flexed position until landing. The downward movement of the skip involves landing in the starting position on the same leg. Immediately repeat the skip with the opposite leg.
summary: The skip is a low-intensity, alternating leg movement with a horizontal and vertical direction.

content: The power skip has a low intensity level. The direction of the power skip is vertical and horizontal. The starting position for the power skip is with one leg lifted to approximately 90° of hip and knee flexion. The arm action for the power skip is double arm. The preparatory movement for the power skip is a countermovement on one leg. Emphasis is on the effectiveness of the skip for the power skip. The upward movement of the power skip involves jumping up and forward on one leg. Move the flexed, nonjumping leg up and into greater hip and knee flexion while jumping. Both arms should be used to assist with the upward movement. The downward movement of the power skip involves landing in the starting position on the same leg. Immediately repeat the skip with the opposite leg.
summary: The power skip is a low-intensity, explosive movement with a vertical and horizontal direction, emphasizing leg drive and arm assistance.

content: The backward skip has a low intensity level. The direction of the backward skip is backward, horizontal, and vertical. The starting position for the backward skip is with one leg lifted to approximately 90° of hip and knee flexion. The arm action for the backward skip is double arm. The preparatory movement for the backward skip is a countermovement on one leg. The upward movement of the backward skip involves jumping backward with one leg and flexing the hip and knee of the nonskipping leg to approximately 90°. Both arms should be used to assist with the movement. The downward movement of the backward skip involves landing in the starting position on the same leg. Immediately repeat the skip with the opposite leg.
summary: The backward skip is a low-intensity, backward movement with horizontal and vertical components.

content: The single-arm alternate-leg bound has a medium intensity level. The direction of the single-arm alternate-leg bound is horizontal and vertical. The starting position for the single-arm alternate-leg bound is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart. The arm action for the single-arm alternate-leg bound is single arm. The preparatory movement for the single-arm alternate-leg bound is jogging at a comfortable pace; begin the drill with the left foot forward. The upward movement of the single-arm alternate-leg bound involves pushing off with the left foot as it contacts the ground. During push-off, bring the right leg forward by flexing the thigh to a position approximately parallel with the ground and the knee at 90°. During this flight phase of the drill, reach forward with the left arm. The downward movement of the single-arm alternate-leg bound involves landing on the right leg and immediately repeating the sequence on the opposite side upon landing.
summary: The single-arm alternate-leg bound is a medium-intensity, bounding drill with a horizontal and vertical direction, alternating legs and incorporating arm swing.

Jum P From b ox

content:
DePTH Jum P
Depth Jumps
The intensity level is High. The direction of jump is Vertical. The equipment is a plyometric box, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high. The starting position is to get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box. The arm action is Double arm. The preparatory movement is to step from box. The downward movement is to land on the floor with both feet. The upward movement is to immediately jump up as high as possible upon landing. Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed. Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm). Note: Upon landing, emphasis should be on jumping up, with minimal horizontal movement. The far right photo shows too much forward movement.
summary: Depth Jumps involve stepping off a box and immediately jumping as high as possible upon landing, emphasizing quickness and explosiveness.

content:
Intensity level: High
Direction of jump: Vertical and horizontal
Equipment: Two plyometric boxes, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high
Starting position: Get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box, facing the second box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
Arm action: Double arm
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor with both feet.
Upward movement: Upon landing, immediately jump onto the second box.
Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
Note: The distance between boxes depends on experience and ability; the greater the distance between boxes, the higher the intensity of the jump. Begin with the boxes placed 24 inches (61 cm) apart.
summary: Description of a plyometric exercise involving stepping from one box to another, emphasizing a quick upward jump and minimizing ground contact time.

content:
Intensity level: High
Direction of jump: Vertical
Equipment: Plyometric box, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high

Arm action: Double arm or none
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor in a squat position (90° of hip and knee flexion) with both feet. (The subject in the center photo should have greater hip and knee flexion.)
Upward movement: Upon landing, immediately jump up as high as possible; land in the same squat position.
Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).

summary: Description of a plyometric exercise involving stepping from a box, landing in a squat, and immediately jumping back up to the same squat position.

content:
Intensity level: High
Direction of jump: Vertical and lateral
Equipment: Plyometric box, 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high; a partner
Starting position: Get into a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box.
Arm action: Double arm
Preparatory movement: Step from box.
Downward movement: Land on the floor with both feet. Have a partner point to the right or left just before you land.
Upward movement: Upon landing, immediately sprint in the direction determined by your partner.
Note: When stepping from the box, step straight out. Do not first jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed.
Note: Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum. Intensity may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm).
summary: Description of a plyometric exercise involving stepping from a box, landing, and immediately sprinting in a direction indicated by a partner.

When stepping from the box for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump, step straight out. Do not jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down for the Depth Jump With Standing Long Jump, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed. content: The Single-Leg Depth Jump has a high intensity level. The Single-Leg Depth Jump involves a vertical direction of jump. Equipment for the Single-Leg Depth Jump includes a plyometric box that is 12 to 42 inches (30-107 cm) high. The starting position for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart on the plyometric box; toes should be near the edge of the box. The arm action for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is double arm. The preparatory movement for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is to step from the box. The downward movement for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is to land on the floor with one foot. The upward movement for the Single-Leg Depth Jump is to immediately jump up as high as possible with the landing foot upon landing. When stepping from the box for the Single-Leg Depth Jump, step straight out. Do not jump up or lower your center of gravity as you step down for the Single-Leg Depth Jump, as these adjustments will change the height from which the exercise is performed. Time on the ground should be kept to a minimum for the Single-Leg Depth Jump. Intensity for the Single-Leg Depth Jump may be increased by increasing the height of the box. Begin with a height of 12 inches (30 cm) for the Single-Leg Depth Jump. The Single-Leg Depth Jump is a very advanced form of the depth jump and should be performed only by those with adequate experience and ability as demonstrated in other versions of the depth jump.
summary: The Single-Leg Depth Jump is a high-intensity plyometric exercise involving a single-leg vertical jump from a plyometric box.

content: Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws has a low intensity level. The direction of throw for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is forward. Equipment for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg); rebounder or partner. The starting position for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart; face the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away. Raise the ball to chest level with the elbows flexed. The preparatory movement for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is to begin with a countermovement. (A countermovement for plyometric throws requires cocking the arms, that is, moving the arms slightly backward before the actual throw.) The arm action for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws is to use both arms, throw the ball to the rebounder or partner by extending the elbows. When the rebounder or partner returns the ball, catch it, return to the starting position, and immediately repeat the movement. Intensity for Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws may be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball. Begin with a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball. The Chest Pass is a type of Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws.
summary: Upper-Body Plyometric Drills are low-intensity exercises involving throwing a medicine ball forward to a partner or rebounder.

content: The Two-Handed Overhead Throw has a low intensity level. The direction of throw for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is forward. Equipment for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg); rebounder or partner. The starting position for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart; face the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away. Raise the ball overhead.
summary: The Two-Handed Overhead Throw is a low-intensity exercise involving throwing a medicine ball overhead to a partner or rebounder.

content: The Chest Pass is a type of Upper-Body Plyometric Drills throws.

content: The Two-Handed Overhead Throw has a low intensity level. The direction of throw for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is forward. Equipment for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg); rebounder or partner. The starting position for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart; face the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away. Raise the ball overhead.
summary: The Two-Handed Overhead Throw is a low-intensity, forward throw using a medicine ball and a rebounder or partner.

content: The preparatory movement for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is to begin with a countermovement. (A countermovement for plyometric throws requires cocking the arms, that is, moving the arms slightly backward before the actual throw.) The arm action for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw is to use both arms, throw the ball to the rebounder or partner, keeping the elbows extended. When the rebounder or partner returns the ball, catch the ball overhead and immediately repeat the throw. Intensity for the Two-Handed Overhead Throw may be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball. Begin with a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball.
summary: The Two-Handed Overhead Throw involves a countermovement, throwing with both arms, and can be intensified by increasing the weight of the ball.

content: The Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw has a low intensity level. The Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw is thrown forward and diagonally. Equipment for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 2 to 8 pounds (0.9-3.6 kg) and a rebounder or partner. The starting position for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart, facing the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away. In the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw, the ball is raised in both hands to a position over one shoulder with the elbows flexed. A countermovement for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw involves cocking the arms, moving them slightly backward before the throw. The Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw involves throwing the ball to the rebounder or partner by extending the elbows. The intensity of the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw can be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball. The starting weight for the Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw is a 2-pound (0.9 kg) ball.
summary: The Two-Hand Side-To-Side Throw is a low-intensity, forward and diagonal throw using a medicine ball and a rebounder or partner.

content: The Single-Arm Throw has a medium intensity level. The Single-Arm Throw is thrown forward. Equipment for the Single-Arm Throw includes a medicine or plyometric ball weighing 1 to 5 pounds (0.5-2.3 kg) and a rebounder or partner. The starting position for the Single-Arm Throw is a comfortable, upright stance with feet shoulder-width apart, facing the rebounder or partner approximately 10 feet (3 m) away. In the Single-Arm Throw, the ball is raised in one hand to a position of 90° of shoulder abduction and 90° of elbow flexion, with the arm rotated so the forearm is perpendicular to the floor. A countermovement for the Single-Arm Throw involves cocking the arm, moving it slightly backward before the throw. The Single-Arm Throw involves throwing the ball to the rebounder or partner using one arm. The intensity of the Single-Arm Throw can be increased by increasing the weight of the medicine ball. The starting weight for the Single-Arm Throw is a 1-pound (0.5 kg) ball. The Single-Arm Throw may also be performed using a natural throwing motion.
summary: The Single-Arm Throw is a medium-intensity, forward throw using a medicine ball and a rebounder or partner.
