summary: Speed and agility development prioritizes proper technique, with secondary methods like sprint resistance and assistance, and tertiary methods like mobility, strength, and speed-endurance training.

content: Program design involves planning at several levels, each with its own set of considerations: microcycles (short-term), mesocycles (medium-term), and macrocycles (long-term). This process is referred to as periodization—the planned distribution or variation in training means and methods on a periodic or cyclic basis.
summary: Program design involves microcycles, mesocycles, and macrocycles, utilizing periodization to strategically distribute training methods.

content: At each level of planning, strength and conditioning professionals must rationally manipulate the following variables: Exercise (or work) interval—the duration (e.g., in seconds) or distance (e.g., in meters) over which a repetition is executed, Exercise order—the sequence in which a set of repetitions is executed, Exercise:relief—the relative density of exercise and relief intervals in a set, expressed as a ratio (also called work:rest ratio), Frequency—the number of training sessions performed in a given time period (e.g., day or week), Intensity—the effort with which a repetition is executed, Relief or recovery (or rest) interval—the time period between repetitions and sets, Repetition—the execution of a specific workload assignment or movement technique, Series—a group of sets and recovery intervals, Set—a group of repetitions and relief intervals, Volume—the amount of work (repetitions × sets) performed in a given training session or time period.
summary: Program planning involves manipulating variables like exercise interval, order, intensity, frequency, and volume.

content: Fatigue management and task specificity are the driving forces in short-range planning of speed and agility development.
summary: Speed and agility development prioritizes fatigue management and task specificity in short-term planning.

content: Fatigue is a progressive process that begins at the onset of work and affects task execution well before failure occurs. It is a normal result of intense activity, but tends to interfere with skill acquisition and performance.
summary: Fatigue is a progressive process that negatively impacts skill acquisition and performance during intense activity.

content: The text describes a tactical modeling procedure for establishing special endurance training criteria. The procedure involves identifying the competition model, including level, scheme, style, system, time period, personnel, and nature and scope of tactical events. Tactical events include intensity levels, outcomes, goals, objectives, settled events (e.g., attack, possession, rally), unsettled/transitional events (e.g., clear, fast break, special teams, turnover), and power play situations.
summary: A tactical modeling procedure establishes special endurance training criteria by analyzing competition models and tactical events.

content: Video recording of specific competition(s) or segment(s) is required with respect to selected tactical events and assignments. Evaluation involves assessing fundamental exercise relief patterns, frequency distribution, central tendency versus variability, subdivisions, and the impact of injuries, penalties, scores, and time-outs on training.
summary: Tactical modeling involves video analysis, evaluating exercise patterns, and considering factors like injuries and scores.

content: Core training and testing tasks should consider workload intensity and duration, position- and situation-specific assignments and techniques. This procedure can be used to establish special endurance training criteria for a sport.
summary: Core training tasks should consider workload, position-specific needs, and the sport's demands for establishing special endurance criteria.

content: The intent of speed-endurance training is to enhance fatigue resistance and tolerance. Speed and agility training involves tactics that purposefully stress the metabolic systems.
summary: Speed-endurance training aims to improve fatigue resistance, while speed and agility training utilizes metabolic stress tactics.

content: Strength and conditioning professionals should apply sound motor learning strategies when teaching movement skills.
summary: Effective teaching of movement skills requires sound motor learning strategies from strength and conditioning professionals.

content: Maximum running velocity has been directly related to muscle myokinase (MK) and creatine phosphokinase (CPK) activities and inversely related to total lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) activity. Enzymes associated with adenosine triphosphate (ATP) resynthesis capacity and pyruvate-lactate interconversion are key determinants of sprinting speed.
summary: Sprinting speed is influenced by enzymes involved in ATP resynthesis and pyruvate-lactate interconversion, with higher MK and CPK activity and lower LDH activity associated with greater speed.

content: Sprint-type training has its greatest effects on the phosphagen pathways, with lesser but significant effects on glycolytic or oxidative metabolism. Intense, brief activities engage the phosphagen pathway, especially in fast-twitch muscle fibers.
summary: Sprint training primarily affects the phosphagen system, with secondary effects on glycolytic and oxidative metabolism, particularly in fast-twitch muscle fibers.

content: These pathways account for much of the energy yield during the transition from rest to exercise, or from one workload to another, in all fiber types. Although intramuscular ATP levels rarely drop more than 40% to 60%, several seconds of intense activity can virtually deplete creatine phosphate (CP); this is highly correlated with sprinting fatigue. Creatine phosphate repletion is achieved oxidatively, with a biphasic time course characterized by initial rapid (half-time 20-22 seconds) and subsequent slow (half-time ~170 seconds) components.
summary: The phosphagen system is crucial for energy transitions, and creatine phosphate depletion is a primary contributor to sprinting fatigue. Creatine phosphate replenishment occurs in two phases: rapid and slow.

content: Adenosine diphosphate levels modulate the initial phase, which seems to be unaffected by intracellular pH, whereas the latter phase is depressed at low pH.
summary: Creatine phosphate replenishment is influenced by adenosine diphosphate levels, with the initial phase unaffected by pH, while the later phase is suppressed at low pH.

content: Sprint training tends to modify muscle contractile characteristics via at least three mechanisms: hypertrophy, especially in fast-twitch fibers; increased development of the sarcoplasmic reticulum; and shifts in myosin heavy-chain isoforms, with both Types I and IIx fibers shifting toward IIa. There is also limited evidence suggesting that sprint training enhances muscle Na+/K+ pump activity and nerve conduction velocity.
summary: Sprint training modifies muscle contractile characteristics through hypertrophy, sarcoplasmic reticulum development, myosin heavy-chain shifts, and potential enhancements in Na+/K+ pump activity and nerve conduction velocity.

content: Much of the evidence on fatigue addresses peripheral factors in working muscle tissue. There is also a central component in involving the motoneural system. Acute performance impairment is usually the combined result of several mechanisms, with their relative contributions varying according to the nature of the task.
summary: Fatigue involves both peripheral factors in muscle tissue and central mechanisms in the motoneural system, with the relative contributions varying depending on the task.

content: Sprint training tends to modify muscle contractile characteristics via at least three mechanisms: hypertrophy, especially in fast-twitch fibers; increased development of the sarcoplasmic reticulum; and shifts in myosin heavy-chain isoforms, with both Types I and IIx fibers shifting toward IIa. There is also limited evidence suggesting that sprint training enhances muscle Na+/K+ pump activity and nerve conduction velocity.
summary: Sprint training improves muscle characteristics through hypertrophy, sarcoplasmic reticulum development, and myosin heavy-chain shifts, potentially also enhancing Na+/K+ pump activity and nerve conduction velocity.

content: Much of the evidence on fatigue addresses peripheral factors in working muscle tissue. There is also a central component in involving the motoneural system.
summary: Muscle fatigue results from both peripheral factors within muscle tissue and central factors involving the nervous system.

content: Acute performance impairment is usually the combined result of several mechanisms, with their relative contributions varying according to the nature of the task. Athletes should conduct speed and agility tasks early in a training session, before other fatiguing activities.
summary: Acute performance decline stems from multiple mechanisms, varying by task. Athletes should prioritize speed and agility training early in sessions to minimize fatigue.

content: Structure training sessions around brief work bouts and frequent 2- to 3-minute rest periods in order to maximize the quality of learning and training effects. Repetition methods are an ideal choice in this case, whereas competitive-trial and interval methods are generally better suited for speed-endurance training.
summary: Optimal training sessions involve short work periods with frequent rest, favoring repetition methods for speed and agility, while interval methods are better for speed-endurance.

content: It can be beneficial to distribute daily sessions into modules separated by recovery breaks, or to subdivide workloads into brief clusters separated by frequent rest pauses, or both.
summary: Training can be structured with daily modules separated by breaks, or workloads divided into clusters with frequent rest pauses, for improved recovery.

content: The challenge is to strike a balance between opposing fitness and fatigue responses. Sound strategies usually have two basic features: a fairly stable menu of training tasks over any given micro- or mesocycle, and regular variation in workload and technique targets, for example on a daily or weekly basis (or both).
summary: Effective training balances fitness gains with fatigue management, employing consistent tasks within cycles while varying workload and technique regularly.

content: Decisions regarding intensity and volume prescriptions are required on a day-to-day as well as an individual session basis. The interaction of these variables drives much of the program design process—hence the value of a practical indicator of training stress like volume-load, the product of work volume and intensity.
summary: Training program design involves daily adjustments to intensity and volume, guided by the concept of volume-load, which considers both work volume and intensity.

content: When conducting speed and agility sessions, the strength and conditioning professional can quantify volume-load in terms of running speeds and distances.
summary: Volume-load in speed and agility training is measured using running speeds and distances.

content: At the lower end is the stimulus threshold required to trigger desired effects, whereas at the upper end is a point of diminishing returns, beyond which further application yields no beneficial—or possibly detrimental—effects. Volume-load progression is a central issue at all levels of planning.
summary: Effective training involves finding the optimal volume-load range, avoiding both under- and over-stimulation. Volume-load progression is crucial at all planning levels.

content: Variable rather than linear strategies tend to yield superior results. A basic tactic at the microcyclic level is to establish a fixed training frequency, for example one day on, one day off or two on, two off, and manipulate simple workload parameters such as intensity (movement speed) and volume (repetition distance and number).
summary: Variable training strategies are more effective than linear ones. Microcyclic planning involves fixed training frequency with manipulated intensity and volume.

content: As workload duration or repetition progresses, glycolytic activity can exceed mitochondrial capacity to oxidize the resulting end products—especially if recovery periods are limited. Acid–base balance in the tissues is disturbed because of coupling between ATPase and glycolysis, not just H+ production via the glycolytic pathway.
summary: Prolonged workload can lead to glycolytic overload exceeding mitochondrial capacity, disrupting acid-base balance through ATPase and glycolysis coupling.

content: The resulting disturbances in excitation-contraction coupling and cross-bridge formation impair the muscle’s mechanical properties and energetic efficiency via depressed RFD, peak force, velocity, power, and CP repletion, as well as prolonged relaxation time.
summary: Metabolic disturbances impair muscle function, reducing force production, velocity, power, and recovery time.

content: Pyruvate-lactate clearance is achieved oxidatively and is facilitated by shuttling of lactate between sites of production (in both fiber types) and consumption (especially in Type I fibers). This mechanism allows oxidative metabolism to buffer glycolysis to some extent while increasing energy yield.
summary: Lactate clearance is primarily oxidative, facilitated by lactate shuttling between muscle fibers. This process buffers glycolysis and enhances energy production.

content: Oxidative and nonoxidative pathways work interdependently during and after intense activity.
summary: Oxidative and nonoxidative metabolic pathways function together during and after intense exercise.

content: Distance-duration training methods are useful when athletes are preparing for intensive sports. Submaximal exercise can be appropriate for acute priming and recovery activities.
summary: Distance-duration training suits athletes in intensive sports, while submaximal exercise is beneficial for priming and recovery.

content: In terms of chronic training effects, however, interval methods tend to increase glycolytic as well as oxidative enzyme activities, acid–base buffering capability, and various indices of lactic power and capacity. Low-intensity speed-endurance training does not, and in fact may reduce nonoxidative enzyme activities.
summary: Interval training improves both glycolytic and oxidative capacity, while low-intensity speed-endurance training may hinder nonoxidative enzyme activity.

content: Although mitochondrial adaptations to interval methods are modest by comparison, the functional benefits may offset the drawbacks. Depending on the nature of the sport, therefore, strength and conditioning professionals should use low-intensity speed-endurance training with discretion.
summary: While interval training offers greater functional benefits, low-intensity speed-endurance training should be used judiciously based on the sport's demands.

content: A practical solution is to subdivide a given volume of preparatory speed-endurance work into segments, effectively rest-pausing as needed to achieve the targeted objectives for power output and task performance. Rather than middle- or long-duration activities, total distance can be structured into sets of shuttles or striders with workloads at or above V.O2max and exercise:relief ratios adjusted accordingly.
summary: Speed-endurance training can be structured with segmented workloads and rest periods, focusing on high-intensity shuttles or striders to optimize power output and task performance.

content: This approach exploits the advantages of intensive, low- or moderate-volume intermittent training while emphasizing task execution rather than just exertion. Even during off-season periods, workloads need not be limited to submaximal levels.
summary: This training approach combines intensive, intermittent training with task-focused execution, even during off-season periods.

content: A superior method of speed-endurance training for many sports is to maximally engage the oxidative pathways in intermittent tasks instead of continuous, submaximal workloads. During pre-season periods, speed-endurance training can then progress to competitive-trial methods.
summary: Maximizing oxidative pathways through intermittent tasks is a superior speed-endurance training method for many sports, progressing to competitive-trial methods during pre-season.

content: The tactical metabolic training procedure outlined previously is a pragmatic way to model these sessions on competition-specific criteria.
summary: The described training procedure effectively simulates competition-specific metabolic demands.

content: According to the principle of practice specificity, the sensorimotor, processing, and contextual demands of training tasks should correspond to the target activity in order to maximize the acquisition, retention, and transfer of motor skills.
summary: Training tasks should mimic the target activity's demands to optimize skill acquisition, retention, and transfer.

content: An optimal level of contextual interference in the form of varied or random practice will enhance learning, although at the expense of short-term performance. Optimal arousal, motivation, and focused attention are necessary to achieve the desired learning and performance goals.
summary: Varied practice enhances learning but may initially reduce performance. Optimal arousal, motivation, and focus are crucial for achieving learning and performance goals.

content: During pre-season periods, speed-endurance training can then progress to competitive-trial methods. The tactical metabolic training procedure outlined previously is a pragmatic way to model these sessions on competition-specific criteria.
summary: This chunk describes the progression of training from pre-season to competitive trials, emphasizing competition-specific training.

content: According to the principle of practice specificity, the sensorimotor, processing, and contextual demands of training tasks should correspond to the target activity in order to maximize the acquisition, retention, and transfer of motor skills. An optimal level of contextual interference in the form of varied or random practice will enhance learning, although at the expense of short-term performance.
summary: This chunk explains the principle of practice specificity and the benefits of varied practice for motor skill learning.

content: Optimal arousal, motivation, and focused attention are necessary to achieve the desired learning and performance goals. A substantial body of evidence supports the 10-year rule for achieving mastery. The acquisition of expertise in a wide range of performance domains, including sport, involves up to 10 years—or 10,000 hours—of regular, guided, deliberate preparation.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of psychological factors and the 10,000-hour rule for achieving mastery.

content: Part practice is preferable for tasks that are highly complex but low in organization. Whole practice is preferable for tasks that are low in complexity but highly organized.
summary: This chunk outlines the best practice methods for different types of tasks based on complexity and organization.

content: Extrinsic feedback is beneficial when a skill is complex or the athlete is a novice, and essential when intrinsic feedback is limited or difficult to interpret. Frequent feedback is important during the early stage of learning, but can be detrimental if the athlete becomes dependent on it.
summary: This chunk discusses the role of extrinsic feedback in motor skill learning and the importance of avoiding over-reliance on it.

content: The verbal instructions should combine demonstration-modeling and verbal instruction, focus on (but not be redundant with) intrinsic feedback, provide information on proper performance as well as error correction, progress from qualitative to quantitative information, and gradually decrease in frequency.
summary: This chunk outlines best practices for delivering verbal instructions during motor skill learning.

content: Motor skill learning generally improves with shorter, more frequent practice sessions. Distributed practice tends to improve long-term acquisition and transfer of continuous skills, as well as acute performance. Massed practice tends to improve acquisition and transfer of discrete skills but can reduce acute performance.
summary: This chunk compares different practice schedules and their effects on motor skill learning.

content: It may be advantageous for novice athletes to begin with blocked practice involving one version of a task until they master the basic technique. The strength and conditioning professional should then introduce varied practice—that is, changing task order or conditions—to help athletes develop specific schemas. Varied practice can reduce acute performance but significantly improves long-term skill acquisition.
summary: This chunk describes the benefits of blocked and varied practice for skill acquisition.

content: Certain types of training can interfere with one another when performed in parallel, at least in previously untrained subjects.
summary: This chunk highlights the potential for interference between different types of training.

content: Advanced athletes can tolerate greater training stress but are typically closer to an overtraining threshold than novices.
summary: This chunk compares the training stress tolerance of advanced and novice athletes.

content: Sequenced training strategies are based on the premise that the delayed effects of certain training stimuli can alter the responses to others. Acutely, involution is a function of the half-life of the tissues that are synthesized during adaptive remodeling—for example, the half-life of glycolytic enzymes is relatively brief, ranging from ~1 1/2 hours to a few days, whereas oxidative enzymes turn over more slowly and myofibrillar proteins have a comparatively greater life span. Chronically, the longer the training cycle, the more stable its residual effect.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of sequenced training and the role of tissue half-life in training adaptations.

content: This allows athletes to maintain the performance gains acquired during one training cycle with relatively small volume-loads during the next training cycle, thus creating an opportunity to strive toward a new training goal. A general rule is to organize training into approximately four-week periods, which seem to represent an optimal biological window for integrating responses over the intermediate term.
summary: This chunk describes the benefits of sequenced training and the optimal duration of training cycles.

content: Sequenced training involves alternating blocks of accumulation and restitution, each with essentially one objective. For example, the accumulation block emphasizes strength development while allocating minimal, that is, maintenance-type, workloads to speed and agility training. The restitution block reverses the emphasis, reducing strength workloads while targeting speed and agility development.
summary: This chunk explains the structure and objectives of accumulation and restitution blocks in sequenced training.

content: Sequenced training is a significant departure from the concurrent strategies commonly used in basic and intermediate applications. Much of the literature describing this method addresses it in theoretical terms but offers limited supporting evidence or practical guidelines. Such strategies are appropriate for experienced athletes, not youths or novices. The duration of the off-season period must be long enough to permit deployment of a series of blocks, which may not be the case in sports with long competitive seasons.
summary: This chunk discusses the applicability and limitations of sequenced training.

content: It is important to understand the principle of dynamic correspondence, as well as the nature of residual or delayed training responses, in order to get the desired effect. Strength and conditioning professionals should use discretion in prescribing workloads during the accumulation blocks, limit their duration to about four weeks, and consider using various fatigue management tactics.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of understanding training principles and managing fatigue in sequenced training.

content: An athlete’s developmental status becomes a dominant consideration at the long-range level of planning. This process is analogous to curriculum design, with the subject matter consisting of movement skills and progression in learning and training objectives, which occurs on two fronts: means, that is, general to special tasks; and methods, that is, extensive to intensive workloads.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of considering an athlete's developmental stage in long-term planning.

content: Years 1-2: Fundamental. Training tasks involve deliberate play rather than performance-oriented activity while emphasizing basic movement competencies and fun. The skills introduced in this stage should be simple but challenging for youth athletes.
summary: This chunk describes the fundamental training stage for young athletes.

content: Years 3-4: Novice (learning to train). Training begins to involve structured practice.
summary: This chunk describes the novice training stage for young athletes.

content: An athlete’s developmental status becomes a dominant consideration at the long-range level of planning. This process is analogous to curriculum design, with the subject matter consisting of movement skills and progression in learning and training objectives, which occurs on two fronts: means, that is, general to special tasks; and methods, that is, extensive to intensive workloads.
summary: Long-term athletic development considers an athlete's age and uses a curriculum-like approach, progressing from general to specialized skills and increasing training intensity.

content: Years 1-2: Fundamental. Training tasks involve deliberate play rather than performance-oriented activity while emphasizing basic movement competencies and fun. The skills introduced in this stage should be simple but challenging for youth athletes.
summary: The first two years focus on fundamental movement skills through playful activities, emphasizing basic competencies and enjoyment.

content: Years 3-4: Novice (learning to train). Training begins to involve structured practice. The program continues to emphasize basic movement competencies and mechanics while starting to target the development of motor abilities.
summary: Years 3-4 introduce structured training, building on basic skills and focusing on motor development.

content: Years 5-6: Intermediate (training to train). Training begins to involve deliberate practice, with balanced emphasis on competency-based and performance-based tasks. The program continues targeting the development of movement techniques and motor abilities.
summary: Intermediate training incorporates deliberate practice, balancing skill development with performance goals.

content: Years 7-8: Advanced (training to compete). Development of specific techniques and abilities gets high priority, and these techniques and abilities are applied in complex tactics and competitive situations.
summary: Advanced training emphasizes specific skills and tactics, preparing athletes for competition.

content: Years 9-10: Elite (training to win). Mastery of specific strategies, skills, and abilities gets top priority. The program focuses on achieving sport performance expertise.
summary: Elite training prioritizes mastery of strategies and skills to achieve peak performance.

content: Movement mechanics and techniques, as well as basic fitness qualities—that is, general preparation tasks—are priorities during the early stages. The intent is to automate these so that the athlete can progressively focus on tactical and strategic targets—that is, special preparation tasks—as he or she advances toward the elite level.
summary: Early training focuses on movement mechanics, basic fitness, and gradually progresses towards tactical and strategic development.

content: The strength and conditioning professional should introduce age-appropriate movement skills such that athletes can practice them at each level with the expectation of achieving proficiency at others. As athletes master each skill, they should review and maintain it while progressing to newer, more complex tasks.
summary: Strength and conditioning professionals should introduce age-appropriate skills, allowing athletes to build upon mastered skills and progress to more complex ones.

content: Program design involves multiple levels of planning: microcycles (short-term), mesocycles (medium-term), and macrocycles (long-term.)
summary: Program design utilizes multiple planning levels: microcycles (short-term), mesocycles (medium-term), and macrocycles (long-term).

content: The text describes a 14-week preseason macrocycle for collegiate or professional American football. The macrocycle is organized into four successive blocks, each three to four weeks in length.
summary: A 14-week preseason macrocycle for American football is outlined, divided into four blocks of three to four weeks each.

content: The premise of the sample outline is threefold: ATP resynthesis during plays is initially achieved via the phosphagen pathways, but lactic and oxidative mechanisms are maximally engaged over a series of downs. CP repletion during recovery is V.O2 dependent. Pyruvate-lactate removal is achieved largely by oxidation in Type I muscle fibers.
summary: The sample outline emphasizes the interplay of ATP resynthesis pathways during plays, with phosphagen systems initially dominant, transitioning to lactic and oxidative mechanisms over multiple downs. CP replenishment relies on oxygen availability, and pyruvate-lactate removal is primarily handled by Type I muscle fibers.

content: Increasing oxidative power is critical for rapidly resynthesizing CP, oxidizing pyruvate-lactate, minimizing glycogen depletion, and thereby sustaining power output with minimal fatigue over the course of the game.
summary: Enhanced oxidative capacity is crucial for efficient energy resynthesis, lactate clearance, and sustained power output during a game.

content: The Accumulation phase (three weeks) includes 12 resistance training sessions distributed over the period on a four-days-per-week schedule and six speed, agility, and speed-endurance sessions distributed on a two-days-per-week schedule. The objective of metabolic conditioning during the Accumulation phase is to maximize oxidative capacity and pyruvate-lactate consumption via three to four repetitive exercise bouts of ≤10-minute duration.
summary: The Accumulation phase (3 weeks) focuses on resistance training (4 days/week) and speed/agility/endurance (2 days/week), emphasizing metabolic conditioning to improve oxidative capacity and lactate clearance.

content: The Restitution phase (four weeks) includes 12 resistance training sessions distributed over the period on a three-days-per-week schedule and 12 speed, agility, and speed-endurance sessions distributed on a three-days-per-week schedule. The objective of speed and agility training during the Restitution phase is to execute sound movement mechanics at sub-maximal speeds. The objectives of metabolic conditioning during the Restitution phase are to enhance pyruvate-lactate tolerance via 2- to 3-minute all-out exercise intervals with 8 to 10 minutes of recovery and to maintain V.O2max.
summary: The Restitution phase (4 weeks) involves resistance training (3 days/week) and speed/agility/endurance (3 days/week), focusing on refining movement mechanics at submaximal speeds, enhancing lactate tolerance, and maintaining VO2max.

content: The second Accumulation phase (three weeks) includes 12 resistance training sessions distributed over the period on a four-days-per-week schedule and six speed, agility, and speed-endurance sessions distributed on a two-days-per-week schedule. The objective of speed and agility training during this phase is to execute sound movement mechanics at full speed, and in assisted and resisted conditions. V.O2max maintenance training continues in this phase. The objective of special endurance training during this phase is to maximally stress the metabolic systems via specific phosphagen-lactic-oxidative interaction, assuming that the athlete trains all-out in each exercise bout and that training continues only as long as the athlete can achieve the desired power output.
summary: The second Accumulation phase (3 weeks) focuses on resistance training (4 days/week) and speed/agility/endurance (2 days/week), emphasizing full-speed movement mechanics, maintaining VO2max, and stressing metabolic systems through intense, all-out training.

content: There are two viable choices for each team’s offense: an ideal approach based on how many plays it takes to successfully reach the end zone, or a real approach based on combined successful and unsuccessful possessions. The ideal approach may be a better alternative since success is the objective of each possession. In the ideal approach, one training session simulates one quarter consisting of three successful touchdown drives. In this example, each Washington Redskins (WR) possession consists of 10 plays whereas each Buffalo Bills (BB) possession consists of 16 plays. Exercise:relief ratios for each team are the same.
summary: Two training approaches are presented: an ideal approach focusing solely on successful drives and a real approach incorporating both successful and unsuccessful plays. The ideal approach, simulating three successful touchdown drives per quarter, may be more effective for maximizing success.

content: In the real approach, one training session simulates a series of quarters, each consisting of 16 total plays for the WR offense (that is, three sets of five to six plays) or 19 total plays for the BB offense (that is, three sets of six to seven plays). Respective exercise:relief ratios for the two teams are 5:36 seconds and 5:27 seconds. Initially, training simulates one quarter of combined successful and unsuccessful touchdown drives; the equivalent of an entire game can be completed in one session with progressive addition of the second, third and fourth quarters.
summary: The real approach simulates a series of quarters with varying play counts for each team, incorporating both successful and unsuccessful plays. Training progresses from simulating a single quarter to a full game.

content: Exercise:relief ratios for each team are the same. Respective exercise:relief ratios for the two teams are 5:36 seconds and 5:27 seconds. The Washington Redskins’ offense averaged 12.3 series per game during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs. The Washington Redskins’ offense averaged 64.7 plays per game during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs. The Washington Redskins’ offense averaged 5.5 net yards per play during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs. The Washington Redskins’ offense had an exercise:relief time of 5.0 ± 1.5 seconds and 36.4 ± 6.6 seconds during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs. The Buffalo Bills’ offense averaged 13.3 series per game during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs. The Buffalo Bills’ offense averaged 74.7 plays per game during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs. The Buffalo Bills’ offense averaged 4.3 net yards per play during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs. The Buffalo Bills’ offense had an exercise:relief time of 5.0 ± 1.6 seconds and 26.9 ± 6.9 seconds during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs.
summary: This chunk describes the offensive performance and exercise:relief ratios of the Washington Redskins and Buffalo Bills during the 1991-1992 NFL playoffs.

content: In the real approach, one training session simulates a series of quarters, each consisting of 16 total plays for the WR offense (that is, three sets of five to six plays) or 19 total plays for the BB offense (that is, three sets of six to seven plays). Initially, training simulates one quarter of combined successful and unsuccessful touchdown drives; the equivalent of an entire game can be completed in one session with progressive addition of the second, third and fourth quarters.
summary: This chunk outlines the structure of a training session designed to simulate a real game, progressively adding quarters over time.

content: The strength and conditioning professional can also manipulate basic intensity and volume parameters. The strength and conditioning professional can select core training and testing tasks based on observed exercise:relief patterns. Examples of core training and testing tasks include sprint or agility drills, such as repeated 40-yard (37 m) sprints or pro agility shuttles. Examples of core training and testing tasks include positional playbook assignments mixed according to play selection, such as blocking drills, pass routes, and running paths for offensive players or coverage, pursuit, and stunt drills for defensive players.
summary: This chunk describes the role of a strength and conditioning professional in tailoring training based on observed exercise:relief patterns and providing examples of core training tasks.

content: The Restitution phase (four weeks) includes 12 resistance training sessions distributed over the period on a three-days-per-week schedule and 12 speed, agility, and speed-endurance sessions distributed on a three-days-per-week schedule. Objectives for speed, agility, and speed-endurance training are the same as in the previous block, but the frequencies have changed.
summary: This chunk details the Restitution phase of a training program, which includes resistance training and speed, agility, and speed-endurance sessions.

content: Strength and conditioning professionals need an integrated plan of action when developing athletes’ speed, agility, and speed-endurance. They should analyze target activities and specifically address the requisite skills and abilities in training. Functional movements involve task-specific force application. These forces are expressed in terms of acceleration, time or rate of application, and velocity. Acceleration, maximum-velocity, and multidirectional skills involve distinct movement mechanics. Collectively, these comprise the skill set that an athlete uses to perform a wide range of tasks and maneuvers.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of a comprehensive plan for developing speed, agility, and speed-endurance, highlighting the role of functional movements and distinct movement mechanics.

content: Athletes should also develop the special endurance needed to achieve specific performance objectives in competition and training. Running speed is the interaction of stride frequency and length. The goal of sprinting is to achieve high stride frequency and optimal stride length, with explosive horizontal push-off and minimal vertical impulse. Agility is an expression of an athlete’s coordinative abilities.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of special endurance for athletes, defining running speed and sprinting technique, and explaining agility as a coordinative ability.

content: Strength and conditioning professionals should classify motor skills rationally and analyze task-specific modes of locomotion and changes in velocity. Strength and conditioning professionals should also prioritize training methods for speed and agility development according to a hierarchy—from the execution of sound movement technique in a specific task, to the development of special skills and abilities in modified conditions, to the development of general skills and abilities. Combining various means and methods of training into a sound program involves planning on multiple levels, from task specificity and fatigue management over the short term to the prescription of developmentally appropriate targets over the long term. These considerations present certain program design challenges that can be resolved with periodization strategies. Part II of section 4, “Program Design,” discusses the steps or variables involved in designing an aerobic endurance training program. Improvements in aerobic endurance performance can be derived only when sound principles are applied during training. Although the fundamental mechanisms responsible for inducing adaptations during training are undefined, it is clear that in order to adapt, the various systems of the body must be challenged by an exercise stimulus (e.g., specificity and overload). The physiological systems that are not involved during the training session or not stressed sufficiently by exercise will not adapt to the training program. Training specificity refers to the distinct adaptations to the physiological systems that arise from the training program. A training effect is limited to the physiological systems used and overloaded during training. Unless training programs are strictly designed to involve and stress a physiological system, there will be very limited or possibly no adaptations in that system. To improve aerobic endurance performance, training programs must be designed to enhance the function of the respiratory, cardiovascular, and musculoskeletal systems.
summary: This chunk delves into the principles of program design for speed, agility, and aerobic endurance, emphasizing the importance of task specificity, periodization, and challenging physiological systems.

content: To improve aerobic endurance performance, training programs must be designed to enhance the function of the respiratory, cardiovascular, and musculoskeletal systems. Successful performance in aerobic endurance competitions involving running, cycling, and swimming is dependent on the athlete’s ability to cover a fixed distance in the shortest time possible. To reach this level of performance, athletes must train hard, yet intelligently, to maximize the physiological adaptations derived from training. The physical condition of the aerobic endurance athlete is of primary importance if that athlete is to perform at optimal levels during competition.
summary: Aerobic endurance performance relies on the respiratory, cardiovascular, and musculoskeletal systems. Athletes must train strategically to maximize adaptations and achieve optimal performance.

content: A training effect is limited to the physiological systems used and overloaded during training. Unless training programs are strictly designed to involve and stress a physiological system, there will be very limited or possibly no adaptations in that system. The physiological systems of the body will adapt to the exercise stress during continued overload. Adaptations within the physiological systems will occur until the tissues are no longer overloaded. This necessitates use of a greater overload.
summary: Training adaptations are specific to the physiological systems stressed during exercise.  Continuous overload is necessary for adaptation, requiring progressive increases in training intensity.

content: Exercise frequency, duration, and intensity are the variables most often manipulated to provide overload to the systems of the body. Numerous types of training programs have been designed for aerobic endurance athletes. These training programs vary in the mode, frequency, duration, and intensity of the activity. What successful aerobic endurance athletes have in common is a training program designed to enhance their strengths and improve their weaknesses.
summary: Training variables (frequency, duration, intensity) are manipulated to overload physiological systems.  Aerobic endurance training programs are diverse, but successful athletes tailor them to their individual needs.

content: As it would be exhausting to review relevant information about training for all possible aerobic endurance sports, only basic aerobic endurance training topics are presented, with specific examples as they relate to running, cycling, and swimming.
summary: This chapter focuses on fundamental aerobic endurance training principles with examples from running, cycling, and swimming.

content: When designing aerobic endurance training programs, it is important to understand those factors that influence and play a significant role in successful aerobic endurance performance. This allows for the development of sound training programs while minimizing unnecessary training that may lead to counterproductive adaptations, fatigue, overwork, or overtraining.
summary: Understanding factors influencing aerobic endurance performance is crucial for designing effective training programs and avoiding overtraining.

content: As the duration of the aerobic endurance event increases, so does the proportion of the total energy demand that must be met by aerobic metabolism. Therefore, high maximal aerobic power (V.O2max) is necessary for success in aerobic endurance events. A high correlation has been shown to exist. Aerobic endurance training programs should be designed to improve V.O2max.
summary: Higher aerobic endurance event durations require greater reliance on aerobic metabolism, emphasizing the importance of V.O2max for success.

content: A high V.O2max is important for successful performance, but other factors may be equally or more important. Other important factors for aerobic endurance performance include a high lactate threshold, good exercise economy, a high ability to use fat as a fuel source, and a high percentage of Type I muscle fibers.
summary: While V.O2max is crucial, other factors like lactate threshold, exercise economy, fat utilization, and muscle fiber type also contribute to aerobic endurance performance.

content: In aerobic endurance events, the best competitor among athletes with similar V.O2max values is typically the person who can sustain aerobic energy production at the highest percentage of their V.O2max without accumulating large amounts of lactic acid in the muscle and blood. Lactate threshold is the speed of movement or percentage of V.O2max at which a specific blood lactate concentration is observed or the point at which blood lactate concentration begins to increase above resting levels.
summary: Sustaining high aerobic energy production and managing lactate accumulation are key to success in endurance events, even among athletes with similar V.O2max.

content: Several studies have shown that an athlete’s lactate threshold appears to be a better indicator of their aerobic endurance performance than V.O2max. The maximal lactate steady state is defined as the exercise intensity at which maximal lactate production is equal to maximal lactate clearance within the body. The maximal lactate steady state is considered by many to be a better indicator of aerobic endurance performance than either V.O2max or the lactate threshold.
summary: Lactate threshold and maximal lactate steady state are considered stronger indicators of aerobic endurance performance than V.O2max.

content: Aerobic endurance athletes must improve their lactate threshold or maximal lactate steady state. Athletes must conduct some training at elevated levels of blood and muscle lactate to maximize training improvements.
summary: Training should focus on improving lactate threshold and maximal lactate steady state, which requires exposure to elevated lactate levels.

content: A measure of the energy cost of activity at a given exercise velocity is referred to as the exercise economy. Athletes with a high exercise economy expend less energy during exercise to maintain a given exercise velocity. Several investigators have suggested that exercise economy is an important factor in successful performance in running events.
summary: Exercise economy, the energy cost of movement, is a crucial factor for successful performance in running events.

content: Aerobic endurance athletes must improve their lactate threshold or maximal lactate steady state. Athletes must conduct some training at elevated levels of blood and muscle lactate to maximize training improvements.
summary: Aerobic endurance athletes benefit from training at elevated lactate levels to improve their lactate threshold and maximize training gains.

content: A measure of the energy cost of activity at a given exercise velocity is referred to as the exercise economy. Athletes with a high exercise economy expend less energy during exercise to maintain a given exercise velocity. Several investigators have suggested that exercise economy is an important factor in successful performance in running events. Better performers have a slightly shorter stride length and greater stride frequency compared to less successful performers.
summary: Exercise economy, the energy cost of activity at a given velocity, is crucial for performance, with better runners exhibiting higher economy and specific stride characteristics.

content: During cycling, exercise economy can be affected by body mass size, cycling velocity, and aerodynamic positioning. For cyclists, an increase in body mass and cycling velocity and an inefficient body position generate greater wind resistance, resulting in a decrease in exercise economy. Elite swimmers are much more economical than nonelite swimmers and use less oxygen at any given swimming velocity. The most profound impact on exercise economy during swimming can be observed when swimming technique becomes more efficient. As improvements in stroke mechanics occur, energy demand for a given swimming velocity is reduced.
summary: Exercise economy is influenced by factors like body mass, velocity, and technique in cycling and swimming, with more efficient performers using less energy.

content: Training to improve exercise economy is critical for aerobic endurance athletes. An improvement in exercise economy can enhance maximal aerobic power (V.O2max) and lactate threshold.
summary: Improving exercise economy is vital for aerobic endurance athletes, as it boosts V.O2max and lactate threshold.

content: An effective aerobic endurance training program must include an exercise prescription specifically developed for the individual athlete. The optimal way to develop a sound training program is to have the factors related to aerobic endurance performance evaluated and then use that information to generate a training program specific to the athlete.
summary: Effective aerobic endurance training programs are individualized, based on performance evaluations and tailored to the athlete's needs.

content: Training programs for female athletes do not have to be different from those used to train male athletes; evidence indicates that males and females respond similarly to training programs.
summary: Training programs for female athletes are not fundamentally different from those for male athletes, as both genders respond similarly to training.

content: The exercise mode refers to the specific activity performed by the athlete: cycling, running, swimming, and so on. When training to improve aerobic endurance performance, the athlete should select activities that mimic as closely as possible the movement pattern employed in competition. This will effectively cause positive adaptations in specific physiological systems of the body. For example, the recruitment of specific muscle fibers and the adaptation of the energy systems within those fibers must be challenged during aerobic endurance training. Selecting the appropriate exercise mode during training ensures that the systems used in competition are challenged to improve. Remember that the more specific the training mode is to the sport, the greater the improvement in performance.
summary: Training should mimic competition activities to optimize physiological adaptations and performance improvements.

content: For an athlete involved in multiple aerobic endurance sports, or one who is interested in a general aerobic endurance fitness program, cross-training or participation in multiple aerobic endurance activities may be warranted.
summary: Cross-training with various aerobic endurance activities can be beneficial for athletes involved in multiple sports or seeking general fitness.

content: Training frequency refers to the number of training sessions conducted per day or per week. The frequency of training sessions will depend on an interaction of exercise intensity and duration, the training status of the athlete, and the specific sport season. Higher exercise intensity and longer duration may necessitate less frequent training to allow sufficient recovery from exercise sessions. The training status of the athlete can influence training frequency, with lesser-trained athletes requiring more recovery days at the initiation of a training period than more highly trained athletes. The sport season that the athlete is currently in can also influence training frequency; an off-season program may include five training days per week, but training frequency may progress to daily workouts (or even multiple workouts per day for a triathlete) in the preseason. Additionally, fewer training sessions may be required to maintain an achieved level of physiological function or performance than to attain that level initially.
summary: Training frequency is individualized based on intensity, duration, athlete status, and sport season, with adjustments made for recovery and maintenance phases.

content: Appropriate training frequency is important for the aerobic endurance athlete, as too much training may increase the risk of injury, illness, or overtraining. A number of studies have shown increased injury rates with training sessions more frequent than five times per week; however, these studies used active individuals in a wide age range, not only young and healthy athletes, as subjects. Conversely, too little training will not result in positive adaptations to the various systems of the body. Research has shown that it is necessary to train more than twice per week in order to increase V.O2max.
summary: Optimal training frequency balances the risk of overtraining with the need for sufficient training volume to induce adaptations.

content: Recovery from individual training sessions is essential if the athlete is to derive maximum benefits from the subsequent training session. Exercise performance has been shown to improve following relative rest from difficult training sessions. Obtaining sufficient rest, becoming rehydrated, and restoring fuel sources are critical issues for the athlete during recovery. Relaxation and avoidance of strenuous physical activity are particularly important following days of high-intensity or long-duration training.
summary: Adequate recovery is crucial for maximizing training benefits, involving rest, hydration, refueling, and avoiding strenuous activity.

content: Postexercise ingestion of adequate fluids is important for replacing the fluid lost during training. If the training session was especially long or intense, then postexercise carbohydrate intake is important for replacing the muscle and liver glycogen stores that were likely depleted. Central to causing training adaptations in the body is the interaction of training intensity and duration.
summary: Post-exercise fluid and carbohydrate intake are essential for replenishing lost fluids and glycogen stores, and the interaction of training intensity and duration drives adaptations.

content: Obtaining sufficient rest, becoming rehydrated, and restoring fuel sources are critical issues for the athlete during recovery. Relaxation and avoidance of strenuous physical activity are particularly important following days of high-intensity or long-duration training. If the training session was especially long or intense, then postexercise carbohydrate intake is important for replacing the muscle and liver glycogen stores that were likely depleted. Postexercise ingestion of adequate fluids is important for replacing the fluid lost during training.
summary: Recovery involves rest, hydration, and replenishing glycogen stores, especially after intense or long training sessions.

content: Central to causing training adaptations in the body is the interaction of training intensity and duration. Generally, the higher the exercise intensity, the shorter the exercise duration. High-intensity aerobic exercise increases cardiovascular and respiratory function and allows for improved oxygen delivery to the working muscles. Increasing exercise intensity may also benefit skeletal muscle adaptations by affecting muscle fiber recruitment. As exercise intensity is increased, greater recruitment of Type II muscle fibers occurs to meet the increased power needs. This training stimulus allows those fibers to become more aerobically trained, thereby possibly improving overall aerobic performance.
summary: Training adaptations are influenced by the interplay of intensity and duration. Higher intensity generally leads to shorter durations and can improve cardiovascular and muscular adaptations.

content: The regulation of exercise intensity is critical to the success of each training session and ultimately the entire program. An exercise intensity that is too low does not overload the body’s systems and induce the desired physiological adaptations. An intensity that is too high results in fatigue and a premature end to the training session. The most accurate methods for regulating exercise intensity are to monitor oxygen consumption during exercise to determine its percentage of V.O2max or to periodically measure the blood lactate concentration to determine the relationship to the lactate threshold.
summary: Proper exercise intensity regulation is crucial for training success. Intensity that is too low or too high can hinder progress.

content: Until recently, obtaining accurate maximal oxygen uptake values required access to a laboratory. At least one company has introduced a gas analyzer system designed for fitness facilities. To date, the system has not been tested for validity or reliability, but the concept is promising. If V.O2max testing is not available, exercise prescriptions can use heart rate, ratings of perceived exertion, metabolic equivalents, or exercise velocity to monitor exercise intensity.
summary: V.O2max testing is the most accurate method for determining exercise intensity, but alternative methods exist when laboratory testing is unavailable.

content: Cycling power– measuring devices are frequently used by professional and top-level amateur competitors. Heart rate is likely the most frequently used method for prescribing aerobic exercise intensity. The reason is the close relationship between heart rate and oxygen consumption, especially when the intensity is between 50% and 90% of functional capacity (V.O2max), also called heart rate reserve (HRR), which is the difference between an athlete’s maximal heart rate and his or her resting heart rate. The most accurate means of regulating intensity using this method is to determine the specific heart rate associated with the desired percentage of V.O2max or the heart rate associated with the lactate threshold. For the greatest precision, this necessitates laboratory testing to identify these exercise intensities.
summary: Heart rate is a common method for prescribing aerobic exercise intensity due to its close relationship with oxygen consumption. Precise regulation requires laboratory testing to determine target heart rates.

content: If laboratory testing is unavailable, then the individual’s age-predicted (estimated) maximal heart rate (APMHR) can be used as the basis for determining exercise intensity. The relationship between V.O2max, HRR, and MHR is shown in table 18.1. Although the Karvonen and percentage of maximal heart rate formulas provide practical intensity assignments, basing them on age-predicted maximal heart rates may entail some inaccuracies (vs. laboratory-tested maximal heart rates) when exercise intensity is being monitored during cycling or running. It has been determined that age contributes 75% of the variability of heart rate; the effects of other factors such as mode of exercise and fitness level must also be considered with the use of heart rate to monitor intensity. Additionally, using estimations of exercise intensity via estimated maximal heart rate equations provides no information about the intensity associated with the lactate threshold. Without some knowledge of an athlete’s lactate threshold, a highly effective aerobic endurance training program cannot be developed.
summary: When laboratory testing is unavailable, age-predicted maximal heart rate can be used for exercise intensity prescription. However, this method may be less accurate than laboratory-based methods, and it does not provide information about the lactate threshold.

content: Ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) can also be used to regulate intensity during aerobic endurance training. The 15-point Borg scale (6) is typically used for RPE, although the category-ratio scale could also be used (7). Laboratory testing is required to ensure accuracy between specific exercise intensities and an RPE scale. RPE can be used to accurately regulate intensity when there are changes in fitness level. The RPE–intensity relationship can be influenced by various external environmental factors such as passive distracters and environmental temperature.
summary: Ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) can be used to regulate exercise intensity, but laboratory testing is needed to establish the relationship between RPE and specific intensities. RPE can be influenced by factors like fitness level and environmental conditions.

content: One MET is equal to 3.5 ml · kg–1 · min–1 of oxygen consumption and is considered the amount of oxygen required by the body at rest. Metabolic equivalent values have been determined for a variety of physical activities. An activity with a MET value of 10.0 requires 10 times the oxygen uptake that is required by an individual at rest. Assigning MET values as part of an aerobic exercise prescription requires the strength and conditioning professional to know (or estimate) an athlete’s maximal oxygen uptake in order to be able to calculate an exercise MET level.
summary: Metabolic equivalents (METs) quantify the oxygen consumption of various activities relative to rest. Exercise prescriptions using METs require knowledge of an athlete's maximal oxygen uptake.

content: Cyclists may use power-measuring cranks and hubs to monitor exercise intensity. The 15-point Borg scale ranges from 6, 'No exertion at all', to 20, 'Maximal exertion'.
summary: Cyclists can use power meters to monitor intensity, and the 15-point Borg scale is a common method for subjective intensity rating.

content: Lying or sitting quietly, doing nothing, or lying in bed awake has a metabolic equivalent (MET) of 1.0. Walking at less than 2 miles per hour (<3.2 km/h) on a level surface has a MET of 2.0. Walking at 2 miles per hour (3.2 km/h) on a level surface has a MET of 2.5.
summary: Basic resting and low-intensity walking MET values.

content: Walking at 2.5 miles per hour (4 km/h) has a MET of 3.0. Walking at 3 miles per hour (4.8 km/h) on a level surface has a MET of 3.3. Walking at 3.5 miles per hour (5.6 km/h) on a level surface has a MET of 3.8. Walking at 4 miles per hour (6.4 km/h) on a level surface has a MET of 5.0. Walking at 4.5 miles per hour (7.2 km/h) on a level surface has a MET of 6.3. Walking at 5 miles per hour (8.0 km/h) has a MET of 8.0.
summary: MET values for various walking speeds.

content: Resistance training using free weights, Nautilus, or Universal equipment at a light or moderate effort has a MET of 3.0. Stationary cycling at 50 W with a very light effort has a MET of 3.0. Calisthenics, home exercise, at a light or moderate effort has a MET of 3.5. Stair stepping with a 4-inch (10 cm) step height at 20 steps per minute has a MET of 3.5.
summary: MET values for light to moderate intensity resistance training and calisthenics.

content: Water aerobics and water calisthenics have a MET of 4.0. Stair stepping with a 4-inch (10 cm) step height at 30 steps per minute has a MET of 4.8. Aerobic dance at a low impact level has a MET of 5.0. Stationary cycling at 100 W with a light effort has a MET of 5.5.
summary: MET values for water-based activities and low-impact aerobic dance.

content: Outdoor cycling at 10 to 11.9 miles per hour (16.1-19.2 km/h) has a MET of 6.0. Resistance training using free weights, Nautilus, or Universal equipment at a vigorous effort has a MET of 6.0. Stair stepping with a 12-inch (31 cm) step height at 20 steps per minute has a MET of 6.3.
summary: MET values for moderate to vigorous intensity cycling and resistance training.

content: Stair stepping with an 8-inch (20 cm) step height at 30 steps per minute has a MET of 6.9. Aerobic dance at a high impact level has a MET of 7.0. Stationary cycling at 150 W with a moderate effort has a MET of 7.0. Swimming laps freestyle at a slow, moderate, or light effort has a MET of 7.0.
summary: MET values for high-intensity aerobic dance and cycling.

content: Calisthenics (e.g., push-ups, sit-ups, pull-ups, jumping jacks) at a vigorous effort has a MET of 8.0. Circuit training including some aerobic stations with minimal rest has a MET of 8.0. Outdoor cycling at 10 to 11.9 miles per hour (16.1-19.2 km/h) has a MET of 8.0.
summary: MET values for high-intensity calisthenics and circuit training.

content: Step aerobics with a 6- to 8-inch (15-20 cm) step has a MET of 8.5. Running at 5.2 miles per hour (8.4 km/h) has a MET of 9.0. Stair stepping with a 12-inch (31 cm) step height at 30 steps per minute has a MET of 9.0.
summary: MET values for high-impact step aerobics and running.

content: Outdoor cycling at 14 to 15.9 miles per hour (22.5-25.6 km/h) has a MET of 10.0. Running at 6 miles per hour (9.7 km/h) has a MET of 10.0. Step aerobics with a 10- to 12-inch (25-31 cm) step has a MET of 10.0.
summary: MET values for high-intensity cycling and running.

content: An activity with a MET value of 10.0 requires 10 times the oxygen uptake that is required by an individual at rest. Metabolic equivalent values have been determined for a variety of physical activities. Assigning MET values as part of an aerobic exercise prescription requires the strength and conditioning professional to know (or estimate) an athlete’s maximal oxygen uptake in order to be able to calculate an exercise MET level.
summary: Definition of MET values and their application in exercise prescription.

content: The 15-point Borg scale ranges from 6, 'No exertion at all', to 20, 'Maximal exertion'. The Category-ratio scale ranges from 0, 'Nothing at all', to 11, 'Extremely strong'. The scales shown are the most recent versions from Borg, 1998 (5). Cyclists may use power-measuring cranks and hubs to monitor exercise intensity.
summary: Examples of subjective exercise intensity scales and tools for monitoring intensity.

content: Running at 5.2 miles per hour (8.4 km/h) has a MET of 9.0. Stair stepping with a 12-inch (31 cm) step height at 30 steps per minute has a MET of 9.0.
summary: MET values for low-intensity activities like running at 5.2 mph and stair stepping.

content: Outdoor cycling at 14 to 15.9 miles per hour (22.5-25.6 km/h) has a MET of 10.0. Running at 6 miles per hour (9.7 km/h) has a MET of 10.0. Step aerobics with a 10- to 12-inch (25-31 cm) step has a MET of 10.0. Swimming laps freestyle at a fast, vigorous effort has a MET of 10.0.
summary: MET values for moderate-intensity activities like cycling, running, step aerobics, and swimming.

content: Stationary cycling at 200 W with a vigorous effort has a MET of 10.5. Running at 6.7 miles per hour (10.8 km/h) has a MET of 11.0. Running at 7 miles per hour (11.3 km/h) has a MET of 11.5.
summary: MET values for moderately vigorous activities like stationary cycling and running.

content: Outdoor cycling at 16 to 19 miles per hour (25.7-30.6 km/h) has a MET of 12.0. Running at 7.5 miles per hour (12.1 km/h) has a MET of 12.5. Stationary cycling at 250 W with a very vigorous effort has a MET of 12.5.
summary: MET values for vigorous activities like cycling and running.

content: Running at 8 miles per hour (12.9 km/h) has a MET of 13.5. Running at 8.5 miles per hour (13.7 km/h) has a MET of 14.0. Running at 9 miles per hour (14.5 km/h) has a MET of 15.0.
summary: MET values for very vigorous running activities.

content: Outdoor cycling at greater than 20 miles per hour (>32.2 km/h) has a MET of 16.0. Running at 10 miles per hour (16.1 km/h) has a MET of 16.0.
summary: MET values for extremely vigorous activities like high-speed cycling and running.

content: Once athletes begin an aerobic endurance exercise program, they need to continue the program to either maintain or advance their aerobic fitness level. Research indicates that aerobic fitness does not decrease for up to five weeks when the intensity of training is maintained and frequency decreases to as few as two times per week (43).
summary: Maintaining aerobic fitness requires continued training, even with reduced frequency.

content: Depending on the goals of the athlete, progression of an aerobic endurance exercise program initially involves increasing the frequency, intensity, and duration of exercise. General recommendations are that individuals always include at least one recovery or active rest day in each week of training.
summary: Aerobic training progression involves increasing frequency, intensity, and duration, with rest days incorporated.

content: Most athletes have the goal of attempting to increase rather than just maintain aerobic fitness. This requires regular progression of the training program. Typically, exercise frequency, intensity, or duration should not increase more than 10% each week (40).
summary: Athletes typically aim to improve aerobic fitness through regular training progression, with gradual increases in frequency, intensity, or duration.

content: At higher levels of fitness, athletes will reach a point where it is not feasible to increase either the frequency or the duration of exercise. When this occurs, progressions in training will occur only through exercise intensity manipulation (40).
summary: At advanced fitness levels, intensity manipulation becomes the primary method for training progression.

content: Exercise progression can manipulate combinations of frequency, intensity, and duration. Progression of training frequency may be limited by constraints such as school and work. It may not be possible for the athlete to incorporate more than one training session each day.
summary: Training progression is influenced by various factors, including time constraints and individual limitations.

content: Training intensity measurement should use the same methods as used in the original exercise intensity prescription. The best method is determined by the equipment available to monitor intensity (heart rate monitor, RPE charts, or machines that provide MET workloads).
summary: Intensity measurement should be consistent with the original prescription and utilize appropriate monitoring tools.

content: Progression of training intensity should be monitored very carefully to avoid overtraining. The duration of each training session will be limited by the same constraints as training frequency.
summary: Careful monitoring of intensity progression is crucial to prevent overtraining, and session duration is also subject to limitations.

content: Athletes who train predominantly outdoors will also be limited by the number of daylight hours, especially in the late fall, winter, and early spring.
summary: Outdoor training is subject to seasonal limitations due to daylight hours.

content: There are several types of aerobic endurance training programs, each with varying frequency, intensity, duration, and progression parameters. Each type incorporates the five design variables and results in regimens created for specific outcomes.
summary: Various aerobic training programs exist, each tailored to specific goals and incorporating different parameters.

content: The intensity for long, slow distance training (LSD) is equivalent to approximately 70% of V.O2max (or Long, slow distance (LSD) training should be performed at an intensity of about 70% of maximum heart rate. The training distance for LSD training should be greater than race distance, or the duration should be at least 30 minutes to 2 hours.
summary: Long, slow distance (LSD) training involves sustained effort at 70% of maximum heart rate or V.O2max, with durations exceeding race distance or at least 30 minutes.

content: The intensity and duration of LSD training is typically characterized as ‘conversation’ exercise.
summary: LSD training intensity allows for conversation, indicating a moderate effort level.

content: Each type incorporates the five design variables and results in regimens created for specific outcomes. The intensity for long, slow distance training (LSD) is equivalent to approximately 70% of V.O2max (or Long, slow distance (LSD) training should be performed at an intensity of about 70% of maximum heart rate. The training distance for LSD training should be greater than race distance, or the duration should be at least 30 minutes to 2 hours. The intensity and duration of LSD training is typically characterized as ‘conversation’ exercise.
summary: This chunk describes the general principles and characteristics of LSD training, including its intensity, duration, and typical effort level.

content: The physiological benefits of LSD training include enhanced cardiovascular and thermoregulatory function. LSD training improves mitochondrial energy production and oxidative capacity of skeletal muscle. LSD training increases the utilization of fat as a fuel. Chronic use of LSD training causes a change in the metabolic characteristics of the involved muscles. LSD training eventually causes a shift of Type IIx fibers to Type I fibers. The increase in fat utilization from LSD training may cause a sparing of muscle glycogen.
summary: This chunk outlines the physiological adaptations and benefits associated with LSD training, focusing on cardiovascular, metabolic, and muscular changes.

content: LSD training should be performed at an intensity lower than the intensity used during competition. Pace/tempo training employs an intensity at or slightly higher than race competition intensity. Pace/tempo training corresponds to the lactate threshold. There are two ways to conduct pace/tempo training: steady and intermittent.
summary: This chunk compares and contrasts LSD and pace/tempo training, highlighting their distinct intensities and purposes.

content: The sample pace/tempo training program for a 50 km cyclist includes a rest day on Sunday. The sample pace/tempo training program for a 50 km cyclist includes a 60-minute LSD ride on Monday. The sample pace/tempo training program for a 50 km cyclist includes a 30-minute pace/tempo ride on Tuesday. The sample pace/tempo training program for a 50 km cyclist includes a 45-minute Fartlek ride on Wednesday. The sample pace/tempo training program for a 50 km cyclist includes a 45-minute easy ride on Thursday. The sample pace/tempo training program for a 50 km cyclist includes a 30-minute pace/tempo ride on Friday. The sample pace/tempo training program for a 50 km cyclist includes a 90-minute LSD ride on Saturday. The two pace/tempo training days in the sample program should be spread out during the week to allow recovery between sessions.
summary: This chunk provides a sample weekly training program for a 50 km cyclist, incorporating LSD, pace/tempo, and Fartlek training.

content: For steady pace/tempo training, exercise duration is shorter than race distance or duration to allow for a higher training intensity. The athlete should cycle at a high intensity or training pace (minutes per mile or per km) during pace/tempo training to simulate race pace. Steady pace/tempo training is continuous training conducted at an intensity equal to the lactate threshold for durations of approximately 20 to 30 minutes.
summary: This chunk describes the characteristics and execution of steady pace/tempo training.

content: The purpose of pace/tempo training is to stress the athlete at a specific intensity and improve energy production from both aerobic and anaerobic metabolism. Intermittent pace/tempo training is also referred to as tempo intervals, cruise intervals, or threshold training. During intermittent pace/tempo training, the intensity is the same as for a steady threshold workout, but the training session consists of a series of shorter intervals with brief recovery periods between work intervals.
summary: This chunk explains the purpose of pace/tempo training and differentiates between steady and intermittent pace/tempo training.

content: During pace/tempo training, it is important to avoid exercising at a higher intensity than the prescribed pace. If the pace/tempo workout seems relatively easy, it is better to increase the distance than to increase the intensity. The primary objective for pace/tempo training is to develop a sense of race pace and enhance the body systems’ ability to sustain exercise at that pace. Pace/tempo training involves the same pattern of muscle fiber recruitment as is required in competition.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on executing pace/tempo training effectively and emphasizes its primary objective.

content: The benefits derived from pace/tempo training include improved running economy and increased lactate threshold.
summary: This chunk lists the key benefits of pace/tempo training.

content: Interval training involves exercise at an intensity close to V.O2max. The work intervals for interval training should last between 3 and 5 minutes, although they can be as short as 30 seconds. The rest intervals for 3- to 5-minute work intervals should be equal to the work interval, thereby keeping the work:rest ratio (W:R) at 1:1.
summary: This chunk defines interval training and specifies the typical duration of work and rest intervals.

content: Interval training permits the athlete to train at intensities close to V.O2max for a greater amount of time than would be possible in a single exercise session at a continuous high intensity. Interval training should not be performed until a firm base of aerobic endurance training has been attained. Interval training is very stressful on the athlete and should be used sparingly.
summary: This chunk explains the benefits and considerations for implementing interval training.

content: Interval training permits the athlete to train at intensities close to V.O2max for a greater amount of time than would be possible in a single exercise session at a continuous high intensity. Interval training should not be performed until a firm base of aerobic endurance training has been attained. Interval training is very stressful on the athlete and should be used sparingly. The benefits derived from interval training include an increased V.O2max and enhanced anaerobic metabolism.
summary: Interval training allows for higher intensity training for longer durations, but should only be implemented after a solid aerobic base and used judiciously due to its stressfulness. It improves V.O2max and anaerobic metabolism.

content: The work intervals for interval training should last between 3 and 5 minutes, although they can be as short as 30 seconds.
