The rest intervals for 3- to 5-minute work intervals should be equal to the work interval, thereby keeping the work:rest ratio (W:R) at 1:1. The sample interval training program for a 10 km runner includes a rest day on Sunday. The sample interval training program for a 10 km runner includes 10 reps of 0.5 km intervals at race pace with a 1:1 W:R ratio on Monday. The sample interval training program for a 10 km runner includes a 10 km easy run on Tuesday. The sample interval training program for a 10 km runner includes a 45-minute LSD run on Wednesday. The sample interval training program for a 10 km runner includes 5 reps of 1 km intervals at race pace with a 1:1 W:R ratio on Thursday. The sample interval training program for a 10 km runner includes a 45-minute LSD run on Friday. The sample interval training program for a 10 km runner includes a 45-minute Fartlek run on a flat course on Saturday. Because the interval runs in the sample program are stressful, the two training days should be spread out during the week to allow recovery between sessions.
summary: Interval training for a 10km runner involves specific work intervals, rest periods, and a weekly schedule that includes easy runs, LSD runs, and Fartlek runs, with rest days strategically placed to allow recovery.

content: Repetition training, or REPS, is conducted at intensities greater than V.O2max, with the work intervals typically lasting between 30 and 90 seconds. Due to the high reliance on anaerobic metabolism, long recovery periods are needed between repetition training sessions. The recovery periods for repetition training are approximately four to six times as long as the work intervals, resulting in a work:rest ratio of approximately 1:6. The ratio of REPS training to other types of training is approximately 1:5. Benefits of REPS training include improved running speed, enhanced running economy, and an increased capacity for and tolerance of anaerobic metabolism. REPS training is also beneficial for the final kick or push of an aerobic endurance race.
summary: REPS training involves high-intensity work intervals followed by long recovery periods, focusing on anaerobic metabolism and improving speed, economy, and race-endurance.

content: Fartlek training is a combination of several types of training. Fartlek training is generally associated with running, but it can also be used for cycling and swimming. A sample Fartlek run involves easy running (~70% V.O2max) combined with either hill work or short, fast bursts of running (~85-90% V.O2max) for short time periods. Athletes can apply the basic format of Fartlek training to cycling and swimming by combining long, slow distance training, pace/tempo training, and interval training. Fartlek training challenges all systems of the body and may help reduce the boredom and monotony associated with daily training. Fartlek training is likely to enhance V.O2max, increase the lactate threshold, and improve running economy and fuel utilization.
summary: Fartlek training is a versatile approach incorporating various training types, adaptable to running, cycling, and swimming. It involves alternating between easy and high-intensity efforts, benefiting overall fitness, V.O2max, and lactate threshold.

content: A sound training program should incorporate all types of training into the athlete’s weekly, monthly, and yearly training schedule. The training year is typically divided into phases that include the off-season (sometimes called base training), preseason, in-season (sport competition), and postseason (active rest). The priority in off-season training is to develop a base of cardiorespiratory fitness. Initially, the training program should be composed of long-duration and low-intensity workouts. As the off-season continues, intensity and, to a lesser extent, duration are increased. The increase in training duration should not be more than 5% to 10% per week (76). Increasing the training duration too much can actually lead to decreases in aerobic endurance performance (19). Periodic increases in exercise intensity occur when an athlete has adapted to the training stimulus and requires additional overload for continued improvements. During the preseason, the athlete should focus on increasing intensity, maintaining or reducing duration, and incorporating all types of training into the program. The strengths and weaknesses of the individual athlete should determine the amount and frequency of each type of training. The in-season training program needs to be designed to include competition or race days in the training schedule. Low-intensity and short-duration training days should precede scheduled competitions so that the athlete is fully recovered and rested.
summary: A comprehensive training program should encompass various training types throughout the year, progressing from base building in the off-season to competition-focused training during the in-season. It should be individualized based on the athlete's strengths and weaknesses, with adequate rest and recovery incorporated.

content: Periodic increases in exercise intensity occur when an athlete has adapted to the training stimulus and requires additional overload for continued improvements. A sound year-round aerobic endurance training program should be divided into sport seasons with specific goals and objectives designed to improve performance gradually and progressively. During the preseason, the athlete should focus on increasing intensity, maintaining or reducing duration, and incorporating all types of training into the program. The types of training employed during the in-season are based on the continued goal of improving weaknesses and maintaining strengths of the athlete. The in-season training program needs to be designed to include competition or race days in the training schedule. Low-intensity and short-duration training days should precede scheduled competitions so that the athlete is fully recovered and rested.
summary: This chunk describes the periodization of training for aerobic endurance athletes, outlining the key phases (preseason, in-season) and adjustments to training intensity and duration.

content: During the postseason, the main focus should be on recovering from the previous competitive season. Low training duration and intensity are typical for this active rest phase, but enough overall exercise or activity should be performed to maintain a sufficient level of cardiorespiratory fitness, muscular strength, and lean body mass. During the postseason, the aerobic endurance athlete should focus on rehabilitating injuries incurred during the competitive season and improving the strength of weak or underconditioned muscle groups.
summary: This chunk details the postseason training phase, emphasizing recovery, active rest, and addressing any lingering injuries or weaknesses.

content: Detraining occurs when the athlete reduces the training duration or intensity or stops training altogether due to a break in the training program, injury, or illness. In the absence of an appropriate training stimulus, the athlete experiences a loss of the physiological adaptations brought about by training. It has been demonstrated that most of the physiological adaptations attained with training regress rapidly toward pretraining levels when the training stimulus is removed. To avoid some of the effects of detraining, the use of other training modes may be beneficial; however, cross-training may only attenuate some of the loss of physiological adaptation normally seen during complete cessation of training. Aerobic endurance athletes can minimize the effects of detraining by continuing to use their primary mode of exercise at reduced frequency and intensity, if possible.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of detraining, its consequences, and strategies to mitigate its effects, particularly for aerobic endurance athletes.

content: Tapering is an important component of the training program as aerobic endurance athletes prepare for major competition. Tapering involves the systematic reduction of training duration and intensity, combined with an increased emphasis on technique work and nutritional intervention. The objective of tapering the training regimen is to attain peak performance at the time of competition. Although most of the available research on tapering has been conducted on swimmers, the use of tapering is not limited solely to these aerobic endurance athletes. Runners and cyclists would likely benefit from some type of tapering regimen prior to competition to allow for achieving adequate recovery, rehydration, and increased muscle and liver glycogen stores.
summary: This chunk focuses on the tapering strategy used by aerobic endurance athletes to optimize performance for competitions.

content: Cross-training is a mode of training that can be used to maintain general conditioning in athletes during periods of reduced training due to injury or during recovery from a training cycle. Cross-training may reduce the likelihood of overuse injuries because it distributes the physical stress of training to different muscle groups than those used during training. Multiple-event athletes also use cross-training to maximize performance in swimming, cycling, and running. The benefits derived from cross-training include adaptations of the respiratory, cardiovascular, and musculoskeletal systems. To be effective in maintaining V.O2max, cross-training must be equal in intensity and duration to the athlete’s primary mode of exercise; cross-training will not improve single-event performance to the same magnitude as mode-specific training only.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of cross-training, its benefits, limitations, and how it can be used effectively by athletes.

content: Resistance training is an important but often overlooked factor in improving performance in aerobic endurance athletes. Of particular importance, Hickson and colleagues demonstrated that although the V.O2max of highly trained aerobic athletes did not improve from resistance training, there was an improvement in short-term exercise performance during both cycling and running. Benefits that aerobic endurance athletes may obtain from performing resistance training include faster recovery from injuries, prevention of overuse injuries, and reduction of muscle imbalances. Increased strength is important for various aspects of aerobic endurance competition, including hill climbing, bridging gaps between competitors during breakaways from groups, and the final sprint.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of resistance training for aerobic endurance athletes, outlining its benefits for performance and injury prevention.

content: Aerobic endurance exercise training programs should be developed in conjunction with periodic performance assessment and structured to enhance the strengths and improve the weaknesses of the athlete. A combination of various training types should be used in aerobic endurance exercise training programs so that all physiological systems involved in successful performance are overloaded and challenged to respond with positive adaptations. Training programs should be developed far enough in advance and with enough structure to ensure enhancement of performance, but with enough flexibility to avoid overuse injuries and overtraining. Activity-specific training results in the best adaptations to training and ultimately the most improvement in performance. The training program should be developed far enough in advance and with enough structure to ensure enhancement of performance, but with enough flexibility to avoid overuse injuries and overtraining.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of individualized training programs, incorporating various training types, and balancing structure with flexibility to optimize performance and prevent injuries.

content: Strength and conditioning programs bring about significant improvements in strength. Eventually, physical adaptations and psychological adjustments will occur less frequently. Athletes may experience performance plateaus or decrements. There may be an increased risk of injury and other symptoms associated with overtraining. To promote long-term training and performance improvements, the strength and conditioning professional should include preplanned, systematic variations in training specificity, intensity, and volume organized in periods or cycles within an overall program.
summary: This chunk discusses the long-term effects of strength and conditioning programs, potential challenges like plateaus and overtraining, and the need for planned variations in training to ensure continued progress.

content: Strength and conditioning programs bring about significant improvements in strength. To promote long-term training and performance improvements, the strength and conditioning professional should include preplanned, systematic variations in training specificity, intensity, and volume organized in periods or cycles within an overall program. This program design strategy is called periodization.
summary: Strength training programs lead to strength gains, and periodization is a key strategy for long-term progress by systematically varying training variables.

content: The concept of periodization was proposed in the 1960s by Russian physiologist Leo Matveyev. American exercise scientists modified Matveyev’s work with special application to training strength and power athletes. This chapter discusses the concept of periodization as it applies to a strength and conditioning program.
summary: Periodization, initially proposed by Matveyev, has been adapted for strength and conditioning programs.

content: A periodized training year can be divided into smaller blocks of time, each with its own goals and priorities. This overall schedule can encompass all aspects of an athlete’s program, including general conditioning, sport-specific activities, and resistance training.
summary: Periodization involves dividing a training year into blocks with specific goals, covering all aspects of an athlete's program.

content: The manner in which the human body reacts to stress was described by Canadian biologist and endocrinologist Hans Selye as the General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS). Selye delineated a three-stage response to stress: alarm, resistance, and exhaustion.
summary: Hans Selye's General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) describes the body's three-stage response to stress: alarm, resistance, and exhaustion.

content: When the body experiences a new stress or a more intense stress than previously applied, the first response is the shock or alarm phase. This phase may last several days or several weeks, during which the athlete may experience excessive soreness, stiffness, and a temporary drop in performance.
summary: The initial alarm phase of GAS involves shock and temporary performance decline due to stress.

content: Next is the resistance phase, one in which the body adapts to the stimulus and returns to more normal functioning. In this phase, the body is able to demonstrate its ability to withstand the stress. The athlete relies on neurological adaptations to continue training while the muscle tissue adapts by making various biochemical, structural, and mechanical adjustments that lead to increased performance. This phase of adaptation is sometimes called supercompensation.
summary: The resistance phase of GAS sees the body adapting to stress, improving performance through neurological and muscular adaptations.

content: However, if the stress persists for an extended time, the exhaustion phase is reached. Some of the same symptoms experienced during the alarm phase reappear (fatigue, soreness, etc.), and the athlete loses the ability to adapt to the stressor. Staleness, overtraining, and other maladaptations may occur when there is no training variety or when the training stress is too great. Non-training-related stress (e.g., occupational issues, insufficient sleep, poor diet) can contribute to the overall stress level and lead to the exhaustion phase.
summary: Prolonged stress leads to the exhaustion phase, characterized by symptoms similar to the alarm phase, and can result in staleness or overtraining.

content: The planned implementation of the meso- and micro-cycles within an overall macrocycle is the basis for changing (varying) the program design variables. Predominantly, it is the intensity and volume assignments of the training and conditioning programs that are manipulated to the greatest extent.
summary: Periodization involves manipulating intensity and volume within macrocycles, meso-cycles, and micro-cycles.

content: Sport-specific training also involves acquiring and perfecting skill-related techniques, but attention (time) dedicated to these outcomes varies based on their relative importance to the match or game schedule. Therefore, periodization involves shifting training priorities from non-sport-specific activities of high volume and low intensity to sport-specific activities of low volume and high intensity over a period of many weeks to prevent overtraining and optimize performance.
summary: Periodization adjusts training priorities, shifting from general conditioning to sport-specific activities to prevent overtraining and optimize performance.

content: The periodization model and the process of moving through the periodization cycles or phases can also be likened to the sequence and evolution of learning academic concepts and skills from simple to complex.
summary: Periodization can be compared to the gradual progression of learning complex concepts and skills.

content: According to Matveyev (15), the major divisions of training are the preparatory, competition, and transition periods. Later, Stone, O’Bryant, and Garhammer (21) added a “first transition” between the preparatory and competitive periods. Thus, the conventional periodization model includes four distinct periods: preparatory, first transition, competition, and second transition.
summary: The conventional periodization model consists of four periods: preparatory, first transition, competition, and second transition.

content: Intensity begins lower and gradually increases, and volume starts higher and slowly decreases as the athlete becomes conditioned. Not all novice athletes, however, are able to tolerate large changes in these variables (15, 22). Also, because advanced athletes invariably train closer to their abilities and have smaller adaptational windows, their volume and intensity assignments are consistently higher, as seen in figure 19.3 (7, 11, 20, 22).
summary: Intensity increases and volume decreases throughout periodization, with adjustments made based on athlete experience level.

content: This modification of Matveyev’s model reveals a shift from lower intensities and higher volumes earlier in the preparation period to higher intensities and lower volumes in the competition period, but the fluctuations are smaller and occur in the upper end of their values.
summary: Periodization involves shifting from lower intensities and higher volumes in the preparatory period to higher intensities and lower volumes in the competition period.

content: The initial preparatory period is usually the longest and occurs during the time of the year when there are no competitions and only a limited number of sport-specific skill practices or game strategy sessions. The major emphasis of this period is establishing a base level of conditioning to increase the athlete’s tolerance for more intense training.
summary: The preparatory period focuses on building a conditioning base before competition.

content: Conditioning activities begin at relatively low intensities and high volumes: long, slow distance running or swimming; low-intensity plyometrics; and high-repetition resistance training with light to moderate resistances. Because high-volume training causes significant fatigue and can involve large time commitments, the athlete is not exposed to optimal conditions (i.e., does not have enough time or energy) for improving sport-specific technique. As a result, technique training is not a high priority during this period (4, 5, 6).
summary: The preparatory period emphasizes high-volume, low-intensity conditioning activities, with less focus on sport-specific technique.

content: The initial preparatory period is usually the longest and occurs during the time of the year when there are no competitions and only a limited number of sport-specific skill practices or game strategy sessions. The major emphasis of this period is establishing a base level of conditioning to increase the athlete’s tolerance for more intense training. Conditioning activities begin at relatively low intensities and high volumes: long, slow distance running or swimming; low-intensity plyometrics; and high-repetition resistance training with light to moderate resistances. Because high-volume training causes significant fatigue and can involve large time commitments, the athlete is not exposed to optimal conditions (i.e., does not have enough time or energy) for improving sport-specific technique. As a result, technique training is not a high priority during this period (4, 5, 6).
summary: The initial preparatory period focuses on building a base level of conditioning through low-intensity, high-volume training, prioritizing endurance development over technique refinement.

content: Periodization is a training strategy. Matveyev's original periodization model involves adjusting training intensity, decreasing training volume, and focusing more on sport technique training. Matveyev's model was modified to include three phases within the preparatory period: hypertrophy/endurance, basic strength, and strength/power.
summary: Matveyev's periodization model structures training into phases, progressively increasing intensity and decreasing volume while emphasizing technique development.

content: The hypertrophy/endurance phase occurs during the early stages of the preparatory period and lasts from one to six weeks. The goals of the hypertrophy/endurance phase are to increase lean body mass, develop muscular and metabolic endurance, or both. Initially, sport conditioning activities in the hypertrophy/endurance phase may not be specific to the athletic event. As the preparatory period progresses, training activities become more specific to the sport.
summary: The hypertrophy/endurance phase of the preparatory period focuses on building muscle mass and endurance through general conditioning activities, gradually transitioning to sport-specific exercises.

content: The athlete may also handle very low to moderate loads for many repetitions in a resistance training program during the hypertrophy/endurance phase. The hypertrophy/endurance phase involves very low to moderate intensity (50-75% of the 1-repetition maximum [1RM]) and very high to moderate volume (three to six sets of 10-20 repetitions).
summary: The hypertrophy/endurance phase utilizes low to moderate weights and high repetitions to promote muscle growth and endurance.

content: The basic strength phase aims to increase the strength of muscles essential to the primary sport movements. The sprinter’s running program in the basic strength phase progresses to include interval sprints of moderate distances and more complex and specific exercises. The resistance training program becomes more specific to the sport (e.g., squats and lunges with free weights) and involves heavier loads for fewer repetitions than the hypertrophy/endurance phase. The basic strength phase involves high intensity (80-90% of the 1RM) and moderate volume (three to five sets of four to eight repetitions).
summary: The basic strength phase focuses on developing strength specific to the sport through heavier weights, fewer repetitions, and more sport-specific exercises.

content: The last stage of the preparatory period is the strength/power phase. Here, the sprinter’s interval and speed training intensifies to near competitive pace, speed training drills are performed (e.g., sled towing, sprints against resistance, and uphill and downhill sprints), plyometric drills mimic sprinting, and the resistance training program involves performing power/explosive exercises at high loads and low volumes. The load assignments for power exercises do not follow the typical %1RM–repetition relationship, but their relative intensities are still elevated during this phase. The strength/power phase involves high intensity (75-95% of the 1RM, depending on the exercise) and low volume (three to five sets of two to five repetitions).
summary: The strength/power phase emphasizes explosive strength and speed development through high-intensity, low-volume training and sport-specific drills.

content: Often, this period provides one week of lower intensity, lower volume, or a combination of both before the beginning of the competition period. The goal for the competition period is to peak strength and power through further increases in training intensity with additional decreases in training volume. Also, practice in skill technique and game strategy increases dramatically as time spent in physical conditioning decreases proportionately.
summary: The competition period focuses on peaking performance through high-intensity, low-volume training and increased emphasis on skill development and game strategy.

content: For example, a sprinter places even more emphasis on speed, reaction time, sprinting-specific plyometric drills, and technique training, much of which is gained through actual competitions. The competition period may last from one to three weeks; but for most organized sports, this period spans the entire competitive season and may last for many months.
summary: During the competition period, athletes prioritize sport-specific skills and competition preparation, with training intensity and volume adjusted accordingly.

content: This prolonged time requires some manipulation of the intensity on a weekly or microcycle basis, but in general the period is characterized by very high-intensity and very low-volume training activities. Typically, this mesocycle will place the athlete in peak condition for only about three weeks; trying to overextend this will inevitably result in overtraining. For sports with multiple major contests spread across multiple weeks or months, the goal is to preserve strength, power, and performance levels by following a maintenance program of moderate intensities and moderate volumes.
summary: The competition period requires careful management of training intensity and volume to maintain peak performance throughout the season.

content: The competition period includes peaking and maintenance. For peaking, athletes use very high intensity (≥93% of the 1RM) and very low volume (one to three sets of one to three repetitions). For maintenance, athletes use moderate intensity (≈ 80-85% of the 1RM) and moderate volume (about two to three sets of about six to eight repetitions).
summary: The competition period encompasses both peaking phases with high intensity and low volume, and maintenance phases with moderate intensity and volume.

content: Between the competitive season and the next macrocycle’s preparatory period is the second transition period. This period, commonly referred to as active rest or restoration, lasts one to four weeks and focuses on unstructured, non-sport-specific recreational activities performed at low intensities with low volumes. Care should be taken to avoid excessively aggressive training immediately after peaking or after a prolonged competitive season. It is important to the athlete’s long-term progress to allow time to rehabilitate any injuries and to rest, physically and mentally. For example, a sprinter may engage in recreational activities such as volleyball, racket sports, and swimming in a leisurely manner and perform low-volume, non-sport-specific resistance training with light loads.
summary: The transition period after competition focuses on active rest and recovery through low-intensity, unstructured activities to prevent overtraining and promote long-term progress.

content: Between the competitive season and the next macrocycle’s preparatory period is the second transition period. This period, commonly referred to as active rest or restoration, lasts one to four weeks and focuses on unstructured, non-sport-specific recreational activities performed at low intensities with low volumes. Care should be taken to avoid excessively aggressive training immediately after peaking or after a prolonged competitive season. It is important to the athlete’s long-term progress to allow time to rehabilitate any injuries and to rest, physically and mentally.
summary: The second transition period, also known as active rest, involves low-intensity, non-sport-specific activities for one to four weeks after the competitive season to promote recovery and prevent overtraining.

content: Periodization involves manipulating training intensity and volume while being respectful of the seasonal demands of a particular sport and athlete. Most scholastic, intercollegiate, and professional sports have an annual schedule that consists of off-season, preseason, in-season, and postseason mesocycles. The off-season is the period between the postseason and six weeks prior to the first contest of the next year’s season. The off-season can be divided into multiple shorter mesocycles if it is overly long (e.g., 16 to 24 weeks). The preseason period occurs next, leads up to the first contest, and commonly contains the late stages of the preparatory period and the first transition period. The competition, or in-season period, contains all the contests scheduled for that year, including any tournament games. Most sports have long in-seasons that may necessitate multiple mesocycles arranged around the most important contests. A long competition period (12-16+ weeks) presents some unique challenges in terms of designing a training program. One solution is to divide the competitive period into multiple three- or four-week mesocycles that peak the athlete’s performance for the most critical contests. The other approach is to design a maintenance program consisting of moderate intensities and low-to-moderate volumes.
summary: Periodization structures training into distinct phases (off-season, preseason, in-season, postseason) with varying intensities and volumes to optimize performance and recovery throughout the year.

content: After the final contest, the postseason or second transition period provides active or relative rest for the athlete before the start of the next year’s off-season or preparatory period. Shorter active rest periods could be used throughout the training macrocycle, not just during the postseason. After each mesocycle, week-long microcycles of relative rest (i.e., low-intensity and low-volume training) can be scheduled prior to the beginning of the next mesocycle.
summary: Active rest periods, both short-term (microcycles) and long-term (postseason), are crucial for recovery and preventing overtraining throughout the training year.

content: The traditional resistance training periodization model is often referred to as linear due to the gradual, progressive mesocycle increases in intensity over time. An alternative to this model involves large daily (i.e., within the week or microcycle) fluctuations in the load and volume assignments for core exercises. This type of periodization model is termed undulating or nonlinear. An undulating program may require an athlete to perform four sets with a 6RM load on the first training day of the week (e.g., Tuesday), three sets with a 10RM load on the next training day (Thursday), and five sets with a 3RM load on the last training day (Saturday). The linear model may have the athlete perform four sets with a 6RM load on the heavy training day with fluctuations in the loads on the other training days (i.e., a “heavy-light-medium” approach), but with no changes in training volume (i.e., four sets of six repetitions are performed each training day).
summary: Two main resistance training periodization models exist: linear, with gradual intensity increases, and undulating, with daily fluctuations in load and volume.

content: Some research studies have shown that the undulating model is more effective in improving strength than the linear model (13, 18, 23). Other evidence suggests that there is no difference in strength improvement between the undulating and linear models (2). An underlying factor that appears to support the undulating model is the absence of accumulated neural fatigue caused by the extended, ever-increasing training intensities common to the linear model (12). Undulating periodization programs have application to sports that have many in-season contests and therefore do not train to peak strength and power. A concern with undulating periodization is that the high relative levels of daily intensity would more quickly result in an overtrained state in an athlete with a higher training status.
summary: Research on the effectiveness of undulating vs. linear periodization for strength gains is mixed, with some studies favoring undulating due to reduced neural fatigue, but potential for overtraining in highly trained athletes.

content: Periodization by its very nature should be undulating with respect to regular fluctuations in microcycle volume and load and linear with respect to the long-term nature of fitness goals (17). An example of one approach to designing a full macrocycle spanning all four sport seasons is described on pages 515 through 521. The program is based on the preseason resistance training program for scenario A from chapter 15, “Resistance Training,” which focuses on a female college basketball center. Background and initial testing information about this athlete is provided on pages 383 and 385. Additional examples of periodized programs have been described in the literature (8, 20, 25). The macrocycle example provided in this chapter begins where the preseason program from chapter 15 left off and shows a continuation of the training program through the in-season, postseason, and the following year’s off-season. The primary focus of the macrocycle example is the resistance training component of scenario A. Although other modes of training are described briefly (e.g., plyometrics, anaerobic conditioning, aerobic endurance), this macrocycle example is not intended to illustrate every aspect or variation of a strength and conditioning program for basketball. Although this macrocycle example is divided into four sport seasons (each one is a mesocycle) that are made up of many two- to four-week microcycles, an alternative, valid periodization model denotes each season as a macrocycle that is divided into multiple mesocycles. After one or two unloading weeks following the off-season, the athlete begins the preseason period of training. For this example, this period covers about 3 1/2 months (mid-August until the first game, possibly mid-November).
summary: This text provides a detailed example of a full macrocycle periodization plan for a female college basketball center, outlining the training progression across all four seasons and emphasizing the importance of both linear and undulating periodization principles.

content: The primary focus of the macrocycle example is the resistance training component of scenario A. Although other modes of training are described briefly (e.g., plyometrics, anaerobic conditioning, aerobic endurance), this macrocycle example is not intended to illustrate every aspect or variation of a strength and conditioning program for basketball.
summary: This macrocycle example focuses on resistance training for basketball, briefly mentioning other training modes.

content: Although this macrocycle example is divided into four sport seasons (each one is a mesocycle) that are made up of many two- to four-week microcycles, an alternative, valid periodization model denotes each season as a macrocycle that is divided into multiple mesocycles.
summary: This macrocycle example uses a four-season periodization model, but other valid models exist.

content: After one or two unloading weeks following the off-season, the athlete begins the preseason period of training. For this example, this period covers about 3 1/2 months (mid-August until the first game, possibly mid-November). The goals of the preseason are to increase the intensity of sport-specific training and the attention given to basketball drills and skills.
summary: The preseason lasts about 3.5 months and focuses on increasing training intensity and basketball-specific skills.

content: The resistance training portion is planned for three times per week and focuses primarily on strength and power outcomes. Other training modes (e.g., plyometrics and anaerobic conditioning) hold high priority, especially if they directly contribute to basketball training.
summary: Preseason resistance training is three times per week, emphasizing strength and power, with other modes prioritized based on basketball relevance.

content: After an unloading week following the preseason, the athlete is ready to begin the competition period of training. The in-season period typically spans November to April, about 20 weeks (including a four-week tournament period, although it could last longer.) The goals for the in-season are to maintain and possibly improve strength, power, flexibility, and anaerobic conditioning.
summary: The in-season lasts about 20 weeks and aims to maintain and improve various fitness components.

content: Resistance training during the in-season may be limited to 30 minutes, one to three times per week. A typical in-season resistance training program consists of an undulating regime of varying volumes of relatively high intensities. Power and basketball-specific non-power core exercises predominate in the in-season resistance training program, with assistance exercises added for balance.
summary: In-season resistance training is shorter and focuses on power, core exercises, and balance.

content: Plyometric sessions should be alternated with resistance training and conducted once or twice weekly during the in-season. On days when no resistance training is scheduled, 15 to 20 minutes of various short sprint intervals could be performed once or twice weekly during practice. Speed, agility, and other running conditioning can be incorporated into practice time during the in-season. Flexibility training can be part of the practice and game warm-up and cool-down during the in-season.
summary: In-season training includes plyometrics, sprint intervals, speed and agility work, and flexibility training.

content: Two to three days of rest should be afforded between resistance training, plyometrics, and sprint interval sessions, depending on the game schedule. The majority of the athlete’s time during the in-season is spent on skill and strategy development, with the remainder devoted to conditioning.
summary: Rest is crucial between in-season training sessions, with the focus primarily on skill development and strategy.

content: The athlete should be able to maintain their condition and peak again if the team continues in the conference tournament. If the tournament game schedules do not allow for more than one resistance training workout a week, the athlete should perform all of the week’s power and core exercises (if time allows) and omit the assistance exercises.
summary: Athletes should maintain their condition throughout the tournament, adjusting training volume if necessary.

content: The postseason mesocycle is a period of active rest with no formal or structured workouts. The active rest period lasts a month (from April 4 until May 1) after the competitive season. Recreational games and fitness activities during the active rest period include swimming, jogging, circuit weight training, volleyball, racquetball, and informal basketball.
summary: The postseason involves active rest with low-intensity recreational activities.

content: All activities during the active rest period are performed at low intensities with low volumes.
summary: Active rest activities are performed at low intensity and volume.

content: The off-season mesocycle lasts about 14 weeks, from the beginning of May to the beginning of August. The goal of the off-season is to establish a base level of conditioning to increase the athlete’s tolerance for more intense training in later phases and periods.
summary: The off-season lasts 14 weeks and focuses on establishing a conditioning base.

content: Testing should be performed during the first week of the off-season so the strength and conditioning professional can determine initial training loads for the exercises in the first microcycle. In later microcycles, when more or other exercises are added, the training loads can be estimated from loads used in similar exercises or can be determined directly from RM testing.
summary: Testing is conducted at the beginning of the off-season to determine initial training loads and is repeated later to assess progress.

content: Other performance tests related to basketball include shoulder and trunk and hip flexibility tests, 12-minute run, 300-yard (274 m) shuttle run, line drill, T-test, vertical jump, and skinfold measurements.
summary: Various performance tests are used to assess basketball-specific abilities.

content: Testing should also be performed at the end of the off-season to evaluate the program, to chart improvements, to discern areas requiring additional training and attention, and to provide foundational information for designing the preseason program.
summary: End-of-offseason testing evaluates program effectiveness and informs preseason planning.

content: The resistance training component holds higher priority in the off-season. An athlete may follow a split routine of four or more training days per week during the off-season.
summary: Off-season training prioritizes resistance training, often with a split routine of 4+ days per week.

content: The basketball player begins the off-season with an all-body, three-times-per-week regime but soon progresses to an upper body–lower body split program consisting of four training days per week (two heavy and two light). These advancements also involve a gradual increase in loading, with an associated decrease in training volume.
summary: Off-season training progresses from a full-body routine to a split routine with increasing loading and decreasing volume.

content: Other training during the off-season includes aerobic endurance exercise to maintain or improve body composition and cardiovascular fitness. These conditioning workouts are scheduled on non-resistance training days.
summary: Off-season training includes aerobic endurance exercise on non-resistance training days.

content: Flexibility training can be emphasized in the warm-up and cool-down portions of each training session during the off-season.
summary: Flexibility training is emphasized during off-season warm-ups and cool-downs.

content: The In-Season Mesocycle for a female college basketball center lasts 5 months (20 weeks) from November 21 to April 3, including tournament play.
summary: The in-season mesocycle for a female college basketball center lasts 5 months.

content: The In-Season Mesocycle for a female college basketball center lasts 5 months (20 weeks) from November 21 to April 3, including tournament play. The In-Season Mesocycle begins after an unloading week from November 15 to 21.
summary: The In-Season Mesocycle for a female college basketball center lasts 5 months and begins after an unloading week.

content: Microcycle 1 lasts 4 weeks from November 22 to December 19 with an unloading week from December 20 to 26. Weekly workout #1 in Microcycle 1 consists of High pull (1 set of 3 reps at 80% 1RM), Back squat (1 set of 3 reps at 6RM), Incline bench press (1 set of 3 reps at 6RM), Lateral shoulder raise (1 set of 3 reps at 10RM), and Seated low-pulley row (1 set of 3 reps at 12RM). Weekly workout #2 in Microcycle 1 consists of Push press (1 set of 3 reps at 75% 1RM), Hip sled (1 set of 3 reps at 10RM), Close-grip decline bench press (1 set of 3 reps at 12RM), Abdominal crunch (maximum repetitions in 60 seconds), and Supine leg raise (maximum repetitions in 60 seconds).
summary: Microcycle 1 of the In-Season Mesocycle lasts 4 weeks and includes two weekly workouts with specific exercises and rep schemes.

content: Microcycle 2 lasts 4 weeks from December 27 to January 23 with an unloading week from January 24 to 30. Weekly workout #1 in Microcycle 2 consists of Hang clean (1 set of 3 reps at 85% 1RM), Front squat (1 set of 3 reps at 5RM), Standing shoulder press (1 set of 3 reps at 5RM), and Close-grip bench press (1 set of 3 reps at 12RM). Weekly workout #2 in Microcycle 2 consists of Push jerk (1 set of 3 reps at 77% 1RM), Step-up (1 set of 3 reps at 8RM), Lying triceps extension (1 set of 3 reps at 10RM), Lat pulldown (1 set of 3 reps at 12RM), Abdominal crunch (maximum repetitions in 60 seconds), and Supine leg raise (maximum repetitions in 60 seconds).
summary: Microcycle 2 of the In-Season Mesocycle lasts 4 weeks and includes two weekly workouts with specific exercises and rep schemes.

content: Microcycle 3 lasts 4 weeks from January 31 to February 27 with an unloading week from February 28 to March 6. Weekly workout #1 in Microcycle 3 consists of Power clean (1 set of 3 reps at 90% 1RM), Hang snatch (1 set of 3 reps at 80% 1RM), Back squat (1 set of 3 reps at 4RM), and Forward step lunge (1 set of 3 reps at 6RM). Weekly workout #2 in Microcycle 3 consists of Bench press (1 set of 3 reps at 4RM), Close-grip decline bench press (1 set of 3 reps at 10RM), Barbell biceps curl (1 set of 3 reps at 8RM), Upright row (1 set of 3 reps at 10RM), Abdominal crunch (maximum repetitions in 60 seconds), and Supine leg raise (maximum repetitions in 60 seconds).
summary: Microcycle 3 of the In-Season Mesocycle lasts 4 weeks and includes two weekly workouts with specific exercises and rep schemes.

content: The Tournament Microcycle lasts 4 weeks from March 7 to April 3. During the Tournament Microcycle, athletes return to Microcycle 2 and progress to Microcycle 3 if tournament play extends beyond four weeks.
summary: The Tournament Microcycle lasts 4 weeks and athletes return to Microcycle 2, progressing to Microcycle 3 if needed.

content: The in-season program incorporates an undulating, nonlinear periodization regime—weekly workout #1 involves heavier loads for fewer repetitions, and weekly workout #2 involves lighter loads for more repetitions, but all are performed at their respective repetition maximums where indicated. The volume assignments are represented as sets × 3 repetitions. The athlete’s training loads are determined from testing at the end of the preseason or estimated from previous training cycles. For each workout shown, the number of sets does not include warm-up sets. Refer to table 15.7 on page 394 for the relationship between the %1RM and the number of repetitions allowed (i.e., the RM load). Refer to table 15.12 on page 408 for the rest period length assignments based on the training goal.
summary: The in-season program utilizes an undulating periodization scheme with heavier loads for fewer reps on workout #1 and lighter loads for more reps on workout #2. Training loads are determined from testing or previous cycles.

content: The off-season mesocycle for a female college basketball center lasts 3½ months (14 weeks) from May 2 to August 7. The initial pretesting period is 1 week from May 2 to May 8.
summary: The off-season mesocycle lasts 3½ months and begins with a week of pretesting.

content: Microcycle 1 lasts 2 weeks from May 9 to May 22 and focuses on hypertrophy/endurance. During Week 1 of Microcycle 1, training occurs 3 days a week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday). During Week 2 of Microcycle 1, training occurs 3 days a week (Monday, Wednesday, Friday).
summary: Microcycle 1 of the off-season lasts 2 weeks and focuses on hypertrophy/endurance, with training occurring 3 days a week.

content: In Microcycle 1, the power clean is performed with 2 sets of 3 repetitions at 80% of 5RM. In Microcycle 1, the power clean is performed with 3 sets of 3 repetitions at 85% of 5RM. In Microcycle 1, the hip sled is performed with 2 sets of 3 repetitions at 90% of 10RM. In Microcycle 1, the hip sled is performed with 3 sets of 3 repetitions at 10RM. content: The employment of strength and conditioning professionals continues to increase. Strength and conditioning professionals will expand their role to allow more active participation in the rehabilitation and reconditioning of injured athletes. Strength and conditioning professionals have unique knowledge and insight regarding optimal athletic function. Strength and conditioning professionals can serve a vital role during the final stages of an advanced rehabilitation program by preparing the athlete for a return to competition. Strength and conditioning professionals have distinct responsibilities during rehabilitation from athletic injury.
summary: The growing role of strength and conditioning professionals in athlete rehabilitation and reconditioning.

content: To fully understand how the strength and conditioning professional can best augment the rehabilitation of injured athletes, one must first recognize the role of each member of the sports medicine team. An understanding of different types of injury and the physiological healing process is essential to hastening the recovery from injury. This chapter is not intended to provide the reader with rehabilitation protocols for specific injuries. The aim of this chapter is to explain the physiological events that follow a musculoskeletal injury, thereby allowing optimal goal setting by the strength and conditioning professional to improve injury outcomes. The information contained in this chapter should ultimately be used to maximize function of the injured athlete.
summary: The importance of understanding the sports medicine team's roles and the physiological healing process for optimal rehabilitation.

content: The rehabilitation and reconditioning approach follows five basic principles. All members of the sports medicine team are responsible for educating coaches and athletes regarding injury risks, precautions, and treatments. All members of the sports medicine team must prevent injuries and rehabilitate those athletes who have sustained injury. Several different professionals play important roles in assisting the injured athlete’s return to the playing field, necessitating effective communication.
summary: The collaborative approach to injury prevention, rehabilitation, and athlete return to play.

content: The information contained in this chapter should ultimately be used to maximize function of the injured athlete. All members of the sports medicine team are responsible for educating coaches and athletes regarding injury risks, precautions, and treatments. All members of the sports medicine team must prevent injuries and rehabilitate those athletes who have sustained injury. Several different professionals play important roles in assisting the injured athlete’s return to the playing field, necessitating effective communication.
summary: This chapter focuses on maximizing function for injured athletes, emphasizing the collaborative role of the sports medicine team in injury prevention, rehabilitation, and communication.

content: The rehabilitation and reconditioning approach follows five basic principles.
summary: The rehabilitation process adheres to five fundamental principles.

content: The team physician provides medical care to an organization, school, or team. The team physician may specialize in a variety of fields, including family medicine, pediatrics, and orthopedics. Specific responsibilities of the team physician may include injury prevention, supervision of athletes during games, injury and illness evaluation and diagnosis, and referral to other professionals as needed. Although not responsible for daily rehabilitation, the team physician plays a crucial role in the overall management of the athlete's health and well-being. The team physician makes the final determination of an athlete’s readiness for return to competition (33). Another important role of the team physician is the prescription of medications as needed, including anti-inflammatory, pain, and cold and flu medications.
summary: The team physician provides comprehensive medical care, specializing in injury prevention, diagnosis, and overall athlete well-being, ultimately determining readiness for return to competition.

content: In the United States, an athletic trainer is certified by the National Athletic Trainers’ Association Board of Certification as a Certified Athletic Trainer (ATC). The athletic trainer works under the supervision of the team physician and is employed primarily by secondary schools, colleges, or professional teams but may also work in a sports medicine clinic. Primary responsibilities of the athletic trainer include management and rehabilitation of injuries resulting from physical activity and prevention of injuries with the prescription of sport-specific exercise and the application of prophylactic equipment (e.g., tape and braces). Specifically, the athletic trainer evaluates acute injuries, provides the injured athlete with therapeutic exercise to hasten the rehabilitation process, treats injuries with therapeutic modalities, and serves as an administrator for the sports medicine team (2). Because the athletic trainer has a significant amount of contact with the athlete, he or she plays a key role in promoting communication among members of the sports medicine team, the coach, and the athlete.
summary: The athletic trainer, under the supervision of the team physician, manages and rehabilitates injuries, prevents future injuries, and facilitates communication within the sports medicine team.

content: A physical therapist (or physiotherapist) with a background in orthopedics or sports medicine can play a valuable role in reducing pain and restoring function to the injured athlete. Physical therapists are typically based in rehabilitation clinics and have a close working relationship with a variety of medical specialists. A physical therapist may be consulted for specific treatment strategies or may supervise long-term rehabilitation. In the United States, physical therapists with expertise in sport injury management may become board certified through the American Board of Physical Therapy Specialties (ABPTS) and obtain the Sports Certified Specialist (SCS) credential. These board-certified specialists are becoming more common participants in the evaluation, treatment, and rehabilitation of acutely injured athletes and often serve in the dual capacity of team athletic trainer and sports physical therapist.
summary: Physical therapists, specializing in orthopedics or sports medicine, play a crucial role in pain management, function restoration, and long-term rehabilitation for injured athletes.

content: The strength and conditioning professional plays a valuable role within the sports medicine team and is an integral part of the rehabilitation and reconditioning process. Ideally, this person should be certified by the National Strength and Conditioning Association as a Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialist (CSCS) to ensure that he or she has the knowledge and background to contribute to the rehabilitation process. In consultation with the athletic trainer or sports physical therapist, this professional uses an understanding of the proper technique and application of several types of exercise (e.g., resistance, plyometric, and aerobic exercise) to develop a reconditioning program to ready the injured athlete for return to competition. Furthermore, he or she possesses an extensive understanding of the role that biomechanics play in a wide variety of sports and activities, which may allow him or her to suggest exercises for advanced rehabilitation and reconditioning of many injuries. The strength and conditioning professional must also communicate efficiently and effectively with other members of the sports medicine team. Other members should be aware of the strength and conditioning exercises that the injured athlete is performing, responses to the exercises, and any additional information deemed important by the strength and conditioning professional. The strength and conditioning professional should answer the following questions: What is the athlete doing? How is the athlete progressing? Do any program changes need to be made?
summary: The strength and conditioning professional, ideally a CSCS, plays a vital role in reconditioning injured athletes, collaborating with other team members and tailoring exercise programs for optimal recovery.

content: The sports medicine team often includes specialized members who assist with the postacute rehabilitation and reconditioning of injured athletes. An exercise physiologist has a formal background in the study of the exercise sciences and uses his or her expertise to assist with the design of a conditioning program that carefully considers the body’s metabolic response to exercise and the ways in which that reaction aids the healing process. Because proper nutrition is crucial in recovery from injury, a nutritionist with a background in sport nutrition may provide guidelines regarding proper food choices to optimize tissue recovery. Ideally, the nutritionist has been formally trained in food and nutrition sciences and is a Registered Dietitian (RD) recognized by the American Dietetic Association’s Commission on Dietetic Registration. Finally, recovering from an injury is often mentally traumatic for an athlete; a licensed psychologist or psychiatrist with a background in sport may provide strategies that help the injured athlete better cope with the mental stress accompanying an injury.
summary: Specialized team members, including exercise physiologists, nutritionists, and mental health professionals, contribute to the comprehensive rehabilitation process, addressing physical, nutritional, and psychological needs.

content: An indication is a form of treatment required by the rehabilitating athlete. For example, a softball outfielder with shoulder impingement must maintain lower extremity function, so the athletic trainer may request that the athlete continue to perform lower extremity strength, speed, agility, and power exercises during the shoulder rehabilitation. Therefore, lower body exercise is indicated. A contraindication is an activity or practice that is inadvisable or prohibited due to the given injury. For example, during the later phases of rehabilitation from an anterior shoulder dislocation, an American football player may require upper body strengthening before being cleared to play. The athletic trainer requests that the athlete initiate upper body strengthening, but the bench press exercise may be contraindicated because it can place the injured shoulder in a vulnerable position.
summary: Indications and contraindications guide the rehabilitation process, ensuring appropriate exercises are prescribed while avoiding those that could worsen the injury.

content: Macrotrauma is a specific, sudden episode of overload injury to a given tissue, resulting in disrupted tissue integrity. Trauma to the bone can lead to either a contusion or a fracture. Skeletal fractures can result from a direct blow to a bone and can be given a variety of classifications (e.g., closed, open, avulsed, incomplete).
summary: Macrotrauma, such as bone contusions or fractures, involves sudden tissue overload and damage, requiring specific classifications and treatment approaches.

content: Macrotrauma is a specific, sudden episode of overload injury to a given tissue, resulting in disrupted tissue integrity. Trauma to the bone can lead to either a contusion or a fracture. Skeletal fractures can result from a direct blow to a bone and can be given a variety of classifications (e.g., closed, open, avulsed, incomplete). Joint trauma is manifested as either a dislocation (complete displacement of the joint surfaces) or a subluxation (partial displacement of the joint surfaces) and may result in joint laxity or instability. Ligamentous trauma is termed a sprain and is assigned a classification of first degree (partial tear of the ligament without increased joint instability), second degree (partial tear with minor joint instability), or third degree (complete tear with full joint instability).
summary: This chunk defines different types of trauma, including bone, joint, and ligamentous injuries, and their classifications.

content: Musculotendinous trauma is classified as either a contusion (if the trauma was direct) or a strain (if the trauma was indirect). A muscle contusion is an area of excess accumulation of blood and fluid in the tissues surrounding the injured muscle; it may severely limit the injured muscle’s movement. Muscle strains are tears of muscle fibers and are further assigned grades, or degrees. A first-degree strain is a partial tear of individual fibers and is characterized by strong but painful muscle activity. A second-degree strain is a partial tear with weak, painful muscle activity. A third-degree muscle strain is a complete tear of the fibers and is manifested by very weak, painless muscle activity. A tendon, like a muscle, can also rupture if the tensile load applied to it exceeds its limit. Typically, the tendon’s collagen fibers are significantly stronger than the muscle fibers to which they attach, so failure is more likely to occur within the muscle belly, at the musculotendinous junction, or at the tendon’s attachment to the bone than within the substance of the tendon.
summary: This chunk describes different types of muscle and tendon injuries, including contusions, strains, and ruptures, and their classifications.

content: Microtrauma, or overuse injury, results from repeated, abnormal stress applied to a tissue by continuous training or training with too little recovery time. Overuse injuries may be due to training errors (e.g., poor program design), suboptimal training surfaces (e.g., too hard or uneven), faulty biomechanics or technique during performance, insufficient motor control, decreased flexibility, or skeletal malalignment and predisposition (17). The most common overuse injury to bone is a stress fracture, and although anthropomorphism (i.e., body type and structure) plays a large role (17), stress
summary: This chunk explains the concept of microtrauma and overuse injuries, their potential causes, and common examples.

content: Other members should be aware of the strength and conditioning exercises that the injured athlete is performing, responses to the exercises, and any additional information deemed important by the strength and conditioning professional. The strength and conditioning professional should answer the following questions: What is the athlete doing? How is the athlete progressing? Do any program changes need to be made?
summary: This chunk outlines the responsibilities of a strength and conditioning professional when working with an injured athlete.

content: Rehabilitation and reconditioning involves the healing of injured tissues and the preparation of these tissues for the return to function.
summary: This chunk defines the purpose of rehabilitation and reconditioning.

content: The timing of events occurring within each phase of tissue healing differs for each tissue type and is affected by a variety of systemic and local factors, including age, lifestyle, degree of injury, and the structure that has been damaged.
summary: This chunk explains the variability in healing timelines based on individual factors.

content: All tissues follow the same basic pattern of healing.
summary: This chunk states the fundamental similarity in healing processes across different tissues.

content: Inflammation is the body’s initial reaction to injury and is necessary for normal healing to occur. Both local and systemic inflammatory responses take place during the inflammatory phase, allowing the eventual healing and replacement of damaged tissue.
summary: This chunk defines inflammation as the initial response to injury and its role in healing.

content: During the inflammatory phase, the injured area becomes red and swollen due to changes in vascularity, blood flow, and capillary permeability.
summary: This chunk describes the physical manifestations of inflammation.

content: After tissues are damaged, a locally hypoxic environment leads to tissue death that allows the release of several chemical mediators, including histamine and bradykinin. Histamine and bradykinin further increase blood flow and capillary permeability, thereby allowing edema, the escape of fluid into the surrounding tissues.
summary: This chunk explains the biochemical cascade triggered by tissue damage.

content: Edema inhibits contractile tissues and can significantly limit the injured athlete’s function. Tissue debris and pathogens are removed from the injured area by increased blood flow and a process called phagocytosis. Phagocytosis allows the release of macrophages, which search for and remove cellular debris that may slow healing.
summary: This chunk details the consequences of edema and the role of phagocytosis in clearing debris.

content: The inflammatory substances present during this phase may noxiously stimulate sensory nerve fibers, causing the injured athlete to sense pain that may further contribute to the decrease in function. This phase typically lasts two to three days following an acute injury but may last longer with a compromised blood supply and more severe structural damage.
summary: This chunk describes the pain associated with inflammation and its duration.

content: Though the inflammatory phase is critical to tissue healing, if it does not end within a reasonable amount of time, the phases that follow may not occur, thereby delaying the rehabilitation process.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of a timely resolution of inflammation.

content: Once the inflammatory phase has ended, tissue repair begins; this phase allows the replacement of tissues that are no longer viable following injury. In an attempt to improve tissue integrity, the original, damaged tissue is regenerated (i.e., tissue identical to the injured tissue is produced), and scar tissue is formed.
summary: This chunk introduces the repair phase and its goal of tissue regeneration and scar formation.

content: New capillaries and connective tissue form in the area, and collagen fibers are randomly laid down to serve as the framework upon which the repair takes place. Because the collagen fibers are positioned somewhat haphazardly, however, the alignment is such that optimal strength of the new tissue is not yet achieved.
summary: This chunk describes the structural changes occurring during the repair phase.

content: Collagen fibers are strongest when they lie longitudinally to the primary line of stress, yet many of the new fibers are positioned transversely, which limits their ability to transmit force. This phase of tissue healing begins as early as two days after injury and may last up to two months.
summary: This chunk explains the functional limitations of newly formed tissue due to collagen fiber alignment.

content: The weakened tissue produced during the repair phase is strengthened during the remodeling phase of healing. Production of collagen fibers has now decreased significantly, allowing the newly formed tissue the opportunity to improve its structure, strength, and function.
summary: This chunk introduces the remodeling phase and its focus on tissue strengthening.

content: With increased loading, the collagen fibers of the newly formed scar tissue begin to hypertrophy and align themselves along the lines of stress. The thicker and more optimally aligned collagen fibers become stronger, which allows the injured athlete’s return to function.
summary: This chunk describes the process of collagen fiber remodeling and its impact on tissue strength.

content: Although strength of the collagen fibers improves significantly, the new tissue will likely never be as strong as the tissue it has replaced. Tissue remodeling can last up to two to four months after injury.
summary: This chunk acknowledges the limitations of scar tissue and the duration of the remodeling phase.

content: Following injury, all damaged tissues go through the same general phases of healing: inflammation, repair, and remodeling.
summary: This chunk summarizes the three phases of tissue healing.

content: Following injury, all damaged tissues go through the same general phases of healing: inflammation, repair, and remodeling. Characteristic events define each phase and separate one phase from another. The process of returning to competition following injury involves healing of the injured tissues, preparation of these tissues for the return to function, and use of proper techniques to maximize rehabilitation and reconditioning. While the goal is a rapid resumption of activity, it is important to remember that each athlete responds differently to injury and thus progresses uniquely during rehabilitation.
summary: Tissue healing progresses through inflammation, repair, and remodeling phases, with the athlete's return to competition involving tissue healing, preparation, and reconditioning, while individual responses to injury vary.

content: The body’s first response to an injury is inflammation, a reaction that is essential for subsequent healing but also important to manage properly so as to not retard the rehabilitation process. The goal for treatment during the inflammatory phase is to prevent disruption of new tissue. A healthy environment for new tissue regeneration and formation is essential for preventing prolonged inflammation and disruption of new blood vessel and collagen production. To achieve these goals, relative rest, ice, compression, and elevation are the primary treatment options. Passive modalities that help reduce inflammation (e.g., ice, ultrasound, and electrical stimulation) are common treatment choices.
summary: Inflammation is the initial response to injury, requiring management to prevent disruption of new tissue and promote healing through rest, ice, compression, elevation, and passive modalities.

content: With increased loading, the collagen fibers of the newly formed scar tissue begin to hypertrophy and align themselves along the lines of stress. The thicker and more optimally aligned collagen fibers become stronger, which allows the injured athlete’s return to function. Although strength of the collagen fibers improves significantly, the new tissue will likely never be as strong as the tissue it has replaced. Tissue remodeling can last up to two to four months after injury.
summary: Collagen fibers in scar tissue strengthen and align with increased loading, facilitating the athlete's return to function, although the new tissue may not fully regain the original strength.

content: During tissue healing, controlled therapeutic stress is necessary to optimize collagen matrix formation, but too much stress can damage new structures and significantly slow the athlete’s return to competition. It should be obvious that when one is choosing the load, it is necessary to consider the phase of healing and athlete type. For example, a stress that underloads a tissue during remodeling probably overloads it during inflammation. Further, a stress that underloads a professional basketball center probably overloads an amateur cross-country runner.
summary: Controlled therapeutic stress is crucial for tissue healing, but excessive stress can be detrimental. Load selection must consider the healing phase and athlete's characteristics.

content: The plane of movement is another necessary consideration. As an example, the medial collateral ligament of the knee is most loaded in the frontal plane during terminal knee extension. Therefore, frontal plane movements should be avoided during early healing phases. However, those frontal plane movements should probably be included in some form during the later phases. The athlete must meet specific objectives to progress from one phase of healing to the next. These objectives may depend on range of motion, strength, or activity. It is the responsibility of the team physician, athletic trainer, physical therapist, or a combination of these professionals to establish these guidelines, which assist the entire team with the athlete’s rehabilitation.
summary: Movement plane considerations and specific objectives based on range of motion, strength, or activity are crucial for progression through healing phases, guided by healthcare professionals.

content: The strength and conditioning professional must consider both the athlete’s subjective response to injury and the physiological mechanisms of tissue healing; both are essential in relation to an athlete’s return to optimal performance. A strength and conditioning professional can provide significant knowledge and expertise in rehabilitation and reconditioning. The strength and conditioning professional should consult with the athletic trainer to determine which types of exercises are indicated and contraindicated for the specific injury.
summary: Strength and conditioning professionals play a vital role in rehabilitation and reconditioning, considering both subjective athlete responses and physiological healing mechanisms.

content: Maximal protection of the injured structures is the primary goal during the rehabilitation and reconditioning phase. Exercises may include general aerobic and anaerobic training and resistance training of the uninjured extremities. If movement of the injured limb is not contraindicated, isolated exercises that target areas proximal and distal to the injured area may also be permissible provided that they do not stress the injured area. Examples include hip abduction and rotation exercises following knee injury (22, 24, 31) or scapula stabilizing exercises following glenohumeral joint injury (25, 42).
summary: Rehabilitation and reconditioning prioritize protecting the injured structures, incorporating general training, resistance training of uninjured limbs, and potentially isolated exercises for proximal and distal areas, avoiding stress on the injury.

content: Although a rapid return to competition is crucial, rest is necessary to protect the damaged tissue from additional injury. Therefore, exercise involving the injured area is not recommended during the rehabilitation and reconditioning phase. After the inflammatory phase, the body begins to repair the damaged tissue with similar tissue, but the resiliency of the new tissue is low. Repair of the weakened injury site can take up to eight weeks if the proper amount of restorative stress is applied, or longer if too much or too little stress is applied. The treatment goal during the repair phase is to prevent excessive muscle atrophy and joint deterioration of the injured area. A precarious balance must be maintained in which disruption of the newly formed collagen fibers is avoided but low-load stresses are gradually introduced to allow increased collagen synthesis and prevent loss of joint motion.
summary: Rest is crucial during rehabilitation to protect injured tissue, while the repair phase focuses on preventing muscle atrophy and joint deterioration through a balanced approach of avoiding disruption and introducing low-load stresses.

content: The treatment goal during the repair phase is to prevent excessive muscle atrophy and joint deterioration of the injured area. A precarious balance must be maintained in which disruption of the newly formed collagen fibers is avoided but low-load stresses are gradually introduced to allow increased collagen synthesis and prevent loss of joint motion. To protect the new, relatively weak collagen fibers, the athlete should avoid active resistive exercise involving the damaged tissue. Too little activity, though, can also have a deleterious effect, as newly formed fibers will not optimally align and may form adhesions, thereby preventing full motion. Early protected motion hastens the optimal alignment of collagen fibers and promotes improved tissue mobility.
summary: The repair phase focuses on preventing muscle atrophy and joint deterioration while protecting newly formed collagen fibers. A balance of rest and controlled movement is crucial.

content: After the inflammatory phase, the body begins to repair the damaged tissue with similar tissue, but the resiliency of the new tissue is low. Repair of the weakened injury site can take up to eight weeks if the proper amount of restorative stress is applied, or longer if too much or too little stress is applied.  As in the inflammatory phase, therapeutic modalities are permissible, but their goal during repair is to promote collagen synthesis. Ultrasound, electrical stimulation, and ice are continued in order to support and hasten new tissue formation.
summary: The repair phase involves tissue regeneration and strengthening, taking several weeks and requiring appropriate therapeutic interventions.

content: Possible exercise forms during the repair phase include strengthening of the uninjured extremities and areas proximal and distal to the injury, aerobic and anaerobic exercise, and improving strength and neuromuscular control of the involved areas. The following exercises should be used during the repair phase only after consultation with the team physician, athletic trainer, or physical therapist. Isometric exercise may be performed provided that it is pain free and otherwise indicated. Submaximal isometric exercise allows the athlete to maintain neuromuscular function and improve strength with movements performed at an intensity low enough that the newly formed collagen fibers are not disrupted. Unfortunately, isometric strengthening is joint angle specific; that is, strength gains occur only at the angles used. Therefore, if indicated, it may be appropriate for the athlete to perform isometric exercises at multiple angles.
summary: During the repair phase, exercise focuses on strengthening uninjured areas, maintaining cardiovascular fitness, and improving neuromuscular control through specific exercises like isometric training.

content: Resistance training is velocity specific; therefore, isokinetic exercise can be an important aspect of strengthening following injury. Isokinetic exercise uses equipment that provides resistance to movement at a given speed (e.g., 60°/s or 120°/s). Because no sport is performed at one speed, however, isokinetic exercise is somewhat limited in its real-world application. Furthermore, most isokinetic equipment allows single-joint exercise only, which permits concentration on a specific muscle or joint but is not always the most functional method of strengthening.
summary: Isokinetic exercise, while beneficial for strengthening, has limitations due to its single-joint focus and inability to replicate the varied speeds of real-world movements.

content: While isotonic exercise involves movements with constant external resistance, the amount of force required to move the resistance varies, depending primarily on joint angle and the length of each agonist muscle.
