content: A comprehensive master plan has been prepared on the nature and scope of the program, and the special requirements for space, equipment, fixtures, and facilities have been dictated by the activities to be conducted (form follows function). The facility has been planned to meet the total requirements of the program, both present and future, as well as the special needs of those who are to be served. Any possible future additions or expansions are included in the present plans to permit economy of construction and costs.
summary: The facility design is based on a comprehensive master plan, considering current and future needs, and allowing for potential expansions.

content: The plans and specifications meet the codes of all governmental agencies whose approval is required by law. The plans of the facility conform to accepted standards and practices. The following factors have been considered for the proposed facility and site: feeder streets (new or existing), sewage lines, parking areas, gas lines, electrical supplies, storm drainage, water supplies, and soil topography.
summary: Facility plans adhere to legal codes, accepted standards, and consider site-specific factors like utilities and drainage.

content: The selection of equipment and supplies has been based on a cost-per-use ratio, as well as on ongoing maintenance costs. Sufficient attention has been given to fire codes, fire and security systems, and emergency escape routes. Window heights are appropriate for privacy, safety, maintenance, and use of natural light. Floor and wall surfaces have been selected according to the following criteria: year-round usage, dust and moisture resistance, stainlessness, inflammability, nonabrasiveness, durability, resiliency, safety, maintenance, and cost per use.
summary: Equipment and material selection prioritize cost-effectiveness, safety, and functionality, considering factors like fire codes, window heights, and floor/wall surfaces.

content: All passageways are free of obstruction so two-way traffic can occur. Every effort has been made to eliminate hazards. Buildings, specialty areas, and facilities are clearly identified. Administrative offices, workout areas, and service facilities are properly interrelated; the same is true for medical, first aid, and emergency rooms.
summary: Facility design prioritizes clear navigation, hazard elimination, and logical organization of spaces for different purposes.

content: Proper maintenance and cleaning of the equipment and facility on a regular schedule will extend the life span of the facility and its equipment.
summary: Regular maintenance and cleaning are essential for extending the lifespan of the facility and equipment.

content: Window heights are appropriate for privacy, safety, maintenance, and use of natural light. Floor and wall surfaces have been selected according to the following criteria: year-round usage, dust and moisture resistance, stainlessness, inflammability, nonabrasiveness, durability, resiliency, safety, maintenance, and cost per use.
summary: This chunk describes the considerations for window placement and the selection of floor and wall surfaces in a facility.

content: All passageways are free of obstruction so two-way traffic can occur. Every effort has been made to eliminate hazards.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of safe and unobstructed passageways within the facility.

content: Buildings, specialty areas, and facilities are clearly identified. Administrative offices, workout areas, and service facilities are properly interrelated; the same is true for medical, first aid, and emergency rooms.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of clear signage and logical spatial relationships within the facility.

content: Special needs of people with physical disabilities have been met, including installation of a ramp into the building at a major entrance.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the inclusion of accessibility features for people with disabilities.

content: Storage rooms are of adequate size and are accessible to appropriate areas. All dead space is used, such as areas under stairwells.
summary: This chunk focuses on the efficient use of storage space within the facility.

content: Low-cost maintenance features have been considered.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of cost-effective maintenance practices.

content: All areas, courts, facilities, equipment, climate control, security, and the like conform rigidly to detailed standards and specifications.
summary: This chunk stresses the adherence to strict standards and specifications throughout the facility.

content: Drinking fountains are conveniently placed in the locker rooms and workout areas or immediately adjacent to them.
summary: This chunk details the strategic placement of drinking fountains within the facility.

content: Provision is made for repair, maintenance, replacement, and storage of equipment.
summary: This chunk outlines the necessary provisions for equipment management.

content: Antipanic hardware is used on doors as required by fire regulations.
summary: This chunk highlights the inclusion of safety features in accordance with fire regulations.

content: Space relationships and equipment are planned in accordance with the type and number of users.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of space planning based on user needs.

content: Warning signals—both visible and audible—are included in the plans.
summary: This chunk outlines the inclusion of warning signals for safety purposes.

content: Ramps have a slope equal to or less than a 1-foot (30 cm) vertical rise for every 12 linear feet (3.6 m). Alternatively, the maximum slope of a ramp can be described as 1 foot (30 cm) for every inch (2.5 cm) of elevation change.
summary: This chunk specifies the maximum allowable slope for ramps to ensure accessibility.

content: Minimum landings for ramps are 5 feet by 5 feet (1.5 m by 1.5 m); they extend at least 1 foot (30 cm) beyond the swinging arc of a door and have at least a 6-foot (1.8 m) clearance at the bottom.
summary: This chunk outlines the minimum dimensions and clearances required for ramp landings.

content: The building has climate control throughout (i.e., heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning, or HVAC). HVAC systems are on both a zone control and an individual room control system. Temperature and humidity are specific to a particular area.
summary: This chunk describes the comprehensive climate control system within the building.

content: Lighting intensity meets approved standards. An adequate number of electrical outlets are appropriately placed throughout the facility and should be 3 feet (0.9 m) above the floor unless otherwise specified. Service area lights are controlled by dimmer units. Natural light, when used, is controlled properly to reduce glare.
summary: This chunk details the specifications for lighting, electrical outlets, and natural light control.

content: Electrical wall plates are located within the wall where needed and are firmly attached. Materials that clean easily and are impervious to moisture are used where moisture is prevalent.
summary: This chunk outlines the specifications for electrical installations and moisture-resistant materials.

content: Drinking fountains are provided in adequate number and are properly recessed in the wall. One wall (at least) of the aerobics room has full-length mirrors. Wall coverings are aesthetically pleasing and match the overall decor of the facility.
summary: This chunk describes the specific features of drinking fountains, aerobics rooms, and wall coverings.

content: The ceiling height is adequate for the activities to be performed in a given area. Ceiling support beams are designed and engineered to withstand stress.
summary: This chunk addresses the ceiling height and structural integrity of the facility.

content: Acoustical materials impervious to moisture are used in moisture-prevalent areas. All ceilings except those in storage areas are acoustically treated with sound-absorbent materials. Skylights in exercise rooms are seldom used because of problems in waterproofing roofs and controlling sun rays.
summary: This chunk outlines the acoustic treatment and considerations for skylights within the facility.

content: Ceiling and crawl spaces are easily accessible for maintenance and repair purposes.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the ease of access for maintenance and repairs.

content: Floor plates, where needed, are flush mounted. The storage areas conform to fire laws. The storage and issue areas are centrally located and are of sufficient number to handle peak periods effectively.
summary: This chunk describes the specifications for floor plates, storage areas, and their location.

content: The doors to storage areas are wide and do not have a riser. The storage areas have appropriate security.
summary: This chunk outlines the features of storage area doors and security measures.

content: The storage and issue areas are centrally located and are of sufficient number to handle peak periods effectively. Adequate storage rooms are conveniently located near activity areas.  The storage areas conform to fire laws. The storage areas have appropriate security. Storage areas have adequate ventilation.  Floor plates, where needed, are flush mounted.
summary: Storage and issue areas are strategically placed and equipped to handle peak demands, with security and ventilation considerations.

content: The doors to storage areas are wide and do not have a riser. All doorways and passageways are of sufficient width to accommodate wheelchairs. The feasibility of electrically operated doors has been considered. All doorways or entryways to toilets, telephone areas, food and refreshment areas, locker rooms, and special rooms are sufficient to accommodate wheelchairs.  All thresholds are flush.
summary: Accessibility is prioritized with wide doorways, flush thresholds, and consideration for wheelchair access.

content: The floor area and dimensions have been determined by the activities to be conducted. Adequate space or buffer zones are provided between activity areas. The floor surface material has been selected to allow for a maximum variety of uses.  The exercise facility floor should be inspected and cleaned daily. Wooden flooring should be free of splinters, holes, protruding nails, and loose screws. Tile flooring should be resistant to slipping and free of moisture or chalk accumulation. Rubber flooring should be free of cuts, slits, and large gaps between pieces. Interlocking mats should be secure and arranged with no protruding tabs. Nonabsorbent carpet should be free of tears and wear areas should be protected by throw mats. The area should be swept and vacuumed or mopped on a regular basis. Flooring should be glued or fastened down properly.
summary: The facility layout and flooring are designed for specific activities, with daily cleaning and maintenance protocols.

content: Wall surfaces were selected to allow their use for activities, cleaning, and maintenance. Wall surfaces should be cleaned two to three times a week (or more often if needed). Walls in high-activity areas should be free of protruding appliances, equipment, or wall hangings. Mirrors and shelves should be securely fixed to walls.
summary: Wall surfaces are chosen for functionality and durability, with regular cleaning and safety considerations.

content: There is a properly installed, high-quality public address system. There are provisions for an intercom system that may be connected with the public address system.  Acoustical standards are met for all rooms. Lighting quality meets all standards. Provisions are included for an emergency safety lighting system. Climate control systems are adequate.
summary: The facility features a comprehensive sound system, meets acoustic standards, and has adequate lighting and climate control.

content: There is a suitable lock-and-key system for doors, storage rooms, light controls, sound system controls, intercom controls, climate controls, and public address systems.  Provisions have been included for repair, maintenance, and installation of ceiling fixtures.  Provisions have been included for proper and necessary signs, both illuminated and nonilluminated, pertaining to areas, exits, and participants.
summary: Security and maintenance are addressed with a comprehensive lock system, signage, and provisions for repairs.

content: Necessary provisions are present for parking, loading, and unloading areas, and ramps are provided wherever necessary.  Figure 21.3 is an example of a secondary school strength and conditioning facility floor plan. Figure 21.4 is an example of a university strength and conditioning facility floor plan. 3 feet (ft) ≈ 1 m.
summary: Parking, loading, and accessibility are considered, with example floor plans for different facility types.

content: The area should be swept and vacuumed or mopped on a regular basis. The stretching and body weight exercise area should be swept and disinfected daily.  Mirrors and windows should be cleaned regularly (especially in high-activity areas, such as around drinking fountains and in doorways).
summary: This chunk outlines general cleaning and maintenance practices for the fitness area, including floor care, mirror and window cleaning, and daily disinfection of the stretching area.

content: Flooring should be glued or fastened down properly.  Mats and bench upholstery should be free of cracks and tears. There should be no large gaps between stretching mats.
summary: This chunk focuses on the condition and placement of flooring and mats, ensuring they are secure, intact, and properly spaced.

content: Wall surfaces should be cleaned two to three times a week (or more often if needed). Walls in high-activity areas should be free of protruding appliances, equipment, or wall hangings. Mirrors should be placed a minimum of 20 inches (51 cm) off the floor in all areas. Mirrors should not be cracked or distorted (replace immediately if damaged).
summary: This chunk details wall maintenance, including cleaning frequency, safety considerations for high-traffic areas, and proper mirror placement and condition.

content: All ceiling fixtures and attachments should be dusted regularly. Ceiling tile should be kept clean. Damaged or missing ceiling tile should be replaced as needed. Open ceilings with exposed pipes and ducts should be cleaned as needed.
summary: This chunk outlines the necessary upkeep for ceiling fixtures, tiles, and exposed pipes/ducts, ensuring cleanliness and structural integrity.

content: The stretching and body weight exercise area mat area should be free of weight benches and equipment.
summary: This chunk specifies that the stretching and body weight exercise area should be clear of other equipment.

content: Equipment should be properly stored after use. Elastic cords should be secured to the base with a safety knot and checked for wear.
summary: This chunk emphasizes proper equipment storage and maintenance, including securing elastic cords and inspecting them for wear.

content: Surfaces that contact skin should be treated with antifungal and antibacterial agents daily.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of daily disinfection of surfaces that come into contact with skin.

content: Nonslip material should be on the top surface and bottom or base of plyometric boxes.
summary: This chunk specifies the safety requirement of nonslip material on plyometric boxes.

content: The ceiling height should be sufficient for overhead exercises (12 feet [3.7 m] minimum) and free of low-hanging apparatus (beams, pipes, lighting, signs, etc.).
summary: This chunk outlines the necessary ceiling height for overhead exercises and prohibits any obstructions.

content: There should be easy access to each resistance training machine station (a minimum of 2 feet [61 cm] between machines; 3 feet [91 cm] is optimal).
summary: This chunk specifies the required spacing between resistance training machines for safe and comfortable access.

content: The area must be free of loose bolts, screws, cables, and chains. Proper selectorized pins must be used. Securing straps must be functional.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of a safe and organized environment by prohibiting loose hardware and ensuring proper equipment function.

content: Parts and surfaces must be properly lubricated and cleaned. Protective padding must be free of cracks and tears.
summary: This chunk outlines the maintenance requirements for equipment parts and protective padding, ensuring smooth operation and user safety.

content: There must be no protruding screws or parts that need tightening or removal. Belts, chains, and cables must be aligned with machine parts.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of a safe and functional environment by prohibiting protruding parts and ensuring proper alignment of equipment components.

content: There must be no worn parts (frayed cable, loose chains, worn bolts, cracked joints, etc.).
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of inspecting and replacing worn equipment parts to prevent accidents and ensure proper function.

content: There must be easy access to each bench or area (a minimum of 2 feet [61 cm] between machines; 3 feet [91 cm] is optimal). Olympic bars must be properly spaced (3 feet [91 cm]) between ends.
summary: This chunk specifies the required spacing between benches and Olympic bars for safe and comfortable access.

content: All equipment must be returned after use to avoid obstruction of the pathway. Safety equipment (belts, collars, safety bars) must be used and returned.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of returning equipment to its designated place and using safety equipment properly.

content: Protective padding must be free of cracks and tears. Surfaces that contact skin must be treated with antifungal and antibacterial agents daily. Securing bolts and apparatus parts (collars, curl bars) must be tightly fastened.
summary: This chunk outlines the maintenance requirements for protective padding, surface disinfection, and secure fastening of equipment parts.

content: Nonslip mats must be on the squat rack floor area. Olympic bars must turn properly and be properly lubricated and tightened.
summary: This chunk specifies the safety requirement of nonslip mats on the squat rack and the proper maintenance of Olympic bars.

content: Protective padding must be free of cracks and tears. Protective padding must be inspected for cracks and tears daily.
summary: Protective padding should be free of damage and inspected daily for cracks or tears.

content: Surfaces that contact skin must be treated with antifungal and antibacterial agents daily. Surfaces that contact skin must be cleaned and disinfected daily. Equipment surfaces that contact skin must be cleaned and disinfected daily.
summary: All surfaces that come into contact with skin must be cleaned and disinfected daily with antifungal and antibacterial agents.

content: Securing bolts and apparatus parts (collars, curl bars) must be tightly fastened. Bolts and screws must be tight.  Fixed equipment’s connection with the floor must be inspected daily. All equipment must be inspected for damage; wear; loose or protruding belts, screws, cables, or chains; insecure or nonfunctioning foot and body straps; improper functioning or improper use of attachments, pins, or other devices daily.
summary: All equipment must be securely fastened, regularly inspected for damage and wear, and have functioning parts.

content: Nonslip mats must be on the squat rack floor area. Nonslip material and mats must be inspected for proper placement, damage, and wear daily.
summary: Nonslip mats should be present and inspected daily for damage and proper placement.

content: Olympic bars must turn properly and be properly lubricated and tightened. Olympic bars must rotate properly and be properly lubricated and tightened. Bent Olympic bars must be replaced; knurling must be clear of debris.
summary: Olympic bars must rotate freely, be properly lubricated, and free of damage or debris.

content: Benches, weight racks, standards, and the like must be secured to the floor or wall. Benches, chairs, boxes must be kept at a distance from the lifting area.
summary: Weightlifting equipment must be securely anchored and kept a safe distance from other objects.

content: The ceiling height must be sufficient for overhead exercises (12 feet [3.7 m] minimum) and free of low-hanging apparatus (beams, pipes, lighting, signs, etc.). The ceiling height must be sufficient for overhead exercises (12 feet [3.7 m] minimum) and free of low-hanging apparatus (beams, pipes, lighting, signs, etc.).
summary: The ceiling height must be at least 12 feet to accommodate overhead exercises and free of any obstructions.

content: Olympic bars must be properly spaced (3 feet [91 cm]) between ends.
summary: Olympic bars should be spaced 3 feet apart.

content: All equipment must be returned after use to avoid obstruction of the lifting area.
summary: Equipment should be returned to its designated spot after use to maintain a clear lifting area.

content: Collars must be functioning.  Sufficient chalk must be available. Wrist straps, belts, and knee wraps must be available, functioning, and stored properly.
summary: Essential lifting accessories like collars, chalk, wrist straps, belts, and knee wraps must be available, functional, and stored properly.

content: The area must be properly swept and mopped to remove splinters and chalk. All flooring must be inspected for damage or wear daily. All flooring must be cleaned (swept, vacuumed, or mopped and disinfected) daily.
summary: The lifting area must be kept clean and free of debris, with daily inspections and cleaning of the flooring.

content: There must be easy access to each station (minimum of 2 feet [61 cm] between machines; 3 feet [91 cm] is optimal).
summary: There should be adequate space between machines to allow for safe and comfortable movement.

content: Functioning parts must be easily adjustable. Parts and surfaces must be properly lubricated and cleaned. Moving parts of equipment must be cleaned and lubricated daily.
summary: Equipment parts should be easily adjustable, lubricated, and cleaned regularly.

content: Foot and body straps must be secure and not ripped. Measurement devices for tension, time, and rpms must be properly functioning.
summary: Straps must be secure, and measurement devices must be functional.

content: Upholstery must be cleaned and disinfected daily. Drinking fountains must be cleaned and disinfected daily. Mirrors must be cleaned daily. Windows must be cleaned daily.
summary: Upholstery, drinking fountains, mirrors, and windows must be cleaned daily.

content: Mirrors must be inspected for damage daily. Trash and garbage must be removed daily.
summary: Mirrors should be inspected daily for damage, and trash should be removed regularly.

content: All equipment must be inspected for damage; wear; loose or protruding belts, screws, cables, or chains; insecure or nonfunctioning foot and body straps; improper functioning or improper use of attachments, pins, or other devices daily. Protective padding must be inspected for cracks and tears daily. Nonslip material and mats must be inspected for proper placement, damage, and wear daily. Trash and garbage must be removed daily. Light covers, fans, air vents, clocks, and speakers must be cleaned daily. Equipment must be ensured to be returned and stored properly after use daily.
summary: Daily equipment maintenance procedures include inspections for damage, cleanliness, and proper storage.

content: Moving parts of equipment must be cleaned and lubricated daily. Aerobic machines and the guide rods on selectorized resistance training machines must be cleaned and lubricated two to three times per week. Ceiling fixtures and attachments must be cleaned and dusted once per week. Ceiling tile must be cleaned once per week. Light bulbs must be replaced as needed. Walls must be cleaned as needed. Damaged or missing ceiling tiles must be replaced as needed. Open ceilings with exposed pipes or ducts must be cleaned as needed. Broken equipment must be removed (or a sign placed on it) as needed. Chalk boxes must be filled as needed. Bar knurling must be cleaned as needed. Rust must be cleaned from the floor, plates, bars, and equipment with a rust-removing solution as needed.
summary: This chunk outlines the frequency of cleaning and maintenance tasks for various equipment components.

content: The list includes maintenance equipment and cleaning supplies. Maintenance equipment includes a file, hammer, pliers (standard and needle-nose), screwdrivers (standard and Phillips), an Allen wrench set, a crescent wrench, a mallet, a carpet knife, a heavy-duty stapler, nuts, bolts, washers, nails, and screws in various sizes, heavy-duty glue, transparent tape, masking tape, duct tape, a drill and drill bit set, lubricant spray, a socket set, and vise grips. Cleaning supplies include disinfectant (germicide), specialty cleaners (wood, wall surfaces, upholstery, etc.), window and mirror cleaner, lubrication sprays, spray bottles (4), paper towels, cloth towels and hand rags (12 or more), sponges, a broom and dustpan, a small vacuum cleaner, vacuum cleaner bags, a whisk broom, a mop and bucket, and gum and stain remover (for carpet and upholstery). These items should be kept in a cabinet or supply closet.
summary: This chunk details the necessary maintenance equipment and cleaning supplies for a strength and conditioning facility.

content: Written policies and procedures are important for a strength and conditioning program. Communicating a plan of action to the strength and conditioning staff and having the plan correctly implemented establish a standard of excellence and help ensure athlete safety. A larger, well-qualified staff is essential for getting the job done right. Legal liability is a common concern to all people responsible for sport activities. There is a heightened need for quality instruction and supervision as well as continual inspection and maintenance of a training facility. Policies and procedures concern both athletes (and other users) and strength and conditioning staff members. Policies are essentially a facility’s rules and regulations; they reflect the goals and objectives of the program. Procedures describe how policies are met or carried out.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of written policies and procedures for a strength and conditioning program, highlighting their role in ensuring athlete safety, legal compliance, and program effectiveness.

content: Before detailing specific policies and procedures, it is necessary to examine program goals and objectives, because they are the basis on which policies and procedures are created. The goal of this chapter is to guide the strength and conditioning professional in creating a policies and procedures manual that establishes and improves the standard of care and excellence of his or her program. A mission statement is vital to the success of any organization; it is the foundation of effective administration. Creating a mission statement requires forward thinking with the end result in mind. A good mission statement provides focus, direction, and a sense of purpose. The following are suggested criteria for an effective mission statement: Is short and sharply focused, Is clear and easily understood, Defines why we do what we do; why the organization exists, Does not prescribe means, Is sufficiently broad, Provides direction for doing the right things, Addresses our opportunities, Matches our competence, Inspires our commitment, Says what, in the end, we want to be remembered for. An example of a holistic mission statement of a strength and conditioning program is: To provide to athletes the means by which they can train consistently, sensibly, and systematically over designated periods of time in a safe, clean, and professional environment to help prevent injury and improve athletic performance. The most foundational goal of a strength and conditioning program is to improve athletic performance. Strength and conditioning professionals in whose programs injuries have occurred realize that injury prevention should also be a goal. Developing a mission statement and a list of program objectives should involve the entire strength and conditioning department, the athletic administration, and the sports medicine departments.
summary: This chunk focuses on the importance of establishing a mission statement and program objectives, outlining the criteria for an effective mission statement and the process of developing it.

content: The following are suggested criteria for an effective mission statement: Is short and sharply focused, Is clear and easily understood, Defines why we do what we do; why the organization exists, Does not prescribe means, Is sufficiently broad, Provides direction for doing the right things, Addresses our opportunities, Matches our competence, Inspires our commitment, Says what, in the end, we want to be remembered for.
summary: Characteristics of an effective mission statement include brevity, clarity, purpose definition, breadth, direction, opportunity alignment, competence matching, inspiration, and lasting impact.

content: An example of a holistic mission statement of a strength and conditioning program is: To provide to athletes the means by which they can train consistently, sensibly, and systematically over designated periods of time in a safe, clean, and professional environment to help prevent injury and improve athletic performance. The most foundational goal of a strength and conditioning program is to improve athletic performance. Strength and conditioning professionals in whose programs injuries have occurred realize that injury prevention should also be a goal.
summary: A strength and conditioning program's mission should encompass athlete training, injury prevention, and performance enhancement.

content: Developing a mission statement and a list of program objectives should involve the entire strength and conditioning department, the athletic administration, and the sports medicine departments. For larger institutions, other support staff (e.g., graduate assistants, interns, secretaries, volunteers, and student-athlete representatives) may also be contributory resources.
summary: Mission statement and objective development should be a collaborative effort involving the strength and conditioning department, athletic administration, sports medicine, and other relevant staff.

content: A mission statement provides focus, direction, and a sense of purpose for a strength and conditioning program. Program objectives are specific means of attaining program goals. If program goals are stated but the ways in which these goals might be attained are not specified, the result may be that athletes never achieve them. Program objectives should encompass all areas of the program to ensure that the goals are attained.
summary: A mission statement guides a program's direction, while objectives define specific actions to achieve program goals.

content: A sample list of objectives that can lead to reaching program goals and prepare a strength and conditioning professional to handle the job requirements includes: Design and administer strength, flexibility, aerobic, plyometric, and other training programs that reduce the likelihood of injuries and improve athletic performance. More precisely, design training programs that create the desired results in body composition, hypertrophy, strength, muscular endurance, cardiovascular endurance, speed, agility, coordination, balance, and power. Develop training programs to account for biomechanical and physiological differences among individual athletes, taking into consideration their ages, sex, training status, physical limitations, and injury status.
summary: Program objectives should encompass designing and implementing diverse training programs tailored to individual athletes' needs, aiming to improve performance and prevent injuries.

content: Recognize acute and chronic physiological responses and adaptations to training and their implications for the design of sport-specific training programs. Educate athletes on the importance of good nutrition and its role in health and performance. Educate athletes about the effects of performance-enhancing substances and their abuse, relevant school policy, legislation, and safe and viable alternatives.
summary: Program objectives should include understanding physiological responses to training, educating athletes on nutrition and performance-enhancing substances.

content: To work toward creating a strength and conditioning program that meets the preceding objectives, the strength and conditioning professional in charge of a facility needs to establish written job (position) descriptions, policies, and procedures and to familiarize both staff and participants with the established goals, objectives, responsibilities, and procedures. In order for a facility to achieve a high standard of excellence, it is imperative that the roles and duties of each staff member be distinct and be clearly delineated.
summary: Establishing clear job descriptions, policies, and procedures is essential for a successful strength and conditioning program.

content: The title and responsibilities of the strength and conditioning professional are continually changing as the field grows and becomes better recognized. Although job responsibilities vary according to institution, it would be prudent to consult Strength and Conditioning Professionals Standards and Guidelines (6) when one is developing job descriptions and responsibilities.
summary: The role of a strength and conditioning professional is evolving, and it's important to consult professional standards for guidance on job descriptions.

content: Guideline 2.2 of Strength and Conditioning Professionals Standards and Guidelines (6) states: The Strength and Conditioning practitioner should achieve and maintain professional certification(s) with continuing education requirements and a code of ethics, such as the CSCS credential. Appendix A of Strength and Conditioning Professionals Standards and Guidelines (6) defines the strength and conditioning professional: Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialists are professionals who practically apply foundational knowledge to assess, motivate, educate, and train athletes for the primary goal of improving sports performance. They conduct sport-specific testing sessions, design and implement safe and effective strength training and conditioning programs, and provide guidance for athletes in nutrition and injury prevention. Certified Strength and Conditioning Specialists consult with and refer athletes to medical, dietetic, athletic training, and sport coaching professionals when appropriate.
summary: The CSCS certification is a recognized standard for strength and conditioning professionals, who apply their knowledge to assess, train, and guide athletes towards performance improvement.

content: The following sections present three hierarchical levels of strength and conditioning staff. In many smaller (and even moderate-sized) institutions, there may be only one or two individuals who must handle all aspects of a strength and conditioning program without the benefit or support of a staff. A high school may have one coach who oversees the weight room while sport coaches and physical education teachers run the classes. The person in charge of the weight room should be considered the director and should carry out the duties required of the head strength and conditioning professional.
summary: Strength and conditioning staff structures vary depending on institution size, with smaller institutions often relying on a single individual to manage all aspects of the program.

content: A strength and conditioning director (referred to hereafter as director)—also commonly referred to as the head strength and conditioning professional—is both a practitioner and an administrator. This person is responsible for the overall strength and conditioning program, facility, equipment, staff, and such administrative tasks as preparing a budget, purchasing equipment, preparing proposals, and working with the school administration and media. Typically, the director is paid a salary by the institution as an athletic department staff member.
summary: The strength and conditioning director is responsible for the overall program, facility, staff, and administrative tasks.

content: Responsibilities of a Strength and Conditioning Professional include developing and monitoring performance training programs for athletic teams, instructing proper resistance training exercise technique and speed development, testing and evaluating physical performance, and overseeing operation and general maintenance of the performance training program as assigned by the director of athletics.
summary: A strength and conditioning professional's responsibilities include program development, athlete instruction, performance evaluation, and program maintenance.

content: A Bachelor’s degree is required but a Master’s degree is preferred (with emphasis in exercise science, exercise physiology, or physical education) for a Strength and Conditioning Professional position. Two years’ experience is required in progressively responsible positions involving development and management of sport-specific individual and group performance training programs for a Strength and Conditioning Professional. Excellent communication, supervisory, and management skills are required for a Strength and Conditioning Professional. The CSCS certification is required for a Strength and Conditioning Professional.
summary: Required qualifications for a strength and conditioning professional include a bachelor's degree, relevant experience, strong communication and management skills, and the CSCS certification.

content: The Strength and Conditioning Director is responsible for developing, presenting, and enforcing the written policies and procedures of the staff and participants in the program. The Strength and Conditioning Director achieves this by having staff and student-athlete orientation meetings and by periodically evaluating the staff’s professional performance. The Assistant Strength and Conditioning Professional is responsible for many of the same duties as the director but is usually only directly responsible for a limited number of teams, not the whole program.
summary: The strength and conditioning director oversees program policies and staff performance, while the assistant director handles a smaller subset of responsibilities.

content: The Strength and Conditioning Professional Standards and Guidelines does not differentiate between head and assistant strength and conditioning professionals regarding certification.
summary: Certification requirements are the same for both head and assistant strength and conditioning professionals.

content: The CSCS certification is required for a Strength and Conditioning Professional. The Strength and Conditioning Professional Standards and Guidelines does not differentiate between head and assistant strength and conditioning professionals regarding certification. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals should also achieve and maintain a professional certification. The strength and conditioning professional—more specifically, the director—is responsible for instructing and educating assistants and supervisors about the facility’s written policies and procedures. Each staff member needs to understand the strength and conditioning facility’s written policies and procedures. The roles and responsibilities of strength and conditioning staff members should be distinct and clearly communicated.
summary: Certification requirements and communication of roles and responsibilities for strength and conditioning professionals.

content: The Strength and Conditioning Director is responsible for developing, presenting, and enforcing the written policies and procedures of the staff and participants in the program. The Strength and Conditioning Director achieves this by having staff and student-athlete orientation meetings and by periodically evaluating the staff’s professional performance. The strength and conditioning director is responsible for all facets of staff, program, and facility operations. The strength and conditioning director designs (or has final review of) all training programs. The strength and conditioning director oversees the athletic performance operation to ensure compliance with institutional, conference, and governing bodies’ rules and regulations. The strength and conditioning director develops and submits the annual budget and ensures budgetary compliance through efficient financial management. The strength and conditioning director generates income and budget available funds for maintenance and improvement of the facility. The strength and conditioning director oversees the selection, installation, and maintenance of performance equipment including cleaning and repair. The strength and conditioning director conducts orientation meetings for student-athletes on such issues as facility rules, the value of proper training and nutrition, and the dangers of banned substances. The strength and conditioning director develops staff work and supervision schedules, assigns duties, and evaluates performance. The strength and conditioning director coordinates time schedules for use of the facility by each sport team and individual student-athletes. The strength and conditioning director assists with on-campus recruiting activities for prospective student-athletes. The strength and conditioning director serves on various departmental, institutional, conference, governing body, and professional committees and task forces. The strength and conditioning director works and communicates with coaches in the athletic department. The strength and conditioning director travels with sport teams (if applicable) and provides remote-site performance training programs, including pregame warm-up. The strength and conditioning director maintains a performance training library for professional development. The strength and conditioning director achieves and maintains professional certification(s) with continuing education requirements and a code of ethics, such as the CSCS credential offered through the NSCA. Depending on the professional’s scope of activities, responsibilities, and knowledge requirements, relevant certifications offered by other governing bodies may also be appropriate.
summary: Responsibilities and duties of the Strength and Conditioning Director.

content: The Assistant Strength and Conditioning Professional is responsible for many of the same duties as the director but is usually only directly responsible for a limited number of teams, not the whole program. An assistant strength coach could have either a full- or part-time salary or, in some cases, could serve as a volunteer, depending on the size of the institution. A high school sport coach who oversees his or her team’s strength training program would be considered an assistant strength and conditioning professional and therefore should follow the duties described in figure 22.5. The responsibilities of an assistant strength and conditioning professional include developing and monitoring strength and conditioning programs for assigned athletic teams. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals assist in fitness testing, computation of results, and evaluation of individual athletes. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals teach proper resistance training exercise technique and assist in spotting when necessary. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals assist in the general maintenance and cleaning of the facility and its equipment. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals anticipate potential risks of injury, take measures to remove them, and, in the event of an injury, have the ability to implement emergency medical procedures. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals assist the director with all tasks for which he or she is responsible and manage the general operation of the facility in the director’s absence. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals effectively and properly motivate athletes to achieve maximum potential in all areas of performance. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals work and communicate with coaches in the athletic department.
summary: Responsibilities and duties of the Assistant Strength and Conditioning Professional.

content: The Facility Supervisor is usually a part-time staff member, often an exercise science student, who is responsible for observing activities within the strength and conditioning facility and cleaning and maintaining its equipment. The Facility Supervisor also assists the director and the assistant strength coaches when necessary. Typically, the Facility Supervisor is an unpaid volunteer who specifically works in the strength and conditioning facility to gain valuable experience in the field.
summary: Responsibilities and typical profile of the Facility Supervisor.

content: The strength and conditioning professional performs other duties and special projects as requested by supervisor. The strength and conditioning professional performs the duties of the assistant strength and conditioning professional and facility supervisor as needed.
summary: Additional duties and flexibility of the strength and conditioning professional.

content: Assistant strength and conditioning professionals assist the director with all tasks for which he or she is responsible and manage the general operation of the facility in the director’s absence. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals assist in the training of student assistants. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals work and communicate with coaches in the athletic department. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals prepare the workout sheets for assigned sports.
summary: Assistant strength and conditioning professionals provide administrative and operational support, working closely with the director, coaches, and student assistants.

content: Assistant strength and conditioning professionals assist in the general maintenance and cleaning of the facility and its equipment. The facility supervisor is responsible for supervising the facility during assigned times. The facility supervisor is responsible for enforcing facility policies and rules. The facility supervisor is responsible for maintaining, checking, and cleaning the facility and its equipment.
summary: Both assistant strength and conditioning professionals and facility supervisors contribute to the upkeep and management of the facility.

content: Assistant strength and conditioning professionals effectively and properly motivate athletes to achieve maximum potential in all areas of performance. The facility supervisor should effectively and properly motivate athletes to achieve their maximum potential in all areas of performance.
summary: Both assistant strength and conditioning professionals and facility supervisors play a role in athlete motivation and performance enhancement.

content: Assistant strength and conditioning professionals anticipate potential risks of injury, take measures to remove them, and, in the event of an injury, have the ability to implement emergency medical procedures. The facility supervisor should anticipate potential risks of injury, take measures to eliminate or reduce them, and alert participants to these risks. In the event of an injury, the facility supervisor should be capable of implementing an emergency medical procedure.
summary: Both roles prioritize safety by identifying and mitigating risks, and responding effectively to injuries.

content: Assistant strength and conditioning professionals keep updated on current strength and conditioning research. Assistant strength and conditioning professionals achieve and maintain professional certification(s) with continuing education requirements and a code of ethics, such as the CSCS credential offered through the NSCA. Depending on the professional’s scope of activities, responsibilities, and knowledge requirements, relevant certifications offered by other governing bodies may also be appropriate. The facility supervisor should be involved with professional activities (e.g., be an NSCA member).
summary: Both roles emphasize professional development through ongoing education and certifications.

content: Assistant strength and conditioning professionals perform other duties as assigned by the director. The facility supervisor should perform other duties as assigned by the director and assistant strength and conditioning professional.
summary: Both roles demonstrate flexibility and willingness to undertake additional tasks as needed.

content: The strength and conditioning director should be prepared to provide a complete list of new equipment that will be needed for the entire year when the athletic director asks for the annual strength and conditioning budget. In some cases, it is a good idea to include a capital outlay category for new construction or new purchases when providing the annual strength and conditioning budget. Only a few categories in a budget have discretionary funds that allow freedom to buy new items. Some expenses, such as facility utilities (e.g., electricity, water, heating) and custodial services, may not be the financial responsibility of the strength and conditioning department. Common categories that can be controlled are telephone, publicity or printing, postage, clerical supplies, computers, travel to professional conventions, and equipment rental. The director should make sure there is money available in the budget before requesting a purchase.
summary: The strength and conditioning director is responsible for budget planning and justification, ensuring appropriate allocation of resources.

content: The director and the assistant strength and conditioning professionals usually have full access to the facility within their professional and ethical discretion. Facility supervisors typically have access to the facility only during normal operational hours if they have been performing their duties and responsibilities to the satisfaction of the director.
summary: Access to the facility is granted based on roles and responsibilities, with varying levels of access for different personnel.

content: Staff members should be strongly encouraged to follow a personal resistance training and fitness program. Staff training should preferably occur when sport teams are not in the facility. The earlier hours of weekdays are usually the best time for staff to work out.
summary: The facility promotes a culture of fitness and provides guidance on scheduling and best practices for staff training.

content: The director and the assistant strength and conditioning professionals usually have full access to the facility within their professional and ethical discretion. Facility supervisors typically have access to the facility only during normal operational hours if they have been performing their duties and responsibilities to the satisfaction of the director.
summary: Access to the strength and conditioning facility is granted to different personnel based on their roles and performance.

content: Staff members should be strongly encouraged to follow a personal resistance training and fitness program. Staff training should preferably occur when sport teams are not in the facility. The earlier hours of weekdays are usually the best time for staff to work out.
summary: Staff are encouraged to prioritize their own fitness and training should be scheduled strategically.

content: If an athlete is also present, the staff member is expected to help monitor the facility and provide spotting assistance when necessary. A staff member should not be permitted to work out when the team for which he or she is responsible is training.
summary: Staff responsibilities include athlete supervision and prioritizing team training over personal workouts.

content: All staff members should refrain from having personal relationships of any type with any of the athletes or other staff members. If a relationship existed prior to an individual’s becoming a staff member, a professionally objective attitude should be maintained while both parties are present in the facility.
summary: Professional boundaries and ethical conduct are crucial to maintain a healthy work environment.

content: Program policies should include a clear description of acceptable coach–athlete interaction. The strength and conditioning professional may be faced with an athlete who is distressed due to personal reasons. Possible areas of concern for athletes might include family and personal relationships, drug use, sexual harassment, and academic demands.
summary: Clear guidelines for coach-athlete interaction and support for athletes facing personal challenges are essential.

content: Strength and conditioning professionals should not attempt to provide counseling. Strength and conditioning professionals should listen to athletes, mirror what they say, and then refer them to an appropriate professional. All such incidents or meetings should be well documented.
summary: Strength and conditioning professionals should provide support and referrals for athletes needing counseling services.

content: All staff members should make a daily effort to increase their knowledge of scientific and practical applications of strength and conditioning concepts. Staff members can increase their knowledge through active involvement in a professional organization like the National Strength and Conditioning Association (NSCA) or an academic institution. Staff members can increase their knowledge through continual personal development (attending conferences and clinics, studying professional journals, giving presentations, asking questions, etc.) or simply playing an active role in the day-to-day activities of the facility.
summary: Continuous professional development is crucial for strength and conditioning staff.

content: Continuing education is a necessary part of maintaining a professional certification and reducing liability exposure, especially in the area of supervision and instruction. Litigation issues in this area often involve questions of ‘professional instructor qualifications,’ which include continuing education.
summary: Continuing education is essential for maintaining professional credentials and mitigating legal risks.

content: It is helpful to have a centralized bulletin board where notices, staff messages, facility hours of operation, evacuation procedures, and other vital information can be posted. When a staff member or athlete first arrives at the strength and conditioning facility each day, he or she should check the bulletin board for new messages.
summary: A centralized communication system ensures that staff and athletes are informed about important information.

content: Signage, including safety and usage instructions as well as warnings and notices, should be posted so as to be seen by users prior to their use of the facility.
summary: Clear signage is crucial for safety and proper facility usage.

content: There are generally three types of outside groups that may come into the strength and conditioning facility. Large groups and the general public should not be allowed inside the facility when athletes are training. A viewing area should be provided so that visitors can see into the facility without disturbing the athletes. Guests of the athletic director or a sport coach may tour the inside of the strength and conditioning facility. A path should be created to allow them to see the activity up close without interfering with the action. Recruits or other special guests receive a customized one-on-one tour.
summary: Access to the facility for outside groups is managed to prioritize athlete training and safety.

content: For each exercise that is included on the sport teams’ workout sheets, including plyometric, agility, and speed development drills, the director needs to inform the staff regarding how the exercise technique should be taught to athletes and also provide demonstrations and instruction. A copy of all programs designed by staff (i.e., the list of exercises to be performed) should be on file in the director’s office before a team begins their program.
summary: The strength and conditioning director provides clear exercise instructions and program documentation.

content: From a safety standpoint, spotting, in conjunction with the use of appropriate safety equipment, is critical. Staff should effectively communicate with the lifter to ensure safe, proper, and effective spotting.
summary: Spotting and communication are essential for safe weightlifting.

content: At the beginning of each school semester or sport season, the strength and conditioning director needs to meet with each head sport coach to determine the initial physical testing schedule and, depending on the strength and conditioning professional’s background and experience, which tests to perform. It can be a challenge to figure out how to test all the athletes from the different sports. At the very least, the strength and conditioning staff needs to determine the overall testing schedule based on the number of athletes and available staff. The strength and conditioning staff needs to reserve the facility (if applicable). The strength and conditioning staff needs to notify the athletes and coaches of the dates and times of testing. The strength and conditioning staff needs to notify the school sports information department of dates, times, location, and so on, so that video or photos can be taken of new records or selected performers (if applicable). The strength and conditioning staff needs to prepare the facility and equipment for testing.
summary: The strength and conditioning staff coordinates and executes the physical testing schedule for all athletes.

content: At the very least, the strength and conditioning staff needs to determine the overall testing schedule based on the number of athletes and available staff. The strength and conditioning staff needs to reserve the facility (if applicable). The strength and conditioning staff needs to notify the athletes and coaches of the dates and times of testing. The strength and conditioning staff needs to notify the school sports information department of dates, times, location, and so on, so that video or photos can be taken of new records or selected performers (if applicable). The strength and conditioning staff needs to prepare the facility and equipment for testing. The strength and conditioning staff needs to develop data collection sheets for each athlete. The strength and conditioning staff needs to explain to the athletes any safety tips, scoring methods for tests, traffic flow procedures, and practice opportunities.
summary: The strength and conditioning staff is responsible for planning and executing athlete testing, including scheduling, communication, preparation, and athlete education.

content: In addition to a testing schedule, a regular training schedule for each team should be established. Ensuring that established supervision and instruction guidelines are followed requires effective scheduling. Training sessions must be scheduled at specific times to ensure that recommended staff-to-athlete ratios are maintained (refer to chapter 21 for staff-to-athlete ratios) and to ensure proper management of users during peak usage times (6). A policy should be developed that designates how priority is assigned in the scheduling process. This is especially important for institutions with smaller facilities, those lacking adequate human resources, and those that have multiple facilities.
summary: Effective scheduling of training sessions is crucial for ensuring proper supervision, staff-to-athlete ratios, and management of facility usage.

content: All staff members must be familiar with every exercise on each athlete’s workout sheet and should not advise an athlete to perform exercises that are not listed on the workout sheet unless this has been suggested by the director or the sports medicine staff. A strong commitment to follow the recommended program is required. Providing and promoting a motivational atmosphere is critical to a successful strength and conditioning program. The strength and conditioning staff needs to develop a strategy of encouraging an athlete to optimal performance and rewarding accomplishment and outstanding effort.
summary: Staff adherence to program guidelines, athlete motivation, and a positive training environment are essential for program success.

content: Some of the more obvious facets of this effort include planning the purchase of awards in advance, choosing how and when the awards will be presented, creating mini-goals and mini-awards as stepping-stones to larger goals, giving equal awards to men and women, posting new school records immediately after they are set, and having reasonable expectations of each individual athlete’s abilities. An award can be motivational, but sometimes only one athlete out of the entire team is the winner, or awards may go to only a few. This type of award is acceptable if it is balanced with ways in which other athletes are rewarded. A weekly newsletter that highlights performance accomplishments is a good way to motivate and reward multiple athletes for a job well done (1, 3).
summary: Motivating athletes through recognition, awards, and highlighting achievements is crucial for program success.

content: Adherence to a facility’s written policies and procedures is integral to the achievement of the strength and conditioning program’s goals and objectives. The best way to satisfy the scheduling needs of all teams without conflicts in facility usage is to survey teams to determine their preferred training times. This information is from NSCA, 2008, Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning, 3rd ed., T.R. Baechle and R.W. Earle (eds.), (Champaign, IL: Human Kinetics.
summary: Adherence to policies, effective scheduling, and understanding team preferences are essential for successful facility management.

content: Two key issues in facility administration are access and supervision. It is ultimately the professional and legal responsibility of the institution to ensure that the strength and conditioning facility is supervised whenever it is occupied. Before an athlete is allowed access to the strength and conditioning facility, preparticipation screening and clearance must be required. Standard 1.1 ‘requires athletes to undergo health care provider screening and clearance prior to participation.’ Procedures should be in place to ensure that documentation of screening and clearance of each athlete is on record in the main office of the strength and conditioning program before athletes are allowed to participate.
summary: Facility access is governed by preparticipation screening, clearance procedures, and the requirement for constant supervision.

content: To focus the attention and efforts of the strength and conditioning staff on a target training population, certain eligibility requirements should be established. The following is a list of typical individuals or groups who are allowed to use the strength and conditioning facility at an institution: Full- or part-time student-athletes participating in an athletic department-sponsored sport, Newly incoming and just-transferred student-athletes who have registered for school and have confirmation of team status as designated by the head sport coach, Students in physical education classes, All athletic department coaching and administrative staff, All sports medicine department staff, Alumni athletes who participated in an athletic department-sponsored sport and completed their eligibility, Individuals and groups approved by the athletic director or strength and conditioning director.
summary: Eligibility criteria define who can access the strength and conditioning facility, typically including student-athletes, coaches, staff, and approved individuals.

content: Other individuals or groups may request access to the strength and conditioning facility. These persons or groups must receive prior approval by the athletic director or strength and conditioning director and should have a prearranged schedule that specifies when they will use the facility so proper supervision can be provided. Use must be preapproved by the athletic director. Use must be preapproved by the strength and conditioning director. The program or session must be supervised by strength and conditioning department staff. The program or session must be scheduled during off-hours when athletes are not present. The individual or organization must supply written proof of additional liability insurance. All participants must sign a release agreement form. All participants must follow the rules and regulations of the strength and conditioning facility. The athletic director and the strength and conditioning director have the right to limit an individual’s or group’s access, if warranted.
summary: Access for non-standard users requires pre-approval, scheduling, supervision, insurance, and adherence to facility rules.

content: The program or session must be scheduled during off-hours when athletes are not present. All participants must sign a release agreement form. All participants must follow the rules and regulations of the strength and conditioning facility. The athletic director and the strength and conditioning director have the right to limit an individual’s or group’s access, if warranted. If a person who has not been approved for access is in the facility, it is the staff’s duty to explain the eligibility criteria to that person.
summary: This chunk outlines facility access protocols, emphasizing off-hours scheduling, required release agreements, adherence to rules, and staff responsibilities for managing access.

content: All participants must sign a liability waiver. Reducing liability for on-premise injuries during use of the facility is critical in any risk management strategy.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of liability waivers and their role in risk management for the facility.

content: The document is titled 'Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning'. The document contains a sample release agreement for facility use by an outside individual or group. The agreement is for use of the ________ strength and conditioning facility.
summary: This chunk describes the document's title, content (including a sample release agreement), and its application to a specific strength and conditioning facility.

content: The individual acknowledges the risks involved in using the strength and conditioning facility and its equipment. The individual assumes any risk to their person should they use the strength and conditioning facility and its equipment. The individual agrees to hold harmless the ________, the coaches, athletic trainers, supervisors, or any other employees in the event of injury.
summary: This chunk details the individual's acknowledgment of risks, assumption of liability, and agreement to hold harmless the facility and its personnel.

content: The individual agrees to modify their workout to conform to the recommendations of the ________ strength and conditioning staff if asked to do so. The individual agrees to leave the strength and conditioning facility if asked to do so by a member of the ________ strength and conditioning staff.
summary: This chunk outlines the individual's agreement to follow staff recommendations and to leave the facility upon request.

content: Strength and conditioning facility policies and rules are important for providing participants with guidelines for conduct and behavior, keeping order, and keeping the program on course toward the goal of providing a safe, clean, and professional training environment. Refer to figure 22.9 for a list of common facility rules and policies. These rules should be posted in the training area where they can be easily seen.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of facility rules and policies for maintaining a safe, organized, and professional training environment.

content: To ensure that athletes adhere to the facility rules, the strength and conditioning director may want to require them to sign and date a copy of the facility rules and guidelines to acknowledge that they understand and will adhere to all rules prior to facility use. This process will reduce the possibility of claims that they did not know the repercussions of aberrant behavior.
summary: This chunk suggests requiring athletes to sign and acknowledge facility rules to minimize liability claims.

content: The facility rules need to be enforced, with possible disciplinary actions posted, documented, and levied in proportion to the offense. First offense: A verbal warning by a staff member, an explanation of the nature and importance of the rule or guideline that was broken, and a reminder of the disciplinary action that will result from a second offense. Second offense: Dismissal from the facility for one day, documentation of the offense by the staff member, correspondence with the athlete’s sport coach, and a reminder of the disciplinary action that will result from a third offense.
summary: This chunk outlines a disciplinary system for enforcing facility rules, starting with verbal warnings and escalating to dismissal for repeated offenses.

content: Third offense: Dismissal from the facility for one week, documentation of the offense by the staff member, correspondence with the athlete’s sport coach, and a reminder of the disciplinary action that will result from a fourth offense. Fourth offense: Dismissal from the facility for the remainder of the year, documentation of the offense by the staff member, correspondence with the athlete’s sport coach and the athletic director, and a reminder of the disciplinary action that will result from a fifth offense. Fifth offense: Permanent dismissal from the facility, documentation of the offense by the staff member, and correspondence with the athlete’s sport coach and the athletic director.
summary: This chunk continues the disciplinary system, detailing escalating consequences for repeated rule violations.

content: The strength and conditioning staff should try to foresee potential emergency situations, if possible. An emergency plan should list strategies for handling emergency situations; provide names, titles, and telephone numbers of important individuals to contact; and identify which rooms are lockdown rooms. All staff members should be familiar with the emergency plan so that they can respond appropriately.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of emergency preparedness, outlining key elements of an emergency plan and staff training.

content: Also, the emergency plan should be in writing and posted for easy reference in several prominent locations in the facility. Every exit in the weight training facility should be marked and should be familiar to all staff members and athletes.
summary: This chunk further details emergency plan implementation, emphasizing accessibility and familiarity with exits.

content: To allow continual monitoring of who is entering and leaving the facility, there should be only one main entrance and exit. Any other exits are to be used only in an emergency or if facility occupants are so instructed by the strength and conditioning staff.
summary: This chunk outlines security protocols, emphasizing a single main entrance and exit for monitoring and limiting access to other exits.

content: The telephone is to be used only by the strength and conditioning staff. Personal calls by the staff should be kept to a minimum. Other individuals should not be allowed to use the telephone unless they receive permission from a staff member. Rules and regulations of certain governing bodies, like the National Collegiate Athletic Association, may restrict or prevent student-athletes from using departmental telephones.
summary: This chunk establishes guidelines for telephone use, prioritizing staff needs and adhering to governing body regulations.

content: To answer the telephone, the staff member should answer with a friendly 'Training facility; this is [staff member’s name]' or 'Good morning/afternoon/evening; [name of program or facility]'.
summary: This chunk provides specific instructions for answering the telephone professionally.

content: Appendix G of Strength and Conditioning Professional Standards and Guidelines provides details on emergency care and planning (6).
summary: This chunk directs readers to a specific resource for further information on emergency care and planning.

content: Every exit in the weight training facility should be marked and should be familiar to all staff members and athletes. To allow continual monitoring of who is entering and leaving the facility, there should be only one main entrance and exit. Any other exits are to be used only in an emergency or if facility occupants are so instructed by the strength and conditioning staff.
summary: Facility exits should be marked and known, with a single main entrance/exit for monitoring.

content: Appendix G of Strength and Conditioning Professional Standards and Guidelines provides details on emergency care and planning (6). The appendix discusses components of emergency care and provides an emergency plan template. It is recommended that professionals consult Strength and Conditioning Professional Standards and Guidelines when developing an emergency plan specific to their own setting.
summary: Emergency care and planning guidelines are provided in Appendix G of Strength and Conditioning Professional Standards and Guidelines.

content: Also, the emergency plan should be in writing and posted for easy reference in several prominent locations in the facility. A list of rules and policies should be posted in the training area where it can be easily seen.
summary: Emergency plans should be written, posted, and rules/policies should be visible in the training area.

content: Prior to participation, athletes must go through a preparticipation screening and clearance procedure. Prior to participation, all athletes must attend an orientation on common risks involved in resistance training, the proper execution of various exercises, and the possible consequences if proper technique is not used.
summary: Athletes must undergo preparticipation screening and orientation before training.

content: Athletes must have a workout sheet, follow it, and record workout contents. If an athlete has an injury that inhibits a portion of the workout, the athlete must receive a modified program from the athletic medicine department that describes which exercises should be avoided and which ones may be substituted.
summary: Athletes need workout sheets, must record workouts, and receive modified programs for injuries.

content: Athletes are required to use locks on the ends of all barbells. No one should squat outside the squat or power rack. Bumper plates are required on the platforms for all power exercises.
summary: Safety protocols for barbell use and power exercises are mandatory.

content: Athletes must not wear weight belts when the belts could contact equipment upholstery. Athletes should move weights from the racks to the bar only. They should never set plates on the floor or lean them against equipment or walls. Athletes should return dumbbells to the rack in the proper order. Athletes should not drop or throw weights or dumbbells.
summary: Proper weight handling and equipment etiquette are required.

content: Athletes should show respect for equipment and facilities at all times; spitting in or defacing the facility is not tolerated and will result in immediate expulsion. The weight room requires concentration. Horseplay, loud or offensive language, or temper tantrums are not permitted.
summary: Respect for equipment and a focused environment are essential in the weight room.

content: The staff offices and telephones are off limits to athletes unless permission to use them is given. Athletes should wear proper training attire, particularly shirts and athletic shoes, at all times.
summary: Staff areas and appropriate attire are expected in the weight room.

content: Athletes should use spotters for exercises that place the bar on the back or front shoulders and exercises that involve a bar or dumbbells moving over the face or above the head. Power exercises are not spotted.
summary: Spotters are required for specific exercises, but not for power exercises.

content: Athletes should immediately report any facility-related injury or facility or equipment irregularity to the supervisor on duty.
summary: Prompt reporting of injuries or equipment issues is necessary.

content: Tobacco, food, chewing gum, glass bottles, cans, alcohol, drugs, and banned substances are not allowed in the strength and conditioning facility; plastic water bottles are acceptable.
summary: Prohibited items are not allowed in the facility, with plastic water bottles being an exception.

content: Supervisors are not responsible for users’ personal belongings or lost or stolen items.
summary: Supervisors are not liable for lost or stolen belongings.

content: Jewelry such as loose necklaces, bracelets, hanging earrings, and watches should not be worn.
summary: Certain jewelry is prohibited during training.

content: Athletes should keep feet off the walls. Athletes should minimize chalk and powder on the floor.
summary: Maintaining a clean and safe environment is important.

content: All guests and visitors must report to the office for signing of the waiver form. Former athletes must have their programs preapproved by a staff supervisor and must sign a waiver form.
summary: Guests and former athletes require waivers and program approval.

content: Athletic department personnel can use these facilities for personal workouts if they do not interfere with the needs of the athletes. Non–athletic department personnel are allowed to use the facilities with permission of the athletic director after signing a waiver form.
summary: Usage policies for athletic department and non-department personnel are outlined.

content: Recognized users may include athletes, students, guests, staff, faculty with permission, former athletes, family members, and visiting teams.
summary: Permitted users of the facility are defined.

content: Equipment leaving the weight room shall be checked out by the supervisor and recorded at the supervisor’s desk.
summary: Equipment checkout procedures are in place.

content: The on-duty supervisors have authority over all weight room conduct and use of equipment and may expel an athlete from the facility for failure to follow instructions.
summary: Supervisors have authority over weight room conduct and can expel violators.

content: In non-life-threatening situations, step 1 is to contact the athletic medicine department; if there is no answer, contact Campus Security.
